"He [Mr Taylor] said this kind of thing must happen when you're fighting war," he said.
The RUF has a reputation for brutality, such as chopping off the arms and legs of civilians, as well as killings and rape.
Mr Blah also said that Mr Taylor mediated in arguments between the rebels. He said on one occasion a rebel commander had disagreed with the Liberian president's judgement but said he had to accept "the chief's decision".
Mr Blah further said that RUF fighters tried to help Mr Taylor's forces against Liberia's Lurd rebels, who eventually forced him from power in 2003.
Mr Taylor sat taking notes during his former deputy's testimony. Mr Blah earlier said that the commander of one of Mr Taylor's units in Liberia "had a habit of eating fellow human beings".
He said he saw Nelson Gaye roast a human hand on a fire and eat it with boiled cassava.
Mr Blah said that Mr Gaye's Marine unit was one of Mr Taylor's forces, which he had been told should not be pursued if they committed atrocities.
But Mr Taylor is only on trial for his alleged role in the Sierra Leone conflict, not that in Liberia.
As Mr Blah took the witness stand on Monday, he was careful not to look at the man he served for nearly 20 years.
He gave the court a detailed account of Mr Taylor's bloody rise to power and acknowledged the use of child soldiers.
Mr Blah became vice-president of Liberia in 2000. He became president after Mr Taylor was forced into exile in 2003.
However, he was only Liberian president for two months, until October 2003, when a United Nations-backed transitional government was sworn in.
He came to the Hague reluctantly, but said he intended to tell the truth. "I'm not going to crucify him - he's my former boss," Mr Blah told the BBC's Focus on Africa programme before travelling to the Hague.
"I have nothing personal against President Taylor - we worked together almost like brothers; we had a revolution going together, so I don't think I'm going to betray him."
'Aggressive' In court, Mr Blah provided a detailed insider's account of the early days of the civil war.
He said he was one of the first 20 or so rebel fighters who received training at a military base in Burkina Faso.
It was not until a larger group was assembled at a military camp in Libya that Mr Blah met Mr Taylor.
They trained alongside rebels from The Gambia, Sierra Leone and the Philippines, all of them supported by the Libyan authorities.
He told the court that when the time came to invade Liberia, three truck loads of weapons were provided by the defence minister of Ivory Coast.
Within days of crossing into Liberia in 1990, Mr Blah says he was briefly detained by child rebel fighters recruited by his own side.
He said they were used because they took orders and were "unreasonable and aggressive".
A lawyer representing Mr Taylor, Terry Munyard, told the BBC his client was not worried about Mr Blah's evidence and if he told the truth there was nothing to fear.
Mr Taylor took up arms in Liberia in 1989, before being elected president in 1997 after a peace deal.
He is accused of sending guns and fighters to Sierra Leone in exchange for diamonds.
During Sierra Leone's decade-long civil war, which officially ended in 2002, tens of thousands of people died and thousands more were mutilated or raped.
Mr Taylor's case has been transferred from Sierra Leone to The Hague for security reasons, although it is still being conducted by the UN-backed Special Court for Sierra Leone.

Original_Search_Title: FG Moves to Convert Coal to Fuel
Headline: Nigeria: FG Moves to Convert Coal to Fuel
Body_Text:
Enugu — THE Federal Government has directed the Project Development Institute (PRODA), Enugu to explore ways of converting coal to fuel as part of measures to reduce over-reliance on petroleum products for energy.
Minister of State for Science and Technology, Dr Alhassan Bako Zaku, who gave the directive when he visited the institute as part of his one day facility tour of Federal Parastatals in Enugu State, said the government was uncomfortable with the underutilization of the large coal deposit in the country.
He asked the institute to do everything within its reach to carry out the directive, saying Nigeria's over reliance on crude oil for the production of fuel in spite of the availability of other natural resources that could generate the product, was unacceptable.
"I have directed the Director General of PRODA to work towards the conversion of coal to fuel. It is something that we can do. We cannot continue to over rely on crude oil for fuel. Since most activities require fuel, there is need to look for other sources to generate the product. Since we are blessed with coal deposits, we can work towards its conversion to fuel," Zaku said.
The Minister pointed out that with the current erratic power supply in the country there was need for the invention of locally made machines that would not rely on electricity to function.
According to him, there was no need to manufacture for instance, Cassava processing machine that can only function with electricity. "We should be aware that some of these processing machines are mostly needed in the villages. The villages may not have electricity. How then can we you justify the invention of processing machines that rely on electricity to function?" he stated.
The minister expressed surprise over the high degree of decay at PRODA and ordered the DG to forward a request on how to improve the situation of things at the institute.

Original_Search_Title: Tanga to Host Sido Exhibition
Headline: Tanzania: Tanga to Host Sido Exhibition
Body_Text:
Tanga — About 210 entrepreneurs from the northern zone regions are to take part in the 8th trade exhibition organised by the Small Industries Development Organisation (SIDO) which begins here today.
Tanga regional SIDO manager Suleiman Mutani yesterday told reporters that the four-day exhibition will be officially opened by the minister for Industries and Marketing, Dr Mary Nagu and closed by the Tanga regional commissioner Mohamed Abdulaziz.
Mr Mutani named other exhibitors who have shown interest to participate in the show as Iringa, Mbeya and Kigoma regions, and Pemba. Others will be from Kenya and Uganda.
He said the exhibition is aimed at promoting products manufactured by small and medium scale entrepreneurs and to learn modern marketing strategies.
The SIDO regional manager said over 50 entrepreneurs have been drawn from Kilimanjaro, Manyara and Arusha regions while 160 are from Tanga Region.
He said various products will be exhibited during the four-day show, listing them food items, handicraft, leather goods and hair styling.
He said Tanga district will display cassava flour and fish processing, while Mkinga district will have cashnuts products, Pangani to showcase coconut by-products and Muheza will show fruit processing.
He said Korogwe district will have on display sunflower oil and vegetables. Hamdeni and Kilindi districts will establish exhibit honey and hides.

Original_Search_Title: Corruption Versus Corruption
Headline: Nigeria: Corruption Versus Corruption
Body_Text:
Abuja — One of the features of Obasanjo's second term as elected president was the setting up of what was called Economic Management Team (EMT), which consisted of those who were called "Technorats". The Economic Management Team usurped the powers of the constitutionally recognised National Economic Council headed by the vice present of the federation. Today, each of these members of the EMT is battling to save his or her neck based on the ongoing exposure of the tenure of the Obasanjo administration.
The team was headed by that graceful African woman, Dr. Ngozi Okonjo- Iweala, now in the World Bank as managing director. She had to take excuse from her current employers to come and face the House of Representatives Committee on Power probe into the monies spent in the power sector between 1999 and 2007. It turned out that as she was busy negotiating Nigeria out of the Paris Club of creditors and giving direction to the reform agenda, some people were busy stealing. She was like holding the horns for others to milk. But she was very frank when she faced the committee and based on that she still retains her integrity intact.
Another member of the team who is also now in the World Bank is that controversial woman, Mrs. Oby Ezekwesili. She headed what was called Due Process Office as presidential aide before joining the cabinet first as Solid minerals minister and later education minister. Her tenure is as controversial as her person. We have not heard the last word on this due process contraption and the woman who el-Rufa'i calls his sister. She was one of the creations of the Obasanjo era who is yet to explain why she wanted to sell the federal unity schools, just because she is obsessed about privatisation.
A member of the powerful EMT is the former chairman of the Economic and Financial Crimes Commission (EFCC) who is now attending a course at the National Institute in Kuru, Jos. Mallam Nuhu Ribadu started very well but like many things Obasanjo initiated, the anti- corruption effort was derailed. Ribadu's EFCC went after only those opposed to Obasanjo and he, unlike those with security background, was talking too much. He ended up politicising himself and his position, thereby tying his destiny to the Obasanjo regime. He is yet to be associated with any sleaze so far. But the probe has just begun so it is too early to celebrate. But Ribadu can not afford to soil his good family name. I don't think so.
Another critical member of the EMT is Prof. Chukwuma Soludo, first as economic adviser and chief executive of the National Planning Commission and now Central Bank governor. He is a brilliant fellow who has a multiplicity of economic models for Nigeria. He was able to get the National Assembly to pass an amendment to the CBN Act which was signed into law by the president a few days to the hand over in May. That amendment provided that any governor or deputy governor of the CBN cannot be removed except by a two-third majority of the Senate. Unfortunately, he was appointed before the law and it cannot take retroactive power so it will only cover his successor. In any case, he is now facing a panel over the Africa Finance Corporation (AFC), one of his pet projects. He is a first class brain but very overzealous.
By after, the most famous member of the powerful EMT is Mallam Nasir el-Rufa'i. He is also a brilliant fellow with a first class mind. He has a lion-heart to take harsh decisions and implement them. He sanitised Abuja and made the city clean and safe. But his tenure is being investigated by the Senate Committee on FCT headed by Senator Sodangi. Trust el-Rufai and his people, they have successfully put Sodangi and his committee on the defensive by making public the plots of land allocated to Sodangi and a member of the committee, Senator Adeyemi in the FCT land allocations during the period under investigation.
What comes out of this is the fact that you cannot wash faeces with urine. A corrupt person cannot probe corruption. Secondly, it is very clear that this process is irreversible and that every public officer must be ready to give account of his or her stewardship. In the end, what Hon. Elumelu and his committee are doing is a continuation of what Vincent Azie, the former auditor-general of the federation started in the first term of Obasanjo. This is going to be very good for the system and the country.
It is now very clear that as Obasanjo kept telling us through a presidential initiative to grow cassava, he was busy with his people helping themselves with our money, houses and lands. The blind trusts are now having eyes after all. And if they are able to steal billions of naira will they be able to steal more years to enable them spend the money? Another funny revelation that came out was that the office of the auditor-general issued a statement that the immediate past GMD of the NNPC, Mr. Funsho Kupolokun discouraged the auditors from auditing NNPC accounts on the pretext that it is too technical which require special training to interpret. Now that NNPC will be probed in public, they will teach all of us that special skill so that the GMD and his petroleum minister for seven years will tell us what we got and how it was spent.
Nigeria is undergoing a silent revolution, and this is happening without any much noise. And in the end, God is answering the prayers of the masses. Twenty-five years after the death of Mallam Aminu Kano, a PRP member is president and he is helping to deal with the corrupt class like at no other time. What was latent is now apparent. May God continue to bless Nigeria! Meanwhile, there are interesting times ahead of us and our beloved country.

Original_Search_Title: Local Governments Must Show Leadership - Guebuza
Headline: Mozambique: Local Governments Must Show Leadership - Guebuza
Body_Text:
Nametil — Mozambican President Armando Guebuza on Saturday urged the members of local governments to stop acting simply as chiefs, and to promote good leadership practices so that their activities are reflected positively in improved living standards.
Guebuza was addressing an extraordinary meeting of the Nampula provincial government, held in Nametil, capital of Mogovolas district. The meeting marked the start of a five day working visit by Guebuza to this northern province.
He argued that good leadership is key to increasing production. The districts could not afford to wait from someone from outside to come along and build houses needed for teachers and other government staff.
He recalled that on many occasions, districts administrators had complained they don't have the staff they need in their districts because they cannot offer them houses. But was there really a shortage of raw materials to make bricks in the districts?
He found it inexplicable that an Administrator could sit back, cross his arms and complain of a shortage of staff, simply because he had never taken the initiative to have decent houses built with locally produced bricks. Guebuza was sure that such houses, of reasonable quality, would help attract the staff needed.
"Let us not hold up development", Guebuza declared. "We must make use of the leadership capacity that many of us have in order to help overcome difficulties. For example, district administrators must be chiefs, but at the same time they must be leaders so that we can achieve good results very urgently".
For the President, leadership must also be expressed positively in food production, particularly when faced with the current international food crisis. "Increased production must begin today and not tomorrow. Nampula enjoys all the conditions to command solutions to the food crisis", he declared. Merely because Nampula already grows a great deal of cassava was no reason not to produce more, and send it to other parts of the country he added.
Guebuza warned that the international food crisis might eventually affect everybody. It was the government's responsibility to reduce the impact of this crisis, he added, and the government had already developed some ideas, which would soon be put before Mozambican society and particularly before the business class.

Original_Search_Title: UNIDO Boss Tasks Country On African Economic Role
Headline: Nigeria: UNIDO Boss Tasks Country On African Economic Role
Body_Text:
Abuja — The director general of the United Nations Industrial Development Organisation (UNIDO), Dr. Kandel Yumkella, has called on Nigeria to rise up to the continental role of providing an economic pedestal for the African continent.
Yumkella, who addressed the local press yesterday in Abuja, also observed that the nation can achieve its targets of Vision 2020 if the necessary things are put in place by the Federal Government.
According to the UNIDO boss, who hinted that the continent of Africa is in dire need of "a big brother", the country has what it takes to turn-around the economy of the most populated black continent.
"We need a big brother around Africa. We need one around which the economy of the African continent can be built. Nigeria has all its takes to be that rallying economic force on the continent and even beyond," he said.
He, however, observed that the country's energy sector would be the deciding factor on how successful it could go to play this Herculean, but all-important role in the continent.
"But there is a need for Nigeria to fix its energy sector, if it truly wishes to be a major player in the world economy. That way, the industries would wake up, flourish, and also provide the much needed jobs for the greater number of the populace in the country," he observed.
On the nation's target to become one of the world's 20 most industrialised nations by 2020, Yumkella particularly called on the Federal Government to pay attention to renewable energy.
According to him, "renewable energy remains the key issue in Nigeria's vision for 2020 targets. Nigeria can achieve its targets of being amongst the 20 most industrialised countries in the world by the year 2020, and even more. But to achieve this, it needs to dedicate itself to ensuring functional infrastructure for any business to thrive," he said.
He regretted that the country has failed to benefit from its cassava initiative, despite being the highest producer of cassava, adding that it has continuously failed to add value to the product and create wealth.
"Nigerian can create wealth by industrialising its cassava," he added.

Original_Search_Title: The Budo Inferno Was a Bomb Waiting to Explode
Headline: Uganda: The Budo Inferno Was a Bomb Waiting to Explode
Body_Text:
Kampala — I must have received one of the first text messages about the Budo fire from a friend in Glasgow, Scotland, inquiring whether I had heard what had happened at my alma mater just minutes after the news broke in Wakiso, late Monday evening.
This could not be my alma mater, better known to us the Old Students as Kabinja. In testament of today's technology, a couple of people in Europe, North America and South Asia knew of this tragedy as it unfolded via sms before the Fire Brigade had even arrived at the school. But with all that said, nobody prepared us for the biggest blow yet - "Death toll: 19 (burnt beyond recognition) with an expectation of it to rise".
This is by every measure a tragedy of epic proportions, and my heart, pain and tears go out to all the families and friends that have lost their loved ones in this fire. It could have been any one of us. Details are now emerging that this is now being investigated as a homicide. What kind of human being could be so heartless?
What could be disappointing than this horrendous occurrence is that this colossal loss of young lives did not have to happen. Many of us who attended this school agreed and predicted at one point or the other in the recent decade that it was not a matter of "if", but rather of "when" such a tragedy would happen, especially since this was not the first time fires had broken out at the school.
In Budo Junior, we found pride to know that many of us common folk went to school with some of the offspring of society's well-heeled. In my times - the 1990s, Abu Mayanja's(RIP) children, Kintu Musoke's daughter, Prince Junju (Kabaka Mutebi's son) among other were some of the reasons it was not hard to believe the school was the premier primary boarding school of the time - and with that fact in mind, premier fees were charged.
No other government boarding primary school in the nation charged more fees than Budo Junior. If you did not pay up promptly, you were instantly kicked out until all debts were settled. Bottom-line, this was not your average primary school where administrative problems with teachers and students were the order of the day.
William Kayongo was the head-teacher of Budo Junior from the late 1980s until early March 2008. He was the master-mind of a concerted and well-orchestrated effort to convince parents, staff and pupils that they were indeed getting the best quality education and care with what 'little" resources they had. For many it worked, but I and couple of others never, even for once, bought it. Something did not add up then - and more so, now.
Budo Junior was also the intersection where hard work by the teachers like Mrs. Bawuuba (widow to a former King's College Budo headteacher), Khaukha,, Mrs. Kasule, Kabuye who have given their lives to this thankless education career and had taught thousands of pupils with a honest thirst of learning.
However, lately, some dangerous precedents set in like top school authorities, which correlates with the deterioration of discipline amongst students, teachers and matrons alike.
A dangerous and reckless culture was tolerated where for example, a pupil losing an eye due to a stone throwing incident was not enough to shock the sensibilities of the student body and authorities for them to carry out a full-scale investigation to determine a culprit.
A corrupt atmosphere was allowed to thrive where parents brought items on top of a customary monetary "gift" every visiting day for the matrons so that they could go easy on their children, and in tandem; administrators looked the other way when obviously dangerous and illegal practices like locking pupils in their dorms at night were complained about.
This was an environment, where physical and corporal punishment were dished out. In this environment of fear and raw intimidation, such incidents were the order of the day and, therefore,went unabated and unreported.
However, What many of us found most disturbing was the fact that as enrollment went up at a rather high and discomforting rate and the schools increased fees significantly; the resources pretty much stayed the same. No new classrooms, dorms, latrines or teachers houses were built. A new multi-storied building was promised and was touted as a mixed-functional structure that would be our saviour, but for the longest time never went up beyond the foundation digging in the late 1990s and early 2000s.
We ate our meals while standing up because there were not enough benches to go around. The diet, as expected anywhere in Uganda, was a staple of posho and beans with an occasional sweet potatoes or cassava, which is fine. However, it did not take a culinary expert to know little or no effort was made to make the meal more palatable. We provided our own extra condiments like ghee and margarine to make the food tastier.
Many a times, hot water was served at break time instead of tea. And the inference was that we provided our own sugar, and tea - never mind that the dorms were always closed. What worried me the most though, was the dangerously low morale in the kitchen staff responsible for preparing meals for more than 1000 pupils.
I was a dining hall prefect in 1996. When I raised my concerns with those in-charge, I was in turn labelled as an ungrateful elitist snob - and having earlier transferred in from Kampala Parents' School did not help my case either. In fact, a teacher nicknamed me 'Kawowo k'emmere" (food spice).
At the sanatorium popularly known as the 'sick bay', it was not an unusual sight to see kids treating fellow kids, especially dressing each other's wounds. On occasions medicine was dispensed by the 12-year-olds in absence of the live-in nurse. Parents were encouraged to bring their own medicine, and for my entirety at the school, I never saw any kind of sterilisation in progress. A syringe was often used and thereafter capped and labelled with one's name only to be used later during the next medicine-round.
The living conditions are what should have raised any amount of red flags there possibly could be. As enrollment kept rising by the term, no more extra dorms, bathrooms or latrines were built. Double-deckers were done away like yesterdays fashion favouring triple-deckers and quarto-deckers. None of this dorms had ceilings.
The wisdom in having another's child lay down, less than a foot from the roof's wood beams stills defeats my comprehension. Dorms were packed until no other bed could fit in. It did not matter whether it blocked the windows and/or doors which effectively made the windows unusable as an emergency exit, but perhaps turned the dorms into an inescapable incinerator incase of a fire once the doors were locked from outside - which is exactly what happened here. When that option was exhausted, classrooms were turned into dorms starting with Mrs. Bombo's P.1 class.
No effort was made to create more bathrooms and when it rained, it was absolute horror to use the latrines as they would overflow with live and crawling maggots from the floor to the ceiling. It still surprises me that Budo Junior School, with all its well-educated alumni all-over the world and its many affluent parents, still has asbestos roofs given all the documented evidence of health dangers of asbestos.
We were fully convinced that this was as best as they came. And you could not get any better all-round education and service for your money anywhere - and many bought into the status quo, unquestionably. Why wouldn't they? After Budo Junior, some of us were accepted to King's College Budo. With its rich history, powerful alumni and superior academic reputation aside, the headmaster, Busuulwa, left no one guessing as to how serious he was about hygiene, welfare, safety and security of all the students under his responsibility.
He made it clear to all students, especially the girls that he was fully responsible for them while they were in school - a stark contrast with the attitude at Kabinja. I noticed in my first week that Busuulwa made his presence visible. And also, that there were no triple and quarto-deckers here; the dorms had ceilings (some even had curtains). Hallways were always kept clear to minimise a fire hazard and windows were never burglar proofed incase of a fire.
My dorm, South Africa, had an emergency exit on each of its three wings. The food although still a staple of posho and beans, had evidence of effort on the part of kitchen staff to make it more palatable and the staff led by Sebwolo had a high and positive morale.
Breakfast was served with bread daily. The sanatorium had a visible sterilizer; syringes were used only once and quickly disposed off. And the nurse, Muddukaki, with all her temper outbursts and shortcomings, most students agreed was the best nurse in the business. The comparison and contrasts went on for weeks and an argument could be made that this is comparing apples and bananas. That is what I thought too, until I found out that from 1992-2000, King's College and Budo Junior paid just about the same in tuition and fees. So my question then was, where had all the Budo Junior money disappeared to? This discovery made me absolutely bitter and mad at the fact that there was obviously something fishy about the whole Budo Junior situation.
I took every platform availed to me to voice my concerns out loud thereafter. Perhaps, the best forum I got was when I was selected by the S.4 government in October 2000 to O'level candidates, who had started their journey at Budo Junior at the annual thanksgiving service with a short speech. These services were usually attended by the parents of the O'level students, many of them movers and shakers in our society. But most importantly, many of the parents were also Old Students of both Budo Junior and/or King's College.
If there was a group or an individual that could initiate change, I believed he/she or them was seated in that chapel. My speech was light-hearted, punctuated with anecdotes and punch lines that kept with the spirit of emphasising the "good Budo (Budo Junior/King's College) times" that had bonded us all together. I, however, intentionally went off-script and warned that as we now laughed about the difficult past, not to forget that it was still a harsh reality to many still at the school. I subtly hinted at the perception of massive corruption and gross negligence that would inevitably lead to a public health and/or safety disaster amongst the pupils - I did not foresee a fire with such a devastating death toll though. As soon as I sat down, I was publicly rebuked and chastised at the pulpit for daring to suggest that things were essentially not world-class at Budo Junior.
With the benefit of hindsight now, many of those I have communicated with agree we should have done more. Watching the start of the daily UBC news broadcast last night as they focused on Nalongo Betty Kigozi as she found out that one of her children was among the dead was at the least; heartbreaking and sickening, a hard kick in the gut and any parent's worst night mare i.e. to bury your own child. Only in this case there wasn't even a body to identify as they were mostly burnt beyond recognition. Nalongo Kigozi could have been any single one of us.
If this was indeed an act of homicidal arson, the culprits should be brought to justice and if found guilty they should receive the maximum sentence allowed. But equally guilty, are those who over the years bred an environment and circumstances that could allow such a massive loss of life of society's most innocent; as a result of pure, and yet unacceptable negligence.
Let us all remember, these were eight and nine-year-old girls that were selfishly used as pawns in a game of greed, corruption, and work politics; and they ultimately paid with their own young lives. This was also a result of an arrogant betrayal of public trust of the worst kind where personal enrichment was prioritised over public health, safety and security.
In China, the Budo Junior team would have signed their own death warrants the moment the first child died. If you can afford to change into your new cars every 17 weeks, but cannot have the judgment to at least buy one fire extinguisher for each dorm and also have a fire exit; and yet as a matter of policy, continue packing in pupils like "soy beans" (quoting Gen. Kayihura) in these dorms/classrooms - then you have no business heading a school.
If you are overseas for more than 25% of the term time representing the Lions' Club or the Rotary Club, but not the school that that pays your salary - then step aside, and let somebody who will be more available and have the children's interest at heart take over the reins of leadership. There was a systemic failure of leadership from the top that has now had us paying a price too high to comprehend - children's innocent lives. There should be a collective outrage at this tragedy that demands nothing short of absolute accountability from this school's leadership.
Current pupils, the school's Old Boys and girls, teachers, Parents everywhere and policy & law makers should ask questions and expect only satisfactory answers. What is sad to note is that it is common knowledge this kind of thing - complete disregard of children's public safety, is going on in boarding schools all over Uganda. And sad as it may be, this is just a tip of the iceberg. Questions like these should be answered; how could one individual receive money, sign for it, and deposit in the bank and withdraw it at-will? Were there any control systems in place or 'segregation-of -duties' as we accountants call it to prevent abuse of office, fraud and embezzlement? Somebody should ask long-time education permanent secretary, Lubanga.
How a government-funded school could have the same head-teacher for over 20 years and what good does this serve? How and who in the ministry of education enabled this to happen? Does the ministry have inspectors that enforce the health and building codes and also make sure that the standards are maintained as the law requires? What was the ministry's response in the last decade when citizens complained that such a disaster was waiting to happen?
As this dark and painful chapter gets written in our country's history, lets hope that the children's lives lost will not be in total vain- and we will learn some kind of lesson to avoid such preventable disasters. Let us not just have another investigation or commission of inquiry whose recommendations will live to gather dust in the cabinet's library. But rather let this mean something to Nalongo Kigozis of this country who have been forced into the unenviable position of losing their loved ones in disasters like these.
Whereas, some are using this to unfortunately to advance their political agenda even before the first funeral has taken place, we should keep our eyes on the ball and not drop it. President Museveni put it best: "You can see the consequences of negligence. Let everybody learn to take full responsibility of their duties".
Fellow citizens, running a government school was never meant to be a for-profit enterprise should be held liable for what goes in their schools. Make them accountable Mr. President and the message to those in such positions, will be sent out loud and clear for all to hear.
The writer is an Old Boy of Budo Junior

Original_Search_Title: RPG Launcher Found in Cassava Patch
Headline: Liberia: RPG Launcher Found in Cassava Patch
Body_Text:
Monrovia — Residents of Careysburg, Montserrado County, have turned over one Rocket Propel Grenade (RPG) launcher and an explosive to the Center for Peace Education and Democracy (COPE).
The arm was given at the end of a three-day awareness workshop on the proliferation of small arms and light weapons in Careysburg last week.
A COPE release said the RPG was discovered in a cassava patch in the area. The arm has since been given to the United Nations Mission in Liberia (UNMIL).
Meanwhile, COPE has ended a three-day awareness and education workshop on the danger, abuse, misuse and effects of small arms in Liberia.
The workshop brought together 30 participants including commissioners, city mayors, paramount and clan chiefs, women and youth groups, among others.
The workshop which was held under the theme: "Assessing the Impact of Small Arms on Sustainable Development in Rural Liberia" was part of COPE's fifth year awareness campaign against the proliferation and danger of small arms in Liberia.
Sponsored by the United States based National Endowment for Democracy (NED), COPE Executive Director Robert G. Miller told the participants that the proliferation of small arms was a major factor affecting development in Liberia.
He observed that the uncontrolled use of small arms suffers development.
He added that the proliferation of small arms in Liberia, Sierra Leone, Nigeria and other countries in Africa created negative impact and, if nothing is done to control the misuse of small arms, development will suffer.
He also called for the speedy ratification of the ECOWAS Convention on small arms if Liberia must be a safe place for development.

Original_Search_Title: What Karamoh Kabba Makes of Attitudinal Change
Headline: Sierra Leone: What Karamoh Kabba Makes of Attitudinal Change
Body_Text:
Freetown — Below is a welcome statement by Karamoh Kabba to guests at the Second Session of the Attitudinal Change Forum organised by the technical team of the Attitudinal Change campaign, Miatta Conference Hall in Freetown.
Mr. Chairman, Madam Sam-Sumana, wife of the Hon. Vice President, Members of the Technical Team of the Attitudinal Change Committee, Honourable Ministers, Members of Parliament distinguished ladies and gentlemen, on behalf of His Excellency President Ernest Bai Koroma, Hon. Alpha Kanu, Presidential and Public Affairs Minister, Hon I.B. Kargbo, Information and Communications Minister, the able and competent "Attitudinal Change" Committee and the people of the Republic of Sierra Leone, it is my great honor to welcome you to yet another session of the Attitudinal Change.
This is the second Attitudinal Change forum I have attended in Freetown, and it symbolizes the high importance the President and the people of Sierra Leone place on the tenet; "let your attitude show your altitude." Improving our personal growth will not depend much on how many qualifications we have like our attitude towards work and everything else we do. These individual positive attitudes will in turn determine the socio-economic growth of our country.
President Ernest Koroma made no mistake when he zeroed in on the need for us to change our attitude as a society of people - the problem of bad attitude is rooted in our common history, sustained by bad governments that focused no premium on national allegiance, patriotism and civic competence.
For a change, I must first thank and welcome the noble ideal of Attitudinal Change of our generation under a competent leadership of President Ernest Bai Koroma for taking the bold step of self-criticism, self-assessment and self-appraisal of individuals and the society as a whole.
We can choose to embark on all the many conspiracy theories abound in our midst on why Sierra Leone is lagging in socio-economic development, but until we change our attitude we will forever gripe about our woes and multiply our despondent condition.
We are about to write a story of courage and determination that will inspire our young ones and help us to forever abandon the stories that have created the need for us to revisit with our attitude.
And I must say that we are about to inspire the rest of the world with which we share this common malaise of self-destruction.
And I must tell you folks, our president is very serious about the need for us to change our attitude - In Kenema, upon awarding degrees to graduands, he took the opportunity to admonish us to abandon our self-acquisition of PhD (Pull Him Down) syndrome of self-destruction and the destruction of others.
And today, we meet here as leaders and people of a society who have identified a serious problem that must be addressed. While I was in Atlanta attending the Youth for Sierra Leone Improvement cultural night upon Dr Sho-sawyer, the group's founder and former chairman's invitation, I listened to Dr Alpha Bah, one of our renowned historian in the Diaspora, articulates: "Any culture that does not enhance the life of a society must be abandoned." And I will add that those cultures that will enhance the life of our society, whether local or alien, be kept or adapted.
My advice to us therefore is that if bad attitude towards our socio-economic development is a cultural thing, we must drop it forthwith.
One bad culture that comes to mind is the culture of dependency. When you tether the goat, you must put the cassava leaves far away from its reach. Our leaders must compose a national anthem of attracting investors to boost up the private sector economy for more job creation. The family, relatives, friends and hometown people pressure on our leaders and people in high places have created two major problems: The tendency to be corrupt to satisfy an endless chain of jobless dependants.
The tendencies to have these dependants roam the streets agitated for cutting off their livelihood by trying the corrupt officials.
It is the plain truth folks: In other developed countries where almost everyone depends on their daily wages, such a clampdown on corruption will only be a fodder for the media and pain for the family.
Together, we must help each other through this difficult but possible task of abandoning the business-as-usual cultures and turn a new page. We owe our children the responsibility to do the right thing.
Nurturance is a very powerful sociological/psychological concept. A child has the tendency for the most part to be likely influenced by the values of the society he or she is brought up in.
Evidently, our generation until now was fond of the culture of a cow grazes where it is tethered which, in our lingo is, 'wusai den tie cow na dae ee dae eat.' In good faith though, of course a cow grazes where it is tethered. But if the cow over grazes and poohs all over the soil, there will grow no new grass shoots.
Let us adhere to the principle of honest grazing of our national grass, let us account for every penny that comes through us, and let us account for every hour we are supposed to spend on the job, let us be creative and innovative at work.
Change is a very difficult thing to settle on; but most time it is the answer. If we have all agreed that Attitudinal Change is the answer to our problem, I welcome you here today to think with us as to how to go about it. The President does not have a ready-made answer nor the Attitudinal Change Committee. We must think together with the president to see his vision come true.
Then I would like to say some words about the organization that has brought all of us here - The Technical Committee of the Attitudinal Change. I am deeply impressed by their enthusiasm and commitment to this national cause.
You are all welcome to express your views to this wonderful open forum And thank you all for coming out today.

Original_Search_Title: Battle of the Mind
Headline: Uganda: Battle of the Mind
Body_Text:
Kampala — AHMED Wagaba is a disabled bouncer, but his disability does not worry him because he believes there is no situation that can't be handled with brains and some calmness, writes Moses Opobo
The legendary British reggae band, UB40, was due on stage in a few hours. Hordes of reggae fans were streaming into the Lugogo cricket oval for the historic concert. The atmosphere was electric.
However, this alone was not explanation enough for why many of the concert goers jostled to share a joke with or shake the hand of one of the day's bouncers, Ahmed Wagaba. Usually bouncers (commonly referred to as kanyamas) are seen as cold, menacing and not to be messed around with.
It is not often that you find men, women and children alike trying to strike a rapport with a muscle man on duty.
However, in an interesting contrast, rather than inspiring dread, Wagaba was the centre of attention as he controlled the crowds at the main entrance to the oval.
It was not that he was not well built and was therefore less intimidating; Wagaba is built alright. It was not just the festive mood either that was making people so relaxed.
It was probably the paradoxical figure Wagaba cut as a bouncer with a glaring physical disability - a lame left leg.
One might consider that a serious liability for one in Wagaba's line of work, but he begs to differ.
"Working as a bouncer is all about skill and brains. The belief that bouncers must rough people up is wrong," says the 27-year-old. "We are actually not supposed to shove anyone, unless that is the only remaining option. Use of force is the last resort.
As a professional bouncer, I know that most of the people we have to deal with are either drunk or high on drugs. Such a person can easily be injured or die at the slightest push. Besides, in the process of engaging this person, you risk injuring or inconveniencing innocent people."
Wagaba insists bouncers are trained to be decent and calm. "Most of the people that look very difficult to deal with calm down once a bouncer quietly shows them his security card," he explains, adding that it is impossible to work solo as a bouncer. "You need to call for help when you are overwhelmed, rather than resorting to force."
That is in the ideal world. Wagaba admits there are times he has been forced to flex his muscle, his non-violent approach to the job notwithstanding.
"I had a regrettable incident at the Brick N' Lace show at Kati Kati Restaurant on New Year's Day," he recalls. "I was in charge of security at the ticketing point when people suddenly pressed forward. Many of them were extremely drunk.
I pushed the two people at the front of the two queues with both palms. In that moment, I forgot that the weights we lift while working out are much heavier than human beings.
The result was that about 20 people crashed to the ground from my push. I knew I had overreacted, and resolved not to repeat that mistake."
Wagaba's popularity with the crowds, and especially the ladies, cannot be attributed to only his limp.
His approach to the job is different from that of many bouncers, including many of those he worked with at the UB40 concert.
While many other bouncers do everything within their means to stand out as muscle men, Wagaba prefers to keep a low profile.
Wagaba, who dropped out of school in P7 due to lack of fees, joined the security business in 1999. "I was drawn into this trade by a bouncer who had a similar disability to mine. Before I met this man, I did not believe that anyone could employ a disabled person as a bouncer.
When I tracked this man down, I discovered that he was also into powerlifting. One day he agreed to take me along with him to Body Flex Gym on Market Street in Kampala, where he used to work out.
"After a week, I also started training. I used to do power lifting, weightlifting and bodybuilding, until I became muscular enough to start work as a bouncer."
To the layman, powerlifting, weight lifting and bodybuilding may sound like the same thing. So Wagaba clarifies:
"Powerlifting means carrying weights which are heavier than you, while weightlifting is carrying weights from the ground and keeping them up in the air. Bodybuilding involves enhancing your muscles generally."
Wagaba says he works out four days a week:
"The first two days I do bench-pressing, which is basically about building the chest, then the other two days I do shoulder/biceps/triceps training."
Born in a family of five boys and five girls, Wagaba was born and raised in Kibuli, Kampala.
He says he was crippled at the age of seven. "My mum told me I was injected with an expired drug when she took me for immunisation at one of the hospitals in Kampala," he says with thinly veiled bitterness. However, he declines to name the hospital, citing legal implications.
"After the injection, my bottom swelled and my leg became paralysed. One year later, I was completely crippled and could not walk. I was confined to a wheelchair and crutches.
After a year, I got tired of the wheelchair, and abandoned it. However, whenever I tried to walk, I would fall down, aggravating my condition."
Then Wagaba's grandmother found some local herbs that helped. "I gradually abandoned my crutches, and by the age of 12, I could walk with no pain at all."
Currently, he is the head bouncer at Diamond Hotel on Luwum Street. But as a child, he dreamt of being a footballer.
"Actually I was my school's goalkeeper. I could dive and jump very high. I also loved boxing." Now though, Wagaba feels strongly that his calling is in powerlifting.
"I want to be a professional powerlifter. My dream is to participate in the powerlifting contest at the Beijing Olympics in August. I have already started training for the Olympic qualifiers that will take place in Egypt in May," he says, adding: "Bouncers in Uganda are not paid well, so you can't bank on it as a means of livelihood forever."
His first job as a bouncer was at Al's Bar in Kansanga. In 2000, he moved on to Uganda Lotto Casino, on Royal Complex in downtown Kampala, before moving on to his current job.
So what does a bouncer eat to keep his body in shape? Yams, cassava, potatoes, fish and bread, Wagaba says, adding that he avoids beef. And contrary to expectations, "I don't eat a lot, by the way".
"I eat everything in moderation. I don't eat a huge meal in one sitting. Instead, I eat small quantities at regular intervals. It is about quality, not quantity," he says.
For now, Wagaba is single and not searching yet: "I need minimal interaction with ladies if I am to achieve my dream of making it to the Olympics. I have to be in shape physically and psychologically. I have decided to avoid sex and women for now."
He has no kind words for fellow disabled people who take to begging on Kampala's streets, instead of finding some work. "Nobody was created to beg, and nobody is too disabled to work. This culture of begging is the reason we who are living with disabilities are despised.
I tried to convince some of these people on the streets that we should form an association, but they were reluctant. If we were organised we could attract sponsorship for activities like football, weightlifting, basketball and wheelchair javelin. In other countries, people with disabilities mint millions of dollars from these sports."

Original_Search_Title: Nursing Empty Wallets - Trials of a Nurse
Headline: Uganda: Nursing Empty Wallets - Trials of a Nurse
Body_Text:
Kampala — THEY can be found walking briskly up and down hospital corridors, attending to the many needs of their patients, but who is attending to theirs? Elizabeth Agiro writes that some nurses have to work up to 15 hours a day just to make ends meet.
IN one of the wards in Mulago Hospital, a nurse pushes a trolley laden with medical equipment as she goes about the evening shift. Dressed in a white uniform, red belt, gloves and a nurse's cap, she has just finished setting up intravenous (IV) drips for two patients.
Several others await her in two other sections of the ward. Since she is the only nurse available to carry out the evening duties, she can only spare a few minutes to talk, as she looks about her anxiously and points me in the direction of the nurses' station.
There, Rose (not real name) complains about the lack of nurses and facilities in the hospital. She gives the example of her colleague who has to work the evening shift all by herself.
In Ward 4A, there are 12 nurses. For now, however, only 10 are on duty because two are on maternity and annual leave respectively. Whereas Rose was lucky to have worked with two other nurses on the morning shift, the evening shift - from 4:00pm to 10:00pm - is usually manned by one nurse.
Rose explains that the ward was originally designed to accommodate 57 patients, but they have to deal with 90 patients daily. With beds woefully insufficient, the extra patients make do with mats on the floors, both in the wards and the corridors.
With such congestion, Rose says the medical fraternity is prone to infections, but the Government has not provided any risk allowance.
"We are at risk of infection because of the air we breathe," she says. Although masks are provided for the medical workers, Rose says they are a liability.
"When you breathe and cough into it, it gets wet, which allows another micro-organisms from another person to seep through the mask and then you inhale them," she says. "You are better off without the mask."
Perhaps the nurses' biggest complaint is that of a low salary. Their payslips show that Rose and a colleague, Teddy, each earn sh486,000 in gross pay. After taxes, they take home sh416,000. Rose spends sh80,000 on rent a month and that does not include water and electricity bills. She buys water at sh50 a jerrycan. In addition, she has to buy food for her four children, three of whom are of schoolgoing age.
"We are living in debt," she says, adding that she gets loans for school fees and then lives on the barest minimum the following month so she can pay back the loan.
"We do most of the work, but the bosses earn most of the money," she says, adding that the last salary increment she received was last year: sh10,000, most of which goes into taxes.
Rose explains that the grading system according to which they are paid, has loopholes, with people at different levels earning the same salary.
"When people upgrade, they should be automatically promoted, but that is not done here," she complains.
The nurses who wear pink uniforms are enrolled nurses and those in white are registered nurses. Enrolled nurses have completed nursing school and perform general duties, while registered nurses are certified to work in specialised areas.
However, whereas those who wore yellow belts on their white uniforms in the past earned less than those with red belts, they now both earn the same amount. In any case, the yellow belts have steadily been phased out. The red stands for Nursing Officer (NO) Grade I.
When one upgrades, they become a Senior Nursing Officer or Principal Nursing Officer. The latter wear black belts. Today, Rose says, they use the single spine pay scale, in which the NO I and II receive the same salary. Registered nurses, meanwhile, are still earning the same amount they did when they were enrolled, she says. "People are not appointed to the posts they are qualified for."
Even with the obvious lack of nurses, Rose says the Government is dragging its feet over employing more. Rose says that last December the Government advertised vacancies for nurses. While several people applied, three months later, no one has been shortlisted.
Rose narrows the problem down to a running conflict between the Government and the Uganda Nurses and Midwives Council. In the past, the council used to set exams and registered those who passed, on payment of a fee. Now, the Uganda National Examinations Board, under the education ministry, is in charge of setting the exams.
For the last three years, however, those who sat exams and passed have not been registered. The council has declined to issue registration certificates because they would like to set their own exams. And yet, without certificates, the nurses can't work.
Other complaints include the chaos that follows the loss of a colleague. Rose explains that when a colleague dies, the hospital provides a car but no fuel for those who would like to attend the burial. Therefore, they have to pool resources so as to get to the burial. Sometimes, the delay in raising the money has caused them to arrive long after the burial has taken place.
When they fall sick, nurses are treated in the general ward. When Teddy joined Mulago almost 30 years ago, there was a sick bay for staff. In the late 1980s, however, it was taken over by the army. Today, if they are to visit the private wing, they have to pay like everybody else, which they can't afford to do.
"God has helped me not to fall sick. I have suffered from simple illnesses like malaria and flu which are easily treated," says Rose adding: "Even when having a baby, you have to pay some money."
Rose also has a bone to pick with the public, which believes nurses don't do much to help patients.
"If we are not working, why do patients come and sleep on the floor and not go to other hospitals?" she asks. "When you tell someone there's no medicine they think you are trying to solicit a bribe so you can get them the drugs. When you direct the patient to a pharmacy, they conclude that you have shares in that pharmacy because they wonder why you have directed them to that particular pharmacy."
For Rebecca (not real name) a nurse in Ward 4B, "there is too much work for us and yet salary is not enough".
She says a year and a half ago, the Medical Workers Union discussed with the Government the possibility of arranging for transport, accommodation and risk allowances, but nothing has been done.
Rebecca has worked at Mulago for more than 15 years. When she first started, she was earning sh1,112. Today, she walks home with about sh400,000 after taxes. Although the salary has increased over the years, so has the cost of living, she says. To save money sometimes, she walks three miles from home to work and eats the cheapest food such as cassava and potatoes.
Thinking she could supplement her meagre income with a side job, Rebecca started working in a private clinic six years ago. She would work 12 hours of the night shift at Mulago and eight hours during the day at the clinic. The additional sh200,000 from the clinic made life a little easier, but "it was so tedious". After two years, she gave up because not only was she putting her health at risk, but also went without pay for months at the clinic.
Today, she makes do with what she earns and her husband's contribution to the welfare of their four children. She and her husband used to meet the home needs jointly but now he divides his income between hers and other households where his other wives and children reside. More often than not, Rebecca provides for her family on her own.
In spite of the efficiency at private health facilities, things are not any better for the staff there. In one such facility in Ntinda, a nursing officer explains that once upon a time, they used to work eight-hour shifts but now they work for 12 hours per shift. "Imagine what that means for a mother who is breastfeeding," she says.
According to a staff memo, only married people who have presented marriage certificates to management are entitled to maternity leave. In addition, when you give birth, you have to wait a minimum of three years before you can get pregnant again. Anyone who flouts this rule will not get leave.
"Imagine if someone is 37 years old (and has to wait three years to have another child)," she says, adding that some people although unmarried would like to have children, but are prevented from doing so by this policy.
And just like in the public hospitals, the salaries here too are lacking. "For my rank, I should be getting sh800,000, but I am earning less than sh400,000," she says.
I have no time for my family
HER day starts at 6:00am, when she wakes up and prepares for the five-mile journey to her place of work. Teddy (not real name) has to report to her station in Mulago Hospital by 8:00am. Thereafter, she works tirelessly for nine hours, attending to patients, setting up a drip here and delivering medicine there, allaying fears and occasionally delivering the bad news of a patient's death to relatives.
Whereas the typical civil servant heads home after that, Teddy's day is nowhere near over. At 6:00pm, she reports to yet another place of work, this time a private clinic, where she works until midnight.
"You reach home very tired and yet you have to wake up early," she says adding: "We don't rest because we want to make ends meet."
Teddy is one of several government-employed nurses who have to juggle jobs to supplement their meagre earnings. At the end of the month, she walks away with sh416,000 after a sh70,000 tax deduction. At the private clinic, she earns an additional sh150,000 a month.
"We are paid little yet we work very hard," Teddy says, adding that they go to the same markets and buy goods at the same price as everyone else. "We can't meet our needs as we would wish to. At the end of the month, what you collect goes into clearing debts."
Despite the lack of accommodation and transport allowances, Teddy, who has worked at Mulago for close to 30 years, has, together with her husband, over the years saved enough to build a house, where they live with their three children. But she still has to deal with the high transport costs, spending a minimum of sh2,000 every day.
Back in the day, she says, there was a nurses' hostel to accommodate those who had just qualified. At the time, she could not afford to look after herself and her then four-month-old daughter, so Teddy sent the child to her mother in the village because she had her brothers to look after as well. Today, all her children are in boarding school and she does not have to worry about the price of food because she has a garden in her backyard.
In order to juggle her two jobs, Teddy was forced to take her daughter to boarding school when she was in P4 because she was never home to look after her.
Before that, she would hire maids to help with her daughter but they never did any chores since they knew she came back home late. The house was never tidy and the child always dirty.
Among her complaints is the fact that the hospital wards are so congested and yet the public shows little appreciation of their plight. "The patients are so many they are now sleeping on the floor and there are few nurses to cater for their every need," she says. "That's why you hear cases of proud nurses. It is because we can't satisfy everyone's needs. In the end, you work tirelessly and no one appreciates you."
Equipment is also sometimes in short supply. "Sometimes when we lack syringes, we ask relatives to provide them," Teddy says, adding that patients' meals are also not consistently available and so sometimes they ask the patients' relatives to provide meals.
A few decades ago, the hospital would provide everything the patient needed.
Teddy's schedule is so hectic that sometimes she only gets to spend time with her family over the weekend, if at all.
What nurses do
Today's nurses are doing "basket work", according to Edith Nassuna, the Senior Principal Nursing Officer Mulago. This involves work meant for doctors, nurses, cleaners and nursing aides. Back in the day, she says, all nurses did was provide nursing care.
They were responsible for the patient's comfort, making beds, preparing meals and administering drugs.
Today, however, she says they check blood pressure, take urine samples, check temperature and monitor drips. Whereas the doctor is supposed to set up the drip, today's nurse does all that.
In the labour ward, the nurse, in addition to helping mothers deliver, cleans up after her and monitors both mother and baby thereafter.
"There are no nursing aides now so the nurse is the cleaner, the doctor and the nurse," she says.
Nassuna complains that nurses are being exported when so many of them are needed back home. "Nurses are going to be exported like coffee to Britain. It is the parent who pays school fees from nursery to the nursing school, but the Briton is going to benefit from cheap labour," she says.
We will address the issue - Govt
THE condition of nurses should improve later this year. The Minister of State for Primary Health, Emmanuel Otaala, says sometime back President Yoweri Museveni ordered a master plan to deal with the remuneration and motivation of health workers. This will be up for discussion soon, Otaala says.
He says the discussions will also cover how Mulago Hospital can be turned into a fully fledged referral hospital. According to the proposals, the least paid medical worker would receive a sh400,000 salary while the highest paid - a consultant, for example - would get sh5m a month.
"This is at par with the current economic situation. Medical workers should not go running around in dual practice," Otaala says. He adds that the ministry will discuss the issue with the President before April 15 and then present the proposals to Cabinet.
He hopes the Government will prioritise the health sector this year because last year they provided for the Commonwealth summit as well as defence. "We would like to provide for health workers so that they perform efficiently," Otaala says.
However, he says he is not aware of any deal between the Medical Workers' Union and the Government over transport, accommodation and risk allowances. "We came into office only a year and three months ago. That matter was not handed to me in a report," he says.

Original_Search_Title: Kumba Prison in a Shambles
Headline: Cameroon: Kumba Prison in a Shambles
Body_Text:
The Kumba Production Prison is in a state of disorganisation and untidiness, a fact made worse by overcrowding, hunger, ill health, torture and extortion.
The prison is currently housing 400 prisoners instead of 200; the initial capacity.
A survey conducted by Niamh Brown, an intern with Global Conscience Initiative, GCI, has revealed that there are five separate cells housing 170 sentenced prisoners. The intern lamented that juveniles are being held in the same enclosure as the sentenced prisoners.
Niamh said there is one building for untried prisoners with a capacity for 50 people, but now hosts 230 prisoners. According to Niamh, there are 36 beds for 230 inmates with above 75 percent of the prisoners forced to sleep on the floor.
The researcher further revealed that the average weekly diet for the detainees consists of cassava and green vegetables, with an occasional scrap of rice and beans. She said the Prison Superintendent, Thierry Fopa Joel, told her that inmates eat meat and fish, which the prisoners refute.
She stated that one warden supervises the cooking of food while a few prisoners prepare it. She also said the prisoners disclosed that their food is always insufficient and they feel hungry most of the time.
Niamh also noted that there is acute shortage of personnel and medical supplies at the prison. She explained that the infirmary is unhygienic and too small to cater for all the prisoners, which leads to further illness instead of recuperation. She said the inmates reported that they are sometimes forced to pay for medical services they are supposed to receive free.
In the area of discipline, Niamh said there is intimidation, verbal abuse, beatings, denial of service and extortion by warders. She added that many inmates complained of lack of access to lawyers, lack of knowledge of their rights and court procedures, lack of judges, all causing delays in the delivery of justice.
It was on the basis of her findings that Global Conscience Initiative organised a workshop on March 22 grouping warders, police, gendarmes, civil society representatives and lawyers to produce a human rights manual for the prison. Unfortunately, the invited prison administrators did not turn up.
Topics presented were based on the general welfare of prisoners, health issues, untried prisoners, discipline, women and juveniles.At the end of the workshop, participants recommended that torture, assault and chaining of prisoners should stop. They proposed that government should increase the budget of the prison administration and improve the working conditions of prison staff.
The participants said access to legal aid be improved and rehabilitation programmes of prisons be developed. They also recommended that communication between personnel and inmates be improved; protection of minors strengthened and dialogue between police, gendarmes, the civil society and NGOs enhanced.
Barrister Daniel Ambo, GCI Chairman, told The Post that it was pathetic that the prison administrators boycotted the workshop. He said the absence of warders does not improve their scope on national and international prison administration.
He, however, stated that the recommendations would be forwarded to the penitentiary for improved relationship in prison administration.

Original_Search_Title: Book Review - Pale Souls And Other Tales
Headline: Uganda: Book Review - Pale Souls And Other Tales
Body_Text:
Title: Pale Souls Abroad and other tales
Author: Ulysses Chuka Kibuuka
Publisher: Fountain Publishers Ltd.
Reviewer: Dennis D. Muhumuza
It is a collection of seven stories in 218 pages that will crack you up and hold your interest to the last word. Kibuuka writes a tale-twister that leaves you laughing at yourself for having no clue some of the stories will end the way they do. Stupor for example is a story of a respected banker who takes a stranded woman to a lodge and they revel and have sex only for him to wake up and find her dead.
Panic sets in as he tries to hide the body. What makes it unforgettable is that the reader, like the protagonist, thinks Nyugunyu is truly dead only to discover at the end she had just fainted badly, as she is met going to church with other women while joking about the "buffoon" of a man that thought her dead.
In High Protocol, a lustful man is given his corrupt master's "jet-black Mercedes 500SEL" to go pick Maria who has just flown in first-class from California. The driver can't wait to meet the boss's beautiful daughter. Much to his disappointment, he finds out that Maria is a cat!
Kibuuka's descriptive power is admirable. In The Naked Womb, one cannot forget the three-year old homeless girl "trudging like an aged woman on the village path, the skin and hair yellowed like bronze from kwashiorkor, a distended abdomen and a thick host of flies, mostly bluebottles -blanketing her eyes in two dark silvery-blue circles and other hordes of the ugly insects burrowing in and out of her ears and nostrils like bees on a hive."
Or the Russian woman who is "very pretty with a compact body, packed like coiled dynamo wires." The stories are filled with fleeting incidents: a man making love to a ghost without knowing it, an old man that "kneads his own shit into the cassava flour to make bait" while on a fishing spree and the woman that had "the nasty habit of losing control of her bowels during orgasm," to mention a few.
It's absurd comedy and upon deep reflection you realise the author hides behind that to bemoan a rotten society teeming with moral corruption, men raping their house girls, medical students abetting abortion, leaders running down the economy, drug-dealing and evil humour like in that anecdote of Banamba's car with seatbelts interwoven with hypodermic needles "that suck the passenger's blood, which Banamba then sells off in gallonfuls to European markets" and all the ill deeds that continue to befoul contemporary society.
The writer is especially critical of western influence and highly patronising, pretentious, selfish and dangerously ambitious individuals.
His knowledge of the country's diverse ethnicities and cultures is intact as presented in the conflict between modernity and tradition.
Kibuuka is arguably Uganda's only existential writer and is the author of other works like For the Fairest and Of Saints and Scarecrows.

Original_Search_Title: Group Reviving Performing Arts
Headline: Kenya: Group Reviving Performing Arts
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Apparently, necessity drives creativity. What began as informal meetings among three friends over roast cassava turned out to be the revival of theatre in Mombasa.
And the group did not only bring back theatre to life, they also brought in the much-needed professional touch.
One of them, Joel Godiah is a graduate of Creative and Theatre Arts from Moi University, Eldoret.
At the end of 2006, Godiah, Hillary Namanje and Abel Sauti formed the Kwezi Multi-Media Artists.
Before the birth of the group, the three had been up and down looking for jobs.
After completing his studies in 2004, Godiah worked briefly with the Phoenix Players and other theatre groups before returning to Mombasa.
Jenkins (Ashik Yusuf), Brenda (Joyner Okonjo) and Dr Hapman Baraza (Suleiman Seifudin) in one of the scenes of the comedy, Cash On Delivery.
"When I came to Mombasa, I met Namanje, who was looking for someone with professional skills in drama," he recalls.
Sauti joined the group later and they set out to "change the face of theatre in Mombasa," and started recruiting new members.
To set their launch apart, they provided lunch, but they had to borrow Sh5,000 for the event from friends.
"The launch was a success. About 15 businessmen attended the event at Little Theatre Club. Others pledged to help," says Godiah.
They raised about Sh14,000 during the launch.
In March, they staged their first production, a British Comedy, Caught in the Net, together with the Little Theatre Club.
Among the audience was the then Minister for Local Government, Mr Musikari Kombo, Mvita MP, Mr Najib Balala and, Ms Esther Passaris of Adopt-A-Light.
Soon, the Imperial Bank commissioned them to write a script and paid Sh20,000. This was the first big break.
The cast held six shows with one at the Kenya National Theatre in Nairobi.
The next play was Parlor Games, featuring 18 artists plus the stage managers. The play exposed high level corruption at our city councils.
"The script was written by Kuldip Sondhi, a hotelier, whose first transcript had more than 100 pages before we edited it to 30," says Sauti.
The unedited script lasted for about four hours, but was reduced to a play of about two and a half hours.
Successful show
The group cast another, Dear Diary, which focused on the plight of the handicapped in the society. Ms Nayisa Karibai commissioned it.
Litttle Theatre Club, Mombasa, where Cash On Delivery will be staged. Pictures: Andrew Kilonzi
"Dear Diary was the first play we charged Sh300 and all the tickets sold out even before the D-day," recounts Namanje.
The artists say the success of the show was partly due Ms Karibai's aggressive marketing.
It was later followed by another play, Goa Saga, in commemoration of the 75 years of the Goan community.
The play, which Sondhi labelled the 'greatest of all-Goa' expressed the huge contribution the community had made in the society, yet they were hardly noticed.
"This time round, we had an international audience and the response was overwhelming," says Godiah.
The group later produced the play, Parlor Storms, a follow up to the first one on the city council.
The latest play, Cash on Delivery, is still hot in town, was the group's first performance at the Reef Hotel.
Namanje is the head of the group, Sauti the secretary and Godiah is the treasurer and in charge of theatre training and research.
Godiah is also a drama teacher at Aga Khan Academy.
Namanje says they are also training new actors and actresses.
There are three sessions per week, divided into physical fitness, theory and acting.
The group has also shot a trial 45-minutes movie known as Stinger that focuses on drug peddling.
They have also started community theatre, where they stage open shows in Mombasa and Malindi. Later, they have a question and answer session.
They plan to produce bigger productions and collaborate with other groups in the near future.
Some of the challenges the group faces are high cost of production and lack of local scripts.
They hope to eventually wean themselves of foreign scripts.

Original_Search_Title: Christian Chases 'Jesus' From Home
Headline: Uganda: Christian Chases 'Jesus' From Home
Body_Text:
Kampala — Residents of Pokongo Rock zone, Tororo district were last week left dumbfounded when a former altar-boy, in the church, chased away a congregation that had gathered for prayer at his home.
Armed with a machete, Pascali Owor in his mid 50s, dispersed the praying group.
It all began when the Catholics in Pokongo, in line with the lent norms, decided to hold a 'way of the cross' on chosen days of the fasting period. A 'way of the cross' is the Catholic depiction of the pain Jesus endured before his death.
One of the stations along the 'way of the cross' for these Christians was supposed to be Owor's home. They would be rudely surprised! Led by the Catechist, the group sang as they entered Owor's compound. To every one's dismay, Owor, came out of his house with a machete claiming the group had gone to take 'his only day's sweat'.
"You people rob us in church and now you have come to take even the little mukene I have struggled to get for my family," Owor said as he headed for the Catechist with a machete in hand. Sensing danger, people abandoned prayers and run into the nearby cassava garden, as others run away.

Original_Search_Title: High Oil Prices Make Essential Commodities Costly
Headline: Rwanda: High Oil Prices Make Essential Commodities Costly
Body_Text:
Kigali — Prices of some essential commodities including vegetables, fuel, grains and detergents have increased, some by 100 per cent, with government officials saying there should not be cause for alarm.
In a mini market survey carried out by The New Times team in several of Kigali's markets , salt has increased from Frw100 to Frw140 per kg while a tablet of soap that used to cost Frw50 has spiraled to Frw100 in most retail shops in the city.
Irish potatoes were being quoted higher from Frw100 to 120 per kg, followed by tomatoes from Frw400 to Frw500 per kg. The price of a kilogramme of beans, the common dish in the country has also increased from Frw300 per kg to Frw400 per kg.
While the price of peas, consumed by the upscale and middle class soared from Frw600 to Frw1,000 per kg in the last two months, beef that used to cost Frw1,000 is now at Frw1,200 per kg.
Attendants at Karo Super market, Gisementi, however, believe they are still paying for the post election violence in Kenya that cut off production and supply of commodities to most countries in the East African region.
"Prices of most commodities are high. They have gone up by almost 40 per cent compared to last year," one of the attendants said. Trade gurus associate the increasing prices in Kenya, a country that suppliers most East African countries with essential commodities to the high world oil prices.
The prices of edible oil and imported rice have gone up substantially during the last two months. USA oil has shot to Frw4,000 from Frw2,900 three months ago. Five kilogrammes of Basimati rice that used to cost Frw5,000 increased to Frw6,500. While Tanzania rice that used to cost 480 per kilogramme has increased to Frw600 per kilogramme.
Commenting on the rise Finance Minister, James Musoni says prices will soon stabilise. "Usually during the first school term, prices tend to be high."
Musoni however, hastened to say that some factors pushing prices higher are more external, citing the increasing prices of oil on the international market that have pushed transport and production costs high.
He reassured Rwandans consumers that the price increase is seasonal and will stabilise soon. As of Thursday, oil rose to $106 a barrel, bolstered by a weak dollar and a drop in fuel stocks.
Expert say, surging fuel prices usually weigh most heavily on the emerging urban middle class, making it a struggle to put fuel in cars or motorbikes daily and to pay home electricity bills, in Rwanda, every body feeling the brunt.
The cost of fuel has been quoted to record prices in the past months, with a litre of kerosene soaring from Frw480 to Frw623 for the first time ever, while diesel and petrol now costs Frw726.
Following a hike in fuel prices, transporters too hiked their charges by an average of 30 per cent per single trip from producing areas to markets in different parts of the country.
The present rise in prices of all essential commodities, including grains, vegetables, meat, bananas, Irish potatoes, wheat floor, posh and cassava since the last two months is attributed this increase.
Gas
The prices of Liquefied Petroleum Gas (LPG) used as a fuel source for industry and domestic use, increased on average by Frw4,000 . The two gas companies, Kobil raised the prices for 11 kilogramme cylinder from Frw25,000 to Frw30,000 while SP oil company increased a 20 kg cylinder to Frw40,000 from Frw30,000.
Meanwhile, LPG consumers have expressed their concern over LPG price rise calling for government intervention.
Musoni said government was sourcing for an investor to distribute the cylinders within Rwanda.
Charcoal
A sack of charcoal, the source of energy for average Rwandans in towns has continued to increase. From Frw5,000 last month to Frw7,000 and Frw10,000, per sack this week in most suburbs in Kigali City.
Minister Musoni attributes the price increase to the high demand. "Most families use charcoal for cooking because of high cost of electricity. But he also said there is a supply constraint as the country heavily depends on imported charcoal. "There is a ban on cutting trees in Rwanda," Musoni said.

Original_Search_Title: Let Gadaffi Lead Africa to a Revolutionary Promised Land
Headline: Uganda: Let Gadaffi Lead Africa to a Revolutionary Promised Land
Body_Text:
The fart of a rich man doesn't stink. Put it another way: Poor hangers- on who throng the court of a rich man in the hope of catching a stray meal, dare not hold their noses no matter how badly the great man pollutes the atmosphere.
If anything they are supposed to burst into spontaneous applause whenever the big man fires off a canister of foul smelling ammonia from the depths of his alimentary canal.
Catharsis is life saving relief. I too could discourse on theological matters especially regarding the authenticity or otherwise of certain old black books universally accepted as sacrosanct. But that is a privilege I will forego. There are Ugandans who are eminently more qualified than myself to establish whether or not the book which is the basis of their faith is a forgery.
