Pastors Patience and Odrek Rwabogo, for example, will, I am sure, issue a spirited defence of the Bible in very short order.
The other matter I will not let pass without comment is Col. Muammar Gaddaffi's oft stated dictum that revolutionary leaders need neither the mandate, via elections, of the people they lead, nor the obligation to relinquish power. Just be revolutionary - whatever that means - and you are free to rule in perpetuity until mushrooms sprout from your head and all your teeth fall out.
I think perhaps the Great revolutionary leader from Tripoli was preaching to the converted when he repeated the statement last week in Kampala. Some people around here have pretty much made up their minds to rule for life. The only point over which I humbly beg to differ with the venerated Colonel is that of abolishing elections. We need these things, Chief.
This is the only opportunity for Ugandans to receive free salt, bits of laundry soap, and half- cooked meat. Is this too much to ask for seeing that this farcical exercise takes place only once every five years?
Some lucky voters receive mysterious envelopes with secret contents, but almost all voters who are so inclined can partake in orgies in which lots of cheap, often harmful alcohol is consumed. The campaign agents all receive free T-shirts; but a lucky few may get bicycles for keeps.
If you abolish elections, what will happen to the Kiboko Squads whose brief is to clobber recalcitrant opposition voters into submission? If elections are abolished what will these cane-wielding squads led by half crazed "majors" beat?
You hear stories of breath-taking amounts of cash spent on these elections. $50 million for a farcical election allegedly spent by just one party to fight an election in a cassava republic is serious money. This does not include the hard cash dispensed by the Electoral Commission to each of the parties, along with other perks such as 4-wheel drive vehicles.
If there is a court case afterwards contesting the outcome of the elections, the legal fraternity gets a windfall, the likes of which they could never dream to see during their entire existence as legal firms. Uganda Shs2.5 billion to hire lawyers to fend off losers has been known to change hands.
All in all, an election is a time for a redistribution of the contents of the Consolidated Fund and no Colonel is going to deprive Ugandans of this five- yearly bonanza. But what is this man doing championing a United States of Africa government?
What kind of Promised Land is he thinking of leading this enslaved continent if he cannot respect such small entities as the national constitutions of small republics?
Is he perhaps applying for the position of continental Emperor with a million armed personnel at his disposal? If his bodyguards cannot respect their counterparts in a foreign country where they are guests, what will happen when their boss straddles the whole of Africa?

Original_Search_Title: Water Shortage Affects Markets in the Capital
Headline: Ghana: Water Shortage Affects Markets in the Capital
Body_Text:
Ahead of the International Water Day celebration, on Saturday, a severe water shortage has hit the various markets in the metropolis, leaving a bad odour permeating the air
A trip to some markets revealed that, due to the shortage of water, most wele sellers cannot wash the hides, which are the delicacy of many Ghanaians properly, but rather keep it in a small amount of water.
Fofo Boye, a trader at the Teshie Satellite Market, disclosed that wele, Ga for cowhide, needs a lot of water during processing, to make it safe for consumption.
She said after the butcher had supplied the wele, in its raw state, the retailer then has to clean it, using water and a knife to scrap the burnt particles off it.
Fofo further said water was then needed again to store the meat, to keep it fresh.
She noted that if enough water is utilized in the washing and cleaning of wele, it can turn out to be very poisonous, due to the butchers using vehicle tyres to singe the cowhide. Fofo said due to this shortage of water, sales of wele has reduced drastically, because people prefer to find alternatives, rather than buy unwashed wele.
Aafio Mansa a trader, who sells cassava at the La Satellite Market, recounted that since the problem of water shortage begun, her wares have been going bad.
She said the inability to get water to soak the cassava to preserve it, has caused her to loose thousands of cedis, because she has to sell the dry cassava to chop bar operators, to use for kokonte powder, at a very low price.
A gallon of water, which cost between GH25p-30, weighs heavily on profits and it also was not easy to find, as you have to walk miles before acquiring the gallon of water.
She lamented that since the shortage begun, she has been making a loss, and therefore appealed to the government to help solve this water crisis, in time to save her business from collapsing, since is was her only source of livelihood.

Original_Search_Title: Taiwanese Firms Want Cassava From Country
Headline: Nigeria: Taiwanese Firms Want Cassava From Country
Body_Text:
Lagos — PROSPECT for more growth in non oil sector of the Nigerian economy continued in Abuja with a disclosure by Taiwanese firms of their readiness to import 500 to 1000 tonnes of cassava produce from the country on trial basis within the shortest possible time.
General manager of NEDTEX Co. and Yearchain Co., Mr. Shao Chang Su, said this during his visit alongside representatives of Aso Multi-Purpose Co-operative Society to Nigerian Export Promotion Council (NEPC) to solicit for assistance, a release signed by NEPC Public Affairs Manager, Ndubeze Okeke, has stated.
Speaking while receiving the visitors, acting Executive Director/CEO of NEPC, Mr. Aliyu Lawal, said the council is always willing to facilitate opportunities for exporters adding that, in line with her statutory responsibility, the council will do all within its mandate to ensure that Nigerian products penetrate the international markets.
On the request from Mr. Aso Vakporaye, the CEO of Aso Multi-Purpose Co-operative Society for assistance to help make the business venture a less stressful one, the NEPC boss assured that the Council would liaise with the Nigerian Export-Import Bank (NEXIM), in an effort to facilitate funding for the company, to enable it commence trial shipment of 500 tonnes of cassava produce to the said Taiwanese firms. NEXIM has been collaborating with the Council in financing export trial orders.
Earlier, Mr. Chang Su disclosed that his companies were willing to have a long term business relationship with Nigerian exporters if the price and quality of their products were competitive and meet international standard.

Original_Search_Title: Governor Chime and the Ebeano Cross
Headline: Nigeria: Gov Chime And the Ebeano Cross
Body_Text:
Lagos — There is need to thank Peugeot Nigeria Plc copy writer for leading a thought for this piece.
Said their latest copy for the promotion of its 407 Series: ' the play time is over.. 407' And so, Governor Sullivan Chime of Enugu state said recently, as his hired crowd unwillingly milled around Okpara Square, venue of his simulated solidarity rally. He told the crowd, 'there is nothing like Ebeano (family) in PDP in Enugu state. The Ebeano is dead and buried.' And so the play time is over.
And he waited for the crowd to rise in ovation. If the ovations. But one wonders how we forget too soon. Perhaps our forgetfulness often stems from our prejudice and our hate for what we intend to forget.
But the man, his facilitator and predecessor, Senator Chimaroke Nnamani, he is drawing into the ring is urging his Enugu people to give him, Chime the governor, support to complete the project the PDP initiated in the state.
Just at the beginning of his Guber campaign, soon after his nomination at the party's state congress, he had sent text to his colleagues in the state Executive Council- EXCO - which read in part ' 'As part of our efforts to perpetuate Ebeano Family in the E.N.S.G (italics mine) 4 our common good, I hereby invite u 2 a meeting with all EXCO members on Sunday 3rd Dec. 2006. D talks will be held over Dinner @ RAYA, Ogui Rd by 7pm. Pls come & help 2 inform others. Sullivan.'
Little over one year of this robust intention for his peers, he now stand before a public gathering to declare the death and internment of the same Ebeano he had intended to perpetuate in the Enugu state Government. What is it that has suddenly overtaken him? Love or hate for self, state or others? Can anybody trust such a person?
Sister Eileen Sweeney helps us to define this phenomenon. When love is unselfish, it transcends the self and thus earns a value .It is the opposite of this, hatred, and utterly selfish that is destructive leading the patron into disillusionment and even to murder.
Does any man really kill what he loves? Not quite except by super inspiration, like Ahraham wanted to kill his only son, Isaac, as an offering to God. It was dead on thought. It is his disposition and action towards benefactors that question his value for charity or the value for charity in politics even out of politics.
Is the Governor acting alone? Is he merely acting out a script for others? Is he a prisoner of scoundrels and scavengers? He could be a warden. But psychologist can attest that men need the support of the opinion of others to reinvigorate a held opinion about an issue or thing. Sullivan truly had an opinion, or in fact hatred for those who loves and love those who love to hate him thus borrowing a leaf from Saul Bellow's Nobel Prize winning novel, Herzog. Bellow made his hero, Herzog, to write to a friend saying, 'self-awareness tends to reveal us to ourselves as monsters'. And one of the qualities that makes us into 'monsters' is hatred. But our friend the Governor has not realised the destructive potentials of hatred: it destroys the hater and the hated.
The governor hates himself the way Okada riders hate and drive themselves to orthopaedic clinic. He loves and fondles the advice of those who hate him, whose only choice of pretending to love him is to work into his closet to fight their enemy that had wronged them in most cases for his sake.In this group are those who nursed gubernatorial ambition with him.
Then, what of the last group? These are men whose hate for all governments that were ever in Enugu is legendary, who gloat over their mocked strong men of wawa politics appellation and who thrilled observers as they fought selves if not their nuclear families. Well, like Bertrand Russell once said, 'few people can be happy unless they hate some other person.' It is here that the resemblance of 'Wawa freedom fighter', Chief C.C. Onoh, salvos and his political adventures in the last forty years or there about come into critical scrutiny vis-à-vis his age and limit of good logic
True, Chief Onoh fought for the realisation of the worth of wawa person and thus proved that a wawa man is as competent and enterprising, if not more, as those local colonialists who had hitherto ascribed to him as possessing visceral disposition by nature. Indeed he reshaped the people's confidence and their ability to rise above his oppressors. To be sure, the wawas are the political block emprising residents of today's Ebonyi and Enugu States which is part of the south-east geopolitical zone.
Before today, those things, including music, that bear the trade mark of Wawa in dialect or social relationship were reprehensive and unworthy of appreciation. Onoh introduced one of such bores and a local delicacy- Okpa - into a national menu. Then the wawas were mocked for drinking tea not with bread but with okpa (to substitute bread) by mockers in self-delusion as the superior race. Ditto to Abacha (sourced from cassava tubers). Today, music produced in wawa lyrics is the toast, if not anthem, in public functions in the whole of the South East geopolitical zone.
We can also give kudos to Chief Onoh for his fighting power that resulted in the creation of two wawa states - Enugu and Ebonyi.
Beyond these, he is a bore if not a nuisance. Onoh has never spoken well of any government. Late Ajie Ukpabi Asika, Senator Jim Nwobodo, Brigadier John Atom Kpera, Commodore Emeka Omeruah, Col Robert Akonobi, Col Sule Aman, Senator Chimaroke Nnamani et al were to him rogues. He was kind to have written two books on Senator Nwobodo and Col Akonobi's alleged thievery. His son, G.B. Onoh's presence in Dr. Okwesilieze Nwodo's government stalled his anti-graft camera on that government.
Indeed, Chief Onoh is a saint whose self-anointed graft hound personality of the people is dubious. To be sure,he is a very wealthy Wawa man who is enhancing this wealth from the treasury of gold left for him by his father, Chief Gabriel Onoh at Enugwu Ngwo in today's Enugu state. Additionally his law firm is the envy of all the lawyers in the South East.And this is why his practice is an additional haven of his wealth.
Nobody should point at him to ask, as former Chairman of the Nigeria Coal Corporation, 'wither the land and other property of that one-time money-spinning organisation?' You can be sure that he would point at successive governments after his tenure to give an account.
Yesterday he dinned with Dr Chimaroke Nnamani and pretended to assist him in warding off mindless attackers until the dying days of his administration when all sorts of accusations were railed against him and he has to join in the fray and provided the supporting missile. Today, he has found way into the closet of Sullivan Chime and is in league with politicians of fortune who are raising the stakes for cremating Sullivan, the governor. When the stake is ready, he will be the first to stoke it.
Would one sympathise with or love the Governor if he ignores the warning, caveat emptor? Perhaps we are safe to the extent that we are all created in the image of God who is all loving. Beyond that, one is inclined to believing that those who discount the value of gratitude deserve no pity when the carpet on which they frolic is pulled off their feet.
Obodoagu wrote from Enugu

Original_Search_Title: A Bit of Arua in Kampala
Headline: Uganda: A Bit of Arua in Kampala
Body_Text:
Kampala — Somewhere in the city is a busy bus park named after a district many kilometres away. Tony Mushoborozi visited and shares the sights, tastes and sounds
A MIRAA-MUNCHING youth staggers under 100kg of cement, while another passes by laden with cartons of margarine. Yet another is almost hidden under a sack of second-hand clothes.
They are loading the goods on trucks bound for West Nile, eastern Congo and South Sudan, I am informed by one of the boys.
They are not speaking Luganda like you would expect in downtown Kampala, but communicate to one another in Lugbara and Kiswahili. This is probably the only place in Kampala where Luganda is not the business language.
The place - Arua Park - is named after a district that is over 500km away.
"In the 1970s," says Vicky Aseru, a tailor, "there was a man called Matiya Ofuta who made a living as a broker between traders from Arua and buyers here in Kampala. He would sit here all day and traders from Arua would make their way to him and he would find them buyers for their goods.
They traded mainly in honey, millet, groundnuts and gold. Gold was sold by Congolese who made and still make their way to Uganda through Arua."
Arua Park is not the official name of this place though.
In government records, that area is known as Munno as it located near the Munno newspaper offices.
Arua Park, which is now the official terminal for buses going to South Sudan, is not just about transport.
Sometimes you may feel the urge to have a meal that can only be found in your home village. Being in the city and hundreds of miles from your grandmother, however, you have to do without. A West Niler, however, will head for Arua Park.
A Lugbara friend led me there to treat me to a traditional meal - inya and whole tilapia in groundnut sauce - at the shockingly low price of sh1,000.
Inya is bread made from millet mixed with cassava flour. It is similar to akaro in my home area of North Kigezi, only that the former has more cassava than millet flour.
I liked it. It was nice, cheap and different, especially since I had never eaten millet with fish. My only disappointment was having to eat it off a plate and not out of a special basket (endiro) like we do back home.
About five years ago Arua Park was notorious as a meeting point for delinquent youths who had escaped from schools and universities around Kampala to smoke marijuana and drink alcohol.
"Four years ago, marijuana smoking was so high in Arua Park that it got frightening," says Aseru, who is also the Munno B LC 1 treasurer and a born-again Christian youth counsellor.
"We used to arrest many youths and cane them, but all in vain. These youths stole to keep the drugs flowing and worse still, they began intimidating passersby.
One day, we brought in a boy who was always stealing car side mirrors. I caned him so hard that it hurt me too. He promised he would never do it again and said he was leaving Arua Park the next day for Nebbi.
"The next day they brought him with another stolen mirror. I looked at the boy and I was so disgusted. I did not know what to do. I sat and looked at him and said to myself: 'This boy is not okay.' When I asked him why he had lied to me and stolen again, he told me he wanted the money to buy brown sugar."
"Brown sugar is a common drug in Kampala," Aseru explains. "It is bought from Kisenyi and Katwe. The addicts put it in a metallic container, light a fire under it and sniff the ensuing vapour."
She continues: "The boy told me that brown sugar gave him immense strength, and doubled his need for money. I told him that I understood, but his life was like money given to him by God and it was his responsibility to use it to buy either useful things or worthless things.
I told him to think about his future, do what he came to do in Kampala and try to stop taking brown sugar, or leave Arua Park. I let him go, and he was never brought to us again.
He later joined a boxing club in Nakivubo and recently he came here to thank me for the advice, and to tell me that he would soon be going oversees for a fight," she adds with a sweet smile, "I have never been happier."
Aseru says that from that day, she realised that speaking to people changed their lives more than anything else.
She has since used the formula to change dozens of other boys with the same problem.
Thanks to her, Arua Park is a peaceful place with less and less intoxicating marijuana smoke floating all around.

Original_Search_Title: Meetings On the Price of Bread
Headline: Mozambique: Meetings On the Price of Bread
Body_Text:
Maputo — Representatives of the Mozambican government, milling companies and bakers have been holding meetings in Maputo to try and find ways of dealing with the ever increasing price of wheat on the international market.
The chairperson of the Mozambican Bakers Association, Victor Miguel, told AIM that the discussions are about creating internal capacity for the production of wheat or the use of alternative raw materials, particularly cassava flour.
Miguel stressed that "Mozambique needs to produce wheat flour as it used to. We have a huge and active population, and good relations with the international community. There are also substitute raw materials, namely cassava".
Miguel denied reports that the state will subsidise the price of bread in the same way that it has promised to subsidise the price of fuel used by the privately owned minibus-taxis. These same rumours also claim that the price of bread will be increased as from this week, because of the increase in the price of wheat on the world market.
"We deny such reports, because they are not true", said Miguel, pointing out that the price of bread has already gone up once this month (on 1 February).
He added that, even if the bakers wanted to increase the price of bread, it would not be because of the current world market price, because Mozambican milling companies have yet to receive wheat at those prices. Only when milling companies increased the price of flour, would the bakers have to consider a further increase in bread prices.
"We are currently consuming wheat flour that we bought at the prices obtaining before 1 February", said Miguel. It was the price of that wheat which led bakers to increase the price of bread as from 1 February.

Original_Search_Title: Tales of Tindi's Art
Headline: Uganda: Tales of Tindi's Art
Body_Text:
His long-time girl-friend from high school at Kitante Hill abandoned him just a few months ago because she had grown up poor and wasn't ready to commit to a guy who was simply surviving.
Indeed, this confirms how Ronnie Tindimwebwa, commonly known as Tindi has struggled through life. But he has made art his source of livelihood. Through art, he has helped finance his two siblings' education and also contributed to the completion of his parents' home.
In the year 2000, Tindi's father was involved in an accident that meant Tindi had to work to support his incapacitated father. "In my S4 vacation I was encouraged to get a career in art by Stella Atal who was in her 'A' Level vacation then and had done art. She led me to Peter Otim's gallery then called Cassava Republic to learn art," said Tindi.
Surprisingly, Tindi is now so passionate for something he never really liked.
"I wanted to be an architect but I wasn't moved by art. When I joined the Cassava Republic, I slowly learnt how to paint from Otim and I used to do the background painting before he drew the final piece. At the end of the month I walked away with Shs 50,000."
This money enabled Tindi return to Kitante where he did Mathematics, Economics, Geography and Art at 'A' Level. Despite the demands of his combination, he kept working. "I would leave school at 5p.m and head to the gallery which is in Kamwokya to work and train with Otim's guidance. Even though I worked less hours, Otim paid me full [wages]. He's my hero," said Tindi.
The art talent has roots. Tindi's mother is part of a church project where she designs simple clothes and tailors them. "Mom used to teach me how to sow and design clothes too," explained Tindi. No wonder, Tindi still has an inclination to fabrics. "I hand-print on the Arapapa clothes," he said.
Tindi, 28, has hundreds of art pieces now, having exhibited first in 2004 though with little results.
His style is semi-abstract art, which gives you 50-50 on interpretation and understanding. He has sent lots of his works to Malaysia and the U.S.A. although dealers have often ripped him off.
"I sent 52 art pieces to Malaysia, but the Malaysia agent told me he had lost them during the Tsunami." Tindi realises that he's been naïve in his business but is sure he can make it big.
Tindi wears a black Rosary around his neck. "Yes, I'm Catholic but it's also my style that people can identify me by. The Rosary is my guide to good spiritual living," he said.
He had wanted to be a priest. From S1 to S2, he was at Nyenga Seminary but was suspended for a food demonstration, only to be called back when he had already joined Kitante Hill.
He has acquired a diploma in architecture and intends to upgrade it at Kyambogo University and has a new girlfriend studying law at Makerere.
Tindi Art is beaming at his studio on Mawanda road and it should sweep you away.

Original_Search_Title: Federal Govt As a Pimp
Headline: Nigeria: Federal Govt As a Pimp
Body_Text:
Lagos — From time, fire has been man's best friend and his worst enemy. On the credit side, we wonder what man would have done with those beautiful meat and yams, without fire.
Eat them raw? Even without considering the role that fire plays in reducing cassava from its raw form to garri, it would be unimaginable how man would have been drinking garri all his life without the ability to convert it, along with the necessary paraphernalia, to the final "eba" form. It is even popularly held that you can drink garri but you cannot drink "Santana: Fire plays a telling role on all of them.
On the debit side, though, it is most reprehensible that a man would deliberately set fire on property. In fact, the law sees arson as the crime of setting fire with intent to cause damage. Although the common law definition of arson restricts it to setting fire on dwellings, statutes have since expanded the crime to include the burning of other structures such as bridges, vehicles and other property. In the winter of 2005, we worked exhaustively in this area, and came to the inevitable conclusion that gas flaring is arson. The only difference might be that while the average arsonist is hidden and difficult to find, the gas flaring arsonist is in the open. Not only does he move about in freedom, he is also the very one who calls the shots in the petroleum industry.
The stand of the law is most unambiguous: where death results from arson, the arsonist is guilty of murder, even where he did not intend to kill. Many of our people in the Niger Delta Region are dying from the satanic practice of gas flaring. It has since become clear that the role of the Nigerian government in this regard has been that of a pimp. In just the same way that the pimp controls the prostitutes and lives on the money they earn, we have since observed that while the residents of the oil producing areas suffer the degradation from gas flaring, our government is busy enjoying the fruits of that evil. This explains the reluctance and foot-dragging on the part of government to enforce the numerous legislations against the practice in Nigeria. We shall return to this later.
In this essay, we shall attempt to restrict our discussions to what is called associated gas. Over the years, all eyes have been on "King oil" while little attention has been paid to our abundant gas resources. Experts tell us that we are blessed with such abundant gas resources that elsewhere, Nigeria is often described as "ocean of gas with a speck of oil".
What we have is that oil and gas are found mixed up in the same wells. In order to get to the oil, which has been described as the lucrative driver, what the oil companies do is to burn off the gas, which is found on the top layer in the well.
While associated gas flaring has been increasingly frowned at in other parts of the world, it has flourished in Nigeria. In this world of waste and wanton destruction of a natural resource, which God graciously blessed man with, Nigeria is conspicuously placed at number one in the entire world. In the whole continent of Africa, Nigeria occupies the first, second, third and" positions.
Worse still, gas burning has contributed more greenhouse gases in Nigeria than all other factors in Sub-Saharan Africa combined. The flares contain a cocktail of toxins that immensely impact on the health and livelihood of local communities, exposing residents of the Niger Delta Region to the high risk of premature death, cancer, respiratory illnesses and asthma.
With crude oil production of about 2.5 million barrels per day in Nigeria, about 2.5 billion cubic feet of associated gas is produced and wasted daily due to the high gas/oil ratio of about 1000 cubic feet per barrel. The more ethical practice would be to gather this associated gas and re-inject it into the ground and so preserve it, so that when crude oil, which is a wasting asset, gets exhausted, we can fall back on the gas reserves. But since the process of gas re-injection is a bit more cumbersome and a little more time consuming, the oil companies prefer the path of least resistance by just flaring the gas. In the eye of the Environmental Rights Action, the Nigerian situation has been aptly seen as "a monstrous and unnecessary state of affairs, especially in a country where more than 66 percent of the population reportedly live below the poverty line and where the benefits of nearly half a century of oil production have gone almost exclusively to the multinationals and the corrupt local elite."
In 1979, the first attempt to compel gas re-injection came in the form of the Associated Gas Re-injection Act. The Act was further amended in 1984 to provide a fine of two kobo per 1000 cubic feet of gas flared. Rather than embarking on re-injection, the oil-producing companies have always opted for the payment of the fines. As a result, the oil companies paid some $2.21 billion in fines for gas flaring in 1986 alone.
The Global Gas Flaring Reduction Initiative promoted by the World Bank is focused mainly on Nigeria. Yet, Nigeria remains the world's worst offender in gas flaring. We have set various dates to end this dangerous practice; we have recommended stiff penalties for non-adherence; yet, gas flaring persists. As an instance, section 4(1) of the Associated Gas Re-injection Act stipulates, "where any person is engaged in flaring, the person concerned shall forfeit the concessions granted to him in the particular field or fields of its in relation to which the offence was committed." Climate Justice concludes that: "Broken promises, shifting commitments, shady deals and ignored legislations mar the history of flare-out targets."
The first day of January 2008 was named as the flare-out date. In May 2000, Dr. Imeh Okupido, Nigeria's Minister of State for Environment at the time, held a meeting with the major oil-producing companies and asked them to set a convenient date by themselves to end gas flaring. He got the following responses: Chevron 2008; Total-Final Elf 2008; Texaco 2005/2006; Agip 2005, and Exxon Mobil 2004. One would have expected that even a professed criminal would respect a target set by himself. Earlier, the Federal Government had set up a Committee, which in a Report called "Vision 2010" also agreed on 2008 as the Zero-flaring Date.
Nothing here vitiates from the fact that at least some sparing gas conservation efforts have been made by some oil-producing companies. As an instance, the pioneering efforts of Nigerian Agip Oil Company in this regard cannot be easily swept under the carpet.
All the same, there is no greater injustice and oppression than the situation in which the people of the Niger Delta Region bear the harmful consequences of gas flaring while the government simply sits on the proceeds from the heinous act. There is no reason why a society, which has been guilty of oppression for years, should not be required to pay compensation for past wrongs. The principle of compensatory justice can be made politically relevant to existing conditions.
Consequently, two quick steps must be taken here: One, the entire vacillation on the flare-out target must end immediately; Government must summon the will to enforce the legislation, which includes dropping its own role as a pimp. Two, the total back-log of earning from gas flaring and associated activities must be calculated with interest and paid to the oil-producing areas where it rightly belongs. This is the moral equivalence of the illegal with-holding of the funds of the Lagos State Local Government Councils during the Obasanjo years. Tragically, the evils of oppression now live with the oppressors!

Original_Search_Title: Rediscovering the Art of Adire
Headline: Nigeria: Rediscovering the Art of Adire
Body_Text:
Wives of Foreign Heads of Mission to Nigeria recently paid a courtesy call to the Nike Art Gallery in Piwoyi, Abuja. Leading the delegation was wife of the Egyptian Ambassador, Mrs. Heba Naguib who explained the need for the visit. She said, "This is in a bid to further bind the diplomatic community to Nigeria culture and art wise. Cultural integration is key for us wherever it is we find ourselves. This is also a very good opportunity to educate us on Nigerian cultural values and art."
Going around the gallery showcasing works of art, being described by the gallery boss, Chief Nike Okundaye, the group expressed great admiration and awe for the pieces on display. Commending how well the paintings, pencil/charcoal, beads and most of all the adire pieces reflected the beauty and fullness of Nigeria and Africa as a whole, Mrs. Adriana Baistrocchi said, "Being in Africa over a long time now, these works are simply fabulous and mind blowing."
Even more fascinating were the revelations at the workshop. Okundaye explained the entire process of making aso oke and tie and dye. From spinning the cotton into balls until they become thread, to weaving them for aso oke and other purposes. It could take as long as three weeks to tie only a five yard piece of fabric before it is eventually dyed. Using cassava paste and feathers from different birds depending on the desired style, it could take up to a week or more to make the patterns. Waxing though, is a lot easier and faster, it was discovered. This left the guests, in awe and admiration. "You cannot complain that our prices are too high because it is a painstaking job. Since we go for the best we cannot afford to cut corners," remarked Chief Okundaye.
They had the opportunity to learn making adire in about ten minutes with school children using handkerchief and rubber band. These they later bought off the children.
Climaxing this was an energetic and participatory dance presentation by the Nike Gallery dance band and gbagyi women under training there. Overwhelmed by what they saw, the group made a donation of one hundred thousand naira, as incentive to further encourage work going on at the gallery.

Original_Search_Title: Manyemen Gets Pulse Of CERAC's Generosity
Headline: Cameroon: Manyemen Gets Pulse Of CERAC's Generosity
Body_Text:
Within the framework of activities marking the celebration of the 55th International Leprosy Day, members of the Circle of Friends of Cameroon (known by its French acronym CERAC), on Sunday offered consignments of gifts to the patients of the Manyemen Leprosy Centre in Kupe Muanenguba Division. Situated some 82 kilometres from Kumba (Meme Division), all roads in the South West led to Manyemen for an occasion that highlighted the First Lady's idea for an ideal society where the most vulnerable are not marginalized.
In her speech, Dr Mrs Diana Acha Morfaw, the personal representative of Mrs Chantal Biya (Founding-President of CERAC) conveyed the First Lady's relentless appeal "for more solidarity in the fight against this disease that in insidious in our society." She noted that the massive turn-out was a clear testimony to the fact that the Founding President's appeal for the consolidation of the fight against leprosy and the rehabilitation of the patients is widely heeded to. She reiterated the First Lady's message for the patients never to give up but to persevere and work hard in order to benefit from the attention and the confidence of fellow Cameroonians.
A clear testimony of CERAC's concern for the patients was best exemplified by the assorted consignments of gifts. In the domain of animal production, the gifts were impressive; notably 500 three weeks old chickens, 16 three months old piglets, bags of feed, medicines such as antibiotics, anticoccidiosis, antistress, vitamins, sulphonamides, antiparasitics, iron and disinfectants. On food processing, foodstuff and household items, a cassava grinding machine, bags of rice, bags of beans, bags of garri, bags of salt, tins of palm oil, lanterns, buckets and cartoons of soap were offered. Worth mentioning are five cartons of various medicines and also assorted clothes. Cognizant that its better to teach someone how to fish than offering him fish, the First Lady's personal representative noted that the gifts are geared principally towards capacity building, poverty alleviation as well as income generating.
A representative of the leprosy patients Mr Godlove Njikang ( the oldest HRC patient, admitted 1954) expressed immense gratitude to Mrs Chantal Biya and CERAC for the gesture; pledging that their work in the poultry and piggery will earn them money and provide them with a constant source of protein. While presenting the Hanseniasis and Rehabilitation Centre Manyemen started in 1953, the Medical Officer incharge of the Presbyterian Medical Institutions Manyemen Dr Nesoah Anthony Ngoe harped on the laudable assistance so far offered by CERAC and the Government. By like Oliver Twist, he pinpointed some difficulties that need to be addressed. He identified the deplorable state of houses in the lone existing Camp that hosts more than 20 patients. "The Camp is more than 50 years old and needs renovation," he pleaded, adding that some basic facilities are lacking, there's no constant flow of water and there's regular electricity blackout.
After hailing what he termed a huge humanitarian gesture, the South West Provincial Delegate of Public Health Dr Mafany Njie disclosed that they are 133 leprosy patients in the South West Province. He assured the CERAC delegation that the gifts will be used judiciously for the intended purpose.
A visit to the poultry and the piggery, gave the CERAC delegation an insight of what the leprosy patients are capable of doing if there's the means. For the patients themselves, the gesture of the First Lady and CERAC, shall ever remain indelible.

Original_Search_Title: Ugandan Enjoys Live Africa Nations Cup
Headline: Ghana/Uganda: Ugandan Enjoys Live Africa Nations Cup
Body_Text:
Kampala — Seven lucky people won a fully paid trip to Ghana where they watched some Africa Nations Cup games. The fans, sponsored by telecommunications giants MTN, returned on Thursday and Daily Monitor caught up with one of the trip winners Mr. Fredrick Kasozi. The commercial officer in Masindi District Local Government reveals his experience in the West African nation.
Qn: How you are feeling now.
This so great. I am really excited. First all I am happy to be back and can't wait to share my experience with my family and friends.
Qn: How many messages did you send and where you optimistic that you were going to win the trip?
I sent 41 messages and to be sincere I just left the rest to chance because I knew I wasn't the only one who had sent. Many other people also wanted to go.
Qn: Was it your first time to win in any given promotion?
No actually the first thing I won was a watch five years ago. And over the years I have won numerous prizes in the soda promotion. I have won T.shirts, caps, money and many more things. But certainly this is the biggest I have won.
Qn: Do you regret that Uganda Uganda Cranes didn't make it to Ghana?
I was really hurt when we failed to qualify. I used to follow the qualification campaign very much but ended up being hurt. I would have loved to watch our boys tussle it out with the rest of Africa.
Qn: Which team did you support during your stay in Ghana?
In the opening game I supported Ghana but my favourite team is Cameroon.
Qn: Who is the soccer player you love most in Africa?
I support Cameroon but I really love Drogba (Didier). I don't know why but I just like him. Eto'o (Samuel) comes second or third.
Qn: How did you feel when Cameroon lost to Egypt?
It was one of the worst experiences I had during my stay in Ghana. I couldn't believe it seeing the Indomitable Lions struggle against Egypt.
Qn: What are some of the things you did besides in Ghana besides watching soccer?
I visited some tourist attractions. They have a famous dish called Gazi. It's dried cassava that is made into flour and then prepared as porridge. I really enjoyed it.
Qn: Any plans to travel back to Ghana for the final stages of the tournament?
I have to get back to work. I will watch on television maybe.
Qn: What's your final word?
I would like to thank MTN. This is a very good gesture to clients.

Original_Search_Title: The Poetry of Pain in the Niger Delta
Headline: Nigeria: The Poetry of Pain in the Niger Delta
Body_Text:
Lagos — The shrieks and murmurs of our departed ones have gravely banished the bewildered and polluted air Over the vortex-filled estuaries, and hinterlands of old where innumerable lengths of moronic drill pipes Pierces through their forlorn skulls, femurs, and ribs to the deep, in search for the Black Gold.
The thickened blood and ardent sweat of their endless labours for time immemorial have produced with grief, this highly prized global mineral of our time that was aspiring to be a human death trap. But in disguise.
Unknown to the seemingly dark world Peopled by black people of divers races, The Black Gold also fought for freedom in its abode several feet beneath the earth's surface according to a divine logarithm of nature, until its first show of "abundance" in 1859 in the USA .
Then in other parts of the world. Africa . Nigeria . Niger Delta. The unequalled decadence that was to follow, with the insurmountable injustice that would remain unabated were cleverly parceled in an inauspicious, inchoate incision.
The dispirited didactic cry of tear-soaked Oloibiri can be heard in the rough, tough, and ever wailing distant seas.
Where unspeakable momentum envelops the search for the Black Gold that once was her daughter; though born like in still birth, yet in rejoicing for her stake in fostering demonstrative patrimony, at least as viewed by two inebriated and immutable friends.
One, whose slippery and sinister motives were aided, by the other's treacherous architects of cant, within. Their historical pages have eroded beyond repair. The wailings of the devastatingly overburdened Niger Delta though faint, can still be heard in the distant lands.
Beyond the horizon's frame, across borders, over the pregnant seas, and desert regions where some of her co-pregnant friends can be found; whose cultures encourage thorough washing of a newborn with Machiavellian precision to eschew body odour, afterwards, while our own cassava leaves for unplumbed, noble purposes are recurrently drowned in the oceanic whirlpool of dynamite shots, followed by incessant spills.
In colossal poverty, our dwindled mothers did not hesitate in surrendering the precious milk on their deflated breasts to the exploring and exploiting, circumstantial brothers.
In abject penury, our necessitous daughters could not hold back their priceless virginity that once defined their astute morality, to voracious stakeholders at theatres of immoral feastings. some of the resulting sperm cells that never breathed, the weeping womb and unfathomable specious menstrual blood and the proverbial partially aborted, unspoken babies would yet add to the already fuming Black Gold reserves.
The shimmering waters of unpredictable destiny are far too deep for the ill-fated children of our time, whose sad rape encounter at age six renders them barren all through their dementedly saddened walk along lifeís pathways.
The Newtonian issue of blood from the vandalized wall, the ensuing decay of a vigorously created door, and the howling smiles of the gruesome beneficiaries are like two equivocal levers that cannot be balanced.
While their victims writhe in sacrilegious pain and misery, the vile actors enjoy momentary pleasure with vicious keen stake.
Where are the likes of the heroic Isaac Boro whose perpetual groaning on ëthe other sideí is more audible than the 1,000,000 dBA noise from the piling of stove pipes?
And the day to day ferocious activities of oilrigs all over his disembowelled historic domain (of influence)? his resurrection today would not bring a change as the fishes he once ate are no more in the rivers.
The animals he fed on are already extinct in our rain forests. The breast that nourished him is flattened beyond restoration. The size of the flowlines via his grave is cardiac arrest weaponry.
And where else can we find the likes of ingenious Ken Saro-Wiwa whose twelve-year streaming tears in the grave nearly drowns him but still overflood the shores of perpetual struggle; for the emancipation of a deprived generation?
Though fallen, his voice yet echoes and thunders sporadically for an awakening in the seemingly dead conscience of a beleaguered and precariously striving people.
Heretofore frog-marched, then silenced in cold blood and global pain. Yet his imponderable footsteps can be heard empathetically in the black days for, and of the black man, in a black climate.
Seemingly endless chances might be for the 21st century cultural virgins.
Though some also came close, but never tasted the indelible pain and ever biting agony of nightmare days and nights, in straps as the human drill bit drills the irrevocable exploratory well of life.
The unlucky wells produced. Our faces were bright; then smeared later. but the countless number of failures at routinely babyish jobs satirically, is the aphorism for such experts of infinite-finite incubus.
The bacchanalian revels that characterize their theatres in a cosmopolitan society without a cosmopolitan character is silhouetted in black faces that radiate black light for the Black Gold. And now, there are no more fathers in the land. No more future in the future in a babyís eyes.
Ours are times of circumstantial childhood fatherliness and regrettable fatherly childliness in ignobly deciduous tears.
When the sweet food in our mouths turns sour in our stomachs, the complex but beautiful landforms made repugnant in our souls, and our crystalline odysseys bedeviled pedagogues in an opaquely outmanoeuvred leadership web by the majority.
All gotten. All forcibly surrendered. Yet insatiable. The "mighty"! but remember: the roof on fire also means no sleep for the dweller.
Biowe wrote from Lagos

Original_Search_Title: BDA Extends Financing to Cattle and Poultry Breeding
Headline: Angola: BDA Extends Financing to Cattle and Poultry Breeding
Body_Text:
Luanda — The State-owned Angola Development Bank (BDA) this year will start financing projects on cultivation of cassava and breeding of poultry and cattle (cows, goats and pigs). the information was released Wednesday in Luanda by an administrators of the institution, Amândio Esteves.
Amândio Esteves said so to the press, at the end of a workshop on Credit Processing, which is an aspect BDA is committed to improving this year, for a better participation in the restructuring of the country's productive chain.
The incentive of BDA to the breeding of bovines will comprise the provinces of Kwanza Norte, Huila, Cunene and Kwanza Sul.
The provinces of Lunda Norte, Lunda Sul, Malanje, Moxico and Kuando Kubango will be supported in the breeding of caprine creatures, whereas the financing for the cultivation of cassava will mainly go to the provinces of the north and east of the country.
The official also assured that the bank will keep its existing financing to the cultivation of maize, beans, cotton and building material.
"We'll extend the experience to the whole country. Our aim is to increase the (productive) capacity of the country ( )", he underlined.
The Angola Development Bank (BDA) was created in 2006.

Original_Search_Title: Government Auctions Smuggled Palm Oil, Gari
Headline: Sierra Leone: Government Auctions Smuggled Palm Oil, Gari
Body_Text:
Turay — Police officers working on the orders of government Saturday auctioned smuggled foodstuff intercepted by customs officials at the Kambia boarder crossing point.
Over five hundred jerry cans of palm oil and bags of cassava flour (gari) and groundnuts were put on sale at Guy Street, some few yards from Krootown Raod Market.
People from all walks of life trooped along the street to buy the commodities.
Palm oil was sold for Le 55, 000 per jerry can while gari was sold for Le 40,000 a bag.
Head of Adelaide Street Police Station, Inspector S. Bangura disclosed that his personnel were working hard to ensure that they monitor the auction.
"There are trained and qualified police officers now working at the borders," Bangura said and added, "We want to set a brighter example in order for people to be law abiding. People should stop exporting the resources of this country without the permission of the government," he said.
He said suspects are helping the police with their investigation.
Marima Turay, a palm oil seller at Krootown Road Market recalled: "We were selling palm oil for Le 1,700 a pint before government started auctioning the smuggled palm oil. We are now selling palm oil for Le 1,000." Isatu Sesay, a beneficiary of the windfall, said government should imprison the culprits for 17 years each.
She called on the police to execute their duties thoroughly at our country's boards.

Original_Search_Title: Price of Bread Set to Rise
Headline: Mozambique: Price of Bread Set to Rise
Body_Text:
Maputo — The Mozambican Association of Bakers (AMOPAO) has warned that the price of bread will rise by up to 14.3 per cent as from 1 February.
Cited in Monday's issue of the Maputo daily "Noticias", AMOPAO chairperson Victor Miguel said a loaf weighing 200 grams, that now costs four meticais, will cost 4.5 meticais - a rise of 12.5 per cent.
But smaller bread rolls will also go up in price by 50 centavos - from 3.5 to four meticais, which is an increase of 14.3 per cent. Such increases show how useful it is for speculators that coins smaller than 50 centavos have entirely disappeared from circulation.
In theory, coins of 20, 10, five and even one centavo exist. But no-one knows where they are. They are certainly never offered as change in shops. Their absence is an excuse for price increases never to be less than 50 centavos.
The justification for higher bread prices is that since October the price of a 50 kilo sack of wheat flour charged by the milling companies has risen by 220 meticais. (The last increase in the price of bread was in September 2007).
Asked whether there was any way to avoid increasing the price of bread, Miguel suggested that the solution would be to increase local production of wheat, thus ending the country's dependence on imports.
"We would like the government to help us by encouraging wheat production", he said. "We have provinces such as Tete and Manica where wheat can be grown".
As for the proposal to produce cheaper bread by mixing wheat and cassava flour, Miguel said "there isn't enough cassava production. But if this production were encouraged, and if we had sufficient quantities, this would also help".

Original_Search_Title: Dining With Artistes At Wasanii
Headline: Kenya: Dining With Artistes At Wasanii
Body_Text:
Recently reopened- after some years of a lull- the Kenya National Theatre bar, just renamed Wasanii bar, has just joined the list of competitive Nairobi restaurants offering ambient space for stylish relaxation.
But what is in a name? If he walked into the Kenya national theatre, William Shakespeare-whose artistic imitation once sat at the national theatre- would wonder. I too wondered when I first saw the Swahili name carefully inscribed on a wooden frame hanging from the balcony. But that was only until I ventured into the renovated space.
Words by Kerry Tabor, a feng shui and sustainable design consultant explain the transformation.
The environmental manager turned interior designer observed that these are the times when hospitality industry cannot afford to ignore an intuitive, creative and precise approach to interior design and placement.
Doubling as an entrainment spot and eatery, Wasanii seems to be caught up in this new reality. Other restaurants in the city are also in a rush to create tantalizing and ambient settings.
To advance further her interior design argument, Kerry said: "In an increasingly complex and depersonalised world, how to live with more balance and harmony is a key question for most of us. Somewhere in all the noise and stress of our lives we create moments of peace, simplicity, beauty and meaning. We yearn to feel connected to the community, nature and spirit.
The current frenzy of homes (and even restaurants) renovations and make-overs reveal our desire to make our immediate environments more personal; and beautiful, in the hope that this will provide comfort from the harshness of the outside world."
At the entrance is an abstract painting that warmly welcomes you. In its abstractness, the piece ushers you into a space freshly decorated with pieces of art.
On the wall is another piece that depicts rare innocence and harmony: there is a child cuddling a lamb as a goat roams in the wild ; then there is another where a nude boy who has been playing with some water trapped in a manger has just spotted a flashing camera.
In ordinary bars, a wooden frame with some wire mesh passes for a counter. At Wasanii, the counter is a traditional hut .
With drums, the piano on standby and other traditional instruments like the flute and nyatiti lying around the restaurant, it is clear that music and dance happens in this place.
Smiling waiters are adorned in khanga costumes while lights are beaded and covered with sisal sack materials that enhance their effect.
"With our carefully designed interiors, menu and music, the idea is to give our patrons an African feel," says Kent Libisu the restaurant co-owner.
There is also a bust of the late Wahome Mutahi who was a frequent patron-probably to keep his spirit alive .
Besides the entertainment and eating that Wasanii offers, it is an important well of creativity that the country has drank from.
For decades, the national theatre bar was a meeting point for artists .
As they drank, they also debated diverse issues that would later inspire their artistic works. They critiqued their works and publicised those in progress. This way, pieces for release cannot be missed.
Some of the playwrights who can trace the success of some of their works back to the bar are John Ruganda who died last month.
He wrote plays like The Burdens and Shreds Of Tenderness, Wahome Mutahi, Francis Imbuga whose Betrayal In The City was highly debated at the theatre bar, David Mulwa, Joe De Graft and even Jimmi Makotsi, who wrote She Ate The Female Cassava, were also regular patrons.
But why is the bar significant? Most artists still do not have a physical addresses. This makes it difficult for them to meet for networking sessions or meet clients.
Newcomers to theatre business require some orientation into the trade. This cannot happen, especially in a case where the older hands have moved to other businesses and rarely meet younger entrants. The bar bridges this gap.
Because it is open to the public, the bar also offers theatre fans a chance to interact with the artistes off the stage. Critics also get a chance to meet them to exchange ideas.
In the 80s- a time when intellectuals and artists in this country were in a cold war against the powers that were-the national theatre bar was one of their meeting points.
While people could not speak freely in other places, here patrons could engage in talk freely- thanks to their poetic licence.
Politicians and even radical academicians found space at the bar, meetings which later yielded organised lobbies for artistes' interests, radical theatre productions and boosted relationships amongst the artistes .
With the return of the bar, artistes have ample space to meet theatre lovers and colleagues.
As I get out of the Wasanii bar, I have no doubt why it is named so.

Original_Search_Title: Southern Investors to Launch Corporation
Headline: Rwanda: Southern Investors to Launch Corporation
Body_Text:
Kigali — In the space of just four weeks, Frw1.52b has been pledged by inhabitants of the Southern Province to form the Southern Province Investment Corporation (SPIC).
This initiative, spearheaded by the Private Sector Federation (PSF), aims to encourage Rwandese to pool resources and invest in bigger opportunities.
On January 10th PSF organised the final meeting for the investors from the south. The meeting, that took place at Prime Holdings Conference Hall, Kimihurura, was to give those pledging an opportunity to meet businesspeople now living and working around Kigali but originally from the South.
The Chairman of PSF, Robert Bayigamba, warned the business community that if they do not pool financial resources for investment, all investment opportunities in the country will be taken up by foreign investors.
The advent of a Rwandan stock exchange marks a good investment opportunity for corporations such as SPIC to invest in companies throughout Rwanda.
Bayigamba clarified that an individual, another company, an association or a cooperative can own shares in SPIC.
At Prime Holdings, 33 investors pooled Frw168b. At a previous meeting held in Nyanza, Southern Province, approximately 110 Southerners pooled Frw1.377b.
The meeting at Prime Holdings was chaired by Southern Province Governor, Fidel Niyosaba. He commended the initiative, saying that it will bring development to the south, create employment and enhance infrastructure development.
Southerners plan to invest the money in several different schemes. They plan to buy LABOPHAR, a pharmaceutical company that is yet to be privatised; to buy Mata Tea factory which is due to be privatised; to establish coffee washing stations; and finally to create a modern cassava mill.
Tribert Rujugiro, the Chairman of Rwanda Investment Group (RIG), stressed that SPIC's transparency will be strictly observed. He urged investors to embrace the spirit of working together that is found in the developed nations of Europe, Asia and America.
Similar initiatives are also making headways in other provinces. NORPIC in the North, whose total sum raised reportedly stands at RwF1b; WESPIC in the West, with RwF1.6b; EPIC in the East, with RwF2.675b; and the Champions Investment Corporation (CHIC), for Kigali residents, which has pooled RwF1.425b.
According to Jean Claude Uwizeyemungu, the PSF Local Investment and Research Manager, SPIC will officially be launched in a few weeks time.

Original_Search_Title: Man Forcefully Circumcised
Headline: Uganda: Man Forcefully Circumcised
Body_Text:
Kampala — WILLY Mafabi, a paraffin vendor in Malaba town, ran out of luck on January 3, when his tribesmen forcefully circumcised him.
According to his kinsmen, Mafabi, who hails from Bubulo county in Manafwa district, had been fleeing to Malaba whenever the circumcision season approached.
On the fateful day, his tribesmen rounded him up at Malaba Taxi Park, tied him and quickly sprinkled cassava flour allover his body. They forced him to carry two heavy stones as they marched him on the streets of Malaba, singing traditional Kigisu circumcision songs.
He was later circumcised at Akolodongo ward in the outskirts of Malaba town.

Original_Search_Title: Choosing an Endless Path to Literacy
Headline: Uganda: Choosing an Endless Path to Literacy
Body_Text:
Kampala — You may fail to recognise him when he is seated in class with his classmates.
But his big and rough jaws due to years of shaving, broad shoulders and muscled arms, betray his age, making him stand out among his Senior One classmates.
Joseph Ochwo, 47, is a student at Mahanga Senior Secondary School, Nagongera sub-county, Tororo district. There was excitement as the headteacher, Raphael Oweri, led me to Ochwo's classroom, S.1B, one rainy afternoon. When he called out for Ochwo, his classmates started chanting: "Our man, Mzee our man". There was an applause as Ochwo, a towering figure, rose up. He was smart in a short-sleeved white shirt, neatly tucked in his navy blue pair of trousers.
"The first day I reported at school, I told my fellow students to call me Joseph or mzee because the majority were fit to be my children.
Early this year, Ochwo was admitted to the school on merit after attaining a second grade (aggregate 15) in the 2007 Primary Leaving Examinations. This was the second time he was sitting for PLE. The first time was way back in 1978. Like in 2007, he had passed in division two, but dropped out of school due to lack of school fees.
Ochwo was able to return to school this year after the introduction of Universal Secondary Education (USE). Perhaps nobody in Tororo was as excited as Ochwo when President Yoweri Museveni announced the introduction of USE in his 2006 manifesto.
"It sounded like a dream. I remember shouting my voice hoarse when the president, in his campaign speech, pledged to introduce free secondary education. The pronouncement gave me a new hope of joining secondary school," Ochwo says.
In 2007, he registered for PLE at Abongit, a primary school under the Universal Primary Education programme in Kisoko sub-county.
'I concentrated for only six months, but my teachers sacrificed a lot of their time to help me pass," Ochwo says.
Away from his wife and two children, Ochwo is busy trying to adjust to the school environment.
"I am happy to be back in school. I feel encouraged when on my way to school people shout: 'Student! student!' My ambition is to become a lawyer," he says.
Dorothy Auma, one of Ochwo's classmates, says she was startled the first time she saw him. "I thought he was a student teacher on teaching practice, but I could not believe it when they introduced him as a fellow student."
"Mzee is generous and everyone in class loves him," she says.
John Okoth, another classmate, says he was inspired by Ochwo's activeness in class.
"He contributes actively and asks intelligent questions."
James Egesa, the chemistry teacher, describes Ochwo as 'academically aggressive'.
The headteacher says Ochwo is a promising student. "In first term, he scored 87% in English. He is good at both written and spoken English," says Oweri.
His first term records show that he scored above average in arts subjects and below average in sciences. Dickson Okello, the class teacher, says Ochwo is disciplined and attends school regularly.
"Although he rides 16km to school, he is always in time, except in a few cases when his bicycle breaks down," says Okello.
In June, he survived an accident on his way to school. For two weeks, he could not go to school because his bicycle had broken down. His school uniform also got torn during the accident. However, Tororo district chairman, Emmanuel Osuna, came to his rescue. He offered him sh100,000 to buy scholastic materials.
If there is any other person whom Ochwo feels indebted to, it is his wife Eunice Awori. For the 14 years they have been married, she has stood by him during hard times.
"In 2006, when I was pondering going back to school, she encouraged me to go ahead with my plans. She even offered to provide for the family as I studied," he says.
Awori sells chicken and dry cassava to sustain the family. Ochwo's going back to school has inspired his children.
"Last term my eldest son was the third in his class, while his sibling was seventh," says Ochwo.
Born to Anastanzia Onyango and Yolina Abbo (RIP) of Abinget in Kisoko sub-county, Ochwo was the ninth of the eleven children. As a young boy, he wanted to study hard for a bright future, but life took a different turn after he received his PLE results.
When he took the result slip to his father, he told him he could no longer continue paying his school fees. That is when Ochwo realised that his future was bleak. None of his elder siblings could raise his school fees. From then, Ochwo had to struggle to survive, moving from one job to another.
His turning point, however, came in 1992 when he joined the Assembly of God church, where he served as a catechist. In 2001, the church acknowledged his potential, along with a dozen others from other parts of the country. They were all sponsored for a two-year course in Theology at Rubaga in Jinja. He successfully completed the course and attained a certificate.
Another great opportunity prevailed in 2003. The church offered a scholarship to its catechists for a three-year course in a theological college in America, that would see them elevated from the status of catechists to pastors.
As fate would have it, Ochwo missed out because he did not have the required qualifications.
"The scholarship required participants to have an O'level certificate, but I did not have it," he says.
The sense of loss was later to compel him to return to school. A typical day for Ochwo begins at 5:00am when he wakes up to go and dig in the garden. By 6:00am he is back home preparing to go to school. When he returns in the evening, he bathes and eats his dinner before settling down for revision until 9:30pm, when he retires. Being a catechist, Sundays are busy for him.
"I prepare my sermons on Saturday evening and by 7:00am on Sunday, I am ready to lead the service."

Original_Search_Title: Why Government is Fighting Anaemia
Headline: Rwanda: Why Government is Fighting Anaemia
Body_Text:
Kigali — It will take many initiatives in order to achieve Vision 2020.
The surprising one and easiest is the support of a comprehensive food fortification programme.
The programme, which is underway, will entail adding vitamins to foods. Food fortification is common in many other countries including the United States and Europe.
Through food fortification, staple products like salt, cassava, or cooking oils are fortified with important micronutrients like iron, iodine, and vitamin A.
In this way, every time a Rwandan eats a meal, they are also going to be getting these vital vitamins and minerals. Why is this so critical to achieving Vision 2020?
Micronutrients are essential to human resource development and creating an entrepreneurial, knowledge based, economy. Consider that iron deficient anaemia, a condition where a person has an inadequate level of healthy red blood cells.
Iron is naturally, found in a few vegetables but is most common in meats, (but) Rwandans eat less meat.
Anemia in children affects their physical growth, mental development, and learning capacity (WHO, 2007) and we cannot expect to evolve to an information-based economy without well-educated children. Beyond just educating our children, anemia affects productivity in the workplace.
The U.S. Center for Disease Control estimates that anaemia among men and women reduce productivity by as much as 30 per cent.
Iodine is another micronutrient essential to achieving Vision 2020.
Iodine deficiency is not just the leading cause of mental retardation in the world, but even more relevant to Vision 2020 is that it reduces IQ by as much as 15 per cent in children.
It is estimated that as many as 50,000 children in Rwanda suffer from mental impairment due to a lack of adequate levels of iodine.
Iodine deficiency is especially prevalent in mountainous areas of the world like Rwanda where the iodine has been leached out of the soils through natural erosion.
While Rwanda has made great strides in correcting this problem through iodizing our salt (the only Rwandan food fortification program in place today), we must improve compliance and consider other food staples to fortify.
In order to reach our aspirations, Vision 2020 for example contemplates 100 percent enrolment in primary schools as one of the 47 indicators. Yet full enrolment is not sufficient if our children arrive at school unprepared to learn and to reach their full potential.
The same is true for other indicators such as the number of technical training centres, to open by 2020 or having 100 percent of our teachers fully qualified.
None of these indicators will, by themselves, lead to accomplishing the ambitions of the four pillars outlined in Vision 2020 - our students in primary, secondary, and tertiary education must be prepared to learn when they enter the classroom.
This is why proper micronutrient health is essential to reaching Rwanda's dream of Vision 2020.

Original_Search_Title: Ebonyi Institutes Awards for Achievers as Varsity Gets New VC
Headline: Nigeria: Ebonyi Institutes Awards for Achievers as Varsity Gets New VC
Body_Text:
ABAKALIKI-Ebonyi State government has instituted awards to honour categories of individuals and institutions that exhibit best virtues and values amongst their peers.
The awards includes: the cleanest local government area in the state, best local government chairman in the state, best development centre coordinator in the state, best local government area junior/senior staff, best GM/chairman of Board and best permanent secretary.
Others are bet Exco member, best DC junior/senior, best junior/senior civil servant, best secondary school principal as well as best primary school teacher.
According to the state Commissioner for Information and Orientation, Mr. Kenneth Uhuo, the criteria for institutional awards like the cleanest LGA shall be the cleanliness of local govern-ment development centres Headquarters, markets, communities, availability/maintenance of toilet facilities, health centres/schools, clearing and maintenance of roads and maintenance of industrial hazards like cassava processing.
Also, the state governor Martin Elechi has announced the appointment of Professor Francis Idike as the new Vice Chancellor of the state university.
Professor Idike, until his appointment, was the Deputy Vice-Chancellor at the University of Nigeria, Nsukka, (UNN). He takes over from Professor Fidelis Ogah, whose tenure expired last September and has retired from active service.
Ogah, who was the premier VC of EBSU, had, during the second convocation cere-mony of the university turned down the offer of Governor Elechi that he continues as the EBSU VC, saying that having spent more than 20 years in the university as an administrator, he deserved some rest.
In a statement by the Chief Press Secretary to the Governor, Onyekachi Eni, Governor Elechi appointed the new Vice-Chancellor and two other principal officers namely; a new Registrar, Mr. Sam Nte Egwu, and University Librarian, Mr. Friday Ibiam.

Original_Search_Title: Restaurant Menu Go Regional
Headline: Rwanda: Restaurant Menu Go Regional
Body_Text:
Kigali — The end of the 1994 genocide saw political calm return to Rwanda. And many Rwandans journeyed from exile home. Fourteen years after they settled, there are still visible tendencies that depict their lifestyles when they were in exile.
Remera, located in the suburbs of Kigali City, is an area that harbours a considerable number of former exiles. The small restaurants in the area are faced with the task of identifying with the diverse customer preferences.
Vision Good 2020 restaurant, is one of the diverse eating places in Giporoso, Remera, that is torn between the those who did not go to exile and the Rwandan returnees.
The owner of the restaurant, refusing to disclose her real name says that she is commonly called 'Mulamu' by her customers.
"It is hard to tell the count of former exiles but they are many in this place," she said. The sole proprietor, who is also a former exile from Uganda, says that most of her dishes are Ugandan.
"I prepare matooke, posho and other food but I add chips to capture those who remained," she says.
Charles Rukundo is also a former exile and a student at St Martyrs Secondary school. He uses the proximity of the school to the restaurants as an advantage to have meals at Mulamu's restaurant.
But before he identified a restaurant that appealed to his taste, he experienced one of the restaurants specific to local dishes.
"I went to one of the native restaurants and found that instead of mashing the matooke they pound it. Since then I have been eating from Mulamu's restaurant," he recalls.
However, Rukundo says that the foreign cooking has over the years drawn the attention of the natives. After his meal at the restaurant, Bosco Nzeyimana, a businessman is more drawn to the foreign dishes than the Rwandan ones.
"I always have lunch here and I like the food. It is well cooked. Growing up, I never liked obugari," he says.
But in Kimihurura just at the outskirts of the City, the one year old Afrika Bite, poses as a restaurant that captures an all round East African taste with endeavors to meet every Rwandan's taste.
"We alternate meals on a daily basis. You can't please everyone but we serve ten dishes. Most of the East African dishes are more less the same," says Daphne Kajugiira."
In Kacyiru, a city suburb, the four year old Barbers' restaurant located on Kigali Business Centre building is faced with the haggles of differing tastes.
"We have people who want different dishes," Tofilo Mukasa, the assistant manager of the restaurantsaid. But he says Rwandans love the retaurant.
"The Rwandan who never went to exile prefer cassava beans, peas, groundnut sauce and chips. Those who where exiled in Kenya like posho, meat and peas. Those from Tanzania have the same tastes as those from Kenya. And those from Uganda love matooke ground nuts and meat," he says.
However, there are restaurants that despite the diverse tastes of the populace they opt to focus on a specific customer base.
Amos Kalisa is seated at Mirembe restaurant also located in Giporoso, having an early lunch.
"I got used to posho in Uganda where I was born and I have failed to eat obugari (local cassava bread)," says another former exile.
"Their groundnut sauce is prepared well," says Kalisa and adds that it is the reason that would keep him missing some of the local dishes cooked by the wife.
"I married my wife here but she doesn't cook that food I was used to when I was in Uganda," he says. But the reality is that after the genocide, most of the former exiles returned with divergent cultures.
"I can't eat obugari," Kalisa reitarates.
Birori Juma, is the owner of Mirembe restaurant that started operating in 2005. He says that most customers are attracted by the Ugandan way of cooking.
"I can't estimate the (customers) but they are many returnees who eat from here compared to the natives." Making entry into the restaurant the atmosphere feels foreign with the common presence of an alien dialect.
Already served and enjoying their lunch, some of the customers are chatting using Luganda, a popular tribal language in Uganda. But as I continue listening there are traces of Kinyarwanda though the mainstream is the foreign lauguage.
Hajati Annet, the head cook recalls that when the restaurant started, all the customers were former exiles, before the then owner died and they had to shut down for a while.
"The place was small and they used to make long queues and as some ate others had to wait outside."
It is exactly lunchtime and outside Tam Tam restaurant, compared to other restaurants there are many people eating. The customers are occupying both the inside and outside of the restaurants.
Chez Innocent , the owner says that the eatery's dishes are local.
"Here we usually receive mostly the Rwandans who did not go to exile and the cooking is purely local," Chez says.
The owner of the one year old restaurant says that his choice of targeting native customers is a competitive strategy directed at a larger number of restaurants in the same area that seem to be targeting former exiles.
So with a past that dictated that some Rwandans make a choice to leave their homeland into exile the consequences are that some have stuck with foreign cultures even in their native land.

Original_Search_Title: Floods Strike Again, Kill Two
Headline: Uganda: Floods Strike Again, Kill Two
Body_Text:
Kampala — Floods that ravaged the north and northeastern region last year are back, killing two UPDF soldiers in Nakapiripirit and Bukwo districts.
Three districts of Kotido, Abim and Kaabong are now inaccessible from Moroto.
About 150 inmates from Nebbi had to be relocated to Arua after Ragem Prison was submerged.
Frank Baine, the prisons spokesman, said: "There was too much rain on the side of DR Congo which is near Ragem Prison."
The 3rd Division spokesman, Capt. Henry Obbo, said a soldier at Lolachat army detachment was washed away by River Lolachat in Nakapiripirit.
"His body was recovered at the banks of the river after a two-day search, with the magazine pouch still strapped on his chest," Obbo said. Obbo said the second soldier attached to the 67th Battalion in Bukwo was swept by the Bukwo-Lukiya stream as he crossed it.
The soldier, Obbo added, was moving with colleagues on the river bank when he was swept away. He said the soldiers often crossed the river and the soldier possibly underestimated the waters.
His body, Obbo explained, was recovered in the Bukwo-Lukiya stream after a four-day search. Obbo did not name the dead soldiers.
He attributed both incidents to heavy rains pounding Karamoja region. "These are the first cases of drowning of soldiers since the deployment of soldiers in Karamoja for disarmament," Obbo said.
As the rains continue to pound the region, travellers from the districts of Abim, Kotido and Kaabong are stranded at Loopei, 30kms from Moroto town.
The World Food Programme lorries ferrying relief food to Karamoja have been stuck between Orungo and Iriiri for the last six days.
In Pingire, Soroti district, about 1,000 homes were affected. LC3 chair Michael Ongwara told an emergency council meeting on Monday that 21 of the 31 villages on the shores of Lake Kyoga had been badly hit.
In Labori, he said, 70 households were displaced. In Osamitoi, Obangin and Omiria and Ongwara, cassava and potato gardens were washed away.
In Tororo district, the disaster committee on Monday closed Bumanda Primary School in Iyolwa sub-county after water filled up its pit-latrines.
Committee chief Richard Gulume said 556 pupils were sent home. Another 180 pupils, he said, were also affected at Osire Community School in Osukuru sub-county.
He said the disaster committee would meet to find ways of helping the victims.
Musa Ecweru, the disaster preparedness minister, told reporters in Kampala that mountainous areas in Mbale and western Uganda could suffer landslides.
Ecweru said Ora Bridge in Nebbi had flooded and was almost swept away. He said in Teso, the rains had washed away some feeder roads.

Original_Search_Title: Kyabazinga Storm Brews
Headline: Uganda: Kyabazinga Storm Brews
Body_Text:
They depend on fene (jack fruit) for survival. Sometimes jiggers harass them. The main tarmac road passing through the sub-region is used for drying cassava because there is little economic activity.
Poverty is the main story. But they have regularly overwhelmingly voted NRM and for President Yoweri Museveni. These fellows are known as the Basoga peasants. However, it appears their marriage is getting rocky.
Why? Apparently because Mr Museveni, has reportedly taken sides in their cultural issues; wanting to influence the election of the next Kyabazinga.
Grapevine has been told that Mr Museveni insists on supporting the allegedly unpopular candidate Wako Muloki to replace his late father Henry Wako Mulooki against the wishes of the majority, who would have preferred Wilberforce Nadiope as their cultural leader. The traditional chiefs, who form the Electoral College, have reportedly been approached and as a result, some of them have withdrawn their initial support for Nadiope.
This has left Busoga sharply divided. Some Basoga are angry that Mr Museveni's reason for fighting Nadiope is that his grandparents were members of the Opposition UPC party.
Museveni aide for Mbarara Municipality
He was accused of being an imposter. He was even arrested and taken to court but the case ended there. The same guy even had the audacity to drag the powerful Principal Private Secretary to the President, Amelia Kyambadde, to court. His name is Hakeem Asiimwe a.k.a Lukenge. After all the tribulations he underwent, Mr Asiimwe has set his eyes on Parliament.
We hear the man wants to give the Mbarara Municipality parliamentary seat a shot and that it could be something of a money-ridden campaign. Sources say the guy decided to try politics after he realised that being rich without a political powerbase was disastrous in today's Uganda.
He has reportedly learnt that guys like Jim Muhwezi would have been suffocated long ago if they weren't politicians yet other rich men have been brought to their knees because they lacked political clout.
District chiefs for Kyankwanzi
