Documents showed that Mr Chinedu and his wife Joyce Akinyi Ochieng attracted the attention of the police after Ms Akinyi deposited Sh4 million at the Koinange Street branch of the Housing Finance Company of Kenya on the morning of July 2, 2003 and a similar amount in the afternoon of the same day.
Banking Fraud Investigations Unit detectives were called and arrested her and found her with an additional Sh 37,000 and US$ 5,100 (Sh 336,600). Although she was not found guilty of money laundering charges, she was compelled to pay Sh 2.5 million in accrued tax on two years' income to KRA.
Documents presented to police indicate that Chinedu holds Cote d'Ívoire passport number 06LF01401 issued on November 14, 2006 and expires on November 13, 2009. His date of birth is listed as November 9, 1970.
However, while applying for a one-year multiple entry visa at the Immigration department in 2000 through Sleek Safaris, Antony Chinedu Ifedigbo presented a Nigerian passport number A0496834 that lists his date of birth as November 11, 1970. Sllek (sic) Safaris and Sports is a Nigerian company specializing in the importation of cassava flour (garri) to Kenya.
The passport describes him as a Nigerian national. Kenya Revenue Authority tax documents show Chinedu's passport as number A2838856 and indicated that Chinedu's birthday is 1969.
The documents also list him as one of the two directors of Elegance Investments Ltd along with Joyce Theresa Akinyi Ochieng.

Original_Search_Title: 'Dead' Butaleja Man Found Alive
Headline: Uganda: 'Dead' Butaleja Man Found Alive
Body_Text:
Kampala — GRIEF turned into joy in Busolwe town in Butaleja district when a man, whom relatives thought had been lynched by a mob, was discovered alive in Malaba town in Tororo district.
A relative found Abdallah Kafunjo, 22, working as a casual labourer, loading and off loading long distance trucks at the customs yard.
He was suspected to have been one of the two thieves, who were last month killed by a mob in Namunsi village, Mbale district.
Residents said the group had been caught breaking into a shop in the village at night.
The bodies were later buried in Mbale cemetery. But a few days after, Abdallah's relatives exhumed the body they thought was his, for a decent burial.
The father, Yusuf Kafunjo, said on Friday they had parted with sh200,000 for the exhumation.
"We could not easily recognise the skeletal remains but relied on information from somebody, who claimed he witnessed the lynching and burial," he said.
"Because Abdallah had always been in and out of trouble, we took the body, thinking it was his."
Kafunjo explained that the report of his son's 'death' for theft was no big surprise because "Abdallah had been expelled from the village in January for theft."
"He used to steal petty things like neighbour's chicken, cassava and sometimes rice from barns, so the villagers got fed up and resolved during a meeting to send him away."
The family was making arrangements for Abdallah's last funerals rites at the end of the month.
But as the good news filtered in, family members jumped up in joy.
However, the relatives are still stuck on what to do with the grave, where the wrong body was buried.

Original_Search_Title: Northern Bypass Brings Mixed Fortunes
Headline: Uganda: Northern Bypass Brings Mixed Fortunes
Body_Text:
For over three years now, and still counting, what the European Union calls Uganda's largest road construction project has been underway with the public solemnly longing for the day its construction will finally end. The eager anticipation for the completion of the road is only self explanatory.
The road promises a new and fresh opening that will divert the now agonising traffic in Kampala's city centre and neighbouring suburbs. The road's completion is the dream of every road-user.
As the road graded its way through the land, in a bid to clear its path, it left lasting effects. As soon as its construction plans were made known, murmurs were stirred up amongst the fated people that lay in its path.
Their murmurs were only genuine.
They faced eviction to create space for the new road. And evicted they were; but with monetary compensation. And on since then, the road has come to leave significant changes both on the physical settings and the general lifestyles of the people that live near it.
Even before its completion, the Northern Bypass is already a source of livelihood for many. It has created settlements and commercial centres that are placed just by the sides of its path.
In Kyebando, a suburb located along the section of the by-pass near Kalerwe, a small trading centre has mushroomed along the road. Countless people stand at various points of the trading centre, buying, selling, and haggling at the price of a given commodity. The centre is characterised with wooden and metallic stalls, and a host of groceries in permanent structures.
A number of market stalls specialising in foodstuffs like fruits and vegetables are common. According to residents of the area, this settlement did not exist until the Northern Bypass crossed their yard. "Here, there was nothing.
It was just a few houses and bush," said a native as he pointed to a salon and wooden restaurant. An attendant at a shop explained that, "after the section of the road that crosses their area was complete, many people started using it and this made it strategic for shops."
These benefits are not exclusive to Kyebando. Many other forms of commerce are taking place along other sections of the road. In Kisaasi, just before you reach Kyebando, many traders occupy the road pavements as they sell off their wares.
Cloth vendors selling off second hand clothes lay them on mats and plastic sheets along the pavements. They stand or sit by their sides as they wait for any passersby.
The food vendors do not miss out either. These range from those that are roasting maize, sweet potatoes, and cassava, to those that are selling raw food.
One such business stood out. A fuso-truck-full of matooke, complimented by other mounds of sweet potatoes lay on the pavements of the road. A plump woman, Esther Nalule, sits in the middle of the matooke piles. She has been selling her food by the roadside for three months now. "I used to sell up in the market (Kyebando market) but I left for some reasons."
"Here though," she continued, "there are many people who pass by the road and I have all of them to myself." Nalule's customer targets are shared by the other traders that occupy the sides of the Northern Bypass. Ashraf, who sells shoes not far off, also says that the number of people that use the road, especially in the mornings and evenings, has increased of late and offers them ready market.
The bodaboda cyclists have also reaped. Kiwatule, Kisaasi, and Kyebando sections of the Northern Bypass all have bodaboda stages. The cyclists are the busiest on the road; whizzing past you every half minute with a passenger or two on the back. The transportation business also includes the passenger bicycles; the ones with soft cushions on the back. They too have designated stages along the road.
The road's influence spreads to the education sector as well. In between Namboole and Kalerwe, at least two schools, are named after the road. In Kiwatule, as the road starts its journey to Ntinda, is Highway Junior School. It is joined by the Bypass Standard Academy in Kyebando.
The bypass' negative effects cannot be ignored either. Residents of Kiwatule complain about deaths caused by accidents. The residents say that over three people die every week along the highway due to accidents.
"The cars that pass through the road over speed yet the road passes through settlements," one resident complained. In Kisaasi as well, residents complain about floods that accumulate due to the new drainage systems that have been created.
"The road disrupted the old drainage system and now the water attacks us in our houses," says a mechanic in Kisaasi.
For all its controversy, the Northern Bypass is set to be a blessing; one that is being enjoyed even before the completion.
The Kisaasi mechanic, in unison with the above beneficiaries of the road says, "We are already gaining a lot from the road. Many towns will come up because of this road. We only hope it can be finished quickly."

Original_Search_Title: Interest Rate - a Slide to the Ugly Past?
Headline: Nigeria: Interest Rate - a Slide to the Ugly Past?
Body_Text:
Lagos — on the 2nd of June, the Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN) increased the Monetary Policy Rate (MPR) by 25 basis points to 10.25 from 10.0 per cent. This would be the third time the apex bank will be increasing the MPR since it was introduced December 2006
It would be recalled that the MPR replaced the Minimum Rediscount Rate (MRR), as the bench mark for interest rate in the economy. While the MRR represents the minimum interest rate banks can borrow from the CBN, the MPR is a short- term interest rate at which banks can predictably borrow from the apex bank. Thus, the MPR serves as an indication for other interest rates be it deposit or lending rate in the economy
The MPR was introduced at 10.0 per cent with spread of 600 basis points around the rate, i.e. 300 basis points above and 300 basis points below. This translates into an upper limit of 13 per cent and a lower limit of 7 per cent.
The first movement in the MPR was announced on June 5, 2007, a 200 basis point reduction from 10.0 per cent to 8.0 per cent as well as a reduction in the width of the interest rate corridor from plus or minus 300 to plus or minus 250 basis points. But that was the last reduction as subsequent movements were increases. The first increase was a 100 basis point upward review to 9.0 per cent in October 2007, the second increase was a 50 basis points upward review to 9.5 per cent in December 2007. Later on, at the beginning of 2008, the MPR was further increased by 50 basis points to 10.0 per cent. Also pertinent is the fact that the increases in the MPR were occasioned by one major factor, which is the need to forestall upward movement in the inflation rate.
For example, in the communiqué that announced the first increase in October 2007, the CBN stated: "The MPC noted that year-on-year headline inflation would continue to remain single-digit in the rest of 2007, but at the upper region of the single digit range. Overall, a combination of stable food prices and a restrictive monetary policy stance is expected to help sustain the headline inflation within single digit. The Committee also noted the challenges arising from rising autonomous private inflows and the attendant risk of further appreciation of the naira/dollar exchange rate. In addition, the Committee also noted the high possibility of substantial fiscal injections in the fourth quarter, arising from the supplementary budget at the federal and state levels.
Similarly, in announcing the increases in December 2007, April 2008 and June 2008, the CBN stated, "The Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) noted that the overall macroeconomic picture in 2007 has reflected improved stability; just as the prospects for the first quarter 2008 appear good. The bank's projections show that the year-on-year (headline) inflation could remain single-digit in the first quarter of 2008, provided CBN continues to take proactive steps to manage the liquidity surfeit.
There remains a strong upside risk to inflation in the near-term due to expected large capital inflows and significant fiscal injections. In anticipation of the imminent fiscal surge and continuing capital inflows; and in order to drive core inflation down to single-digit as well as to sustain headline inflation along its present path, the Committee decided to take the following proactive actions.
"The MPC noted that the staff estimates indicate that inflation could remain within single-digit through the third quarter of the year if necessary actions are taken. In this regard, the Committee emphasized that the release of grains from the strategic grains reserve together with restrictive monetary policy would be important elements in keeping inflation under control in the months ahead. In the light of the uncertainties mentioned above, the Committee decided to: Raise the MPR by 50 basis points from 9.5 per cent to 10.0 per cent.
"The Committee noted that the Federal Government budget as approved, and the distribution of part of excess crude oil proceeds, would lead to high liquidity injection in the next two quarters of the year. It also noted that the actual and potential impact on liquidity of recent fiscal disbursements from the excess crude oil account and other disbursements scheduled for June 2008, would be considerable.
"The MPC observed that monetary policy has to be forward looking. The policy, therefore, has to ensure that concerns about price stability and real activity levels are recognised and actions taken to address them. In view of the sharp growth of credit to the private sector by 96 per cent and of M2 by 62 per cent on a year-on-year basis by March 2008, and as fiscal expansion is all time high, threats of resurgence of inflation are very high. In addition, there is the threat of imported inflation owing to global rise in prices.
In the light of the foregoing, the Committee decided to: Continue and even strengthen the use of instruments such as open market operations (OMO) and special sale of foreign exchange; Signal the tightening of the stance of monetary policy by: Raising the MPR by 25 basis points from 10.0 per cent to 10.25 per cent, and increasing the CRR by 100 basis points from 3.0 per cent to 4.0 per cent with effect from June 9, 2008."
Thus, it can be seen that the main motive for increasing the MPR has been anticipated upward pressure on the inflation rate. This, according to the CBN, is occasioned by three factors namely: rise in food prices, unprecedented increase in credit to the private sector and lastly, huge fiscal injection into the economy. Of these three factors, the most severe is the huge fiscal injection into the economy via the monthly statutory allocation and monetisation of the excess crude oil revenue.
The CBN Governor, Professor Charles Soludo himself alluded to this fact during his dialogue with the organised private sector two weeks ago. While explaining the rationale for the latest increase in the MPR, he stated, "If you decide that you don't want inflation to go beyond ten per cent and you also want economy to grow at ten per cent, it goes by simple arithmetic that the aggregate money supply should not grow by more than approximately 20 per cent. But if then on the government side, government is already pumping in much into the system that it accounts for increase in aggregate money supply for about 15 per cent.
The implication is that the private sector must be constrained not to grow more than the remaining five per cent. I am just saying it in a very simplistic manner it is a little bit more complicated than that. But it is just to drive home the point that when government spending, and I said this when I went to talk to the one of the committees of one of the arms of the National Assembly, I said, if you are concerned about interest rate, you should have started during the budgeting session. You should have started by controlling how much you are pumping into the system.
"Because the more (money) government pumps in, the less (money) private sector has to spend for you to maintain a certain level of inflation. If you have both sectors, this one is pumping in unlimited amount, that one is pumping in unlimited amount, the only thing that can adjust is inflation. Because there is only so much that the system can absorb, there is only so much cassava that is available, there is only so much rice that is available, then you are pumping money here, you are pumping money there. If government spends so much credit, the private sector will naturally have to slow.
"And what is the mechanism through which that happens? It is through the price which is interest rate. So when you are looking at government spending, when you are looking at the budget going up, it then immediately gives you a sense as to how interest rate will go. If government spending is expansionary, then the Central Bank as the monetary authority, will have to pursue a tight monetary policy, which will then be, we, trying to mop up liquidity that is being put into the system. Mopping up liquidity means interest rate will have to go up."
The import of the above is aptly revealed in the fact that as at June 1999, fiscal injection into the economy through monthly statutory allocation was N30 billion. Presently, total fiscal injection through statutory allocation and excess crude reserve fund is more than N400 billion. Hence, to ensure this does not lead to increased inflation, the apex bank had to implement tight monetary policy as it has been doing in recent times i.e increase the MPR, increase cash reserve ratio and roll out treasury bills to mop up liquidity. Yet, the ultimate effect of these is increase in interest rate.
Interestingly, all the previous increases in MPR were not accompanied by increase in lending rate. The last increase, however, seems to be the last straw that broke the camel's back, as banks responded by slightly adjusting their lending rates upward. Perhaps in anticipation of the upward review of the MPR, banks started increasing their lending rate in the last week of May. As noted by the Money Market Association of Nigeria in its May Monthly report, "Lending rates went up marginally in the month of May in the 24 Deposit Money Banks (DMBs), as average normal lending rate was 17.5539 per cent, marking 0.14 per cent increase against 17.5283 per cent recorded in April. While Prime lending rate also rose up to 17.4792 per cent, representing 0.08 per cent increase against 17.4643 per cent obtainable in April.
Further, in its outlook for June, the report warned, "Deposit and Lending rates will trend upward in the month of June, as Banks and Discount will pay more to mobilise funds." The implication of the above is that, if the major threat to inflation and which is why the apex bank is increasing the MPR, resulting to increase in interest rate, is huge fiscal injection into the economy, the economy might be gradually sliding to the era of high interest rate. That was the ugly era when lending rates hovered between 25 and 35 per cent.
This is because from all indications, the likelihood of a drastic reduction in the monthly fiscal injection is very low. In fact, with some advocating for the scrapping of the excess crude reserve account and the sharing of all the excess crude oil revenue, fiscal injection on a monthly basis might shoot up to unimaginable volume. With little or no possibility of drastic reduction in fiscal injections, the only way to avoid a gradual but steady rise in interest rate is for the CBN to devise another means of managing the liquidity injections that will not affect interest rate.
Unfortunately, the CBN governor did not give any indication of such alternative. He only explained to the concerned private sector operators why the apex bank had to increase MPR and by extension, interest rate. In his usual scholarly manner, he made the operators to realise that in as much as there is the threat of high inflation, the apex bank, in order to reduce money in the system especially to the private sector, would have to raise the MPR and by extension, interest rate. This, of course in the light of increased oil earnings and willingness of the fiscal authorities to spend more and hence pump more money into the system, means the private sector should expect further increase in interest rate.

Original_Search_Title: Govt Pays Sh1.7b for Controversial Land
Headline: Uganda: Govt Pays Sh1.7b for Controversial Land
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE Government has so far paid sh1.7b for a piece of land on Mutungo Hill, Kampala, in a deal, which is being disputed by Mengo, the public accounts committee has learnt.
The total value for the land is sh2.96b. The Government bought the land from Muhammed Buwule and Noel Bisamaza. Buwule was to be paid sh2.4b and Bisamaza sh330m.
However, Mengo royals led by Kabaka Ronald Mutebi, have claimed ownership of the land and taken the matter to court.
A report by the Auditor General (AG) into the transaction revealed that there was a difference of sh500m, which he said should be explained. Earlier reports had indicated that in 2006, the lands ministry paid sh1.2b for the two plots.
The AG's report revealed that Bisamaza was eventually paid sh560m instead of sh330. Lands' permanent secretary, Musoke Gabindadde, said he was not involved in the negotiations of the new prices.
"It is the chairman of the Uganda Land Commission who is responsible for allocating land, who was involved in the negotiations," he said. Gabindadde was appearing before the committee to answer queries raised in the AG's report for the financial year 2006.
According to a letter written to the secretary to treasury, Chris Kassami, by former lands permanent secretary, Eng. Kabanda, the land was bought for ISO for security reasons.
The committee chairman, Nandala Mafabi, said Bisamaza's land had no major developments on it apart from maize, cassava and potato gardens.
Mafabi said the committee would summon the Uganda Lands Commission secretary to explain the matter.

Original_Search_Title: Bendel Brewery Owes Banks, Staff N1.7 Billion
Headline: Nigeria: Bendel Brewery Owes Banks, Staff N1.7 Billion
Body_Text:
EDO state Government says indebtedness of Bendel Brewery to its suppliers, distributors, banks, and salaries of workers currently stands at N1.7 billon.
The state commissioner for commerce and industry Dr Abieyuwa Osemwenkhae told reporters that of the amount the brewery was owing a bank the sum of N700 million.
Dr Osemwenkhae however said that part of the loans was taken illegally in connivance with bank officials just as she added that the shares of the Edo state Government and workers which stood at 24 percent and 19 percent respectively were yet to be sold.
The commissioner said that the state government has approved the sum of 162 million as credit facilities for small scale industrialist and corporate societies under its micro credit scheme.
According to her state government has concluded plans to channel its micro credit loans to beneficiaries of the scheme through Micro Finance Bank to solve the problem of non- payment of loans by some beneficiaries.
Disclosing that the 603 primary and secondary co-operative societies have been registered in the state, Dr Osemwenkhae said the Co- operative College which was earlier closed down will be reviewed as the sum of N11.5 million have been approved for it.
On the cassava processing company in Uromi Edo central senatorial zone, she disclosed that the sum of N99 million has been into the company to enable it to be completed in the third quarter of 2008.
She said the contractors handling the various jobs have been mobilized while works has reached an advanced stage with a new transformer has been installed in the company to link with the national grid.

Original_Search_Title: Govt Spending Responsible for Increase in Interest Rate - CBN
Headline: Nigeria: Govt Spending Responsible for Increase in Interest Rate - CBN
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN) has attributed the recent increase in interest rate to increased spending by the three tiers of government.
It would be recalled that two weeks ago, the apex bank increased its bench mark interest rate, the Monetary Policy Rate (MPR), by 25 basis points to 10.25 percent from 10 percent. This would be the third time the apex bank would be increasing the rate since it was introduced last year at 8.0 percent. The increase prompted upward adjustment of interbank interest rate while lending rate also increased slightly. This generated wide-spread concern in the economy, especially in the real sector.
Speaking at a parley with the organised private sector in Lagos, yesterday, Governor, Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN), Professor Chukwuma Soludo, said that the interest rate increase was prompted by the need to curb inflationary pressure occasioned by increased fiscal spending by the three tiers of government.
"Explaining the linkage among government spending, inflation and interest rate, he stated, "The true message that I want to leave with you is that the key objective is inflation, having stable prices. You cannot manufacture in a regime of high and variable inflation. You cannot plan and invest in such a regime. If inflation runs amok, investment would fall. If that is the objective, what is the instrument to accomplish that objective?"
"If you want to bring inflation down, you have to consider the two major economic agents that spend - government and the private sector. And there is a level of existing goods in the system and, therefore, only a consistent level of spending would go with a given level of inflation".
"If you decide that you don't want inflation to go beyond 10 percent and you also want the economy to grow at 10 percent, it goes by simple arithmetic that the aggregate money supplied should not grow by more than approximately 20 percent. But, if on the government side, it is already pumping in much into the system, that accounts for the increase in aggregate money supply for about 15 percent. The implication is that the private sector must be constrained not to grow more than the remaining five percent.
"I am just saying it in a very simplistic manner it is a little bit more complicated than that. But it is just to drive home the point that when government spending, and I said this when I when to talk to the one of the committees of one of the arms of the National Assembly, I said, if you are concerned about interest rate you should have started during the budgeting session. You should have started by controlling how much you are pumping into the system.
"Because the more (money) government pumps in the less (money) private sector has to spend for you to maintain a certain level of inflation. If you have both sectors, this one is pumping in unlimited amount, that one is pumping unlimited amount, the only thing that can adjust is inflation.
"Because there is only so much that the system can absorb, there is only so much cassava that is available, there is only so much rice that is available, then you are pumping money here, you are pumping money there. If government spends so much credit to the private sector will naturally have to slow.
"And what is the mechanism through which that happens. It is through the price which is interest rate. So when you are looking at government spending, when you are looking at the budget going up, it then immediately gives you a sense as to how interest rate will go.
"If government spending is expansionary, then the Central Bank as the monetary authority, will have to pursue a tight monetary policy. Which will then be, we try to mop up liquidity that is been put into the system. Mopping up liquidity means interest rate will have to go up."

Original_Search_Title: Food Prices Still Stable
Headline: Uganda: Food Prices Still Stable
Body_Text:
Kampala — FOOD prices in Kampala's major markets have remained constant for the past two weeks, weekly mini-surveys have shown.
Traders said the stable prices are due to "increased supply of produce from the villages, especially maize, cassava and matooke.
"The rains have temporarily stopped, so the roads are somehow okay, allowing transportation of more produce to urban centres," a trader said.
"We expect more to come later this month and probably, prices will come down, assuming other factors are constant," the trader added.
Maize was sold at sh1,000 a kilogramme, while maize flour went for sh1,400, the same as last week.
Beans were sold at sh1,700, while rice was sold at sh2,200, the same as last week's price.
Groundnuts and simsim were sold at sh2,800, while millet flour increased to sh2,200 a kilogramme.
Cassava flour costs sh800, while a bunch of matooke fetches between sh7,000 and sh10,000. A heap of fresh cassava and sweet potatoes is selling at sh1,500.
The price of a sachet of salt increased to sh600, while a bar of soap remained at sh1,600. Meat costs sh5,000 a kilogramme, while chicken goes for sh10,000, up from sh7,000.

Original_Search_Title: UN Says 1,400 Villagers Displaced By Bandits
Headline: Central African Republic: UN Says 1,400 Villagers Displaced By Bandits
Body_Text:
About 1,400 displaced people are living in the village of Kamba Kota in the north of the Central African Republic in terrible health and security conditions after fleeing attacks by armed bandits on their villages, according to a report today from the United Nations Development Programme (UNDP).
Since the beginning of the year there has been a surge in attacks by bandits across the northern CAR. Groups of between 10 and 30 armed men - known locally as "coupeurs de route" or "zaraguinas" - roam the region, killing or assaulting villagers and travellers, kidnapping both children and adults, looting property and burning homes. In this case, the bandits reportedly killed 37 people in villages to the north of Kamba Kota in Ouham province.
The joint mission of the UN refugee agency (UNHCR) and the UN Office in the Central African Republic (BONUCA), who located the displaced people, have expressed concern about their health and security.
Currently they depend on river water and cassava leaves for food, while living in huts made of branches and foliage. Access to health care is problematic since the local health centre charges fees.
However, with help from aid agencies they have been able to start building a school which will soon be operational. The UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs (OCHA) estimates that 1 million people in the northern CAR have been affected by either civil conflict or the banditry, with nearly 200,000 internally displaced and another 108,000 fleeing as refugees to neighbouring Cameroon, Chad and Sudan.

Original_Search_Title: Aponye Excels in Local Dishes
Headline: Uganda: Aponye Excels in Local Dishes
Body_Text:
Kampala — It's preached world over that everybody has got to, at least once in a while, eat un-fried because it's believed to be safer than fried one.
But are there many big hotels in Uganda where such food is served? Just a handful of them serve "boil" food and Aponye Hotel on William Street is one of them.
Dining at this four-star hotel has always been great given the fact that they serve fried and un-fried both exotic and local food. With a big restaurant split into two parts, the hotel caters for all tastes and occasions and now offers a large range of local and exotic dishes in elegant surroundings complimented by live music.
The local menu includes the popular dishes from posho to rice, matooke, cassava, millet, sweet potatoes, and eshabwe - all prepared in a local manner.
The hotel's stew is also prepared in a local way and it is 100% boil. Several folks, ranging from ministers to businessmen, flock the hotel at lunch hours to have a taste of the local food.
They also serve a buffet on every weekday at Shs5,000 plus breakfast and dinner in both ala carte and ready-to-eat arrangement.
However, according to the hotel's cooking staff, it's customers between the age of 25 and 50 years who appreciate this kind of food.
The young ones prefer some spiced stuff and the good news is that Aponye Hotel serves it as well and on a daily basis.
Besides the high quality wholesome traditional cuisine, Aponye's recipes also blend the simplicity and freshness of market fresh Mediterranean ingredients with superb presentation.
The hotel boasts of a well-stocked bar with a panoramic view of the Kampala central business district and soft music usually emanates from the speakers strategically placed in the sealing.
The bar offers a selection of one of the best wineries in the local region.
The 52-bedroom structure is as vibrant and sophisticated as most big hotels in upper Nakasero hence offering upscale accommodations, distinctive amenities, and attentive service - all in a pulsing urban setting.
The deluxe property also has shopping area with exclusive boutiques and jewellery.
All guest rooms feature luxury en-suite bathrooms, hairdryer, towelling robes, direct-dial telephone, colour digital television sets with broadband internet connection, radio, mini-bar, air conditioning and large balcony.
They also offer conference facilities for up to 600 delegates in their Ballroom, which has been refurbished.
With four-star accommodation in downtown, the hotel really comes into its own for high level incentives; and then the easy access to the city central business district and wide range of facilities ensure the uniqueness of any incentive event.

Original_Search_Title: Land Bill Debates Vital - House Speaker
Headline: Uganda: Land Bill Debates Vital - House Speaker
Body_Text:
Kampala — DISCUSSIONS about the Land Amendment Bill are for the benefit of all Ugandans, the Speaker of Parliament, Edward Sekandi, has said.
It was not wise for people not to contribute to the Bill, Sekandi added.
"If you leave the Bill to be passed without your contributions, you will be oppressed. But if you attend the meetings meant to discuss the Bill, you will have the opportunity to adjust it according to your interests," Sekandi told residents of Kisenyi LCI in Kabonera sub-county, Masaka district on Wednesday.
"If you completely decline to present your views about this Bill, then you give a chance to those who presented it to pass it in their interest."
He emphasised that the Bill was aimed at protecting both the landlords and tenants but not aiding the Government to grab their land.
However, Mengo insists that the Bill was designed to grab Buganda kingdom's land, although the Government maintains that it intends to halt rampant evictions in the country.
"The country is not manned on good will. There must be laws which must be followed for the people to live peacefully. Those were old days when residents relied on good will to solve their problems."
Sekandi was responding to claims that in the past, the bibanja holders (tenants) and their landlords stayed in harmony without laws.
He distributed 450 sacks of cassava cuttings to the residents.

Original_Search_Title: Dakar - Africa's Expressive City
Headline: Senegal: Dakar - Africa's Expressive City
Body_Text:
Kampala — IN Kampala, the boys are seen squatting, polishing ladies' nails. In Dakar, there are no nails to polish but plenty of shoes to shine. This girl is sitting with two elderly women, waiting for customers (or patrons as men who solicit for sex are called in Dakar). She is having her shoes polished. In comes a man in a checkered jacket.
He is in urgent need of her services so she borrows her colleague's slippers and dashes upstairs with the man.
The shoeshine goes on with his business like nothing has happened.
This is Dakar for you, day or night. I enjoyed the way the girls watched me and tried to capture my darting eyes. There was this particular girl outside my hotel that I had been studying for a while.
She had a robust physique vibrating with youthful energy and was aware of it.
She seemed to be the only one who cared less about my presence. Perhaps she knew that I was the only one who knew who she really was.
She cared less for the little men she took upstairs. All she identified with was their money.
To her, it was a game of poker in which she had all the aces; a race in which she was sprinting far ahead of all the other fat, tired and sloppy athletes in flowing African garb. The latter, I must say, took note of all detail.
They scanned their surroundings hoping that any roach would suffice. They are the ones who kept winking and mumbling sweet nothings at me perched above them.
I was there and yet unattainable for despite the willingness of my body, my spirit and soul were thousands of miles away with my little girl and true conqueror.
In the past, I would have put to good use those lonely nights in a foreign hotel. Not that I was in the habit of engaging sex workers; I dread the dirt and disease they often carry.
I had seen this poor girl who, in broad daylight, wore her yellow (yes, they were yellow!) knickers half-way her long skinny legs due to some unthinkable affliction in the place they ought to have been. Poor thing!
Abdoulaye Wade (president of Senegal) is building where his predecessors stopped. He has widened and modernised the highways.
In fact Dakar could pass for another city in France. The traffic is well directed as most of the roads are one-way. It took me time to realise that it was more convenient to cross at the zebra-crossing, and that one has to look left, look right and look left again before crossing the road since they drove on the right side of the road!
Whereever I go, my heart feels out for the pulse of the people. Dakar has a vibrant upper class gauging from the sophisticated beauties behind humongous wheels, the likes of Hammers and Chevys.
I was reliably informed they have their own Bel Air, complete with huge mansions and Olympic-size swimming pools.
But my stethoscope was on the ordinary folk on the street. There were too many vagrants and beggars.
Most of them able-bodied, I observed. At the market place where I had my regular meals, it was a common sight to see people eating half-way through their meals only to leave a portion for the little boys with begging tins and street men.
It was a touching sight of communalism. I am hesitant to point out, without offending some, that the Islamic teaching of sharing has only exacerbated the problem of dependence in Dakar.
The problem is made worse by the shortage of food. For the entire 10 days I spent in Dakar, my diet consisted of rice, bread, fish and beef. I wonder what happened to cassava, potatoes and yams in this region. Fruit, though in plenty, were unaffordable as most of it was imported.
For the fast-food lovers, forget the Rollex. There was a mega sandwich; pieces of meat with lots of onions, an egg, mustard and mayonnaise, all wedged in 30 centimetres of French bread.
This stuff could knock out even the hungriest night reveller. Dakar is an expensive city. Hotel rooms range from sh12,000 to sh50,000 CFA (1 CFA is the equivalent of about 4 shillings. Hotel Du Marche, where I was holed up, was down at the bottom of the comfort chain.
No wonder half of it was used as a brothel. It was from here that I departed to mingle with the high and mighty of the art world attending the Dak' Art 2008 Biennale.
I met and engaged important art curators, critiques, acclaimed artists and art financiers from all corners of the art world. At the major exhibitions and discussion forums, French was the lingua. So possessing a limited vocabulary, I resorted to what an artist does best; observing phenomena.

Original_Search_Title: Moses Blah Returns From the Hague
Headline: Liberia: Moses Blah Returns From the Hague
Body_Text:
Monrovia — Amidst death threats, former Liberian President Moses Blah is back home following speculations that he would not return to the country due to fear of reprisal from supporters of ex- President Charles Taylor.
Mr. Blah returned Monday from The Hague, Netherlands, where he testified in the war crimes trial involving ex-Liberian President Charles Taylor. His dramatic testimonies at The Hague prompted angry reactions from a number of associates of Mr. Taylor, including former Maritime Commission Benion Urey who has threatened lawsuit against Mr. Blah.
There were also media reports that the family of Mr. Blah had been threatened as a result of his explosive testimonies which appeared to have linked Mr. Taylor to crimes committed in neighboring Sierra Leone.
Former President Blah told this paper via telephone Wednesday that he was suffering from cold, but his health remains generally stable. Mr. Blah said he needed time to rest due to long flight.
Asked about security around his domicile, the former Liberian leader said President Ellen Johnson-Sirleaf had ordered SSS Director to increase the presence of state security personnel around him and at his residence. According to him, the UN Mission in Liberian (UNMIL) has also reinforced security at his residence and around him and family as well.
Mr. Blah testified at The Hague about a week ago after he was subpoenaed by the war crimes court. He was considered an inner circle of Mr. Taylor whose testimonies were said to have been vital to the prosecution.
Before traveling to The Hague, Mr. Blah said he would not crucify his former boss, but tell the truth.
In one of his testimonies, he told the court that the RUF often cut off civilians' arms and legs with machetes. He said Mr. Taylor rejected complaints from RUF rebels about atrocities committed by his men.
Mr. Blah told the court that RUF rebel leader Foday Sankoh personally complained to Mr. Taylor about the rape, looting and killings committed by Liberians helping the RUF.
"He [Mr. Taylor] said this kind of thing must happen when you're fighting war," Blah said.
Former President Blah also explained how RUF commander "Musquito" was killed in Liberia by Taylor's men led by Gen. Benjamin Yeaten.
Mr. Blah earlier said the commander of one of Mr Taylor's units in Liberia "had a habit of eating fellow human beings".
He said he saw Nelson Gaye roast a human hand on a fire and ate it with boiled cassava.
Mr. Blah mentioned that Mr. Gaye's Marine unit was one of Taylor's forces, which he was told should not be pursued if they committed atrocities.
In court, former President Blah provided a detailed insider's account of the early days of the civil war.
He said he was one of the first 20 or so rebel fighters who received training at a military base in Burkina Faso and later Libya.
Blah told the court that he was not making up stories against his former boss, and that even if an electric machine is used to look in his head, he would still recount what he had said.
Taylor faces 11counts of war crimes and crimes against humanity which he denied. Mr. Blah, who briefly succeeded Mr. Taylor as president in 2003 - after rebels forced him into exile - is the most senior figure to have testified in The Hague.

Original_Search_Title: Museveni Blames Courts for City Crime
Headline: Uganda: Museveni Blames Courts for City Crime
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE weak judicial system is to blame for the wave of crimes in Kampala and other parts of the country, President Yoweri Museveni said yesterday.
"The problem is the judiciary because some of these criminals had been arrested before but were released by the courts," Museveni told the press at the State Lodge in Arua.
He revealed that although he had not fully internalised where the problem originated from, preliminary information indicated that many of the apprehended suspects were ex-convicts of similar crimes.
He called for the strengthening of the law to plug the loopholes which criminals take advantage of to secure an early release.
Sunday Vision reported that some of those arrested in connection with the recent spate of murders and robberies were criminals who had been convicted six years ago.
Asked how murderers could be free after six years, Kampala Extra Police chief Edward Ochom said they pleaded guilty to burglary, which is easier to prove but carries lesser sentences than murder or robbery.
Museveni suggested that part of the solution would be to convict armed criminals in the General Court Martial.
"We will take them to the Court Martial where we won't kill them but confine them," Museveni said.
According to the 2005 UPDF Act, anybody found in unlawful possession of arms or equipment ordinarily being the monopoly of the defence forces shall be subject to military law.
Asked about the fate of the Juba peace talks, Museveni said the country would remain safe with or without the agreement.
He said Kony's movements were being closely monitoried.
"Kony can't come and disturb us. If he tries he will be dealt with accordingly because the army's capacity now is overwhelming.
"It doesn't matter who will support him. We will crush them together."
Museveni said right from the start, he did not believe in Kony's commitment to talking peace.
Civil leaders and other people who had been visiting Kony in Garamba were only trying to save his neck, he said.
"It seems they have failed. So he will either come out to sign the agreement or he will be brought back horizontally."
Museveni had resumed his tour in the West Nile region to drum up support for his anti-poverty programme.
Earlier, on Monday, at a rally at Uleppi trading centre in Arua, he called on all Ugandans not to mix politics with the Prosperity-for-All programme.
He appealed to Movement supporters to accomodate opposition members when implementing the programme.
"We, Movement people, should defeat the opposition by doing good works. Welcome everyone to SACCOs. Receive them so that they also benefit from the programme."
He pointed out that poverty was the only remaining challenge the Movement government was committed to eliminating.
"By getting rid of poverty, we shall get rid of the opposition because it revolves around poverty."
Museveni advised the people of the area to engage in money generating activities like growing fruits, upland rice, cassava and coffee.
Museveni warmly welcomed all former members of the opposition in West Nile sub-region who have crossed over to NRM.
At a rally at Onduparaka Primary School in Ayivu, Arua district, on Monday, over 30 people from crossed from the FDC to the NRM and the President welcomed them.

Original_Search_Title: Blah Gives More Details
Headline: Liberia: Blah Gives More Details
Body_Text:
Liberia's ex-President Charles Taylor rejected complaints from Sierra Leone rebels about atrocities committed by his fighters, his ex-deputy has said.
The RUF rebels were notorious for mutilating civilians but their leader Foday Sankoh complained to Mr Taylor about Liberian troops, said Moses Blah.
Mr Blah told Mr Taylor's war crimes trial that his former boss responded by threatening to withdraw his troops.
Mr Taylor denies 11 counts of crimes against humanity and war crimes.
Mr Blah, who briefly succeeded Mr Taylor as president, is the most senior figure to testify in The Hague.
He is accused of helping Sierra Leone's Revolutionary United Front (RUF) rebels in their brutal conflict with the government.
Mr Blah told the court that RUF rebel leader Foday Sankoh personally complained to Mr Taylor about the rape, looting and killings committed by Liberians helping the RUF.
"He [Mr Taylor] said this kind of thing must happen when you're fighting war," he said.
The RUF has a reputation for brutality, such as chopping off the arms and legs of civilians, as well as killings and rape.
Mr Blah also said that Mr Taylor mediated in arguments between the rebels. He said on one occasion a rebel commander had disagreed with the Liberian president's judgement but said he had to accept "the chief's decision".
Mr Blah further said that RUF fighters tried to help Mr Taylor's forces against Liberia's Lurd rebels, who eventually forced him from power in 2003.
Mr Taylor sat taking notes during his former deputy's testimony. Mr Blah earlier said that the commander of one of Mr Taylor's units in Liberia "had a habit of eating fellow human beings".
He said he saw Nelson Gaye roast a human hand on a fire and eat it with boiled cassava.
Mr Blah said that Mr Gaye's Marine unit was one of Mr Taylor's forces, which he had been told should not be pursued if they committed atrocities.
But Mr Taylor is only on trial for his alleged role in the Sierra Leone conflict, not that in Liberia.
As Mr Blah took the witness stand on Monday, he was careful not to look at the man he served for nearly 20 years.
He gave the court a detailed account of Mr Taylor's bloody rise to power and acknowledged the use of child soldiers.
Mr Blah became vice-president of Liberia in 2000. He became president after Mr Taylor was forced into exile in 2003.
However, he was only Liberian president for two months, until October 2003, when a United Nations-backed transitional government was sworn in.
He came to the Hague reluctantly, but said he intended to tell the truth. "I'm not going to crucify him - he's my former boss," Mr Blah told the BBC's Focus on Africa programme before travelling to the Hague.
"I have nothing personal against President Taylor - we worked together almost like brothers; we had a revolution going together, so I don't think I'm going to betray him."
'Aggressive' In court, Mr Blah provided a detailed insider's account of the early days of the civil war.
He said he was one of the first 20 or so rebel fighters who received training at a military base in Burkina Faso.
It was not until a larger group was assembled at a military camp in Libya that Mr Blah met Mr Taylor.
They trained alongside rebels from The Gambia, Sierra Leone and the Philippines, all of them supported by the Libyan authorities.
He told the court that when the time came to invade Liberia, three truck loads of weapons were provided by the defence minister of Ivory Coast.
Within days of crossing into Liberia in 1990, Mr Blah says he was briefly detained by child rebel fighters recruited by his own side.
He said they were used because they took orders and were "unreasonable and aggressive".
A lawyer representing Mr Taylor, Terry Munyard, told the BBC his client was not worried about Mr Blah's evidence and if he told the truth there was nothing to fear.
Mr Taylor took up arms in Liberia in 1989, before being elected president in 1997 after a peace deal.
He is accused of sending guns and fighters to Sierra Leone in exchange for diamonds.
During Sierra Leone's decade-long civil war, which officially ended in 2002, tens of thousands of people died and thousands more were mutilated or raped.
Mr Taylor's case has been transferred from Sierra Leone to The Hague for security reasons, although it is still being conducted by the UN-backed Special Court for Sierra Leone.

Original_Search_Title: FG Moves to Convert Coal to Fuel
Headline: Nigeria: FG Moves to Convert Coal to Fuel
Body_Text:
Enugu — THE Federal Government has directed the Project Development Institute (PRODA), Enugu to explore ways of converting coal to fuel as part of measures to reduce over-reliance on petroleum products for energy.
Minister of State for Science and Technology, Dr Alhassan Bako Zaku, who gave the directive when he visited the institute as part of his one day facility tour of Federal Parastatals in Enugu State, said the government was uncomfortable with the underutilization of the large coal deposit in the country.
He asked the institute to do everything within its reach to carry out the directive, saying Nigeria's over reliance on crude oil for the production of fuel in spite of the availability of other natural resources that could generate the product, was unacceptable.
"I have directed the Director General of PRODA to work towards the conversion of coal to fuel. It is something that we can do. We cannot continue to over rely on crude oil for fuel. Since most activities require fuel, there is need to look for other sources to generate the product. Since we are blessed with coal deposits, we can work towards its conversion to fuel," Zaku said.
The Minister pointed out that with the current erratic power supply in the country there was need for the invention of locally made machines that would not rely on electricity to function.
According to him, there was no need to manufacture for instance, Cassava processing machine that can only function with electricity. "We should be aware that some of these processing machines are mostly needed in the villages. The villages may not have electricity. How then can we you justify the invention of processing machines that rely on electricity to function?" he stated.
The minister expressed surprise over the high degree of decay at PRODA and ordered the DG to forward a request on how to improve the situation of things at the institute.

Original_Search_Title: Tanga to Host Sido Exhibition
Headline: Tanzania: Tanga to Host Sido Exhibition
Body_Text:
Tanga — About 210 entrepreneurs from the northern zone regions are to take part in the 8th trade exhibition organised by the Small Industries Development Organisation (SIDO) which begins here today.
Tanga regional SIDO manager Suleiman Mutani yesterday told reporters that the four-day exhibition will be officially opened by the minister for Industries and Marketing, Dr Mary Nagu and closed by the Tanga regional commissioner Mohamed Abdulaziz.
Mr Mutani named other exhibitors who have shown interest to participate in the show as Iringa, Mbeya and Kigoma regions, and Pemba. Others will be from Kenya and Uganda.
He said the exhibition is aimed at promoting products manufactured by small and medium scale entrepreneurs and to learn modern marketing strategies.
The SIDO regional manager said over 50 entrepreneurs have been drawn from Kilimanjaro, Manyara and Arusha regions while 160 are from Tanga Region.
He said various products will be exhibited during the four-day show, listing them food items, handicraft, leather goods and hair styling.
He said Tanga district will display cassava flour and fish processing, while Mkinga district will have cashnuts products, Pangani to showcase coconut by-products and Muheza will show fruit processing.
He said Korogwe district will have on display sunflower oil and vegetables. Hamdeni and Kilindi districts will establish exhibit honey and hides.

Original_Search_Title: Corruption Versus Corruption
Headline: Nigeria: Corruption Versus Corruption
Body_Text:
Abuja — One of the features of Obasanjo's second term as elected president was the setting up of what was called Economic Management Team (EMT), which consisted of those who were called "Technorats". The Economic Management Team usurped the powers of the constitutionally recognised National Economic Council headed by the vice present of the federation. Today, each of these members of the EMT is battling to save his or her neck based on the ongoing exposure of the tenure of the Obasanjo administration.
The team was headed by that graceful African woman, Dr. Ngozi Okonjo- Iweala, now in the World Bank as managing director. She had to take excuse from her current employers to come and face the House of Representatives Committee on Power probe into the monies spent in the power sector between 1999 and 2007. It turned out that as she was busy negotiating Nigeria out of the Paris Club of creditors and giving direction to the reform agenda, some people were busy stealing. She was like holding the horns for others to milk. But she was very frank when she faced the committee and based on that she still retains her integrity intact.
Another member of the team who is also now in the World Bank is that controversial woman, Mrs. Oby Ezekwesili. She headed what was called Due Process Office as presidential aide before joining the cabinet first as Solid minerals minister and later education minister. Her tenure is as controversial as her person. We have not heard the last word on this due process contraption and the woman who el-Rufa'i calls his sister. She was one of the creations of the Obasanjo era who is yet to explain why she wanted to sell the federal unity schools, just because she is obsessed about privatisation.
A member of the powerful EMT is the former chairman of the Economic and Financial Crimes Commission (EFCC) who is now attending a course at the National Institute in Kuru, Jos. Mallam Nuhu Ribadu started very well but like many things Obasanjo initiated, the anti- corruption effort was derailed. Ribadu's EFCC went after only those opposed to Obasanjo and he, unlike those with security background, was talking too much. He ended up politicising himself and his position, thereby tying his destiny to the Obasanjo regime. He is yet to be associated with any sleaze so far. But the probe has just begun so it is too early to celebrate. But Ribadu can not afford to soil his good family name. I don't think so.
Another critical member of the EMT is Prof. Chukwuma Soludo, first as economic adviser and chief executive of the National Planning Commission and now Central Bank governor. He is a brilliant fellow who has a multiplicity of economic models for Nigeria. He was able to get the National Assembly to pass an amendment to the CBN Act which was signed into law by the president a few days to the hand over in May. That amendment provided that any governor or deputy governor of the CBN cannot be removed except by a two-third majority of the Senate. Unfortunately, he was appointed before the law and it cannot take retroactive power so it will only cover his successor. In any case, he is now facing a panel over the Africa Finance Corporation (AFC), one of his pet projects. He is a first class brain but very overzealous.
By after, the most famous member of the powerful EMT is Mallam Nasir el-Rufa'i. He is also a brilliant fellow with a first class mind. He has a lion-heart to take harsh decisions and implement them. He sanitised Abuja and made the city clean and safe. But his tenure is being investigated by the Senate Committee on FCT headed by Senator Sodangi. Trust el-Rufai and his people, they have successfully put Sodangi and his committee on the defensive by making public the plots of land allocated to Sodangi and a member of the committee, Senator Adeyemi in the FCT land allocations during the period under investigation.
What comes out of this is the fact that you cannot wash faeces with urine. A corrupt person cannot probe corruption. Secondly, it is very clear that this process is irreversible and that every public officer must be ready to give account of his or her stewardship. In the end, what Hon. Elumelu and his committee are doing is a continuation of what Vincent Azie, the former auditor-general of the federation started in the first term of Obasanjo. This is going to be very good for the system and the country.
It is now very clear that as Obasanjo kept telling us through a presidential initiative to grow cassava, he was busy with his people helping themselves with our money, houses and lands. The blind trusts are now having eyes after all. And if they are able to steal billions of naira will they be able to steal more years to enable them spend the money? Another funny revelation that came out was that the office of the auditor-general issued a statement that the immediate past GMD of the NNPC, Mr. Funsho Kupolokun discouraged the auditors from auditing NNPC accounts on the pretext that it is too technical which require special training to interpret. Now that NNPC will be probed in public, they will teach all of us that special skill so that the GMD and his petroleum minister for seven years will tell us what we got and how it was spent.
Nigeria is undergoing a silent revolution, and this is happening without any much noise. And in the end, God is answering the prayers of the masses. Twenty-five years after the death of Mallam Aminu Kano, a PRP member is president and he is helping to deal with the corrupt class like at no other time. What was latent is now apparent. May God continue to bless Nigeria! Meanwhile, there are interesting times ahead of us and our beloved country.

Original_Search_Title: Local Governments Must Show Leadership - Guebuza
Headline: Mozambique: Local Governments Must Show Leadership - Guebuza
Body_Text:
Nametil — Mozambican President Armando Guebuza on Saturday urged the members of local governments to stop acting simply as chiefs, and to promote good leadership practices so that their activities are reflected positively in improved living standards.
Guebuza was addressing an extraordinary meeting of the Nampula provincial government, held in Nametil, capital of Mogovolas district. The meeting marked the start of a five day working visit by Guebuza to this northern province.
He argued that good leadership is key to increasing production. The districts could not afford to wait from someone from outside to come along and build houses needed for teachers and other government staff.
He recalled that on many occasions, districts administrators had complained they don't have the staff they need in their districts because they cannot offer them houses. But was there really a shortage of raw materials to make bricks in the districts?
He found it inexplicable that an Administrator could sit back, cross his arms and complain of a shortage of staff, simply because he had never taken the initiative to have decent houses built with locally produced bricks. Guebuza was sure that such houses, of reasonable quality, would help attract the staff needed.
"Let us not hold up development", Guebuza declared. "We must make use of the leadership capacity that many of us have in order to help overcome difficulties. For example, district administrators must be chiefs, but at the same time they must be leaders so that we can achieve good results very urgently".
For the President, leadership must also be expressed positively in food production, particularly when faced with the current international food crisis. "Increased production must begin today and not tomorrow. Nampula enjoys all the conditions to command solutions to the food crisis", he declared. Merely because Nampula already grows a great deal of cassava was no reason not to produce more, and send it to other parts of the country he added.
Guebuza warned that the international food crisis might eventually affect everybody. It was the government's responsibility to reduce the impact of this crisis, he added, and the government had already developed some ideas, which would soon be put before Mozambican society and particularly before the business class.

Original_Search_Title: UNIDO Boss Tasks Country On African Economic Role
Headline: Nigeria: UNIDO Boss Tasks Country On African Economic Role
Body_Text:
Abuja — The director general of the United Nations Industrial Development Organisation (UNIDO), Dr. Kandel Yumkella, has called on Nigeria to rise up to the continental role of providing an economic pedestal for the African continent.
Yumkella, who addressed the local press yesterday in Abuja, also observed that the nation can achieve its targets of Vision 2020 if the necessary things are put in place by the Federal Government.
According to the UNIDO boss, who hinted that the continent of Africa is in dire need of "a big brother", the country has what it takes to turn-around the economy of the most populated black continent.
"We need a big brother around Africa. We need one around which the economy of the African continent can be built. Nigeria has all its takes to be that rallying economic force on the continent and even beyond," he said.
He, however, observed that the country's energy sector would be the deciding factor on how successful it could go to play this Herculean, but all-important role in the continent.
"But there is a need for Nigeria to fix its energy sector, if it truly wishes to be a major player in the world economy. That way, the industries would wake up, flourish, and also provide the much needed jobs for the greater number of the populace in the country," he observed.
On the nation's target to become one of the world's 20 most industrialised nations by 2020, Yumkella particularly called on the Federal Government to pay attention to renewable energy.
According to him, "renewable energy remains the key issue in Nigeria's vision for 2020 targets. Nigeria can achieve its targets of being amongst the 20 most industrialised countries in the world by the year 2020, and even more. But to achieve this, it needs to dedicate itself to ensuring functional infrastructure for any business to thrive," he said.
He regretted that the country has failed to benefit from its cassava initiative, despite being the highest producer of cassava, adding that it has continuously failed to add value to the product and create wealth.
"Nigerian can create wealth by industrialising its cassava," he added.

Original_Search_Title: The Budo Inferno Was a Bomb Waiting to Explode
Headline: Uganda: The Budo Inferno Was a Bomb Waiting to Explode
Body_Text:
Kampala — I must have received one of the first text messages about the Budo fire from a friend in Glasgow, Scotland, inquiring whether I had heard what had happened at my alma mater just minutes after the news broke in Wakiso, late Monday evening.
This could not be my alma mater, better known to us the Old Students as Kabinja. In testament of today's technology, a couple of people in Europe, North America and South Asia knew of this tragedy as it unfolded via sms before the Fire Brigade had even arrived at the school. But with all that said, nobody prepared us for the biggest blow yet - "Death toll: 19 (burnt beyond recognition) with an expectation of it to rise".
This is by every measure a tragedy of epic proportions, and my heart, pain and tears go out to all the families and friends that have lost their loved ones in this fire. It could have been any one of us. Details are now emerging that this is now being investigated as a homicide. What kind of human being could be so heartless?
What could be disappointing than this horrendous occurrence is that this colossal loss of young lives did not have to happen. Many of us who attended this school agreed and predicted at one point or the other in the recent decade that it was not a matter of "if", but rather of "when" such a tragedy would happen, especially since this was not the first time fires had broken out at the school.
In Budo Junior, we found pride to know that many of us common folk went to school with some of the offspring of society's well-heeled. In my times - the 1990s, Abu Mayanja's(RIP) children, Kintu Musoke's daughter, Prince Junju (Kabaka Mutebi's son) among other were some of the reasons it was not hard to believe the school was the premier primary boarding school of the time - and with that fact in mind, premier fees were charged.
No other government boarding primary school in the nation charged more fees than Budo Junior. If you did not pay up promptly, you were instantly kicked out until all debts were settled. Bottom-line, this was not your average primary school where administrative problems with teachers and students were the order of the day.
William Kayongo was the head-teacher of Budo Junior from the late 1980s until early March 2008. He was the master-mind of a concerted and well-orchestrated effort to convince parents, staff and pupils that they were indeed getting the best quality education and care with what 'little" resources they had. For many it worked, but I and couple of others never, even for once, bought it. Something did not add up then - and more so, now.
Budo Junior was also the intersection where hard work by the teachers like Mrs. Bawuuba (widow to a former King's College Budo headteacher), Khaukha,, Mrs. Kasule, Kabuye who have given their lives to this thankless education career and had taught thousands of pupils with a honest thirst of learning.
However, lately, some dangerous precedents set in like top school authorities, which correlates with the deterioration of discipline amongst students, teachers and matrons alike.
A dangerous and reckless culture was tolerated where for example, a pupil losing an eye due to a stone throwing incident was not enough to shock the sensibilities of the student body and authorities for them to carry out a full-scale investigation to determine a culprit.
A corrupt atmosphere was allowed to thrive where parents brought items on top of a customary monetary "gift" every visiting day for the matrons so that they could go easy on their children, and in tandem; administrators looked the other way when obviously dangerous and illegal practices like locking pupils in their dorms at night were complained about.
This was an environment, where physical and corporal punishment were dished out. In this environment of fear and raw intimidation, such incidents were the order of the day and, therefore,went unabated and unreported.
However, What many of us found most disturbing was the fact that as enrollment went up at a rather high and discomforting rate and the schools increased fees significantly; the resources pretty much stayed the same. No new classrooms, dorms, latrines or teachers houses were built. A new multi-storied building was promised and was touted as a mixed-functional structure that would be our saviour, but for the longest time never went up beyond the foundation digging in the late 1990s and early 2000s.
We ate our meals while standing up because there were not enough benches to go around. The diet, as expected anywhere in Uganda, was a staple of posho and beans with an occasional sweet potatoes or cassava, which is fine. However, it did not take a culinary expert to know little or no effort was made to make the meal more palatable. We provided our own extra condiments like ghee and margarine to make the food tastier.
Many a times, hot water was served at break time instead of tea. And the inference was that we provided our own sugar, and tea - never mind that the dorms were always closed. What worried me the most though, was the dangerously low morale in the kitchen staff responsible for preparing meals for more than 1000 pupils.
I was a dining hall prefect in 1996. When I raised my concerns with those in-charge, I was in turn labelled as an ungrateful elitist snob - and having earlier transferred in from Kampala Parents' School did not help my case either. In fact, a teacher nicknamed me 'Kawowo k'emmere" (food spice).
At the sanatorium popularly known as the 'sick bay', it was not an unusual sight to see kids treating fellow kids, especially dressing each other's wounds. On occasions medicine was dispensed by the 12-year-olds in absence of the live-in nurse. Parents were encouraged to bring their own medicine, and for my entirety at the school, I never saw any kind of sterilisation in progress. A syringe was often used and thereafter capped and labelled with one's name only to be used later during the next medicine-round.
The living conditions are what should have raised any amount of red flags there possibly could be. As enrollment kept rising by the term, no more extra dorms, bathrooms or latrines were built. Double-deckers were done away like yesterdays fashion favouring triple-deckers and quarto-deckers. None of this dorms had ceilings.
The wisdom in having another's child lay down, less than a foot from the roof's wood beams stills defeats my comprehension. Dorms were packed until no other bed could fit in. It did not matter whether it blocked the windows and/or doors which effectively made the windows unusable as an emergency exit, but perhaps turned the dorms into an inescapable incinerator incase of a fire once the doors were locked from outside - which is exactly what happened here. When that option was exhausted, classrooms were turned into dorms starting with Mrs. Bombo's P.1 class.
No effort was made to create more bathrooms and when it rained, it was absolute horror to use the latrines as they would overflow with live and crawling maggots from the floor to the ceiling. It still surprises me that Budo Junior School, with all its well-educated alumni all-over the world and its many affluent parents, still has asbestos roofs given all the documented evidence of health dangers of asbestos.
We were fully convinced that this was as best as they came. And you could not get any better all-round education and service for your money anywhere - and many bought into the status quo, unquestionably. Why wouldn't they? After Budo Junior, some of us were accepted to King's College Budo. With its rich history, powerful alumni and superior academic reputation aside, the headmaster, Busuulwa, left no one guessing as to how serious he was about hygiene, welfare, safety and security of all the students under his responsibility.
He made it clear to all students, especially the girls that he was fully responsible for them while they were in school - a stark contrast with the attitude at Kabinja. I noticed in my first week that Busuulwa made his presence visible. And also, that there were no triple and quarto-deckers here; the dorms had ceilings (some even had curtains). Hallways were always kept clear to minimise a fire hazard and windows were never burglar proofed incase of a fire.
My dorm, South Africa, had an emergency exit on each of its three wings. The food although still a staple of posho and beans, had evidence of effort on the part of kitchen staff to make it more palatable and the staff led by Sebwolo had a high and positive morale.
Breakfast was served with bread daily. The sanatorium had a visible sterilizer; syringes were used only once and quickly disposed off. And the nurse, Muddukaki, with all her temper outbursts and shortcomings, most students agreed was the best nurse in the business. The comparison and contrasts went on for weeks and an argument could be made that this is comparing apples and bananas. That is what I thought too, until I found out that from 1992-2000, King's College and Budo Junior paid just about the same in tuition and fees. So my question then was, where had all the Budo Junior money disappeared to? This discovery made me absolutely bitter and mad at the fact that there was obviously something fishy about the whole Budo Junior situation.
I took every platform availed to me to voice my concerns out loud thereafter. Perhaps, the best forum I got was when I was selected by the S.4 government in October 2000 to O'level candidates, who had started their journey at Budo Junior at the annual thanksgiving service with a short speech. These services were usually attended by the parents of the O'level students, many of them movers and shakers in our society. But most importantly, many of the parents were also Old Students of both Budo Junior and/or King's College.
If there was a group or an individual that could initiate change, I believed he/she or them was seated in that chapel. My speech was light-hearted, punctuated with anecdotes and punch lines that kept with the spirit of emphasising the "good Budo (Budo Junior/King's College) times" that had bonded us all together. I, however, intentionally went off-script and warned that as we now laughed about the difficult past, not to forget that it was still a harsh reality to many still at the school. I subtly hinted at the perception of massive corruption and gross negligence that would inevitably lead to a public health and/or safety disaster amongst the pupils - I did not foresee a fire with such a devastating death toll though. As soon as I sat down, I was publicly rebuked and chastised at the pulpit for daring to suggest that things were essentially not world-class at Budo Junior.
With the benefit of hindsight now, many of those I have communicated with agree we should have done more. Watching the start of the daily UBC news broadcast last night as they focused on Nalongo Betty Kigozi as she found out that one of her children was among the dead was at the least; heartbreaking and sickening, a hard kick in the gut and any parent's worst night mare i.e. to bury your own child. Only in this case there wasn't even a body to identify as they were mostly burnt beyond recognition. Nalongo Kigozi could have been any single one of us.
If this was indeed an act of homicidal arson, the culprits should be brought to justice and if found guilty they should receive the maximum sentence allowed. But equally guilty, are those who over the years bred an environment and circumstances that could allow such a massive loss of life of society's most innocent; as a result of pure, and yet unacceptable negligence.
Let us all remember, these were eight and nine-year-old girls that were selfishly used as pawns in a game of greed, corruption, and work politics; and they ultimately paid with their own young lives. This was also a result of an arrogant betrayal of public trust of the worst kind where personal enrichment was prioritised over public health, safety and security.
In China, the Budo Junior team would have signed their own death warrants the moment the first child died. If you can afford to change into your new cars every 17 weeks, but cannot have the judgment to at least buy one fire extinguisher for each dorm and also have a fire exit; and yet as a matter of policy, continue packing in pupils like "soy beans" (quoting Gen. Kayihura) in these dorms/classrooms - then you have no business heading a school.
If you are overseas for more than 25% of the term time representing the Lions' Club or the Rotary Club, but not the school that that pays your salary - then step aside, and let somebody who will be more available and have the children's interest at heart take over the reins of leadership. There was a systemic failure of leadership from the top that has now had us paying a price too high to comprehend - children's innocent lives. There should be a collective outrage at this tragedy that demands nothing short of absolute accountability from this school's leadership.
Current pupils, the school's Old Boys and girls, teachers, Parents everywhere and policy & law makers should ask questions and expect only satisfactory answers. What is sad to note is that it is common knowledge this kind of thing - complete disregard of children's public safety, is going on in boarding schools all over Uganda. And sad as it may be, this is just a tip of the iceberg. Questions like these should be answered; how could one individual receive money, sign for it, and deposit in the bank and withdraw it at-will? Were there any control systems in place or 'segregation-of -duties' as we accountants call it to prevent abuse of office, fraud and embezzlement? Somebody should ask long-time education permanent secretary, Lubanga.
How a government-funded school could have the same head-teacher for over 20 years and what good does this serve? How and who in the ministry of education enabled this to happen? Does the ministry have inspectors that enforce the health and building codes and also make sure that the standards are maintained as the law requires? What was the ministry's response in the last decade when citizens complained that such a disaster was waiting to happen?
As this dark and painful chapter gets written in our country's history, lets hope that the children's lives lost will not be in total vain- and we will learn some kind of lesson to avoid such preventable disasters. Let us not just have another investigation or commission of inquiry whose recommendations will live to gather dust in the cabinet's library. But rather let this mean something to Nalongo Kigozis of this country who have been forced into the unenviable position of losing their loved ones in disasters like these.
Whereas, some are using this to unfortunately to advance their political agenda even before the first funeral has taken place, we should keep our eyes on the ball and not drop it. President Museveni put it best: "You can see the consequences of negligence. Let everybody learn to take full responsibility of their duties".
Fellow citizens, running a government school was never meant to be a for-profit enterprise should be held liable for what goes in their schools. Make them accountable Mr. President and the message to those in such positions, will be sent out loud and clear for all to hear.
The writer is an Old Boy of Budo Junior

Original_Search_Title: RPG Launcher Found in Cassava Patch
