Headline: Liberia: RPG Launcher Found in Cassava Patch
Body_Text:
Monrovia — Residents of Careysburg, Montserrado County, have turned over one Rocket Propel Grenade (RPG) launcher and an explosive to the Center for Peace Education and Democracy (COPE).
The arm was given at the end of a three-day awareness workshop on the proliferation of small arms and light weapons in Careysburg last week.
A COPE release said the RPG was discovered in a cassava patch in the area. The arm has since been given to the United Nations Mission in Liberia (UNMIL).
Meanwhile, COPE has ended a three-day awareness and education workshop on the danger, abuse, misuse and effects of small arms in Liberia.
The workshop brought together 30 participants including commissioners, city mayors, paramount and clan chiefs, women and youth groups, among others.
The workshop which was held under the theme: "Assessing the Impact of Small Arms on Sustainable Development in Rural Liberia" was part of COPE's fifth year awareness campaign against the proliferation and danger of small arms in Liberia.
Sponsored by the United States based National Endowment for Democracy (NED), COPE Executive Director Robert G. Miller told the participants that the proliferation of small arms was a major factor affecting development in Liberia.
He observed that the uncontrolled use of small arms suffers development.
He added that the proliferation of small arms in Liberia, Sierra Leone, Nigeria and other countries in Africa created negative impact and, if nothing is done to control the misuse of small arms, development will suffer.
He also called for the speedy ratification of the ECOWAS Convention on small arms if Liberia must be a safe place for development.

Original_Search_Title: Zimbabwe in Nigeria - Its Tourism Potential
Headline: Nigeria: Zimbabwe in Nigeria - Its Tourism Potential
Body_Text:
Zimbabwe tourist village is a tourist haven that has remained unexploited over the years. It is located in Uke, a small village in Karu Local Government, Nasarawa State, thirty kilometers away from Abuja, the nation's capital.
Though, the village does not boast of any social amenities, it is a tourist destination to both Nigerians and foreigners alike. It has been a place for lovers of nature, as well as those on holiday and picnic.
When you go to Zimbabwe, you cannot help but marvel at the semblance of the place to a real country side.
The presence of women trading in various kinds of grains, and other food items, such as pepper, tomatoes, yams, and potatoes is really a sight to behold.
Sitting in tents, most of which are made of straw and raffia, with their goods displayed around the tents, they beckon on tourists, to patronise them. It is even more interesting that they do so in their local languages, which are not understood by the tourists.
The exotic cars parked around the place go to show the caliber of people who patronise the tourist village.
In the village, there are several tents where freshly tapped palm wine, which is about the major attraction to the village, is sold.
The palm wine sellers, sitting around their gourds of palm wine, call on you as you approach them, to have a taste of their wine, in order for you to make a choice.
There are also those who sell beer and other alcoholic beverages in the market. Those selling fresh fish, as well as bush meat are not left out as they are positioned at various corners, ready to serve those drinking something to go with the drinks.
In recent times, a trend of roasted fresh fish, has taken over other forms of food people eat when drinking, this is coupled with the health benefits of fish. There are a lot of drinking spots, especially in Abuja, where they have fish in ponds, and the patron's choice of fish is killed and prepared. This system is referred to as 'point and kill'.
Zimbabwe tourist village also boasts of the point and kill system, but theirs is however different, which is also an attraction to the place. In Zimbabwe, what they have is chicken point and kill.
The chickens are put in a cage, like 'brides', waiting for their suitors, who after paying their 'bride price', have them killed.
Another interesting thing here is the expertise with which those selling the chicken prepare it. It does not take them more than thirty minutes to kill and prepare a chicken, over red hot coals and I bet you, the taste of the chicken is absolutely delicious.
Zimbabwe tourist village has also presented the people business and employment opportunities, while they enjoy themselves.
There are those who sell plastic kegs in the market to enable those who want to buy palm wine and take away carry it conveniently.
The culture of a people, which is an important aspect of tourism, is usually not far from them, and they use every opportunity that presents itself to showcase their rich culture to the world. Rich African music fills the air in the arena as traditional music artistes are on hand to thrill the tourists to rich songs, which rendition is in their local languages.
The musicians go about their singing and playing of instruments in a way that only goes to show how proud they are of their origin.
Where there is music, it is only natural that there should be dancers. The women are not left out of the fun as they dance gracefully to the music.
While some people may prefer the foreign brewed drinks, there are those who prefer to take their drink fresh, which is fresh palm wine, and the brewers are on hand to meet their demand.
Manager is a shop owner at the tourist village. He started selling there six years ago. He said, "I was working in Lagos State before I was sacked. I decided to come back to Abuja and look for another job, but when I got to Abuja, I searched fruitlessly and the job never came. While I was in Abuja, I used to come to Zimbabwe with my friends during the weekend to relax, but I had already heard about the place when I was in Lagos from my friends who used to visit the place whenever they come to the north".
He continued "when I was coming to the place as a patron, I saw that the people doing business there were making a lot of money and since I did not have a job at the time, I decided I would get a space and start selling drinks myself. I have been selling here for six years now and I can say the business is good, because mine has grown tremendously and I now have two shops. My wife manages the other one, while I take charge of this one.
Manager, however, complained that there is no electricity in the village and that is the major challenge he faces as his customers usually complain that the drinks are not cold. He also said he usually has to close shop early because customers begin to leave as soon as it starts getting dark; otherwise, the village has the potential of being a twenty four hours tourist site.
Terwase , a resident of Abuja, said he likes going to drink at Zimbabwe because each time he is there, he feels as though he is at home in Benue State. Asked if he does not consider travelling thirty kilometers just to go and drink as being stressful, he said "I do not consider it stressful or a difficulty in any way, because what they offer here is what I get only when I travel to my village, and it will cost me more, if I have to travel to my village so I rather think they are bringing the good things nature has provided closer to us." He also said the Zimbabwe tourist village is one place he likes bringing his friends to, especially those who are not Nigerians, so that they can appreciate what nature has endowed Nigeria with.
For Amos, native of Bauchi State, who sells roasted chicken, he saw people doing the business and having seen that it was profitable, he decided to join them.
Amos, who has been in the business for five years, attributed his being in the business to patronage. He disclosed "I enjoy what I am doing because there is very high patronage. People come from Abuja, Suleja, Portharcourt, Lagos and even overseas to patronise us. Even though the patronage is dwindling because other people have copied our concept here and taken it to places like Nyanya, Karu, and Mararaba, we still have patrons coming everyday, because nobody can do what we do better than us. I sell at least fifty chickens everyday, and the smallest chicken here goes for N 1000 , while bigger ones are sold at N 1200 or N 1300, depending on the size ".
He further revealed that he feels fulfilled doing the business, especially because he has minimize the problem of unemployment in the country by employing people who work for him. He said "right now, I have seven apprentices under me and I thank God for giving me the opportunity to impact positively on the lives of people around me."
Another tourist to the Zimbabwe tourist village, Mr Simeon, who brought his friends that visited for the Salah celebration to the place said the village atmosphere is very conducive for relaxation, it is very natural and the local brands they sell there such as palm wine, bush meat, and 'burukutu' are things that are not found in many drinking spots, so he usually goes there once in a while to enjoy himself.
Harare is the capital of Zimbabwe, and even here, there is a spot known as 'Harare resort'.
The spot offers live band entertainment, and also has a viewing centre, where they broadcast European matches, for sports loving tourists.
Located behind the Harare spot is a small river, called 'river Uke'. The ambience of the river is serene, as well as scenic, and has the calming effect only nature can offer. Some tourists go to the river to swim, while others hold picnics by the river side.
But how did the name Zimbabwe come about? Zimbabwe tourist village is an off shoot of a market which started twenty one years ago. According to Chief Chindo Danjuma, the Mai'ungwa Birnin Gbagyi, Uke, and the head of the family, on whose land the Zimbabwe tourist village is situated, the market was opened twenty two years ago by his late uncle, Mr Waziri.
Waziri, a soldier, went on peace keeping mission to Zimbabwe, and when he returned, opened two shops on the family land by the road side where he sold house hold provisions, palm wine, fresh fish and bush meat.
At that time, there was no other shop in the village and almost everybody in the village used to buy from the retired soldier.
Chindo said "Waziri used to tell anyone who cared to listen that he is from Zimbabwe, as such, everybody started calling him Zimbabwe. Consequently, when they want to go to his shop, they would say they are going to Zimbabwe, and that is how the name stuck till date".
He stated that other people began to come to the market with various business ideas, thus the market expanded and became very popular.
He further explained that because the land belongs to the family, they have not sold any plot, but give out portions of the land to anyone who wants to do business there, on tenancy agreement. He said the traders pay N1000 to the family every month for rent and security, adding that since the inception of the market, there has never been a robbery incident. He, however, maintained that because there is no electricity at the place yet, they have given the traders closing time, which is 9:00 pm. He also disclosed that plans are under way for electricity to be connected to the village so that the market can be opened 24 hours.
Tourism is as old as mankind itself, and as long as man exists, he will continue to seek pleasure and relaxation.
Tourism can be traced back to the days of King Solomon in the Bible, who reigned around 10 BC. King Solomon, during his reign, built a temple and the Queen of Sheba, an Arabian monarch traveled to the splendid court of Solomon to see the temple built by the wise king, as well as exchange gifts with him. Suffice it to say also that the journeys of the early Christians can be said to be tourism activities.
Nigeria is richly blessed and endowed with rich tourism potentials capable of making the country an ideal destination for tourists all over the world.
While some tourist destinations in Nigeria have been exploited, some however, remain unexploited.
A myriad of tourist sites exist in Nigeria. Some of them are the Nike Lake Resort, Enugu, Bar Beach Lagos, Yankari Game Reserve Bauchi, Rayfield resort Jos, Obudu cattle ranch, Cross River , Jos Wild Life Park, Gurara Water Falls Niger State, and of course, Zimbabwe tourist village, Nasarawa state.
The tourism potential inherent in Zimbabwe cannot be over emphasised; the village can be developed to be a core tourist site.
There ought to be direct intervention by the government and private sector, in the provision of projects which are capital intensive and cannot be under taken by individuals, such as roads, water and electricity in this village as it will go a long way in providing an economic environment, that would generate employment and promote income earning.
The federal government through the ministry of culture and tourism should see the development of the Zimbabwe tourist village and other tourist sites in the country, as a means of breaking away from the mono-economic dependence of the country. The government should effectively utilise the abundant tourism potential in the country, as this would boost the revenue accruing from the non-oil sector.
The ministry should strive towards developing a diverse range of tourism products that will increase the comparative advantage of the country in the tourism sector. Developing Zimbabwe presents ample prospects in this direction.
The government of Nasarawa State should take a cue from the Cross River State government that has made the state a choice tourist destination, by harnessing the untapped opportunities in Zimbabwe tourist village. This village has a potential of being the best tourist site in northern Nigeria. The state government should see tourism as a flourishing business that will create meaningful employment, thus creating wealth and development in our rural areas. This will go a long way in making the place of the state in the global market place rise.
Tourism in Nigeria should go beyond holding elaborate carnivals once in a while, and television shows on cultural edifices and artifacts. Natural endowments should be developed, such as the 'river Uke' that can be developed into a resort, for lovers of nature.
A sector such as tourism has socio-economic worth which runs into billions, if fully developed and activities of people trying to encourage tourism should attract attention.
It is hoped that by the time efforts are made towards repackaging tourism resources and exploring new tourist sites, Nigeria will be in a better position to attract tourism from outside the shores of the country. In this yuletide season, take time out to visit a tourist site with your friends and family. Tourism is life.

Original_Search_Title: Business Activities Pick Up in Jos
Headline: Nigeria: Business Activities Pick Up in Jos
Body_Text:
Jos — Business activities are picking up in Jos, the Plateau State capital, following the violence that engulfed parts of the state capital as the long queues experienced in some commercial banks in the state disappeared shortly after the Christmas holiday.
Besides, new markets are springing up in the tin city.
LEADERSHIP observed that a new vegetable market has been opened along Laminga dam road while people now abandon the popular Yandoya market in Gangare for Gada biu yam market.
Similarly, Igbo traders also fled the Kwararafa market to Chobe junction were another market is fast developing as they were seen displaying their wares on the ground for their kinsmen to purchase.
However, the 6.00am to 7.00pm curfew imposed in Jos and Bukuru metropolis did not go down well with night club owners and beer drinkers in the state capital.
Few of them who spoke to
LEADERSHIP in Jos called on the state government to relax the curfew to 10pm to enable them have a enough beer before going to bed.
According to them, some of them leave their offices around 5 to 6pm and before they could settle down to take one or two bottles of their favourite it is curfew time.
While other beer drinkers cultivate the habit of going to drink as early as 3.00pm before 7.pm they would have taken enough thereby beating the 7.pm curfew.
Indeed, it was jubilation for house wives in the state capital as they celebrate their husband's continuous stay in the house playing with the kids because of the curfew while free girls who hang out at night could not be found because of the curfew.
Some of the house wives who spoke to
LEADERSHIP over the development urged the state government not to relax the curfew until security of lives and property is guaranteed in the state capital.

Original_Search_Title: We Didn't Steal State Funds, Says Egba
Headline: Nigeria: We Didn't Steal State Funds, Says Egba
Body_Text:
Lagos — Chairman of Bayelsa State House of Assembly's committee on finance and appropriation, Hon. Alfred Egba, in this interview with a select group of journalists, spoke on the controversies surrounding the petition against him and some other members of the House and other issues. Zacheaus Somorin brings excerpts
I s it really true that you have a special relationship with the governor and could it be true that you have become so rich because of the relationship?
I am a part of the government of Bayelsa State . I love people with a passion for discipline and probity. Maybe because of my training, I am in love with people who are straight, who have nothing to hide. Agreed, as a member of the House, I am close to the governor, just like every other person. But let us get this straight: As a lawmaker, I look at areas that we can partner for the ultimate good of our people. I see the governor as a man with listening ears, who yields to superior argument, who does not hesitate to do what is right. Having seen these qualities, I had no hesitation to join him in the efforts to transform our state and right the wrongs of the past. If that is what people call closeness to the governor, then no apologies. But what gladdens my heart is that the closeness is changing the face of my constituency and the face of Bayelsa State in all its ramifications.
When you talk of becoming so rich because of my relationship with the governor, I will tell you yes and no. Yes because I have become so rich in knowledge on how to administer a people and do so transparently. I have become so rich in knowing that truth pays far more than falsehood and that in politics, you should avoid people whose major interest is how to destroy the other man, like the biblical Judas, when money is involved, or like the biblical Jacob who could sell his position just to eat porridge yam.
I was in far away United States for a short training course when I read on the internet that I and a few other members of the House of Assembly had been dragged before the ICPC and the EFCC for the umpteenth time. As a person, I am not surprised, just that as a trained journalist, I would have expected that the newspaper in question would have attempted to reach us for our own side of the story before going to press. But that is just as well. Our lawyers will handle that appropriately. What I can say here is that we are not strangers to these agencies. The same people and their paymasters whom we all know have taken us there before. In the end, we came out clean. Since I was born, I have never seen evil subdue righteousness. It will never start with our generation. But I wish these people well.
On the other hand, let me state without mincing words, that there has never been a time that I got involved in any deal as claimed by my accusers. I challenge them to publicly show evidence on this claim if they are sure of what they are saying. As a legislator, I get all my entitlements as and when due and nothing more. Anybody who has contrary information should come out openly with them. I am a gentleman and no matter the extent of provocation, I will refuse to lose my temperament. That is all I can say for now.
You said that the 2008 budget was well implemented, but there are people who believe that the government took too much loans and has also impoverished the state?
Questions like this make me sad. I know that there are people who hardly see anything good in what the other person is doing. We have many of them and painfully they still exist in our state. Yes, the government took loans but what people should ask is, loans for what? I said earlier that the 2008 budget was fashioned in such a way as to allow for deficits. The loans were necessary and that was why the House of Assembly approved those loans. That is why you can see the kind of projects in the state today. That is why you can see the number of new roads in the state. That is why you can find the kind of uncommon development that this state has never witnessed in the last eight years. Come to think of it, if the government was not prudent, if the government was corrupt and financially undisciplined, the Transparency International would not have commended the governor for his bias for financial prudence and discipline.
For your information, a bill on fiscal discipline and another on procurement are on the way. A man who is not prudent with finances could not have done this. In fact, all our people are encouraged to be a part of this move to enthrone a regime of transparency through legal means. Instead of sitting down to criticize and make a mountain out of a mole hill, they should come and contribute during when the time for a public hearing comes, so that we can have an acceptable procurement law and a law on fiscal discipline.
You look at what has happened in other parts of the country and you will discover that the Bayelsa State Government has fared well. I will say that those casting aspersion on the government should re-channel their energy towards coming back home to contribute their ideas on how to move our state forward. In some countries and even in some states of this country, people send individuals and corporate bills to the parliament for consideration. Every patriotic Bayelsan must come and say how the state can develop. The money and time they spend in publishing sponsored advertorials aimed at maligning the person and office of the governor could be used in transforming the state. Rather than spend money on newspaper advertorials, they can use those funds to pay consultants who will develop initiatives and bills that will end up in laws that will benefit our people. They spend money to petition the EFCC and the ICPC and the rest.
The governor has listening ears. I have not heard any of them who say he tried to talk to the governor and he refused to listen. I think we need to change our mindset about governance and live with the realisation that power comes only from God.
You were the arrowhead for the probe of your senior colleague and former commissioner over contracts awarded during his tenure. He has dragged you to court. What is your reaction to this?
A point of correction please! I was not the arrowhead, it was a parliamentary decision. This sounds strange to me because I have not received any summons from anybody. In any case, when I get to that bridge, I will cross it. The lawyers will be there to look at the case. But let me say that if it is true, it will be a very sad development. Except I am being accused of what I said outside the chambers, then I will say okay. But as far as I know, I am protected if I said anything on the floor of the House.
But let me say that this is not good for our democracy. My bother, Seriake Dickson is a member of the House of Representatives and a lawyer, so he knows even better than I do. In the House where he is a member, they have been inviting people to come and explain issues. So, do we all go to court and stop the House from sitting or take those who sponsored the motion to court? My senior colleague knows this. As a lawmaker and politician, I know that whatever we do is for posterity. What we should do is to join hands with the government to develop this state. Bayelsa is the only state in this country with 8 local governments. We have people in the National Assembly and I believe that what they should do should be to partner the State Assembly so that we see how we can get the newly created local governments recognised and listed in the constitution which is about to be reviewed. They can do much more because they are men of intelligence and integrity.
I can tell you that anybody that has been following the trend of politics in Bayelsa State in the last eight years will not be surprised at what is happening. We have always been blackmailing our leaders. We are the only homogenous state in the Niger Delta and we are supposed to work together. Ironically, nobody has come out with anything to say this is what evil thing the governor has done. I believe that anybody who is criticising the governor should come out with a solution. For instance, if you say why should the governor go and borrow money, you should say no, Mr governor, you ought to have done so and so, rather than just criticising for the sake of it. This is where I have headache about what is going on in our state. But I am confident that since all of them have now seen that the governor has no ill intentions and that he means well for all our people, things will change in the coming days.

Original_Search_Title: Standing Rule of Law On Its Head - a Critique of Yar'Adua's Basic Mantra
Headline: Nigeria: Standing Rule of Law On Its Head - a Critique of Yar'Adua's Basic Mantra
Body_Text:
The English common law which was received by many of British colonial territories is replete with fictions. One of the tasks that I have set for myself in the next two years or so, God willing, is to do a book-length critique of the fictional underpinnings of the common law.
We can refer to the fiction of the rule of law. Here the impression is created that law, an intangible category, is capable of governing society suo motu, i.e., on its motion or on its own independently of other factors or agents. This is clearly confusing and misleading. It is social forces composed of human beings bonded by same ideological interests that rule or govern.
In truth, therefore, there can be no such thing as the rule of law. The promoters of the rule of law, both of its classical and modern variants, seek an artificial blurring of the essential class divisions in society by pointing to law as ruler and as a leveler. E. P. Thompson captured this point in his 1975 article, Whigs and Hunters, (see Lloyd's Introduction to Jurisprudence, pp. 1054-1060).
He argued that law 'is clearly an instrument of the de facto ruling class: it both defines and defends these rulers' claims upon resources and labour power-it says what shall be property and what shall be crime-and it mediates class relations with a set of appropriate rules and sanctions, all of which, ultimately, confirm and consolidate existing class power. Hence the rule of law is only another mask for the rule of a class'.
We should not be understood as rubbishing the concept of the rule of law or its respect by the state. Indeed, without respect for just laws which is essentially what the rule of law ought to be about anarchy would consume society and the transition to genuine democracy of the people would be imperiled or impossible.
The present administration of President Umaru Yar' Adua has made adherence to the rule of law its main article of faith. Indeed respect for the rule of law can be said to be its basic organizing principle. It is therefore necessary to examine how this has played out in practice. This review must be predicated on a brief exposition on the concept of the rule of law.
A.V. Dicey popularized the concept of the rule of law in his celebrated work, Introduction to the Study of the Law of the Constitution. He formulated three broad principles in relation to the unwritten Constitution of England. The Principles are as follows:
a. No person can be punished or made to suffer except for a distinct breach of law established in the ordinary legal manner before the ordinary courts. This presupposes that rule of law is incompatible with exercise of wide, arbitrary or discretionary powers of constraint by persons in authority
b. Equality before the law. Dicey conceives this element as the equal subjection of all classes to the ordinary law of the land. There is no place for the exemption of officials or others from the duty of obedience to the law which govern other citizens or from the jurisdiction of the other tribunals.
c. Constitutional law is for Dicey a consequence of the rights of the citizens as defined and enforced by the courts.
The last postulation is irrelevant to us as we have a written constitution that sets out the right of citizens in Chapter 4. The first and the second formulations of the rule of law are of interest to us and relevant to this review. We shall refer to them as often as may be necessary.
Lack of Fidelity to law
The claim of this government for the respect for the rule of law has been tested by the unfolding Ribadu saga. The former Chairman of the EFCC was removed from office without complying with the enabling law. Section 3(2) of the EFCC (Establishment) Act 2004 provides that 'a member of the Commission may at any time be removed by the President for inability to discharge the functions of his office (whether arising from infirmity of mind or body or any other cause) or for misconduct or if the President is satisfied that it is not in the interest of the Commission or the interest of the public that the member should continue in office'.
The Inspector General of Police, apparently working in cahoots with some people bent on derailing the war against corruption and rubbishing its modest achievements at the time, woke up one day and suddenly remembered that as an Assistant Inspector General it was in Mr Ribadu's interest to proceed on a one-year Senior Management course at the National Institute for Policy and Strategic Studies at Kuru.
To douse public anger at the surreptitious removal of Ribadu, the Police said that he was not being removed and that he would return to his office as chair of EFCC after his course. After the course, the man was literally dragged out of the graduation hall by officers acting as usual on orders from above.
His certificate was initially not released to him until public uproar again forced government to retrace its step. While he was undergoing the course a substantive Chair of the EFCC in Mrs Farida Waziri was appointed putting it beyond any doubt that Mr Nuhu Ribadu had been illegally removed as the chair of the EFCC.
Yet all the President needed do was to openly and courageously declare that Mr Ribadu was removed from office on account of any of the three grounds listed in section 3(2) of the EFCC Act, namely that he was unable to perform the functions of the office or that he has mis-conducted himself or that it was not in the Commission's or the public interest for him to continue in office.
That would have been the end of the matter. However, fearing that the public would easily link with the corrupt cabal that had threatened to deal with Mr Ribadu by all means, fair and foul, the Presidency resorted to fishing for other arguments. It suddenly discovered that Mr Ribadu's re-appointment was not confirmed by the Senate. Of course there was no such requirement for senate confirmation in respect of re-appointment.
More tellingly, the government failed to tell Nigerians why Ribadu's name was not sent to the Senate for confirmation, if that was indeed the problem. Subsequently, the government came up with the argument that it was interested in building an enduring anti-corruption institution and not necessarily individuals. But it has failed to respond to the question why it did not appoint a convicted armed robber to head the institution if the character of individuals is irrelevant.
Mr Ribadu has only a few days ago been dismissed by the Police Service Commission for acts of insubordination. The purported dismissal was done by an executive body that knew of the pendency of the suits filed by the officer contesting the issues on which he was dismissed. Now if that was not a vicious trampling on the rule of law, nothing can ever be. The Supreme Court had in the case of Ojukwu v Military Governor of Lagos State warned against parties and others frustrating pending cases by their actions.
In that case the apex court deprecated the acts of Lagos State as amounting to executive recklessness. The good thing is that the Police Commission is subject to the control and direction of the President by virtue of section 158(1) of the Constitution. Many people expect the President to redeem the image of his administration by directing the Commission to retrace its step. If he does not so act many critics would be justified to conclude that the President himself is the arrowhead of the forces bent on hu
miliating Mr Ribadu.
The NICON imbroglio is another pointer to the lip service paid by the administration to the rule of law. The State had put in place a morally questionable law that permits the parcelling out of the collective patrimony of the country to a few speculators who deceive themselves and the public with the appellation of capitalists. That law is in violation of section 16 of the Constitution that provides that the major sectors of the economy shall be operated by the state.
To be fair to this administration the cornering law (alias Privatisation Act) was not promulgated by it. But it has embraced the law gleefully and with undisguised enthusiasm. Well the point is that although Mr Jimoh Ibrahim had 'bought' NICON under the cornering law, the Yar' Adua government disregarded an existing law to take over the company without any order from any court of law thus acting so brazenly and arbitrarily contrary to the dictates of the rule of law. It again took negative reaction from the public for the government to settle the matter out of court. Up till the time of writing this review the government and its apologists have not been able to give any justification for its manifestly illegal conduct.
Inequality before the Law
Dicey's second formulation of the rule of law is that everybody is equal before the law. That formulation clearly does not apply to the treatment of persons standing criminal trials in Nigeria under the present administration. It appears that those who are accused of stealing billions of Naira as Governors or public officials are more equal than all other category of defendants. Bail is granted to them as a matter of course and upon the most liberal terms possible.
Of course, the passports which are deposited with the court as conditions for the bail are routinely released for them to attend to their health challenges abroad or to hold personal meetings overseas. Meanwhile, people accused of stealing one or two tubers of yam have been awaiting trial without bail and languishing in prisons across the country for upward of ten years.
The whole thing is truly disgusting. I do not suggest that accused persons are not or should not be entitled to bail. No. what I do not understand is the special way these ex-public officials are treated. One of the former governors accused of stealing billions of Naira was only last week or so asked to pay a paltry fine of N3 million! It seems that the administration's understanding of the rule of law is the use of law to protect public officials who are accused of looting the treasury. If so, then theirs is a warp understanding of Dicey and the rule of law. They are standing the concept on its head.

Original_Search_Title: What Karamoh Kabba Makes of Attitudinal Change
Headline: Sierra Leone: What Karamoh Kabba Makes of Attitudinal Change
Body_Text:
Freetown — Below is a welcome statement by Karamoh Kabba to guests at the Second Session of the Attitudinal Change Forum organised by the technical team of the Attitudinal Change campaign, Miatta Conference Hall in Freetown.
Mr. Chairman, Madam Sam-Sumana, wife of the Hon. Vice President, Members of the Technical Team of the Attitudinal Change Committee, Honourable Ministers, Members of Parliament distinguished ladies and gentlemen, on behalf of His Excellency President Ernest Bai Koroma, Hon. Alpha Kanu, Presidential and Public Affairs Minister, Hon I.B. Kargbo, Information and Communications Minister, the able and competent "Attitudinal Change" Committee and the people of the Republic of Sierra Leone, it is my great honor to welcome you to yet another session of the Attitudinal Change.
This is the second Attitudinal Change forum I have attended in Freetown, and it symbolizes the high importance the President and the people of Sierra Leone place on the tenet; "let your attitude show your altitude." Improving our personal growth will not depend much on how many qualifications we have like our attitude towards work and everything else we do. These individual positive attitudes will in turn determine the socio-economic growth of our country.
President Ernest Koroma made no mistake when he zeroed in on the need for us to change our attitude as a society of people - the problem of bad attitude is rooted in our common history, sustained by bad governments that focused no premium on national allegiance, patriotism and civic competence.
For a change, I must first thank and welcome the noble ideal of Attitudinal Change of our generation under a competent leadership of President Ernest Bai Koroma for taking the bold step of self-criticism, self-assessment and self-appraisal of individuals and the society as a whole.
We can choose to embark on all the many conspiracy theories abound in our midst on why Sierra Leone is lagging in socio-economic development, but until we change our attitude we will forever gripe about our woes and multiply our despondent condition.
We are about to write a story of courage and determination that will inspire our young ones and help us to forever abandon the stories that have created the need for us to revisit with our attitude.
And I must say that we are about to inspire the rest of the world with which we share this common malaise of self-destruction.
And I must tell you folks, our president is very serious about the need for us to change our attitude - In Kenema, upon awarding degrees to graduands, he took the opportunity to admonish us to abandon our self-acquisition of PhD (Pull Him Down) syndrome of self-destruction and the destruction of others.
And today, we meet here as leaders and people of a society who have identified a serious problem that must be addressed. While I was in Atlanta attending the Youth for Sierra Leone Improvement cultural night upon Dr Sho-sawyer, the group's founder and former chairman's invitation, I listened to Dr Alpha Bah, one of our renowned historian in the Diaspora, articulates: "Any culture that does not enhance the life of a society must be abandoned." And I will add that those cultures that will enhance the life of our society, whether local or alien, be kept or adapted.
My advice to us therefore is that if bad attitude towards our socio-economic development is a cultural thing, we must drop it forthwith.
One bad culture that comes to mind is the culture of dependency. When you tether the goat, you must put the cassava leaves far away from its reach. Our leaders must compose a national anthem of attracting investors to boost up the private sector economy for more job creation. The family, relatives, friends and hometown people pressure on our leaders and people in high places have created two major problems: The tendency to be corrupt to satisfy an endless chain of jobless dependants.
The tendencies to have these dependants roam the streets agitated for cutting off their livelihood by trying the corrupt officials.
It is the plain truth folks: In other developed countries where almost everyone depends on their daily wages, such a clampdown on corruption will only be a fodder for the media and pain for the family.
Together, we must help each other through this difficult but possible task of abandoning the business-as-usual cultures and turn a new page. We owe our children the responsibility to do the right thing.
Nurturance is a very powerful sociological/psychological concept. A child has the tendency for the most part to be likely influenced by the values of the society he or she is brought up in.
Evidently, our generation until now was fond of the culture of a cow grazes where it is tethered which, in our lingo is, 'wusai den tie cow na dae ee dae eat.' In good faith though, of course a cow grazes where it is tethered. But if the cow over grazes and poohs all over the soil, there will grow no new grass shoots.
Let us adhere to the principle of honest grazing of our national grass, let us account for every penny that comes through us, and let us account for every hour we are supposed to spend on the job, let us be creative and innovative at work.
Change is a very difficult thing to settle on; but most time it is the answer. If we have all agreed that Attitudinal Change is the answer to our problem, I welcome you here today to think with us as to how to go about it. The President does not have a ready-made answer nor the Attitudinal Change Committee. We must think together with the president to see his vision come true.
Then I would like to say some words about the organization that has brought all of us here - The Technical Committee of the Attitudinal Change. I am deeply impressed by their enthusiasm and commitment to this national cause.
You are all welcome to express your views to this wonderful open forum And thank you all for coming out today.

Original_Search_Title: Prices of Food Items Soar At FCT Markets
Headline: Nigeria: Prices of Food Items Soar At FCT Markets
Body_Text:
Barely few days to Christmas and New Year prices of food items have increased at various markets in the Federal Capital Territory (FCT).
A visit to the markets by our reporter revealed that the prices of food items like rice, beans, yams, tomatoes, pepper, onions, chicken, fish, meat and groundnut oil have increased by slightly.
For instance, a bag of rice that sold for N7,500, now sells for between N8,500 and N9,000 depending on the brand.
A mudu of bean sold for N220 as against N200, while five average size tubers of yam sold at N1,500 instead of N1,200.
Similarly, prices of tomatoes, pepper and onions increased drastically, as a basket of tomatoes that sold for N2,500 now sells for N3,200, while pepper that formerly sold at N3,000 now sells for N4,000.
The prices of chicken, meat and fish have also skyrocketed as a heavy chicken was selling for N2,500 yesterday compared to the previous price of N1,500.
Meat and fish buyers were seen complaining of the high cost at Nyanya and Utako markets.
Some market traders that spoke to our reporter attributed the sudden increase to increase in transportation.
"For the past few days now, transportation fares to bring tomatoes and pepper have increased, hence the increase of the prices of our items," a marketer said.
Also speaking, one of the buyers who preferred anonymity said the increase in food items is making it difficult for people to really shop for the Christmas and New Year.

Original_Search_Title: Osaze - I Don't Have Time for Women Now
Headline: Nigeria: Osaze - I Don't Have Time for Women Now
Body_Text:
Super Eagles star, Osaze Odemwingie speaks on his experience in the Russian League. The former France-based reveals to Complete Sports that he has no plan to get married in the next four years. Excerpts...
Now, the season has ended in Russia, how would you describe your season personally?
Well, I'm satisfied with my performance all season. At least, I didn't have any injury and I think I played well but it didn't go too well for my team. We finished seventh which was not too good for us. And internationally, the Africa Nations Cup in Ghana was also not too satisfactory.
It was only in the Olympics in Beijing that I really had something to cheer about. Then of course, the Super Eagles qualified for the second round and the draws were positive for us. I also scored 10 goals to emerge second highest scorer in the Russian League. In general, I have a feeling that the year was not a bad one even though it was not up to what I expected.
What will you say accounted for your club's inability to qualify for Europe?
I think instability because sometimes we would lose at home and go away to win some games even when you didn't expect us to win. So I think this was the reason why we could not end up among the top three in the country and I think the team spirit is very low at the moment because we have a young squad, too young I can say and that in a way, affected our ability to create chances because our midfield is still weak.
But the fans of your club believed your absence during the Olympics cost them a lot as the most important player in the team.
Yes, because we dropped out of the top 5 by just two points or so and they believed my absence affected the team because we could have won a couple extra games if I had been around. And I think that is part of the problem I was talking about. Lokomotiv are a very big club and should not be depending on a single player. When I left, there was nobody else to look up to and that is really unfortunate for a club of our size.
Do you still habour the dreams of moving to England?
Yes, because it is obvious a lot of Nigerians back home expect me to and keep asking me when am I moving to the Premier League. It is a place where Nigerians can get to watch me every week. My contract here is running down and I think my priority this time will be England. That's where people want to see one and that's where I also want to be soonest.
OK, if you were to pick, which team will you like to join in the Premier League?
I don't want to mention a name but let me say that any of the top 5 teams will interest me, personally, I like Arsenal a lot.
Is it because they have young players?
Not really. I have always supported them even when they had great players like Papillo and Bergkamp and Henry. They play the kind of football I like and I have been following them for many years but now, there are a lot of other teams like Man Utd, Man City and Chelsea. But the one I like most honestly is Arsenal.
You started with La Louviere in Belgium and it's like you have fallen in love with Belgium. Is that why you bought a house there?
Yes I like Belgium. It's like my home in Europe and each time I have free days I always go there because I like the country and the people are really interesting and friendly. They are very nice so I like staying there when I holiday in Europe and the location of my house is like a border between Belgium and France.
So I shuttle between Russia and Beligum when I have the chance because I still have a lot of friends over there. Besides, there are many Nigerians here and its easier to get Nigerian foods. That's why I like it here a lot.
So, which Nigerian food do you get to eat there?
Pounded yam of course. Rice is something I can cook at home normally, so I always have pounded yam when I eat outside.
You were born in Uzbekistan, how come you fell in love with pounded yam?
(Laughs) I think when I got back to Nigeria and needed to be stronger, that was what I was eating. You need to eat pounded yam which is a solid food in other to be strong.
Ok, I dont know whether you have heard about the injury of Yakubu Aiyegbeni and that he is out for the rest of the season.
Yeah, I first heard it from you and then confirmed it from someone else. Sincerely it's a pity especially at this moment when we are approaching the second round of qualifiers for the World Cup. Having him in the team is always a moral booster and I think its going to be difficult to fill his place. I can only wish him quick recovery so that he can be ready for next season.
Osaze is 27 years old, so when are we expecting the wedding bells to ring?
Well, I haven't think about it very much because I had to focus on my career and I didn't want anything to distract my attention. I just want to focus on one thing as I would not want to start a family and begin to move them around each time I have to move as a footballer. So when I finally decide, I will be in a position to keep my family in one place and I think this should happen in the next four or five years. Then, I will be in my thirties.
Finally Osaze, you talked about a plane crash scare you experienced in Moscow the last time. How did it actually happen?
Well it was supposed to be a one-hour flight normally but when the flight started getting longer than we thought, two hours we were still on the air, we got to know that the weather was not good. So we flew into Moscow but then they had to clear the run way of snow, so we still hung in the air for some time before we finally landed.
We seemed to be in the air for ages and for those who had a phobia for flying, it was really a frightening experience. We finally landed even though the visibility was very poor. It also affected other planes that were flying all over Moscow at the time because they were not permitted to land. It was really frightening.
Does that affect your thinking about flying?
During winter, I won't like to fly again. That's one thing for sure.
So if you have another match like that with your club, do you mean you will come back by road?
(Laughs) No, I will just make sure I don't play the last match of the season again.
Osaze, thank you very much.
Thanks too.

Original_Search_Title: Oki Sends SOS to Govt
Headline: Nigeria: Oki Sends SOS to Govt
Body_Text:
MATRON of Our Lady of Mercy Orphanage Home, Effurun, Delta State, Madam Rita Oki have appealed to Delta State government and other well meaning persons to come to the aid of the home even as she commended members of the National Association of Seadogs, NAS, Umalokun Zone, Warri, for donating gift items worth over N500,000.00 to the orphanage.
Madam Oki made the call, Weekend in Effurun, Uvwie council area of the state when members of NAS, a.k.a. Pyrates Confraternity, led by their leader, Mr. Misan Ukubeyinje, donated bags of rice, beans, garri, salt, gallons of vegetable oil, palm oil, kerosene, cartons of biscuit, detergent, beverages, bags of fairly used clothes, sleepers, crates of eggs, tubers of yams and other items to the motherless home.
Speaking on behalf of the Umalokun Zone, Capon Misan Ukubeyinje said the donation is in keeping with the humanistic ideals of the confraternity, saying that it is a yearly affair and charged Madam Oki to see the gift items as their contribution to making this yuletide a happy one for the children of the home just as he commended her for her dedication to duty.
Mr. Ukubeyinje who further promised to continue their support for the home told the matron to always get in touch with the confraternity as they are prepared to render their little assistance at all times.
Responding on behalf of the home, Madam Oki who said the arrival of the various items was timely as she has already run short of some basic ones such as palm oil, saying that "I am short of words. I don not know how to thank you people except to say that God would continue to bless all of you abundantly".

Original_Search_Title: Chief Frowns On Behaviour of Minister
Headline: Ghana: Chief Frowns On Behaviour of Minister
Body_Text:
Techiman — The Adontenhene of the Techiman Traditional Area, Nana Asare Twi Brenpong has descended heavily on the Brong Ahafo Deputy Regional Minister, Hon. Abraham Kwadwo Kwakye, for failing to accord the Techiman Tradtional Council respect when he visited the area after the Tuobodom shooting incident.
Nana Adontenhene made this allegation at the Omanhene's palace when the National Security capos visited the town to assess the proposed Air Strip site and to have more information regarding the Tuobodom shooting incident when gun shots were fired at the Omanhene's convoy.
The chief said the Minister acted childishly when he told Nananom that he had come not to listen to them but rather to have a look for himself.
The Adontenhene of the Techiman Traditional Council who sounded furious said the incident that happened at Toubodom when the recognized chief of the Omanhene, Nana Obeng Ameyaw was celebrating the Fodjour Yam Festival was very bad and must be condemned.
He stressed that the Kingmakers of the traditional council have in one accord handed the matter to the police to thoroughly investigate so that peace would prevail before, during and after the December general election.
He called on people in authority to study and know how to manage conflicts when the need arises, since it is very essential in a developing country as Ghana.

Original_Search_Title: Arewa Traders Reject Obi's Relocation Sites
Headline: Nigeria: Arewa Traders Reject Obi's Relocation Sites
Body_Text:
Onitsha — Arewa traders in Onitsha, Anambra state, have strongly appealed to the Sultan to come to their rescue as the state government has again perfected plans to deploy soldiers from the 14 Field Engineering Regiment (FER), Onitsha, to unleash another mayhem on them and forcefully eject them from the land.
Efforts to relocate them by Governor Peter Obi proved abortive as the traders occupying the head bridge market, Onitsha, had earlier refused about four new sites shown to them for relocation.
The traders tasked the state government to compensate members of the Arewa Motor Owners Association and its afore-mentioned co-occupants who sustained losses during the unlawful invasion by government agents and destruction of shops occasioned by the invasion, demanding payment of compensation to families of their colleagues who lost their lives during the raid when they were shot by soldiers on January 26, this year.
The Hausa traders under the umbrella of "Arewa Traders Community and Amalgamated Traders and Transporters", comprising Arewa Transport Owners Association, Hausa Onion Sellers Association, Big Yam Sellers Association, Oshogbo Motor Park Owners Association, Gombe/Maiduguri Park Owners Association, Wetuobi Park Owners Association and Northern Goat Dealers Association, have been at loggerheads with the state government over the past one year which is lingering in this present administration of Governor Peter Obi.
The chairman of the Niger State Traditional rulers' council and Etsu Nupe, Alhaji Yahaya Abubakar, after inspecting the sites advised the Hausa community to give the state government maximum co-operation to execute its good plans for the relocation.
Abubakar who represented the Sultan of Sokoto, Alhaji Sa'adu Abubakar, during a courtesy call on Governor Obi at the Government House, Awka, in company of the Emir of Suleja, Mallam Auwal Ibrahim, said the state government has good intentions to relocate the Hausa community based in Onitsha.
The royal father however, requested the Governor to give them all necessary support and assistance to settle down easily at the new sites and commended Governor Obi for the remarkable achievements recorded within a short time.
The Estu Nupe who lamented the deplorable condition of Federal roads in the state, called for immediate intervention by the Federal Government.
Responding, Governor Obi said he places peace on top of the State's Integrated Development Strategy (ANIDS) to pave way for sustainable development, adding that he will resort to the use of dialogue than force to carry the Hausa community and other residents along in the execution of government's policies and programmes.
The running battle between the government and the traders dates back to January, this year when the state government issued a quit notice to the traders to vacate the land to enable government renovate, decorate and beautify the place in such a way that visitors coming into or going out of the state from the Asaba end would see Onitsha as a neat city because it amounts to an eye-sore for visitors to come in and the first thing they would set their eyes on is a dirty goat market located along the expressway.
But the traders refused to heed the government's quit notice because according to them, the parcel of land they are occupying is a federal government property hence they are tenants to the federal government and not to the state government.
This refusal, forced the government to unleash a team of soldiers from the Onitsha Army barracks on the traders which turned out to be bloody as no fewer than four traders were allegedly shot dead, while several others were injured, even as the traders refused to move from the site till date.
However, government, in a recent move, notified the Sultan of Sokoto, Alhaji Muhammed Sa'ad Abubakar to prevail on the people to accept the relocation, Abubakar sent a delegation to Onitsha to join the government and the traders to inspect the new sites the government wanted to relocate them to.
On arrival, government officials showed the delegation four new sites but a few days after the inspection exercise, the traders made up their mind to reject the four new sites. In a press statement issued in Onitsha at the weekend, the spokesman of the traders, Alhaji Talim Abubakar, gave five reasons for rejecting all the new sites. They equally gave six conditions to even accept any reconciliation moves with the state government.
According to the traders, the proposed sites are not within the city centre. The sites are in the forest and no effort is being made by the government to make them habitable. The sites will take them far away from their customers and the strategic location of their people.
They also adduced reasons for rejecting the new sites on the grounds that one of the proposed sites is presently subject of dispute and litigation between the state government and the Abada Traders Association whom they said are claiming to be the owners of the land, having bought same outright from the state government some time ago, adding that one of the sites is directly lying and situate under the electricity high tension power poles, hence not safe for habitation or business considering the hazard.
On their conditions for reconciliation with the state government, the traders re-affirmed their earlier position to the Sultan that they can only relocate if the state government provides a permanent site for them within Onitsha South council area for easy accessibility of their customers, adding that the site should be spacious enough to accommodate expansion and growth. They also said the site should be fenced and a certificate of occupancy issued and delivered through the Sultanate council to the leaders of the various groups occupying the present land, adding that the state government shall exempt them from paying any form of taxes or levy for a period of two years for the use and occupation of the proposed site to allow for development.

Original_Search_Title: Marble Arch And Parktonian Hotels Awka
Headline: Nigeria: Marble Arch And Parktonian Hotels Awka
Body_Text:
Lagos — Marble Arch opened in 2000 and has been a very popular place ever since. Nigerian Breweries had just concluded its Star Quest show there when I visited the hotel, surprisingly for the very first time last month.
Its swimming pool is obviously a very popular facility and some ladies and gentlemen were easing the discomfort of the hot African sun by cooling off in the pool, while a few others relaxed by the pool bar where drinks are cheaper than in the main.
I was pleasantly surprised to discover Hajia Maero Baba was General Manager of the hotel. In our chat in the bar, she revealed she had worked at Hamdala Hotel Kaduna, had been a member of the team that opened Arewa Suites Hotel in Abuja before moving south-eastwards to Awka about two years ago to manage MAH.
Built by Chief Sam Nwosu, Marble Arch Hotel has 75 rooms and Odili Oputa, the Accountant, had given me a run down before Hajia returned to meet me, while I waited for Chef Amos to whip up the various 'swallows' and soups that MAH is loved for.
One tip here. If you want to eat Tuwon Shinkafa in Awka, Marble Arch is the place to go. You will also get Amala, Eba, Pounded Yam and Semovita dutifully accompanied by Ora, Onugbu, Afang, Ofe Nsala. Egusi, Okro, Ogbono and Ofe Akwu (available on Sunday mornings only!).
MAH also offers continental meals and I ate Fried Rice and with my bottle of water, my bill was just N900. I made a mental note to check out the Beef or Chicken Chimichanga with French Fries next time I visit Awka and was told that the meal is spicy meat with soy sauce.
Ugba, Nkwobi and Spicy Stockfish are also popular treats at the hotel, where Wednesday night is Chinese night, Thursday is BBQ night with a live band by the pool, Saturday is Buffet Special @ N2,000 per person and Sunday is Family Buffet from 1pm to 5pm during which adults eat all they can for N2,000 and kids eat for N1,200.
Accomodation is affordable, starting from N5,000 for a Business Suite, N9,000 for the Executive Suite and N15,000 for the Ambassador Suite.
Back to the Bar Royale, Jackson Udoh, the Barman, was an amiable 'host' serving up chilled drinks and drawing my attention to kilishi and other snacks available at the bar. Cocktails cost from as little as N400 and the bar menu has the Seafood Skewer, Beef, Chicken or Mixed Skewer for just N1,000, Prawns Cocktail or Snaid De Sinnamon for the same price
I had heard nice things about the Parktonian Experience and decided to check if it was for real. I received a very warm welcome through the gate, into the air-conditioned lobby, then on to the bar and restaurant. Both are large and comfortably seat over 30 persons each.
Parktonian Hotel which is on the Enugu - Awka - Onitsha expressway, prides itself as being hospitality redefined.
From my chat with lovely Tina in the restaurant, I learnt that Ofe Nsala, Onugbu and Egusi were diners' favourites with the staples like Pounded Yam, Semovita and Eba. Rice variants exist and Lunch or Dinner will cost you about N1,000..
Breakfast offers options like Potatoes, Yam and Cereals and cost from N250.
It took major investigative effort to find out that a gentleman named Obinna Obiora built and owns the lovely hotel and I wondered why such an accomplishment should be a secret.
Built barely 3 years ago, it is still looking brand new. Its well appointed rooms cost from N7,000 for a Studio, to N15,000 for the Super Executive Suite. There are Promo offers which result in lower room charges for certain categories of guests, on request.
Jephthah Omoviye, the General Manager, was out when I visited, but most graciously telephoned later to say hello.
I had enjoyed a nice, very cold star beer while watching a musical on the flat screen tv in the bar and set off on my drive to Enugu where duty called. Have a great new week folks.

Original_Search_Title: '99 Days in Agege'
Headline: Ghana: '99 Days in Agege'
Body_Text:
Like it happened to the people in the Biblical days, who used 4,000 years instead of 40 days, one of Ghana's representatives in the just-ended Africa Hockey Club Championships, Reformers traveled by road for over 36 hours to the tournament venue.
And as Lucky Dube of blessed memory sang in his 'Remember me' track, we wandered up and down the thick forests of 'SOWETO' in search of the main route linking to the Abuja road.
Indeed, it was a tiring journey but thanks to the Prisons' brave drivers -Samuel 'Keteku' and Wiafe, who drove for several unending hours.
The team left Accra at 5pm on Monday and arrived in Nigeria on Wednesday, a day before the start of the tournament, tired and with swollen feet.
The road map to Abuja is poorly designed, as we struggled in vain on many occasions to locate sign posts to guide us, so we eventually fell on the inhabitants of the villages liking the main road.
One of such attempts threw us overboard and we found ourselves in a bush we nicknamed 'the Evil forest', where we encountered no vehicle after traveling for many kilometers at midnight.
However, after going through the hustle and bustle of a long travel, we eventually reached our destination. The Reformers team gave a good account of themselves by finishing fourth in the competition, losing 0-1 to fellow Ghanaian opponents CEPS, in the third-placed game.
It was the defending champions, Heartland of Nigeria, who retained the trophy by beating Kenya's Telkom 5-2 in the final of the women category.
Ghana's Golden Sticks beat Nigeria's Yobe Desert 4-2 to finish as bronze medalists, while African hockey giants, Sharkiah of Egypt beat fellow Egyptian side Police by 5-2 in the final of the male category.
But it was all good, there was a clear demonstration of stick work, and players like Exchequers' Alfred Armah, Golden Sticks' Joseph Appiah and Kenya's Edna Simbi were some of the finest in the ten-day hockey fiesta.
Life in Abuja, a state designed for the affluent is extremely expensive, unlike Lagos where we passed the night on our return to Accra.
We even located a place where they sell kenkey, and it was like manner from Heaven after days of taking nothing apart from their local delicacy of 'Eba' with 'Egusi' stew or pounded Yam with okro soup, popularly known as 'draw back'.
Next year's competition will be hosted by Uganda on a date to be announced later.
All the four reps, Reformers, CEPS, Golden Sticks and Exchequers have safely arrived in the country.

Original_Search_Title: Fighting Poverty With Smes
Headline: Nigeria: Fighting Poverty With Smes
Body_Text:
At a recent Lagos forum, an economist with the World Bank remarked that the potential for small and medium scale enterprises, SMEs, in Africa, and also in Nigeria remain just potentials.
If Nigeria , he added, is ever to overcome poverty, more attention has to be directed towards SMEs. It is strange that in this age, we still need World Bank officials to remind us of which path to tread regarding basic economic action. We found ourselves in the current economic bind because we ignored the latent capacities within, while perpetually embarking on wild goose-chases called foreign direct investment.
Our approach to SMEs is noted in official statements that mean nothing. Action remains mere words. A good example of government's best attempt at assisting the SMEs is the requirement that banks put aside a part of their profit for lending to SMEs.
Every bank has billions of Naira in its vault - the SMEs cannot access the money since only a few of them can meet the conditions that the banks set. Some of the conditions are similar to the ones for regular lending.
Nigeria has lost the role of cottage industries in the economy to bureaucracy. In many countries, thousands of families are gainfully employed making single items on which entire families depend. For instance, if a family specialises in quality leather works; the appropriate machinery and other inputs are obtained with capital from a bank, while the labour is shared among family members using the residential premises as land for the industry. In some cases the bank helps with marketing the products.
There is no shortage of Nigerians with skills and entrepreneurial abilities to produce pottery, beads, entertainment, Nigerian wares and other products that could be exported. SMEs producing water, fruit juice, custard, pounded yam, detergents, are dying as a result of the perennial power outages, lack of clean, potable water, dearth of access roads, and insecurity. Where there are resources to buy generators, the runaway price of diesel continues to make electricity a luxury.
SMEs already sagging under so many burdens are further encumbered by multiple taxes; at start-up, they will have to obtain a plethora of permits which are often duplicated at the state and local government levels.
The doors to credit are firmly shut against them. Banks with easy money to make from short term lending to traders and purchasers of foreign exchange do not want to share in the burden of SMEs. The various intervention agencies and industrial development banks do not reach the people.
They warehouse the billions appropriated annually for poverty alleviation and make a show of it.
Government should create a one-stop investment policy to get the banks to lend the billions of Naira they have accumulated over the years to SMEs because they are the sector with capacity to arrest unemployment and reduce poverty.
When poverty is reduced, disposable income rises and the economy regains its vibrancy. Attention to the SMEs has become mandatory with the prevailing uncertainties in the economy.

Original_Search_Title: States to Compute Own GDP, Says Daggash
Headline: Nigeria: States to Compute Own GDP, Says Daggash
Body_Text:
Minna — The National Planning Commission (NPC) said yesterday that in collaboration with National Bureau of Statistics (NBS), it has developed a framework, which will enable states to compute their own gross domestic products (GDPs).
Minister and Deputy Chairman, NPC, Senator Sanusi Daggash, disclosed this yesterday in Minna, Niger State, at a joint meeting of the National Council on Development Planning (NCDP) and the Joint Planning Board (JPB).
This is coming as the Niger State Governor, Muazu Babangida Aliyu, said "the height of development that any country could attain depend largely on the understanding of the basic element of interactive forces that unfold into real economic growth and development."
GDP is the basic measure of the output of an economy in a given year. GDP, which is a key parameter for measuring the performance of a nation's economic performance, measures the values of goods such as cement, eggs, yam, tomatoes and beer produced, amongst others along with the values of services such as medical services, hotel services, amongst others.
Presently, in Nigeria, GDP is measured on an aggregate basis. Already, Daggash revealed, that the National Economic Council (NEC) has endorsed a memorandum on the framework, which it had earlier presented to it.
Daggash said the development was part of the efforts aimed at deepening the GDP computation process and determining the size of the economy of each state of the Federation and the Federal Capital Territory (FCT).
He expressed the hope that, the computation of states' GDP will "generate considerable healthy competition among the states of the Federation as is currently being practiced in other parts of the world such as the United States of America." He said, some states have already initiated moves to partner with the Federal Government in the computation of their GDP.
He however cautioned that, "no state of the Federation can individually determine its own GDP in isolation of others as all states' GDP within the Federation must sum of to the National GDP."
The process, he added, also has to be properly sequenced to avoid duplication of efforts and resources .

Original_Search_Title: Essumeja Wants C'ttee Report Made Public
Headline: Ghana: Essumeja Wants C'ttee Report Made Public
Body_Text:
Concerned citizens of Essumeja, in the Amansie East district, have expressed grave concern over the delay, of a five-member Judicial Committee of the Kumasi Traditional Council (KTC), in releasing a report in respect of the Essumeja chieftaincy dispute.
The Committee, headed by Nana Yamfohene, was said to have completed findings two months back, but was yet to submit its report.
Other members of the Committee are Nananom Seikwahene, Achirensuahene, Nana Sabronumhene and Kyeame Boakye Yam.
The Committee was charged with determining the eligibility of Nana Adwoa
Pokuaa Nyankonpon, who has reigned as Queen mother of the Essumeja Traditional Area since 1979.
One Adwoa Abora from the lineage of Nana Abena Nyanta and Nana Afua Fremah II, both past queenmothers of Essumeja, is contesting the claim of the queenmother, Nana Pokuaa Nyankonpon, that she is a royal of the Aduana Royal family of Essumeja.
Meanwhile, the people of Essumeja are brooding over the impropriety of the Queenmother - leaving unceremoniously at a time the Black Stool was reported missing.
The Black Stool of the Aduana Royal Family of Essumeja, which plays a significant role in the celebration of the Odwira Festival of Asanteman, has been reported missing since August 29, this year. It was believed to have been stolen by thieves who broke into the stool room at the palace.
The Sanaahene, Nana Owusu Koko, the Ankobeahene, Nana Kwadwo Nyarko and Gyasehene, Nana Twum Barima Appau, are helping the Essumeja police in their investigations, while a stool attendant, Akwasi Adansi Bonah, who first detected the theft, has been quizzed by the local police and granted bail.
The queenmother's nomination to replace Nana Oduro Numapau III, who died in 2003, has been rejected by the kingmakers, causing a stalemate in the en-stoolment of a chief for the town.

Original_Search_Title: Between Atiku and Mbeki
Headline: Nigeria: Between Atiku and Mbeki
Body_Text:
NIGERIANS have been singing the praises of South African democracy because of the way Thabo Mbeki left the office of president.
He would have remained if he had wanted to do so, many say, clapping for a man who in these times has become a flag the continent of Africa can fly that it still has people who cherish name and order and discipline and would display these values even when the trumpet of intrigue sounds. So there is our Thabo Mbeki standing tall as the synonym for democracy.
If only Nigeria can learn from South Africa and Mbeki! Hadn't that country given us Mandela and Desmond Tutu as institutions that stand for principles and truth? And so on, and on, and on. I associate myself with these sentiments so that no one will accuse me of lacking in understanding of the issues. The truth, however, is that it is because I understand the issues that I endorse Mbeki's action, but for other reasons.
He was a man who knew when to fight and when to run away so that he would be alive to fight another day. I will explain the way I see it from many angles to show that human beings are the same everywhere and are affected by situations which force them to take certain actions that may make or unmake them. You can be made rather than marred, according to Prof Ukpabi, one-time dean of student affairs at the University of Nigeria before the civil war, if you learn to cooperate with the inevitable. Yes, cooperate with the inevitable, with a situation which, everything considered, you discover you cannot help. Oh yes, cooperate or you die a wretched death, like Mbeki would have died if he had been as rigid as some would have advised.
Let me tell you this thing about cooperating with the inevitable before we relate it to South Africa and Mbeki and Zuma on the one hand, and then Nigeria and Atiku and Obasanjo on the other. I believe I have told the story before but when the need arises, like now, it pays to repeat the story so that we can draw the lessons it teaches.
You see, we were students who had all it took to be in a class of their own. That was when Nigeria had only four universities - Ibadan, Nsukka, Zaria and Ife We had our beds made, our clothes washed and ironed, our food prepared by cooks and served by stewards- three types of meals you could choose from, and three times a day! One day, we came to the refectory and discovered that only bread and tea was served.
No akamu and akara and baked beans and yam and stew! In the afternoon, and again in the evening, we came to the refectory and found bread and water! No rice and beans or pounded yam and eba with egusi soup or ogbono soup or bitter leaf or some other vegetable soup, plus chicken or fresh fish or oporoko or beef.
The second day, and the third day, and up to Saturday, we had the routine of the previous five days - bread, bread and bread. No one would tell us what was happening. But what was the problem? The cooker in the kitchen had packed up. The engineers who ought to have attended to it were too proud to seek help.
The servicing instructions were in German and they knew no German. The nearest centre that help could come from was the University College Hospital at Ibadan. We wrote an editorial in which we drew attention to the glaring incompetence and lack of concern of the engineering department; that although they knew not what to do, they were too proud to ask for help.
The chief engineer was the target of all the blows. Why would he not tell us that they had problems? The students read the editorial, which was published in the weekly Record of the University of Nigeria's Department of Journalism, and marched on the refectory, destroying everything they set their eyes on during the rampage.
We were accused of practising yellow journalism, but I did not accept, as the editor then, that there was anything yellow about a public officer being exposed for being too full of himself to seek help where there were obvious lapses in his area of responsibility.
Dean Ukpabi later addressed us and told us of the need to learn to accommodate what you are not in a position to change. This story fits the facts before us as you can see in the picture we want to paint of what is happening in South Africa and has been happening in Nigeria, the roads taken by the persons involved right there to make the difference between order and discipline on the one hand and greed and abuse of power on the other.
While not jumping the gun, some people did not accept, as the call for cooperating with the inevitable implied, that you should be slaves if you have no guns to fight the oppressors. They say look for the guns and make a statement with what the barrels emit. The Nigerian situation was clear and straight to the point. Join them if you cannot beat them. But is that the same with South Africa?
South Africa was one of those four or five countries that were the heads of the commonwealth after the Statute of Westminster was enacted by the British Parliament in 1931 officially proclaiming the Commonwealth a free association of self-governing dominions united by a common allegiance to the Crown.
There was the United Kingdom, Canada, Australia, South Africa and New Zealand which were white settlements that together with the United Kingdom calling the shots, dominated the wealth that was supposed to be common to member countries of the British Empire where the sun would never set. Two years before amalgamation of the protectorates of northern and southern Nigeria in 1914, the African National Congress had been formed in South Africa.
It was a non-violent organization that was out to promote the interests of the blacks in a multi-racial society which was from 1948 to experience the most dehumanizing suppression of people's rights through segregation laws that were unknown to any other country in the last century.
In building up the ANC, the leaders deliberately in the early 40s began to recruit younger and more outspoken members.
The leaders did not programme deliberately to remain forever in charge so that they would create the impression that they were indispensable. This was the time Nelson Mandela, Oliver Tambo and Walter Sisulu helped to found the ANC Youth Wing.
This was also the time the Nigerian Youth Movement which graduated from the Lagos Youth Movement of the 30s came into focus before the formation of the National Council of Nigeria and Camerouns led by Herbert Macaulay.
Before independence, other political parties and leaders had emerged in Nigeria - Nnamdi Azikiwe and the NCNC; Ahmadu Bello and the NPC, and Obafemi Awolowo and the Action Group.
As at independence in 1960, the blacks in South Africa were struggling to be the human beings that God had made them to be, but only 11 per cent of the population was in control of some 85 per cent of the land area.
