The Pan Africanist Congress did not find this funny and broke away from the ANC changing the war cry that South Africa belonged to those who live there, black and white; to claim that South Africa belonged to blacks.
What happened in the 60s and 70s with the killings of protesting Africans is history Nigeria can only read about because the country has never, except in isolated cases, risen up to take their destiny in their hands in opposition to fraudulent leadership.
Don't tell me about the Civil War and some pockets of resistance that petered out.
I mean a deliberate and planned and highly orchestrated and sustained act of defiance against abuse of power.
The struggle against apartheid both internally and externally, is the type of confrontation I have in mind, and it was waged by many - Mandela and co in prison for 27 years, having been charged with sabotage in the 60s; those outside who pricked the conscience of the world; and the international community which sustained sanctions against apartheid.
Oliver Tambo and Thabo Mbeki were some of the intellectual giants that fought the war from hidden trenches. With the dismantling of apartheid, the white minority regime conducted an election in 1994 in which the blacks participated fully and Mandela's ANC won a landslide victory.
Thabo Mbeki was his deputy. The Nationalist Party which had orchestrated discriminatory laws since 1948 was the same body that dismantled the obnoxious laws.
It also was in a position to write up the results of the elections, but the environment was not suitable for doing what we have perfected in Nigeria. In 1999, Mbeki won the elections and repeated the feat in 2004.
In neither of these elections was rigging on the scale of writing up the results of elections before they are held and only going through the motions in front of television cameras to show the world that people queued up and voted without intimidation.
So in South Africa where Mbeki was president, there are two houses - a 90-member upper house like our Senate, and the National Assembly which is like our House of Representatives, 400 of them elected on the basis of population.
The latter is the ruling body and it is the leader with the majority in this lower house that becomes the president.
That was the position that Mbeki lost to Zuma, his estranged deputy president who had been sent out of office for corrupt practices. Even after Zuma had had a breather, South Africa's version of our EFCC went after him introducing fresh charges which the High Court said had political undertones.
The dye was then cast. Mbeki would be where he was as president but he fought and lost the battle of leader of the party in a clean contest which Zuma won. So, being leader of the party, he would be president when the next elections are due.
But Zuma had been humiliated and he was entitled to his pound of flesh. So when the court pointed to the influence that was visible in the work of the prosecuting body and said Mbeki had a hand in it, the party advised him to quit.
People praised him for leaving with his head held high. The fact is that if he had not thrown in the towel to live to fight another day, the party would have voted him out in parliament and replaced him with an interim president.
If there is anything we can learn from South Africa, it is the modified presidential system which has the party calling the shots.
Here the party is not calling any shots, and little wonder then that we have no two states projecting any agenda by any of the parties because they do not have what can be projected.
We will look at the Nigerian scenario next week and show that what we entrench and nourish as a way of life is what will make or mar us.

Original_Search_Title: Tuobodom Calls for Government's Intervention
Headline: Ghana: Tuobodom Calls for Government's Intervention
Body_Text:
Nana Baffour Asare II, a rival chief of Tuobodom in the Techiman Municipality, has appealed to the Government of Ghana to help solve the town's longstanding chieftaincy problem.
The call comes after security officers averted bloodshed on Tuesday, October 7th, 2008 during the celebration of the annual Fordjour Kese Yam Festival by a rival chief of the same town, Nana Obeng Ameyaw Barima.
According to Nana Baffour Asare II, the problem if not solved could lead to further disturbances in the town since two chiefs cannot rule one traditional area of which history reveals the right owner.
In a statement to the press, Nana Baffour Asare II called on the Ministry of Chieftaincy and Culture to look into the matter taking into consideration history and traditional authority.
Nana Asare II, also the Omanhene of the Tuobodom Traditional Area and Nana Ameyaw Barima, who doubles as the Akyempemhene of Techiman Traditional Area, pay allegiances to different traditional rulers - the Asantehene, Otumfuo Osei Tutu II, and the Paramount Chief of Techiman, Oseadeyo Akumfi Ameyaw IV, respectively.
The Omanhene of Tuobodom recalled from the history of the area that the then Tano-Subin Local Council which comprised Tuobodom, Tanoso, Buoyem, Nchiraa, Ofuman, Subinso, Branam and Nwoase were in existence before Techiman, therefore the Techiman Traditional Council can under no authority rule Tuobodom through their Akyimpinhene.
Nana Asare Baffour II also said that his "ancestors raised the Tuobodom township by hunting with gun and dogs and did it on the bases of peace," therefore, the present generation and posterity must preserve that tradition and avoid war.
He vowed not to do anything that may mar the peace, stability and democracy which Ghanaians were enjoying, especially as parliamentary and presidential elections were so close.
He urged that the incident that happened on February 16, 1996 when his palace was burnt including other houses must not be repeated and appealed to all chiefs within the country to help make the country a peaceful one by adhering to history in all aspects.
Nana said the town is patiently waiting for government's response to his appeal.

Original_Search_Title: Removing Obstacles to Shariah Implementation
Headline: Nigeria: Removing Obstacles to Shariah Implementation
Body_Text:
The Islamic legal system, Shariah, was reintroduced in the country when former Governor Ahmed Yarima adopted it in Zamfara State in 1999.
Subsequently, 10 other states in the North embraced the system in apparent response to the wishes of their people. The states are Sokoto, Kebbi, Kano, Jigawa, Katsina, Bauchi, Gombe, Borno, Kaduna and Yobe.
However, the introduction of the legal code was not without concern by most Nigerians as it generated anxiety in many quarters. For some, the introduction of shariah was unconstitutional, even as former President Olusegun Obasanjo expressed optimism that "politically-motivated shariah will fizzle out, while genuine shariah will remain with Muslims". However, records show that the initial enthusiasm generated by the legal code is gradually waning in most of the 11 states where it is being implemented. There is ample evidence to show that some of the "shariah governors", as they are fondly called, have failed to ratify the numerous sentences imposed by the various Shariah Courts.
The sentences include limb amputation and death by stoning. Absolving themselves of allegations of deliberate refusal to ratify the sentences, the governors in turn accuse the Shariah Court judges of "hurriedly passing such sentences without following due legal process". But some concerned Islamic scholars blame the difficulty in implementing the legal system on constitutional constraints and lacuna. So far, no fewer than 23 persons are currently awaiting amputation and death by stoning in Bauchi State alone. Many of the convicts have remained in prison custody for about seven years without the ratification or otherwise of their sentences by the state government.
Among the convicts awaiting amputation at the Bauchi Central Prison is 45-year-old Hussaini Maidoya, a yam seller, who was sentenced for chopping off his wife's leg over suspicion of infidelity. Those awaiting death by stoning in the state include Yunusa Rafinchiyawa, 35; Jibrin Babaji, 22 and Umaru Tori, 45. Rafinchiyawa was convicted of having 13 marathon sexual affairs with his friend's wife, while Babaji was sentenced for sodomising some underage boys. Tori was convicted of impregnating his 15-year-old stepdaughter.
Observers, however, say that prolonged incarceration of such convicts without executing their sentences is a violation of their fundamental human rights. Hajiya Aisha Hassan-Baba, a former Director-General, Nigerian Legal Aid Council, says the prolonged imprisonment of these convicts because of a governor's failure to ratify their punishment contradicts democratic and human rights principles. This, perhaps, rationalises the anxiety expressed by some Islamic scholars over the manner of implementation of the code in some Northern states.
The Islamic scholars accuse the governors of taking advantage of the Islamic legal system to achieve their selfish political ambitions. "Ours is to arrest and arraign the violators of shariah in court within the limits of our power," says Alhaji Muktar Ahmed, the Chairman of the Bauchi State Shariah Commission. But the Emir of Bauchi, Alhaji Suleiman Adamu, believes that the execution of the shariah sentences will not only reduce crime but also deter persons who derive pleasure in maiming and killing innocent persons.
Adamu's view is shared by Sheikh Ibrahim Aliyu, a Misau-based Islamic scholar. Aliyu opines that the governors' refusal to ratify the punishment of amputation and death by stoning for adultery and sodomy is anti-Islamic. According to him, it is better for the affected governors to ratify the sentences than to face the wrath of Allah. It is, perhaps, against this backdrop that the third National Conference of Shariah Implementing States was held recently in Bauchi. The conference, with "The Muslims and Constitution Review" as the theme, was attended by the Chairmen of Shariah Commissions in the 11 states, Grand Khadis, Khadis, Shariah Court Judges and Islamic scholars. Some representatives of independent shariah courts from Lagos and Oyo states also attended as observers.
According to the organisers, the conference was to provide an opportunity for shariah implementing states to review their performance and reach a common position to be submitted as a memorandum to the National Assembly for constitution amendment. Most Islamic scholars at conference blamed the difficulty in implementing shariah on constitutional constraints. In his paper entitled "Muslims and Constitutional Review", Khadi Mohammed Khalil, a Kano State Shariah Court Judge, advocated an amendment to Section 36 (12) of the Constitution to give shariah a constitutional status. Section 36 (12) of the Constitution states that "no Nigerian - man or woman - shall be convicted or sentenced except in line with a written law." According to him, the implication is that all the verses of the Holy Quran, prophetic traditions and ulama's expositions in various Islamic books of Fiqh have to be codified if the Islamic legal system is to comply with the spirit of the 1999 Constitution. The Fiqh forms the basis of shariah.
Since evidence is a fundamental requirement in any trial under the Islamic legal system, Khalil suggested the de-listing of the Evidence Act 1990 Cap 112 from the Exclusive Legislative List. The amendment, he argued, would enable shariah implementing states to legislate on the Evidence Act in accordance with Islamic norms. Khalil also cited Section 277 (2) Sub-sections a, b, c, d and e of the Constitution, the Will Act, the Marriage Act, as well as the Land Use Act of 1978 as major impediments to the effective implementation of shariah.
He said that Section 277 of the Constitution, which confines the jurisdiction of the Shariah Court of Appeal to only "personal matters", should be amended to give room for the application of shariah in all ramifications for Muslims. "Shariah implementation in Nigeria can only be based on a sound and solid foundation if only the Shariah Court of Appeal, which is a court of record, enjoys unlimited jurisdiction in both civil and criminal matters such as treason, fraud, economic crime as well as human rights abuses," said Khalil.
According to him, the Will Act, which makes it lawful for any Nigerian to bequeath his property to any person of his or her choice, poses a serious challenge to shariah implementation because the Act contradicts the Islamic law of inheritance. The Islamic scholar also advocated the review of the Land Use Act to confer constitutional jurisdiction on shariah courts and Shariah Court of Appeal to enable such courts to handle land disputes among Muslims. For him, the Marriage Act must also be amended to exclude Muslims from falling within its purview. "Appeals involving Islamic matters should terminate at the Shariah Court of Appeal since not many of the Supreme Court judges have the requisite knowledge in Islamic jurisprudence," said the Kano-based Islamic scholar and Khadi.
Alhaji Garba Gadi, the Bauchi State deputy governor, corroborates Khalil's opinion. Gadi appealed to the National Assembly to give constitutional status to shariah enforcement agents (Hisbah) in order to legalise their operations and ease their operations. Analysts, however, maintain that the reintroduction of the criminal aspect of shariah should not be seen as an end in itself, but a means to an end. They say a lot more work needs to be done to allay fears in some quarters that shariah is masochistic and chauvinistic.
Adamu is of the News Agency of Nigeria, NAN.

Original_Search_Title: Central College Cleans Prampram Health Centre
Headline: Ghana: Central College Cleans Prampram Health Centre
Body_Text:
Tema — Over 100 students of the Miotso campus of the Central University College (CUC), last Saturday embarked on a clean-up exercise to rid the Prampram Health Centre of filth.
The exercise, which formed part of activities lined up to mark the University's 10th Anniversary, saw the students cleaning gutters, rooms, windows and the frontage of the Prampram Health Centre, to enable the sick, who throng the facility for medical treatment, to breath fresh air.
The theme for the 10th Anniversary of the CUC is "10 Years of Training Transformational Leaders."
A similar programme was held by students of the College, at the Mateheko campus of the College.
The exercise, which was done in collaboration with ZoomLion, a waste management company, according to the Organiser of the event, Rev. Charles Nsiah, who is also the Assistant Chaplin of the Miotso campus of the University College, said they felt that it was their social responsibility to help the community they now call their home.
Speaking in an interview with the Tema File, Rev. Nsiah disclosed that "We decided to clean up the Prampram Health facility, because it is more or less the only public health centre around us, apart from the one at Ningo."
According to him, it was the hope of the CUC to train people to acquire faith, excellence and integrity for the next generation.
The University College also donated two bags of rice, one bag of sugar, two gallons of cooking oil, toilet rolls, crates of soft drinks, and some tubers of yam, estimated at a cost of GHÂ¢700, to the Hearts of the Father Outreach, which is referred to as the Home of Refuge for Children, at Old Ningo.
The Amasha Optical Service, based in Accra on the Spintex road, also made themselves available to provide free eye screening and free eye glass frames for the students, natives and residents of the Miotso community, where the College is located.
The Chairman of the Anniversary Implementation Planning Committee, Prof. Joseph Gogo, on his part told the Tema File that a number of programmes had been lined up for the celebration of the 10th Anniversary of the College.
He said the programmes lined-up for the anniversary include a health walk through the principal streets of Accra, Anniversary lectures, expected to commence from Monday October 13, 7th Graduation Ceremony at the Miotso campus on November 1, and a thanksgiving service and awards ceremony for staff of the University College.

Original_Search_Title: Cruising At Chelsea Piers (1)
Headline: Nigeria: Cruising At Chelsea Piers (1)
Body_Text:
Lagos — By all standards, it was the most exciting cultural lunch cruise with breathtaking river views of majestic Manhattan high-rise buildings and the imposing statute of liberty. The sun was about to set on the horizon amid the chilling weather. Since the day was Sunday, Chelsea Piers was spilling over with many foreign tourists and fun seekers. Besides, many New Yorkers could be seen walking briskly into the sports village, Maritime Center or the retail shops of the Piers to have a nice time.
Getting to Chelsea Piers on a Sunday morning when some of the subway rail lines were under repairs was no mean achivement. A day earlier, Richard John Neuhaus, Editor-in-Chief, First Things, had invited me to a brief coffee-meeting at a beautiful Italian restaurant very close to 23rd Street. Besides being an avid reader of First Things, arguably the most authoritative news magazine in the world successfully promoting religiously informed public philosophy in the public square, I had been longing to meet Neuhaus face-to-face to clear a few things about the marketing of political values in the market place of ideas.
So, if you like, I needed to kill two birds with one stone: to be in Manhattan in good time to meet Neuhaus face-to-face as well as punctually participate in the Cruising at Chelsea Piers. The lady bus driver and the bus passengers were most kind and generous in assisting me in locating the restaurant near 23rd Street. Considering the madhouse Lagos, I have never seen such an open-hearted public spiritedness and hospitality. There I was a desperate Nigerian clutching to the arm a somewhat dirty-looking handbag in search of John Richard Neuhaus in a public bus. But none of those passengers considered my look, nationality, identity and my mission before rendering me the much-needed assistance. The American secular humanism can be appealing especially when one is in great difficulty. In Nigeria our anti-humanist behaviour in the public space mocks our deep religiousity. Lagos is always compared to New York. But given the uncivilized behaviour in Lagos, New York is far ahead of Lagos in many things. Whereas in New York, a pedestrian can easily cross the street, in Lagos the motorists and okada riders are so lawless and reckless that they hardly give a pedestrian a right of way to cross the street. So, aside from the provision of public infrastructure, the greatest challenge facing Governor Fashola is taming the several mad human beings in Lagos.
Anyway I got to the Italian restaurant in good time. Neuhaus, who had been to Nigeria in the 60s, was inquisitive in knowing a few things about the socio-cultural and religious life in Nigeria over a cup of coffee. Time ran very fast. Being a very busy man, he needed to go. I too needed to dash off to Chelsea Piers. Located within the Hudson River Park between 17th and 23rd Streets, Chelsea Piers is simply described as something out of this world. First opened in 1910, the Piers are of great importance in the history of the United States. The Titanic is said to have sunk on April 12, 1912 during her maiden voyage while en route to Chelsea Piers. The United States 1963 Olympic Team in which Jesse Owens won four gold medals is said to have sailed to the Berlin Olympic Games from Chelsea Piers. Chelsea is advertised as a piece of haven where anyone can enjoy a healthy, fun-filled recreation. Chelsea Piers is New York City's most popular sporting paradise. Most important, Chelsea Piers served as a base for warships during the First and Second world wars.
But on that fateful Sunday Chelsea Piers served another purpose: it served as a rendezvous for different people from different parts of the world who had gathered in New York City to evaluate the human rights violation in New York especially among Afro-Americans and Hispanics. By chance I was one of the three invited Nigerians. No sooner had I traced Chelsea Piers than we were ushered into a magnificent vessel with the bold inscription: Spirit Cruises, featuring festive lunch, live entertainment, Dj music and dancing, front-row skyline view of the statute of liberty, Brooklyn Bridge, Manhattan Bridge, former site of the World Trade Center etc. I think all in all, we were about forty people comprising men, women. young and not so young, children and even infants from different races cruising in the Spirit Cruises also dubbed the Spirit of New York.
"Ladies and gentlemen", a voice rang out, "you are most welcome to this special cruise. Enjoy yourselves. Have a wonderful view of New York's most magnificent edifices and historical monuments. Over there is the Statue of Liberty, the gift of France to the United States. This is Manhattan Bridge, one of the wonders of the world, built in 1888. Down there used to be World Trade Center before it was blown down. Infact months after it was destroyed, many offices, including my office in Manhattan were still covered with thick black smoke. In a few hours from now we would address you and show you some CDs "
It was a grand lunch buffet, a good break from mulching McDonald's hand burger and washing it down with strange canned drinks. Gardin salad, classic potato salad, grilled vegetable lasagna, Spanish rice, wine, fruit juice were on display for our consumption. In truth, I was not missing isi-ewu delicacy, pounded yam with vegetable soup, garri and egusi soup, amala, ewedu etc. A few days before I arrived to New York City, the news of Josh Hoffing, a 13-year old Manhattan boy who had raised huge sum of dollars to provide water in Ethiopia had filtered all over the place. At table the Jamaican lady spoke about the joy in giving. Like many Manhattan boys, Hoffing would have been roaming about the streets of Manhattan mulching hand bugger or listening to music, but instead he chose to contribute to the welfare of the poor and thirsty Africans in far away Ethiopia. At the young age of 13, Hoffing appreciates that it is better to give than to receive. His photograph was splashed in many New York dailies for his uncommon spirit of charity. Also on board were many student volunteers who take time off between studies to render assistance to the needy, helpless and victims of human rights abuses in New York. Happily Fashola has a plan. He should go on and execute it no matter whose ox is gored.
I met a beautiful young Virginian lady on board who volunteered to go to New York to spend one year assisting young Afro-Americangirls who have fallen victims of infanticide, suicide, rape and discrimination. The interesting thing about this lady is that she is No. 8 in a large family of 11. Her retired parents still live in Virginia. Moved by the generousity of her parents, she has volunteered to go to New York to assist the suffering Afro-American girls. Linda, another American lady from New York, kept thanking God for the gift of life. She recalled that her poor mother almost killed her in the womb. She came into existence from boy-friend-girl-friend relationship. The idea of infanticide was sold to her mother. But she refused to commit infanticide. To show gratitude to God for keeping her alive today, Linda now counsels out-of-wedlock pregnant girls in New York ex-gratis. She also secures accommodation and job for stranded abused New Yorkers. In my presence, one of the abused Afro-American girls came to her office with worn-out shoes. Linda spontaneously appealed to us to contribute one dollar each to buy new shoes for the girl. Before I knew it, someone went down stairs and came back with new shoes for the girl. Upon seeing the new shoes, her face lit up and she came back to life. It dawned on me that poverty does not only reside in Africa. Chris, our chief host, spoke eloquently about his human rights projects in New York City for 23 years. With only a few dollars at his disposal, he has been able to set up many human rights offices in both uptown and down New York at the sacrifice of some pleasure. For example, I noticed that Chris did not own any personal car. He owns a working open-van that conveys the student volunteers to many places. His wife and children find time to collaborate in his many projects.
I almost started weeping when H. Reese Butler 11 finished narrating his story. Butler told us that he had escaped death severally from several ghastly accidents. His young wife committed suicide from depression. After the death of his wife, he elected to set up the Kristin Brooks Hope Center and The Youth America Hotline, a non-profit organization solely for preventing suicide. Approximately 29,000 Americans (that is, about 10.7 per 100,000 of American population) commit suicide every year. With this staggering number, the Kristin Brook Hope Center was incorporated in 1998 to rescue Americans bent on committing suicide with the aid of this sign: "Learn the Warning Signs, call 1.800. SUICIDE for help".

Original_Search_Title: Kifumbira Slum, a Melting Pot of Prostitution, Booze and Filth
Headline: Uganda: Kifumbira Slum, a Melting Pot of Prostitution, Booze and Filth
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE drizzle,which occasionally broke out into a heavy rain storm, went on unabated for the best part of the night.
And when it rains, Kifumbira is the last place anybody would want to be. Kifumbira is not as well known as other slums like Katanga or Kisenyi yet it is right in the heart of Kampala City.
The most we see of Kifumbira is from the road that links Bukoto to Kamwokya or from Mawanda Road and even then it is only for a fleeting moment. The slum neighbours Mulago, Bukoto, Kamwokya and stretches all the way down to Kalerwe.
With a sprawling population of close to 1,200, Kifumbira, like most other slums never sleeps and is kept alive by the thriving prostitution, cheap local brew and video shacks.
The low income housing caters for the blue collar worker - the ilk of hawkers, boda boda riders and taxi touts. And while Kifumbira is not supposed to sleep, on this occasion it did because the heavens refused to relent.
Without the rains, Kifumbira is filthy. No, I tell a lie: It is like a rubbish dump. The sides of the narrow pathways are strewn with rubbish - from plastic bags, sugarcane peelings to the odd shoe to matooke peelings.
The drainage systems are clogged with stagnant water; slime that makes breathing almost impossible - not for the residents but for visitors like myself. But that is before the rains. When it rains, Kifumbira turns into hell, perhaps the last place on earth to live.
Life would be easier facing the war lords in Taliban-controlled Kandahar in Afghanistan than living in Kirumbira when it rains. It becomes a quagmire of mud and slime. With every step you take, you are apprehensive about putting your foot down because you are not quite sure of what you are stepping on.
The drainages overflow, spewing out whatever filth they collect and depositing it down the narrow walkways or at the doors of the houses. But life will still go on. Everybody will simply hop over the slime. Nobody seems to care.
Even worse, the fact that there are not enough pit latrines does not seem to bother residents. They only have three public toilets and a few others which are on the verge of collapse.
Further down in the swampy marshland that divides Kifumbira from Bukoto, men can be seen going to the 'open air toilets' and doing their stuff in plastic bags. Others do not bother to go that far.
As long as the reeds in the marshland provide some cover, that will suffice. Yet the marshland is not only used as an open toilet but as a source of income for the bricklayers and the women who grow yams.
It is easy to get lost in Kifumbira during the day. At night, it is even worse. It is pitch black and I have no idea where I am. The footpath I am on is so slippery that one mistake could see me wallowing in the filth of the drainage.
Shadowy figures occasionally pop out of nowhere with a lantern, demanding to know who you are. Every homestead is playing music, mostly kadongo kamu while those who are brave enough sit on the little veranda space they may have, willing away the rain by drinking malwa or tot packs of cheap waragi.
Things are not looking up for the prostitutes who are huddled in Blue Corner Club. The rain has killed business. The Kifumbira prostitute is in a different class. They are vulgar, dirty and so ugly that one has to wonder as to what kind of man would want to sleep with them.
Sipping on tot packs, they occasionally shout obscenities at each other or ridicule would-be clients, often boda boda men or askaris by saying: "You can't afford me" or "you 'finish' too fast."
When the day breaks, there is still no sign of the heavens relenting.
But, undeterred by the rains, at MJ Pork Joint down Green Valley Lane, the 'hard-cores' have settled down to a pot of malwa. The proprietor of the joint is a lady whose girth rivals that of a Japanese sumo wrestler.
To complement her girth she has a bust so large it often appeared to hinder what she was trying to do. Taking up a spot in the corner, she would lie on a mat, getting up now and again to fill the pot with hot water.
The men are not drunk. They are beyond that. Red eyed, they can hardly sit upright on their stools. When they try to walk, they hit the floor, then get up and fall into the slimy mud.
Again, nobody cares because this, after all, is Kifumbira and in here, that display of drunkenness is deemed normal.
When the women wake up and set about preparing breakfast and lunch, it is a sight to behold.
They all have young children and while the children wallow in the filth of the narrow walkways, (which are an open toilet for them and the dogs), life goes on.
If a kid does pupu, the mother will scream, then get a sugarcane peeling and simply push the pupu into the drainage. To clean her hands, she just wipes them on her filthy dress and carries on from where she had left off.
While the food they cook has the most delicious smell, I figure that if I ate it, I would be on the fast track to Mulago with anything from Hepatitis E to cholera to hookworms. I give the food a miss.
Since Kifumbira spans such a large area, I contract the services of Dumba, a boda boda rider to show me the neighbourhood. A good portion of the male residents are hawkers, especially shoe hawkers.
They sit in small dark shacks polishing shoes to mak them attractive for potential buyers. Then there are the dobbi men, washing heaps of clothes, also for sale.
The others, who do not appear to have any sort of meaningful employment either drink malwa or play ludo.
"At one point the idlers used to play o'mweso but now ludo is the in game," Dumba tells me. And, like in any other slum, ludo here is played with such an intense fever and competitiveness that you have to be made of steel to play along.
The women, if they are not in the cooked food trade, either sell charcoal, matooke or tomatoes. In Kifumbira, nobody buys a sack of charcoal or a bunch of matooke. The matooke, once it is delivered early in the morning is either broken into clusters or individual bananas for sale.
The mud houses are on the verge of collapse with the iron sheet roofs held down by bricks or anything heavy, it is easy to conclude that the average income here is less than sh100,000 per month.
One of the most interesting features about Kifumbira is that during Christmas, it becomes a ghost town. Then, scores of buses line up to take the residents back home for the festive period.
Kifumbira factfile
The name Kifumbira was born out of the number of Bafumbira who migrated there in the late 1950s
The Bafumbira are the dominant tribe with the other main tribes being the Langi and Acholi who migrated there during Idi Amin's reign
Kifumbira has an area called Acholi Quarters which is predominantly occupied by northerners
Once the Bafumbira come to Kampala, they settle at Kifumbira as a transit stop until they find their feet
Kifumbira has a population of close to 1,200 residents but keeps on growing because of the population explosion
Rent in Kifumbira is relatively cheap, at between sh20,000 and 30,000 for a one room house with no privacy, no running water or toilet
Officials estimate that in the next 10 years, there will be a housing crisis in Kifumbira because of the population growth
Nabbingo Garbage Removers charge residents sh500 to collect their rubbish once a week.

Original_Search_Title: Police Avert Bloodshed At Tuobodom
Headline: Ghana: Police Avert Bloodshed At Tuobodom
Body_Text:
Techiman — THE timely intervention by the police averted what could have been a bloody clash between two rival chiefs at Tuobodom, in the Techiman Municipality on Tuesday. The feuding chiefs involved in the skirmishes, pay allegiances to different traditional rulers -the Asantehene, Otumfuo Osei Tutu II, and the Paramount Chief of Techiman, Oseadeyo Akumfi Ameyaw IV respectively.
On Tuesday, October 7th, 2008, at about 2pm, confusion ensued between the two factions when the chief who pays allegiance to Techiman, Nana Obeng Ameyaw Barima, who is the Akyempemhene of Techiman Traditional Area, was celebrating the climax of the annual Fordjour Kese Yam Festival and was riding in a palanquin through the principal streets of Tuobodom.
According to reports gathered by The Chronicle, the other faction that pays allegiance to the Otumfuo, led by their chief, Nana Barfour Asare sent a warning message to the Techiman faction not to extend their celebration to their territory. When approaching the area of the Ashantis, gun shots were fired towards their direction which compelled them to return to their durbar grounds.
The presence of the security, made up of the police and army managed to control the situation in the town. Traditionally, the Paramount Chief of the Techiman Traditional Area, Oseadeyo Akumfi was invited as the Chairman of the occasion. Ironically, the Ashanti faction is closer to the Techiman Township, so the paramount chief had to pass through their territory to the durbar grounds.
When the security were busily controlling the situation in the town, some of the youth belonging to the Ashanti faction barricaded the Techiman-Tamale main road with timber logs, tomato boxes and other materials after being hinted that the Paramount Chief of Techiman was to attend the festival as the Chairman for the occasion.
On the arrival of Osagyefo Akumfi Ameyaw and his entourage, made up of five of his sub-chiefs, they noticed that the road was blocked, so Nana ordered his guards to remove the road blockade, but this led to the firing of gunshots from the opposing faction from all directions, which subsequently injured five people who were with Oseadeyo Akumfi Ameyaw.
However, the Chief of the Ashanti faction, Nana Barfour Asare, argued that Oseadeyo Akumfi and his people were the first to fire gunshots on their way to Tuobodom, and he being the Chief, will not sit down unconcerned for some other chiefs to intimidate him and his people on his land, hence the reaction.
The Techiman Municipal Police Command when contacted claimed that the situation was under control, with the massive presence of a combined Police force the Army.
To ensure peace, the Omanhene of Techiman Traditional Area called an emergency meeting among his sub-chiefs to talk to the youth of the area not to mar the peace in the area, but to allow the law to take its own course.
Interacting with The Chronicle after the emergency meeting, Oseadeyo Akumfi described the incident as a plot to assassinate him by some group of people who he believed were motivated by some high powered personalities in the country.
He said such a calamity was not envisaged by him, or any member of his entourage.

Original_Search_Title: Price of Fresh Tomatoes Crashes in Delta
Headline: Nigeria: Price of Fresh Tomatoes Crashes in Delta
Body_Text:
The price of tomatoes in Asaba, Delta State, has gone down to N6,000 from N7,000, for a large basket according to a survey by the News Agency of Nigeria (NAN).
The reduction was also recorded in other major towns.
It will be recalled that the same quantity of tomatoes, which sold for N11,000 in Asaba, Ughelli and Warri in June crashed to N7,000 in August.
Some sellers, who spoke to NAN , attributed the reduction to the increase in the production of the commodity.
Mrs. Onyebuchi Eluaka, a seller in Asaba, said that tomatoes from the northern part of the country, which were usually scarce between June and September, were now available.
She said that between October and May the production of the commodity was always high.
In spite of the reduction in the price of fresh tomatoes, the price of tin tomatoes is still high in Delta.
Mrs. Eunice Okoli, tin tomatoes seller in Asaba, said that the price of tin tomatoes was still high as a carton of the product (gino) still went for N2, 500 in Asaba.
Meanwhile, a market survey by the Delta Ministry of Economic Planning, which was made available to NAN , showed that the prices of some foodstuff had remained stable in the past one week.
According to the survey, a 100 kg of brown beans, which sold for N20,000 in Asaba last week still sells for the same price.
The survey also showed that three kilogrammes of garri which sold for N220 in Asaba and Sapele last week went for N220.
The price of rice was also stable, as a bag of caprice, which sold for N8,000 in Asaba last week went for N8,000, while in Ughelli and Warri, a bag of caprice sold for N8,100.
The price of yam has not changed as a medium sized tuber of yam, which cost N300 in Ughelli and Warri last week went for N300.
The survey further showed that a kilogramme of beef, which sold for N800 in Agbor, Ughelli and Warri, went for N800, while in Asaba, the same quantity of beef sold for N850.
The stability of prices cover such commodities as frozen fish, ogbono and eggs that sell for N600 for one kilogramme, N300 for a milk cup and N300 for one dozen respectively in Asaba, Agbor and Warri.
(NAN)

Original_Search_Title: Adeyinka Junaid Holding On
Headline: Nigeria: Adeyinka Junaid Holding On
Body_Text:
Lagos — Yinka Junaid, Managing Consultant/CEO Mark Calthers Consulting Limited's foray into the corporate world is a total deviation from his early dream of becoming a film producer. After his initial ambition of studying English language was truncated, he went ahead to study History.
Determined to succeed, he moved to the corporate world where he traversed for the next 13 years, gathering experience. Junaid had always believed in the power of possibility. Today, he sits atop an HR firm that trades in ideas. His clientele are in the big leagues. Recently, he decided to pursue his childhood dream and establish Tommy's House Play, a multi-media production outfit. Yinka Junaid Spoke with FUNKE OLAODE.
In the beginning He was neither born with a silver spoon nor into riches. The only legacy he acquired from his engineer father was quality education. But Adeyinka Junaid is one young man who believes in passing through life fulfilling his childhood ambitions. Meeting him in the cozy room of his Lagos office that Wednesday morning, his smile, warmth and his carriage was simplicity itself. As he recounts every significant stage of his career life, you can't but marvel at the restiveness of youth whose determination has helped to break even against all odds. Who is Yinka Junaid? "I was born in Ile-Ife in Osun State over 40 years ago into the Junaid family of Ijebu-Ode in Ogun State. My father was a mechanical engineer when I was born. His family lineage had been in Ile-Ife long before he settled there. So, I lived all my life in Ile-Ife, which in a way impacted positively on me. Though I didn't grow up within the university setting, we had the best in terms of education. My late father was an educated man which exposed all of us to education quite early. A lot of my peers at Ife then were children of university lecturers and all us went to the same schools. It was a close knit society where everybody knew each other".
"I had my primary education at St. Peters Anglican Primary School, Ile-Ife and St. John's Grammar School, Ile-Ife. The school had produced many great men who are doing well across the globe. After my secondary education, I proceeded to Obafemi Awolowo University Ile-Ife where I graduated with Bachelor of Arts degree in History in 1988. After graduation, I was posted to Otukpo in Benue State for the National Youth Service Corps. I served in a teacher's College. I enjoyed the place and still have life time friends. They are very warm and nice people. It was in Otukpo that I first ate fresh yam dug from the ground and was immediately prepared for pounded yam".
My dream was to be a film producer
With a father as an engineer, what could have pushed the young Junaid to History? "It is funny because History wasn't my first choice. I wanted to read English Language. In fact, it was my first and second choice when I was filling my JAMB form. Then I had flair and still have a flair for writing. I just wanted to read English Language which I believe would boost and enhance my writing skills. But when the admission letter came from JAMB, I saw History. We tried all we could to have it changed but it wasn't possible. That was how I got stuck in History".
"Another inspiration that wanted me to pitch my tent with English Language was that I wanted to be a film producer. Unfortunately, there was no school running that in Nigeria. I applied to schools outside the country but being from a very humble background, there was no way I could pursue that dream"
Junaid had been raised to face the challenges of life and never allow a humble background to be an impediment to attaining higher height. "By offering me History, my dream was almost killed. For me, I always love to be the best and never allow any challenge to weigh me down. Ultimately I knew that my life was in my hand. So I summoned up courage and settled down to study History which JAMB imposed on me".
I was jobless for two years
Lagos has always been a center of attraction where millions of Nigerians throng everyday for greener pasture. Armed with a Bachelors of Arts degree, Junaid saw Lagos as a land of opportunity where he could realize his dreams. "There was no job in Ile-Ife when I finished my youth service, which prompted my decision to move down to Lagos in 1991. And for two years I was jobless doing nothing. The first one year, I didn't do anything and the second year I was driving danfo on the streets of Lagos". What was his experience like as a graduate behind a danfo wheel? "I have no regret because I was jobless for one year and saw this opportunity to drive a bus and make money. Of course, I jumped at it and I was making money. Believe me, driving a danfo is a money making venture. I didn't own the bus but I was making 200 percent more than the usual amount I was supposed to deliver everyday. And you know what? I was never broke."
"I enjoyed it because I wasn't one of those drivers on the queues. My usual route was Ojuelegba to CMS. It was strenuous and at the same time nice. If you want to sample people's opinion about what is going on in Nigeria today, go and drive a danfo where you have different people. Inside a danfo bus at any point in time, you would have well educated people with different academic qualifications. After my experience, I found out that when you see people in a danfo or molue you don't have to look down on them. They are the less fortunate members of the society".
My foray into the corporate world paved the way for my success
The "I can do" spirit which he imbibed right from his childhood paved the way for his success. After his sojourn as a bus driver, Junaid got a job with Ranx Xerox as Copy Service Executive where his career took a turn for the better for the next ten years. "I joined Xerox H. S. Nigeria Limited in 1992 as copy service executive. In those days, Rank Xerox had fleets of photocopying centers. I was actually assisting the manager who ran the centers all over the country. After four months on the job, the management of Xerox called me and said they had been monitoring my performance since I joined the company. They said there was an opening in Human Resource department. That was how I was elevated as Personnel Support Executive. I worked in Xerox for the next 10 years contributing my own quota to the upliftment of the company. I went through various departments and units. Whatever it is that I am today Xerox laid the foundation for me because I went through a lot of training both locally and internationally".
Having been exposed to the corporate world, Yinka Junaid's move after Xerox in 2000 was Linkserve, an internet service provider in Nigeria where he spent the next two years. "I joined Linkserve as Corporate Sales Manager. I later pioneered the Abuja branch of the company where I served as its first branch manager. I was transferred back to Lagos and became the Special Project Manager".
Not done with gathering corporate experiences, in 2002, he moved to Vic Lawrence & Associates, human resource and recruitment consultancy outfit as Senior Consultant/Group Head, Strategy & Business Support. And for the next one and half year, he was in that company displaying his wealth of experience in the identification of strategic business issues and drivers in clients' organizations. Effective implementation of management development solutions which include: identification and analysis of strategic needs, research, design and development content and material and delivery and facilitation of management development programmes.
I opted out of paid employment
He was lucky to have worked in various reputable organizations where he displayed his intellectual ability. Junaid decided to opt out of paid employment to become his own boss. "I opted out of paid service in 2003 and founded my own outfit called Mark Calthers Consulting Limited, an HR firm in 2004. "We are into management development and training, e-recruitment of all calibers of staff of various organizations. We are also into document authentification. We have relationships with all the universities, polytechnics and other institutions across the country. How has it been in the last four years? "Well, like any other new business it was tough initially. You know funding the office, getting equipment to work with and so on. We thank God that things have picked up. We have some credible organizations that believe in us. We work with Nigeria NLNG, OANDO, Addax Petroleum, Diamond Bank etc. We are still growing and I believe with our strong team and fresh ideas the sky is our limit".
Fulfilling my childhood dreams
Junaid has always had a vision that guided his career pursuit. With the success story of Mark Calthers Consulting in the last four years, he is ready to pursue his childhood dreams. "I decided to pursue my early dream and that is why I established Tommy's Play House. It is a full production house for event management, film/Television production, multi-media productions and broadcast equipment rentals. Our vision is to have a world class production. When you watch DSTV you would be amazed at the quality of production. And that is what our company is trying to achieve. We have already started with a programme called "Beacons of Islam" which kicked off before the Ramadan. We thank God today that it was a success because it is the first Islamic programme of it's type. It is a magazine programme that showcases the lives and successes of prominent Moslems in the society. Another programme we are working on is called "The Council" which is a satirical expo on Nigerian society. It is a programme that looks at Nigerian problems, issues in a satirical manner and proffer solutions to them".
Considering all the challenges, did he think he would still achieve his dream? "I believe in holding on to my dream. I believe if you want something so much you would work towards achieving it. Somewhere, somehow, someday you will achieve it. I don't believe anything is impossible. The advent of Tommy's Play House was just like my consulting outfit we started from the scratch. We have not gotten to where we are going yet. In whatever we do, we do it with quality as our watchword and perfection as the yardstick. With that you are laying foundation for substance because if you do what you are supposed to and do it well, lucky breaks will come".
Managing the two outfits together isn't a problem. According to him, he has already built a very strong team to oversee the affairs of the companies. "In both organizations, we have credible, qualified and competent personnel running these organizations. My own is just to see to the overall supervision and strategy of the organization".
My wife is a pillar in my life
Junaid as a corporate player is also a family man. He admits his life is structured around his family. He talks passionately his children and wife of 13 years whom he describes as his pillar. "I have been married for 13 years to my wife, Funke, a lawyer who I met 15 years ago. The Union is blessed with three boys. Tomiwa is 12, Damola is 10 and Yinka is 6. I met my wife in Lagos when she came back from England where she was studying. Honestly, behind every successful man is a supportive wife. My wife is a wonderful woman who has been supportive of my dream. She has been the pillar and my strength when I was weak. She stood by me when things were rough and never made me inadequate even when the chips were down. My children too are lovely wonderful boys. They have been supportive in their own little ways. At times, they go with me to the location and stay in the background. I don't joke with my family and that is why the little free time I have I spend with them".
Believing in power of possibility
Yinka Junaid had always believed in himself and the power of possibility, the assumption that has worked for him over the years. "Life, they say, is not a bed of roses, if you are not from an affluent background, you have to work your way to the top. I have an assumption that has worked for me over the years and that is the power of possibility. We all need to work hard. There is nothing new and the principles are there and they work everytime. If you work hard, definitely one day the doors shall be opened on to you. Work, pray, believe in God and don't take No for an answer. You should have a vision and know what you want to be in life and seriously work at it. If you do that I believe the sky will be your limit".

Original_Search_Title: Task Force - Hawkers Flee From Items in Zone 3
Headline: Nigeria: Task Force - Hawkers Flee From Items in Zone 3
Body_Text:
Hawkers in Wuse Zone 3 market in Abuja, yesterday fled from their items after seeing the Task Force men of the Abuja Environmental Protection Board (AEPB).
The force task had earlier received information of continual disturbance and illegal erection of stalls in the market which consequently creates discomfort for traders operating legally.
The force confirmed that hawkers equally roast yams in the market, raising smoke that pollute the environment.
Mr Joseph Bathlomew, who led the team of armed policemen and members of the force, said when they entered the market, most of the hawkers were taken unawares and took to their heels, abandoning assorted items including cooking-pots, tripods and half-roasted tuber of yams.
He added that some hawkers in the market have devised a new strategy to avoid arrest and prosecution.
"A ten passenger NISSAN bus with registration number XF 798 ABJ was also in the market loaded with interior decorations displayed for sale, but the owner of the items was nowhere to be found."
Mr Sani Amara, a senior staff of the force, added that the owner must have sighted their vehicle at a distance and disappeared. "All efforts to ignite the engine proved abortive while the steering wheel was locked, but next time we will tow such vehicles."
When City News interviewed one Eno Grace Victor on the effects of hawkers activities on legal trades in the market, she said illegal erections have blocked their markets but there is little they can say because the hawkers are familiar faces. "The setting of fire in the market to roast yams is disgusting in an environment like this.

Original_Search_Title: My Four Babies are Compensation for Lost Family Members
Headline: Nigeria: My Four Babies are Compensation for Lost Family Members
Body_Text:
September 5, 2008 will always be a day to remember in the lives of Mr and Mrs Abosede Ojo: They made history in Lagos State Island Maternity Home by delivering quadruplets. The quadruplets, a boy and three girls, came as a surprise to the parents and the hospital management who were expecting triplets as revealed by scan.
The management's joy knew no bounds as the medical director, Dr. Olumuyiwa Solanke, apex chief matron, Mrs Abike Ayodele, Dr. Britto and midwife staff nurse, Mrs Adeola Koiki that took the delivery and the neo-natal matron and nurses were working round the clock to make sure all was well with the "miracle babies" as they were called by the apex matron. Two of the quadruplet in the incubator.
When asked why they were being called "miracle babies," Mrs Ayodele said: "The mother was referred to Island General Hospital from Orile Agege General Hospital on the account that she was having pregnancy induced hypertension and she had been draining for few hours before coming to the hospital and the fact that she was carrying triplets.
Normally, in such case, she should have gone through caesarean operation. But to our surprise, she had already dilated up to nine centimetres that we had no choice than to take her on normal vagina delivery immediately. The other two of the quadruplet.
"The miracle there was that the delivery did not give her stress because the babies were coming out by themselves and it was when we were expecting the placenta to come out that we saw the legs of the fourth baby which scan itself did not reveal. The weight of the babies is another miracle, the first baby, a boy, came out at 10.12 p.m. weighing two kilogrammes, and the three girls followed at 10.25, 10.30 and 11.00 p.m respectively with 1.5 kilogrammes each. To me and all that took the delivery, it is miraculous and right now they are no longer on fluid. They can feed well from their mother's breast like other babies."
Solanke, the medical director, also spoke on the delivery. His words: "We received the gifts of these babies with joy and give glory to God because nothing could have happened without Him. The rate of multiple pregnancy which we know here is just about 20-30 per 1000. Quadruplet is even rarer because we have one in 500 deliveries. In the case of Bose, the mother of the babies, she is too lucky because God was behind her to have delivered the babies by herself because if she had not come here, the mode of delivery for quadruplets is elective caesarean operation but Orile-Agege General Hospital that referred her to Island does not have neo-natal care unit.
This is the miracle of God and since this one happened like this, we have to praise Him and let everybody know that even though you are infertile now, you don't know what God is going to do for you. Luckily for her, their weight is okay. People of Lagos State should come out and assist them because breastfeeding and taking care of four babies at a time is not easy especially for the couple's status.
"I even learnt the parents live in a room self-contained apartment which I know will not be good enough to keep the babies that are miracles to all of us in Lagos State. Though government had sent their representative here and I know they will do something about it, the government and the people of Nigeria, not only Lagos State, should come to their aid. The hospital management has already written off their bills. This is our first quadruplets delivery which even came through normal delivery."
Sunday Vanguard also spoke with Britto who was there at the delivery room with Mrs Koiki, the midwife, and he said this was the first time he would be partaking in the delivery of quadruplets. He said: "It is just God that was at work because the delivery was wonderful and I think twining is common in Ekiti State where the couples come from which I learnt is because of one particular yam they eat. The labour ward should be given kudos and the neo-natal ward because the survival of the babies depends on God and those in neo-natal."
The lucky mother of the babies, Mrs. Abosede Ojo, from Itapa Ekiti, was full of smiles when Sunday Vanguard visited the neo natal ward where the babies were kept. She said she had to thank God for giving her the babies because she knew they had come to fulfil a purpose. She said when she went for scan and was told she was carrying three babies, she was down in her spirit on how she was going to take care of the babies with no experience as the babies were the first whereas her job could hardly feed two people not to talk of five, saying now that they are six in the family, it is just God that could help them.
Abosede who said she got married in 2006 at thirty five spoke about her experience. Her words: "I was afraid when I heard that I was carrying three babies. I was a fashion designer before but when I had accident sometimes ago, it affected my right leg and that is why I am limping. I couldn't operate sewing machine again and I had to learn how to cut bread nylon and that is what I do. I started thinking round the clock how I was going to survive with the news but thank God my husband followed me to do the scan and he talked to me that God will help us out. Now that the babies are four, I give glory to God.
It was from the scan place that we were advised not to go back to the private hospital where we registered before and I went to register at Orile-Agege General Hospital. I was told I would be operated on but when I got to the hospital, I was referred to Island General Hospital since I had started dilating." When I was told they are four, I started thinking again but people around me consoled me that it is the work of God and I believe God will surprise me. God have even started with us because the Island hospital management has written off our bills and I just want to thank them for their love. They have not allowed me to feel anything. Though I know we will not be staying here forever, I know well-meaning Nigerians will come to our aid for the children to have a better life."
The husband, Mr Oluwole Ojo, 40, from Ijurin Ekiti in Ekiti, said the news came to him as a surprise Father of the children, Oluwole Ojo, and was happy but at the same time worried about how to take care of the family of six with his meagre salary as he said he is a level six assistant executive officer with Itire/Ikate Local Government Development Council. He said he was relying on God to take care of the babies as he heard from Him even before the delivery that He had finished the work and that he should not be afraid. "I started thanking God while she was in delivery room because I knew He would fulfil His promise.
Apex Matron of Lagos Island Hospital, Mrs. Abike Ayodele: Now that the babies are four, I believe that God will send people that will help us. Though we have not got any assistance except from the hospital that wrote off our bills, I believe God will touch the hearts of government and people of Lagos State and Nigeria as a whole. I already know now that it is not easy from the pampers I buy. What they use in a day is what one baby will use in one week. It is quite a big one that I alone can not cope with. I also believe they are compensation from God for four people I lost in my family last year. I lost my two sisters, my mother and my nephew. It was not easy but God has compensated me. I call the babies Okiki which means fame," he added.
On whether he was going to have more babies since this is just their firstborn, Ojo said, "I will run". Run to where? "I will run away from the mother!" Why? "Until we do family planning."

Original_Search_Title: Wives, Children of Man With 86 Wives Protest in Minna
Headline: Nigeria: Wives, Children of Man With 86 Wives Protest in Minna
Body_Text:
Fifty wives and 23 children of Alhaji Bello Masaba on Thursday stormed the Niger State secretariat in Minna to protest what they called the "illegal detention" of their bread winner.
The aggrieved wives and children arrived the state secretariat at about 10.15a.m in three buses and carried placards with various inscriptions. Some of the placards read: "we are not married to the state sharia commission, but to Mohammed Bello," "Niger sharia commission should detain us with Mohammed Bello," our children's future looks bleak with our husband in detention," "we are not under Nigeria laws and not under Etsu Yahaya victimisation" and "we are legally married to Mohammed Bello" among others.
The first wife of Bello Masaba, Hajiya Hawawu, and the second wife, Hajiya Raliat, who led the protest, told our correspondent that they were at the secretariat to register their protest to the state Attorney- General and commissioner for Justice over the inhuman treatment being meted out to their husband.
Hajiya Raliat, who claimed to be 76 years, denied the accusation that they were living in inhuman condition. She stated that "I am 76 years old, yet I look younger than my age, we, the household members of Mohammed Bello, are even enjoying more than most so called big men and top government functionaries.
"On daily basis, we slaughter one cow, cook two bags of rice, two bags of beans, two bags of gari and two bags of yam flour, do you see any sign of suffering among we the wives that are here or the children," she asked.
The spokesperson for the children, Fatimat, who claimed to be a 'Jam bite', told our correspondent that their father was being detained illegally. Fatimat, who said she is 19 years old, disclosed that apart from the 86 wives and the over 130 children which their father catered for, he also takes care of over 100 other dependents. She explained that the future of the wives and children as well as other dependents was now in jeopardy, following the detention of their bread winner.
According to her, "we don't want the state sharia commission to assist us; we rely solely on Allah and our father for survival." Though, efforts by our correspondent to get the reaction of the state Attorney- General, Barr. Adamu Usman, proved abortive, the permanent secretary in the ministry of Justice, Barr. Ndagi Usman, said since the matter is in court, he could not comment on it. His words: "we are aware of their visit, but we will plead with them to give peace a chance and let them go back to Bida."
The wives and children of Masaba later left the secretariat when they learnt that a detachment of mobile police men was being sent to disperse them. Bello Masaba, who was arrested in the early hours of Monday, was ordered to be remanded in prison custody by an upper sharia court in Minna.

Original_Search_Title: Ho Health Insurance Scheme Intensifies Educational Programme
Headline: Ghana: Ho Health Insurance Scheme Intensifies Educational Programme
Body_Text:
Ho — The management, of the Ho Municipal Health Insurance Scheme (NHIS), has intensified its educational activities to increase the membership of the scheme, particularly as the chiefs and people of Asogli are preparing to celebrate their annual Yam Festival, with the opening of 22 new registration centres in the municipality.
The management of the scheme observed that more people registered during the Yam Festival, hence their decision to take advantage and organise a membership-drive programme in the municipality.
The Scheme Manager of the Ho NHIS, Mr. Prosper Pi-Bansah, made this known when the Management donated 100 T-shirts, specially made to promote activities of the NHIS in the area, to the Asogli Yam Festival Planning Committee, and a cash of GH¢800.
Presenting the items, Mr. Pi-Bansah said the gesture formed part of the social responsibility to the people in the community where the Scheme operates, noting that the donation was a long term investment, and should not be seen as a mere donation to the Asogli people, since the move was strategic to the growth and development of the scheme.
He explained that a similar donation made towards the festival last year, yielded positive results, as 18,000 people were registered under the scheme during the festivities, through a well-designed programme, in collaboration with the Planning Committee of the festival.
Mr. Pi-Bansah disclosed that so far management of the scheme had paid GH¢1,600 to card bearing people, who were treated free at the various health facilities in the municipality, adding that people should realise that sound health was key to wealth creation, as free medical services would help people who would not have money to attend hospital, and so they should do so without fear.
The Scheme Manager used the opportunity, and appealed to the chiefs and people of Asogli, to educate their subjects on the importance of the scheme, so as to enable them to register to derive the needed benefit, which he observed, would definitely improve their health status.
Receiving the items, the Vice Chairman of the Asogli Yam Festival Planning Committee, Mr. David Bansah, thanked the management of the Ho Municipal Scheme for the gesture, and said it would not only promote activities of the scheme, but enhance the organisation and celebration of the festival.
He noted that the management of the scheme, continued to support the Asogli Traditional Council during its annual Yam Festival, adding that such efforts were highly appreciated by the council, and said the council would also play its role to ensure that more people registered under the scheme.
Mr. Bansah lauded the activities of the scheme, to freely register the aged in the municipality, a move he said, clearly demonstrated concern for the elderly in the society, and urged other organisations to emulate the management of the NHIS, in order to ensure accelerated development.

Original_Search_Title: Poor People Can Live Longer - Expert
Headline: Nigeria: Poor People Can Live Longer - Expert
Body_Text:
Poor people can assured to live longer than their wealthy counterparts.
This was the declaration made by renowned medical expert and managing partner, Eko Corporation Plc owners of Eko Hospital, Dr. Sunny Kuku(OFR) last week at a seminar in Lagos .
Delivering a paper titled "Your Lifestyle and Longevity", Kuku said that the major controllers of a healthy life are diet, exercise, non-smoking, relaxation, stress and regular medical check-up.
According to Kuku, while most poor Nigerians are complaining about their inability to afford most of the salivating fatty foods usually eaten by rich people, the carbohydrate foods such as garri, beans, yam, eba, fufu and so many others which they are left with is what is actually needed by the body in order to attain a long life.
"Carbohydrates should represent about 60-70 per cent of the diet which is the opposite of what most people believe and preach. So, poor people are lucky because those foods often regarded as poor man's foods are the best".
The doctor advised that fatty foods should be avoided as much as possible because only about 15 to 20 per cent of fat was needed in the diet.
"It is the fats in the diet that leads to obesity", he said.
On protein, Kuku stated that although protein such as fish, meat, chicken etc are essential for body building, the recommended content of proteins in the diet is just 10 to 15 per cent.

Original_Search_Title: Ramadan - Prices of Perishable Items Rise in Sokoto
Headline: Nigeria: Ramadan - Prices of Perishable Items Rise in Sokoto
Body_Text:
As Ramadan fasting enters its sixteenth day, the price of perishable items has increased Sokoto.
LEADERSHIP investigation showed that prices of commodities had by about 95 per cent in the last two days in sokoto metropolis, in spite of warning by scholars of the importance of the month of fasting.
For example, a tuber of yam goes for about N400, instead of N250, while a bucket of potatoes which formerly sold at between N350 was sold at N550.
Other essential commodities which had experienced increases in price are, a bag of rice which now sells N8,500 against N7,800, while a bottle of groundnut oil formerly selling for N260 has increased to about N300 within the period of the commencement of the fasting.
It would be recalled that the Sokoto State government, under Governor Aliyu Magatakarda, had few weeks to the fasting, ordered for grains and assorted commodities worth N1.7 billion as part of the government's effort to cushion the suffering of the people during the Ramadan fasting.
It also created over 80 free feeding centre across the 23 local government areas in the state.
Malam Garba Malami, a petty trader at the Sokoto market, told LEADERSHIP that the hike was due to the demand of the commdities.
He accused middlemen of being behind the increase.
Malami stressed that the only way to tackle the problem was to involve the Federal Government, calling on wealthy individuals who engaged in holding commodities in order to gain huge profit to stop the act.

Original_Search_Title: A Night At the Hilltop
Headline: Nigeria: A Night At the Hilltop
Body_Text:
The Abuja Night Life was at the Kesten Hilltop last night. It is located along CBN/Area One road. The location makes it the best isolated home-at-night in Abuja.
At 8pm, the ANL took the front seat, looking at the goings-on as usual. Flashy cars: Camry 2008, Honda End of Discussion, assorted jeeps, FG marked vehicles, those of NASS, name them, began to troop in. The occupants are equal - one to one or two to two. That was the ratio. Or unmathematically, one man, one woman; two men, two women. Each pair walked majestically to the hill and selected the hill steps that would suit their tastes. Shortly vehicles filled the parking space to the brim.
The Abuja Night Life turned to the back, where it was happening. Like the name implied, the hilltop is beautifully decorated with step-like-rock formation perfect indeed to excite any tourist or a pastoral poet. Each step seemed to play host to a category of people, but it is a matter the ANL would dig into soon.
The ground floor (if I'm correct) was hotter, because I did not climb up. Tiv delicacies like pounded yam, nkwobi and fish pepper soup were served. Behind situated shades where beer boozers downloaded their things. The prices were not high.
Then came the appointed time. While the ANL was introspecting why an isolated hill has become a beehive of activities, the popular Tiv lyric Swangeee filtered into the scene.
Six beautiful, gracefully tall and elegant girls, about twenty years of age, walked into the stage. Everywhere stood still. Swangeee…. Swangeee took over, all became onlookers.
They stood in pairs and began the turn-bend-twist-rotate dancing steps. They wore the same colour of wrappers, tops and looked alike. The decency in their dress attracted the ANL. The crew wondered over the contradiction of the beauty of the girls and the presumed 'danger' zone of Kesten.
The shock then. Around 10pm, the tempo of dancing changed. The dancers began twist-dancing steps too romantic to bear. Then more people started trooping in. Some however climbed the hilltop. The ANL observed they were moving one man, one woman. It was frightening but they continued the journey upper. Some climbed with packets of cigarette, others with bottles of beer. They moved to the area the ANL could not understand.
Back to the stage. The beating was tense, the dancers were tenser. The crew of the ANL appreciated that. At midnight, the dancers began to display. They twisted swangeee until their wrappers uniformly went off. They wore short skirts that barely covered their hot legs. They increased the tempo of the dance, rotating their waists beyond human understanding. Shortly the skirts rolled off their bodies and gave in to 'tights'. People were carried away.
The dance continued. The dancers romantically removed their tops and showcased their bras and other ones.
To avoid temptation, the ANL vacated the hot seat but was still tempted on his way out. Many 'corner-corner' things or 'sweet sweet' activities were littered along the exit.

Original_Search_Title: Residents Warned Against Ritual Killers
Headline: Nigeria: Residents Warned Against Ritual Killers
Body_Text:
Following the miraculous delivery of a mother and her 10-month-old baby from the claws of ritual killers, passengers have been told to be on the alert to the craftiness and antics of disguised cultists commercial transport operators.
Fatsuma Hassan, 35, from Bula village in Akko local government area, accosted a supposed commercial motorcyclist at Shongo Motor Park in the state capital with the intention of going to Checheniya quarters, only to find herself on ritual slaughter slab.
The bike man, after a minor check on his plugs, brought out a N500 note asking if Fatsuma could assist with change and as soon as she sighted the money, she lost control of her senses, mounting the motorbike like a zombie.
She recovered to find herself standing before a huge black gate, while she tried to protest being brought there, she was forced through the gate into the dark house.
In the darkroom were three other victims; two fully dressed teenage girls suspected to be fried yam and egg vendors and a stark nude lady with shaven head, armpits and private part.
While she had been forcefully undressed, hands tied behind her and was being shaved like the other lady, her crying baby boy crawled up to two open barrels and fiddled with their liquid contents.
Just then an imposingly bulky woman emerged from an adjourning room and queried the motorcyclist, "didn't I tell you never to bring me a nursing mother? Look at the nuisance this small boy has become!". "Untie her now, let her take her son and go" she added.
Fatsuma said she was led on to another bike outside the gate and she again lost her senses until she found herself at Dadinkowa motor park from where she eventually traced her way to her father's house at Unguwa Missau where she suffered convulsive feat for some time.
Father of the lady, Hassan Dahiru, who warned passengers to be conscious of ritual' activities, said they were thankful to God for the deliverance and were more interested in Fatsuma's full recovery and return to normal life rather than reporting to the police.
Contacted for confirmation, the Police PPRO, ASP Mohammed Baba Yola, said no such issue had been reported to the station.

Original_Search_Title: Flood Cuts Off Communities, Ministries in Lafia
Headline: Nigeria: Flood Cuts Off Communities, Ministries in Lafia
Body_Text:
Many residents of   Lafia,   the   Nasarawa State capital, including government establishments are now cut off from the city centre due to flood  often caused by  heavy downpour.
Most of these residents and establishments, which  are mostly  within Lafia  metropolis along Tudun Kauri and environs,  have to use alternative roads while their vehicles are parked mostly outside their premises before getting to their destinations.
The establishments affected are   federal, state ministries and parastatals like the Federal Road Safety Commission (FRSC), Federal Ministry of Education, the state Ministry of Education,  Tourism Board and Scholarship Board, Nasara Water, among others.
The flood also affected communities such as Agyaragu Tofa, which is one of the yam havens in the state, while residents close to the NAFDAC office long Makurdi road are also crying out for immediate action so as not to be affected.
Resident of the area who spoke to leadership recount their losses as the flood oftentimes got into their bedrooms and spoil many of their belongings.
Efforts to speak to the Commissioner for Works and Transport, Salihu Ofoku, proved abortive.
But reliable sources told leadership that the road had become a subject of controversy between the state government and Lafia local government on who is responsible for the maintenance of the road.

Original_Search_Title: Flood Cuts Off Communities, Ministries in Lafia
Headline: Nigeria: Flood Cuts Off Communities, Ministries in Lafia
Body_Text:
Lafia — Flood occasioned by persistent heavy down pour has cut off several communities in Lafia, Nasarawa State. Also cut off by the flood which is threatening residents of Tudun Kauri, are offices of the federal and state ministries as well as parastatals which include those of the Federal Road Safety Commission (FRSC),
Federal Ministry of Education, Nigerian Metrological Agency, state ministry of education, tourism board, scholarship board, Nasara Water Board, among many others. Agyaragu Tofa, which is also one of the yam havens in the state, is among the communities cut off by the deplorable state of the road.
Staff of the affected ministries and agencies including commissioners, permanent secretaries and other top personnel had trouble getting to their respective offices. The water stagnated at a point between the residence of the chairman of the State Independent Electoral Commission, Mallam Musa Agyo, and that of staff of the Daughters of Mary Schools preventing motorists, pedestrians and even motor cyclists from getting to their destinations.
Daily Trust learnt on a visit to the area that the water stagnated several days after a heavy down pour because of lack of drainage system which would have allowed it easy passage. Staff of the affected agencies and ministries had to go in circle through the access link by the Nigeria Customs Service office to get to their respective destinations and even that, according to a staff of one of the ministries, Joshua Sabo, is not easy as the access road is already deplorable due to constant use.
Top officials of the state urban development board and the director personnel management of Lafia local government area were not on hand for comment when Daily Trust visited the two offices yesterday. However, a source in one of the affected ministries said the situation has been reported to the appropriate authority for action.

Original_Search_Title: 10-Month-Old Baby Rescues Mum From Ritualists
Headline: Nigeria: 10-Month-Old Baby Rescues Mum From Ritualists
Body_Text:
Lagos — It was divine intervention a as a 10-month-old baby in Gombe, has saved his mother from being murdered and used for rituals.
According to the mother, Fatsuma Hassan, 35, resident of Bula village, near Gombe, she and her son had left home for Gombe last week.
She said: "When we arrived Gombe and disembarked at Shongo motor park, a commercial motorcyclist approached me and enquired where I was going, I told him Chenchenya area.
"But as I was about to climb the bike, he removed N500 note, requesting for change and immediately I set my eyes on the note, I went off my senses.
"The next thing, I saw myself in front of a house with black gate and was forced to enter it."
The victim said that while in the house, she was forcefully undressed and her ampits, genital and head completely shaved: "However, my baby boy, who was dropped on the floor became restless, crawling from one end of the house to the other and crying ceaselessly".
"At a point, he was crawling toward two containers of liquid substance, an action that infuriated a woman in the house, who then ordered that my son be restrained".
