This is a funny interpretation when one realises that Gakem is the first boundary town between the North and East, captured by Federal troops at the early stages of the Civil War, and Kuapu in Ibo means "Pack and go".

Original_Search_Title: The Other Side of Madalla Market
Headline: Nigeria: The Other Side of Madalla Market
Body_Text:
Madalla Market located in Suleja, Niger State, is one of the many weekly markets which attracts people from different parts of Abuja and Niger State. Weekly Trust visited the market and brought you the other side of this sprawling trading place.
Like any other weekly market in the FCT, the Madalla market is a rendezvous for both traders and customers. It attracts customers and traders from different parts of Abuja, Suleja in Niger state and other places like Kaduna and Jos. However, for residents of the area where the market is located, the market has its disadvantages. Apart from traders' encroachment into the main road which narrows into Zuba, it also causes traffic congestion on the Abuja-Madalla road.
People do travel from Kano bringing foodstuff like rice, fish in trucks as well as from Jos bringing yams, rams and goats. People from the eastern part of the country are always around and in the Madala market on its market days. They come from as far as Akwa Ibom, Enugu and Abia states. They also come with different types of food stuff including crayfish, snails, utazi, and okazi amongst others.
The variety of foodstuffs and its affordability in this market are the major attraction to people.
Interestingly, egusi, guinea corn, millet, ogbonno, maize and vegetable oil are preserved in the market for all seasons, which Weekly Trust learnt are cheaper than in any other market in the town. It was also learnt that even on non market days, traders are seen more relaxed in their kiosks making their sales, particularly those selling pork meat.
Since many people come from far and near to patronise the market, the space there is too small and is always chocking especially on market days for motorists plying that route. The situation is such that people are prone to accidents at the slightest cause.
Weekly Trust learnt that lives lost in accidents every market day due to the congestion.
Most motorists expressed concern over the unavoidable traffic jam on the road especially areas bordering the market.
They attributed the problem to lack of enough space for traders and parking spaces for vehicles.
The Market's Supervising Officer (Sarkin Kasuwa), Idris Tanko Salisu Madalla, also attributed the congestion to lack of adequate space for the traders to operate.
He said, "the location of the market is dangerous as it often causes accidents especially on market days."
Salisu Madalla said an appeal has been made to the Federal Capital Territory and the Niger State government to fence the market to stop people from selling close to the road.
Treasurer of the Suleja Local Government Area, Alhaji Lawal Attahiru he said that the Local Government had recently gone into partnership with a Public Private Partnership firm (PPP).
He also revealed that within a few month's time, work will commence for the rehabilitation of traders selling by the road side of the market. Meanwhile, there is no hope of moving the market from its present location.
He also said security personnel are doing everything possible to ensure the safe passage of motorists plying the Madalla road.
A trader who simply said her name which Blessing, complained of the non-availability of enough space in the market.
She attributed to the increasing number of traders, saying they have over-stretched the little space available and pleaded with the market authorities to expand the market.
Apart from the population, Mark Agwu, a trader, said pedestrians, water cart pushers and wheel barrow operators also contribute to the congestion in the market.
"The activities of these people cause congestion on the road and these often leads to frequent accidents."
He said, series of reports have been made to the authority concerned with little or no action. Agwu also accused the market's union leaders of wrongful allocation of stalls to traders along the road. This, he said, has been causing accidents.
Another trader, Ahmadu Musa, suggested the construction of a pedestrian fly-over as a solution to the traffic jam and frequent accidents.

Original_Search_Title: Weeping Before Rivers Panel
Headline: Nigeria: Weeping Before Rivers Panel
Body_Text:
Former Rivers Governor Celestine Omehia last Wednesday wept before the Rivers Truth and Reconciliation Commission which sat in Abuja to receive testimonies from politicians of the state's origin. Omehia, while recalling the kidnap of his mother, broke down and wept profusely.
According to the former governor, who was sacked by the Supreme Court, Governor Rotimi Amaechi had his (Omehia's) father arrested when he (Amaechi) was the Speaker of the Rivers State House of Assembly over chieftaincy and land matters. Though Omehia told the commission that he was not certain of the role Amaechi played during the abduction of his mother, he dismissed any attempt at reconciliation between him and the governor whom he said has the 'knife and the yam' in any move to end hostilities.
"It is something that himself, his wife and my wife would sit down and talk about. If we do it in public, opinions that could be destructive could come in. He is busy now, when he is ready, he has the knife and the yam. When he is ready, let him call me," the former governor who is a cousin to Amaechi said.
The former governor also denied having any relationship with cultists just as he debunked claims of paying ransom to secure the release of his mother who was kidnapped by militants.
From the testimony of Omehia, it is very clear that the commission has a long way to go in unearthing the personalities and factors responsible for Rivers' bloody past. The truth may be unfolding now but the prospects of reconciliation seem threatened by clouds of irreconcilable differences

Original_Search_Title: River State Drama
Headline: Nigeria: River State Drama
Body_Text:
Lagos — WHEN Doctors Peter Odili and Abiye Sekibo, one a former Governor of Rivers State and the other a former Minister of Transport, appeared at the Truth & Reconciliation Commission (TRC) earlier on this week, their supporters clapped enthusiastically whenever they felt that their heroes were performing well.
These bouts of exuberance irritated Justice Kayode Esho, the esteemed veteran jurist who is chairing the TRC; and he sternly and repeatedly informed the enthusiastic clappers that the proceedings were "NOT a drama".
I was there when all this was happening; and I must say, with all due respect to Esho, that while his desire to avoid trivialization is totally laudable, a drama is precisely what the TRC's proceedings have become.
And it's not just an ordinary little forgettable drama about minor issues either. It is a huge, wide-ranging drama of epic proportions, one that revolves around major household names who have wielded enormous amounts of influence, acquired lurid reputations and attracted a plethora of alarming murder, mayhem, militancy, cultism and corruption allegations.
There is, undoubtedly, something immensely theatrical about a public forum in which famous protagonists with colourful personalities and their accusers step into the spotlight, recite their scripts, sling tons of mud at each other and assure the Gallery that their opponents are lying through their teeth.
The TRC - which was set up by Rotimi Amaechi, the current Rivers State Governor, and is being televised live from Abuja and Port Harcourt - boils down to an ensemble of star actors - and a vast cast of fascinating supporting characters (fans, foes, lawyers) - providing the nation with juicy gossip and multiple storylines that relate to long-running sagas.
If this free entertainment isn't a soap opera scenario, I don't know what is!
It is also worth noting that the TRC hearings are not just a soap opera. They can also be described as no less than THREE different types of drama.
In addition to the soap opera angle, there is the WhoDunnit angle, "WhoDunnits" being a UK nickname for novels, films or plays that challenge audiences to rack their brains, examine several complex and often misleading clues, unravel mysteries and decide who committed a particular crime.
Vanguard readers who aren't familiar with British pidgin English might wish to know that "Dunnit" is an ungrammatical version of "Did It".
At any rate, when witnesses take to the TRC stage and confidently tell the world that Odili, Sekibo and other folks are personally or indirectly responsible for a number of heinous assaults on humanity, the guys in the "dock" vigorously deny the charges that are being levelled against them.
And, given the shortage of concrete evidence (much of the proceedings revolve around hearsay), it is left to we onlookers to temporarily assume the roles of police detectives and decide, largely on the basis of our instincts, WhoDunnit...ie, whether the so-called Villains are guilty or innocent.
Even if we conclude that they are innocent, we still have a headache on our hands because outrages such as arson, decimated communities and political assassinations have certainly scarred the existences of many Rivers people since l999; and we have to try to work out who the real culprits were.
Meanwhile, in addition to the soap opera and WhoDunnit angles, there is the Tragedy angle...as in it is absolutely tragic that Amaechi is now so much at loggerheads with his former mentor (Odili) and former colleagues (Sekibo et al) that he felt moved to establish a forum in which they can be discredited.
Some observers have - with Amaechi's bitter rift with his previous "family" in mind - gone as far as calling the TRC "a circus" and "a farce" - which is (abject apologies to the decent and distinguished Justice Esho!) yet another type of drama...and an especially unserious type of drama at that.
I find it very difficult to write about matters relating to Rivers State Governments, past and present, because I am emotionally involved..
I genuinely like most of the leading players; and when they are fighting, I go through massive emotional traumas, wake up crying every morning and find it easier to sit on the fence and try to calm everyone down than take sides.
But there are times when it is necessary to abandon cowardly diplomatic stances and take the risk of antagonizing friends who are not going in the right direction. And I feel obliged to point out that Amaechi has the yam and the knife and should temper his power with graciousness and gratitude.
Amaechi would not be Governor today if Odili hadn't loved him and consistently promoted him. OK, so they fell out last year. But what's a few months of quarrel compared to at least 2 decades of assistance?
True Peace and Reconciliation will only be possible if Amaechi displays a sincere willingness to become a leader who can forgive and unite factions.
Let's quit washing our dirty linen in public and end this nightmare that is maximizing divisions that CAN be overcome, distracting us from important development-related tasks and encouraging the outside world to regard Rivers Men as a bunch of thieving, useless, immature, gun-toting thugs.

Original_Search_Title: The Bar's New Kingmakers and '08 Election
Headline: Nigeria: The Bar's New Kingmakers and '08 Election
Body_Text:
Lagos — Against the background of the forthcoming NBA presidency elections in August, OSAHON H. IHENYEN expresses strong opposition to what he calls godfatherism in Nigerian Bar Association Politics.
Come August 24- 29 2008 Nigerian lawyers shall once again be called upon to elect the next President of the biggest Bar Association in Black Africa.
Following the debacle of 1992 in Port Harcourt a number of electoral reforms have been wittingly and unwittingly put in place by men of the wig and gown, one of which is the delegates voting system which restricts the number of voters at the NBA biennial conference to a few delegates voting instead of having all practicing, fee-paying Nigerian lawyers exercising their right to vote at the election, the delegates voting system does not conform with the tenets of democracy. However, those who are for this system of delegates voting say that it is better to have only accredited delegates as against the old method whereby all practicing fees paying members of the association vote; they are quick to complain that under the former dispensation lawyers queue up for hours unend to vote and that with the present upsurge in the number of Nigerian lawyers it might take days for lawyers to conduct the elections.
What in God's name is wrong with lawyers voting for days to elect officers to run the affairs their association? Better to have lawyers vote for days than have a bar association that prides itself as being the watch dog of the Nigerian government, political parties et al but that shows bad example by having just a few people vote in a highly undemocratic biennial bar elections.
Proponents of the delegates voting system should be told that it is possible for an average Nigerian young man/women to grow up in this country become a lawyer, practice for years and even retire from practice never to have experienced proper democratic voting either in his beloved association or in Nigeria, because he is disenfranchised by the voting system at the bar elections and of course not allowed to vote by the political thugs or god fathers of politicians who harass him/her out of polling booths in the larger Nigerian society.
SUGGESTIONS:
Since the major argument against the voting by all Nigerian lawyers appear to be that elections at the bar conferences may become rancorous, unmanageable and may take days to conclude one may suggest that voting at the elections should actually commence weeks before the annual bar conference by having practicing fees and branch dues paying lawyers vote at the branch level on a date to be set aside for that purpose at least a month to the annual bar conference. This shall result in some kind of revenue drive for the bar association both at branch and national levels, as this may encourage some lawyers who wish to be part of the voting process to pay their annual practising fees and branch dues promptly.
On the other hand, the list of voters may be increased by allowing lawyers who are eighteen years post call (on attaining what I tag "bar age of majority") and who have paid their practising fees up to date or who have paid their practising fees for the current year to vote. This system though undemocratic in some way at least will increase the number of lawyers who are eligible to vote instead of this present undemocratic system. Even the Constitution of Nigeria stipulates that a lawyer can become a High Court Judge, Court of Appeal and Supreme Court Justice, at ten, twelve and fifteen years post call respectively, so why not have lawyers ten years and above vote at bar elections.
Again, since the length of voting time may well be a problem, one may suggest that voting at the bar elections should begin from day one of the bar conference instead of the present situation whereby lawyers wait till the last day of conference to cast their votes. Counting of votes should begin right from the first day. This will make it possible for the results of the elections to be announced latest by noon of the last day of the conference.
By the foregoing we shall have a more democratic NBA and the Nigerian politician will have a thing or two to learn from the NBA as against the present undemocratic system, by lawyers who are quick to accuse the PDP and the average Nigerian politician of being undemocratic. I wonder what moral justification, we the Nigerian lawyers indeed the leadership of the bar have to accuse the Nigerian politician of being undemocratic when even the NBA is guilty of practising a highly undemocratic delegate voting system, thus justifying crass illegality. NBA should remove the log in her eyes so that she can clearly see the speck in the eyes of the average Nigerian politician.
The words "her" and "she" are used advisedly so as to portray the NBA as a beautiful maiden who as ceaser's wife must be above board, and be seen to be above board in order to bear good democratic fruits and serve as a shining light to the Nigerian society.
The annual general meeting that preceeds the biennial bar elections should take steps to stop this highly undemocratic system. Our highly respected President Agbakoba SAN should please take note.
ZONING SYSTEM:
One of the fall outs of the 1992 Port Harcourt show of shame is the zoning system that has since the rebirth of NBA been quietly practiced. This unwritten zoning system which has been put in place by Nigerian lawyers especially in the election of the president of the Association has thrown up the likes of Okpoko, SAN Warri Branch and South-South. Olanipekun, SAN Ilorin Branch, South West, Ojo SAN Ilorin Branch, North Central (who our brothers from the core North believe actually stole the Northern quota because according to them he is a Yoruba man from the South who after stealing the North's mandate abandoned them to become A.G of Federation and gave the mandate to another Yoruba man Odogiyan to complete his term). Agbakoba, SAN Lagos Branch, South East.
The zoning arrangement is now presently being formalised with the zoning of the presidency this year to the South West, with two SANS Akeredolu and Adesina contesting.
The present zoning system just like it's twin brother "Delegates Voting" though bad enough, but there does appear a new undemocratic twist to the zoning formula.
On Saturday April 19, 2008 some Nigerian lawyers gathered in the country home of an erstwhile President of the Bar Association Wole Olanipekun, SAN at Ikere Ekiti under the auspices, name, appellation and style of "Egbe Amofin Oduduwa" i.e. the Association of Yoruba Lawyers with the sole purpose of adopting Rotimi Akeredolu of Ibadan Branch as the sole candidate of the Yorubas for the presidency of the NBA. What this means is that because the NBA has zoned presidency to the South West a few Yoruba lawyers who are in support of the adoption of Akeredolu met at the home of former President Olanipekun a very senior Lawyer and egbon whom I respect a lot and indeed campaigned for as one of his "foot soldiers" to become President in 2002; who doggedly fought President Olusegun Obasanjo to a stand still for his supposedly undemocratic attitude in his approach to national issues, has now decided not only to "adopt" and indeed "anoint" Akeredolu as the next NBA president but has gone ahead to take advantage of the present zoning system by the bar to "elect" a president for the Bar, using all kinds of blackmail, harassment, subtle intimidation to cow Adesina and all other contestants from the South West to abandon the idea of becoming President.
Emboldened by the so-called adoption by the "God fathers", Akeredolu visited us at Ikeja branch of NBA, in a grand standing and very arrogant speech he said addressing us lesser mortals, that he is a Senior Advocate and he has been one for 10 years unlike Adesina who has only been a SAN for six months, that he has been adopted by the "powers that be" and "anybody who is not happy about this should go and hit his head against the wall". Haba Aketi! With due respect to you, you need a lesson or two in humility, you have been taking too many lessons on arrogance from Lamidi and other exponents of amala and gbegiri politics. But the Bible tells me that even God resists the proud. We the Lawyers of Ikeja Branch of NBA feel insulted by your Adedibuish arrogant posture and we demand an unreserved apology. Note further that your apology must be through the same medium. We are waiting.
Meanwhile, the "God fathers" in the Yoruba lawyers forum should be told that when the proponents of the zoning system proposed the zoning arrangement the idea behind the zoning system is that a zone, in this particular case South West shall produce Presidential candidates i.e. two or more candidates from the zone for the members of association to choose from at the conference in August. NOT that the South West and indeed a few individuals or a cabal, cartel by whatever name called shall sit down in the country home of one of their members after a heavy meal of pounded yam "anoint" or "elect" a President for the Bar. If this is allowed one wonders what lesson or pontification we have for the Adedibus, the Chris Ubas, and their ilk, who are the "godfathers" of "godsons" in the larger Nigerian society.
This is condemnable by all right thinking Lawyers. Please allow Adesina and other interested candidates to run for NBA Presidency, so that Nigerian Lawyers may have a choice.
- Ihenyen, a lawyer, is based in Lagos

Original_Search_Title: FG Set to Promote 12 Cultural Festivals to Global Standards
Headline: Nigeria: FG Set to Promote 12 Cultural Festivals to Global Standards
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Federal Government has identified 12 cultural festivals for promotion to international standards, Tourism, Culture and National Orientation Minister Adetokunbo Kayode said yesterday.
The minister said at the News Agency of Nigeria (NAN) Forum in Abuja that the promotion would boost tourism in the country.
"In the past one year, we have been making very giant strides to hit international tourism market with Nigerian cultural festivals.
"We took a decision to have 12 national festivals; we have been doing a lot of advocacy with the states on the need to upgrade these festivals," he said.
He listed the festivals to include Eyo Masquerade in Lagos, the Calabar Carnival, Enugu New Yam Festival, Argungu Fishing Festival and the Opobo Boat Regatta.
Kayode said the festivals could be modified to meet the needs of international tourists and boost the national economy.
For instance, he said that the Osun Osogbo festival could be repackaged beyond its celebration component to attract traditional worshippers from abroad, especially blacks in the Diaspora.
"I think the Osun Osogbo grove can be a pilgrimage site. To sustain the system and keep alive this culture, we need to find a way to package the Osun River water and sand for exportation," he said.
Water from the river is reported to have medicinal and curative ingredients, including making barren women bear children.
Cultural festivals are not religious festivals; we have to make people understand that we are not doing religion.
Even though some traditional religion is embedded in it, we are not concerned with it. The emphasis here is the spectacle that we are concerned with; the religious part of it will be done in the secret by people who are entitled to do so.
"We are not involved in the spiritual aspect of the festivals but the spectacle," the minister explained.
He said the culture and tourism ministry had held discussions with the Lagos State Government on making the Eyo Masquerade festival an annual event and holding it at a place like the Tafawa Balewa Square.
This would attract international tourists who would marvel at seeing a sea of people clad in white robes, caps and veils, he said.
The minister said: "The new yam festival will also be upgraded to a national festival which we hope to rotate round the eastern zone."
He said the various states had been encouraged to organise festivals at the local government level, which would be adopted into national festivals.
Kayode said that the festivals were not all about making money but promoting and projecting the country's image positively.

Original_Search_Title: We Must Redefine Our Democracy
Headline: Nigeria: We Must Redefine Our Democracy
Body_Text:
Lagos — Take away the first few years after Independence and the Second Republic (1979-1983), Nigeria has yet to taste the fruits of Democracy while its citizens are akin to shareholders of a distressed bank.
The proclamation of the Obasanjo Administration in 1979 after the Buhari-Idiagbon draconian days quickly followed by the Babangida eight years of the locusts and Abacha's tyranny of five years hoisted some banner of illusion of democratic rule in Nigeria.
Alas, the Otta-based General blurred the line of demarcation between civil rule and dictatorship as all institutions of democratic rule were subverted and corrupted. The principle of separation of powers was observed in the breach as Obasanjo was the Executive President, the overlord of the parliament and the interpreter of even Supreme Court decisions for selective obedience. He even raised lieutenants whose past time was kidnapping governors and burning down government offices in their states.
But by far his most indelible mark in the sapping of our democratic institutions of their moral fibre was the subversion of the right of Nigerians to freely choose their leaders.
The 2003 and 2007 elections conducted by Obasanjo remain the worst violation of the people's will.
The one year of Yar'Adua so far has proved right that the output of an event only reflects the processes that went into it. A regime that is not put in place by the people will definitely find it impossible to solve the problems of the people. No matter the good intentions of Yar'Adua, for as long as he is just a front for the cabal that holds Nigeria down, we can only be witnessing policy somersaults and incoherent programme execution.
By the way, the bye-elections held so far under Mr. "Rule of Law" has been just as embarrassing as the ones Maurice Iwu manipulated under Obasanjo's supervision. Kogi, Adamawa and FCT have been just a subtler version of "do or die".
It was Amical Cabra who said the people are not interested in the ideas in anybody's head except the positive changes such ideas bring to bear on their lives. So, the question is what dividends have the people enjoyed in the last one year?
We have been bored with all the billions of dollars the man in Otta spent to generate darkness for us, but Yar'Adua has yet to spend a Kobo on the sector despite the so-called emergency he declared in the sector. We have only enjoyed more darkness for it.
Perhaps the food crisis has been the major test of the regime's ability to respond to crises. Its response has been a big floundering. Allocating N80billion to import rice is nothing but Cabal politics at its worst display. Apart from creating rice billionaires and millionaires, the food crisis is the only thing that will not be resolved. How will imported rice resolve the scarcity of garri, yam, tomatoes, pepper and other staples.
Perceptive Nigerians are equally worried how the administration is handling the anti-corruption war. Under the guise of the rule of law,the former Chairman of the Economic and Financial Crimes Commission (EFCC) Mallam Nuhu Ribadu was sent on compulsory study at Kuru and replaced with Ibrahim Lamorde as Acting Chairman.
Another acting chairman, Rtd AIG, Farida Waziri has been appointed to replace the Acting Chairman. Why not go through the due process of appointing a substantive chairman before removing the acting chairman. It may suggest a regime under pressure from the patrons of corruption eagerly in need of soft landing. There is no other reason for the undue haste and the intensity of the antigraft war can only be the worse for it.
So what are we celebrating on May 29? Absolutely Nothing! May 29 represents the day the Cabal sold Nigerians a dummy for democracy. It was a day we were supplied something different from what we ordered. It is a celebration of impunity and the wanton rape of the sovereignty of the Nigerian people.
No, May 29 can't be our democracy day. It is a reminder of the fact that we have to struggle for genuine democracy. It is a nudging that we must do something about our democracy devoid of democrats, political parties without principles and government without governance.
The alternative to May 29 remains June 12. June 12 represents all our dreams. It is the day the votes counted and were counted. That was the day Nigerians resolved the National Question by voiding geography with Abiola defeating Tofa in Kano; set aside religion as a Muslim-Muslim ticket won convincingly in Christian dominated states.
We must de-anul June 12. The spirit of June 12 is what we need to move forward as a nation. That should be our real democracy day. For as long as we deny that, we continue to run from the destiny of this nation. And if a nation turns its back on its destiny, the faster it runs, the farther it gets from its destination.

Original_Search_Title: Edo And Election Dividends
Headline: Nigeria: Edo And Election Dividends
Body_Text:
Lagos — Every dividend has a product to which it owes its existence. The dividends from share-holding cannot be credited to proceeds from the sale of yams. In that sense, the dictionary of Nigerian politics has been invaded lately by some inappropriate applications. For instance, everything that comes from government has tended to be subsumed under one nebulous term, dividends of democracy.
A man works for government. At the end of the month, he gets some wages paid to him. We are not concerned here with the size of such wages even if we know that some of them are at the slave level. But an employer turns round and calls the wages dividends of democracy in a way that stubbornly suggests that all those who work in non-democratic systems do not get any salary at the end of the month.
Yes, you build a house and pave a street. So do cats and dogs. It is a misnomer to call those sand-fillings, electricity projects, street cleaning and the rest of them dividends of democracy. These are all expectations of governance. These are things, which the most authoritarian regimes, including even those to which democracy is anathema, also provide. In Nigeria, the military administrations, including that of Strongman Abacha or, for that matter, any government in power anywhere, must employ people who sweep around the offices, clean the gutters and also provide basic amenities for the people, without calling these provisions dividends of democracy.
Yes, you could talk of dividends of democracy. Here, you would be dealing in the realm of Chapter IV of the 1999 Constitution, which talks largely of fundamental rights and the rule of law. This is certainly one area where the PDP Administration in Edo State, which is most vocal in proclaiming dividends of democracy, has failed very abysmally. This is a regime that relishes in the disobedience of Court Orders. Right from its second day in office, when it embarked on the dissolution of the legally constituted State Independent Electoral Commission (SIEC), which it met in place in utter defiance of a Court Order to the contrary; to the illegal reconstitution of the same body largely with its cronies, made up essentially of PDP card carrying members, again, in contravention of the State electoral law, which stipulates that members of the Commission should be non-politicians; from the initial blunt refusal of the administration to organize elections into the local government councils in the state but instead chose to run the Councils with Care-taker Committees, contrary to the provisions of section 7 of the Constitution of the Federal Republic of Nigeria, 1999, which guarantees only "The system of local government by democratically elected local government councils"; even when the regime's hands were finally forced into those local government elections, what we saw in those "landslide victories" was an exercise in thuggery, killing, maiming and everything bad, instead of elections..
Knowing what we know now, it is crystal clear that a regime, which shot its way to power via massively rigged elections cannot be laying claim to the provision of the dividends of democracy. History is replete with the fact that in Edo State, the general elections of April 2007 and their younger brother, the sham called local government election of December 2007 remain the worst elections in known human history.
Talking of dividends, we soon got close to what could be called the dividends of elections. After the disaster of April 14, 2007 dubbed the gubernatorial election, the Election Petition Tribunal was set up. The PDP administration approached the Tribunal with the attitude of "if you can't get it, you should scatter it". Knowing very well that it had a bad case, it suddenly began to ask and hope for the cancellation of the election and consequently, a re-run. It was at this point that the administration embarked on a number of scattered road and gutter projects. Essentially, those projects were intended to be vote catchers. The idea was that in the event of a re-run of the gubernatorial election, the people would say, "yes the original mandate was stolen quite alright but since Prof. Osunbor is working hard (or hardly working?) let's give him our votes". These are scattered projects that do not even conform to any appropriations guidelines. But that is not the issue here.
Incidentally, the level of awareness on the part of the electorate is now very high. They know a ruse when they see one. Many of them argue very strongly that the credit for those dividends of election should rightly go to Comrade Adams Oshiomhole for, if he had not dragged Prof. Osunbor to the Tribunal, the latter would not have had any need to pretend to be working.
Sadly enough, the very first pointer to the fact that the Osunbor-led administration was not genuinely intent on developing the areas could be found in the fact that since the 20th of March, 2008 when it has become increasingly clear that the re-run option was not coming into play, the administration has slowed down considerably on the execution of the projects. We hear that some of the contractors have started moving their equipment away from site. We are also told that up till now, some of the contractors have not properly mobilized to site and no one is now keen on pushing them any more. The big question remains, is the PDP aware of the implications of abandoning those dividends of election. In fact, the only way to convince the people that the projects were not just intended to catch votes is to pursue them with the original vigour and get them finished before the rains descend heavily.
Evidently, ours is a land of ironies; a land of obvious contradictions. One would think that it is ludicrous for a whole administration, which is headed by a Professor of law; an administration that has an avalanche of lawyers, to approach a very serious Tribunal with a brazen attitude of 'destroy it where you can't get it'. How on earth could anyone be demanding in a loud voice, that an election which it claims to have won should be annulled? Everywhere this came up for discussion, we kept being reminded that it has a parallel in the Bible. Even the ones who have never seen the four walls of a classroom still talk of King Solomon's "Don't divide the child". If we could not 'divide the child' back to the biblical days of Solomon, why would anyone think that the child could now be divided, particularly with the independent, steadfast and upright judiciary that Nigeria is currently endowed with?
Indeed, the Judiciary has lived up to its expectation as the last hope of every citizen. In all of this, it has received a lot of bashing. We have seen attempts to offer them financial inducements, blackmail them, threaten their lives but they have remained resolute and undaunted. Again, we must say that while the Judiciary remains praise worthy, we must condemn every effort to derail it. But for the Judiciary, we could as well have long kissed democracy goodbye in this country. It is therefore clear that the destroyers and killers of democracy cannot lay claim to providing its dividends.
Enter the Wild-Wild West. Understandably, if the Judiciary must retain its integrity, it must do some in-house cleaning from time to time. The National Judicial Council (NJC) is not happy with developments in the Oyo State Judiciary. In pursuance of the powers conferred on the JNC under paragraph 21(d) of Part I of the Third Schedule to the 1999 Constitution, it has gone ahead to dish out a number of disciplinary measures to some members of the Oyo State Judiciary. Hear Danladi Halilu, Secretary to the NJC: " Hon Justice Iyabo Yerima of Oyo State High Court has been suspended pending when her recommendation for compulsory retirement by the State Governor, Adebayo Alao-Akala is effected.. Hon Justice Afolabi Adeniran, the immediate past Chief Judge was also recommended for compulsory retirement from office Though Hon Justice I.O. Olakanmi's pending suit in the court challenging his removal by the former State Governor, Alhaji Rasheed Ladoja has stalled the NJC investigation of allegations of corruption against him, the NJC says he stands removed unless otherwise stated by the courts" (THE NATION, Sunday, 20th April, 2008, p.8).
Our worry: In spite of the NJC pronouncements on the Judges, we hear that they are still presiding over their Courts in Ibadan, even when the NJC has made it clear that any actions taken by them will be considered invalid. However, we hear that lawyers have started boycotting their courts since the beginning of April.
Over verdict: For the JNC recommendations to become effective, they must be implemented by the Governor of Oyo State, Alao-Akala. In the overall interest of the people of Oyo State, this is not the time to play politics with the Judiciary. Governor Alao-Akala must act quickly to remove the current log-jam in the State's judiciary. He has no choice in the matter. He, too, needs the judiciary to function properly in office. He cannot throw a spanner in the works and expect the wheel of progress and justice to move on.

Original_Search_Title: Women Who Know How to Extract Their Pound of Flesh
Headline: Nigeria: Women Who Know How to Extract Their Pound of Flesh
Body_Text:
Lagos — NEVER let it be said that you deliberately sent your husband packing. Over the years, women have devised numerous methods of getting rid of difficult husbands in such clever ways that they (the women) would always look like the wronged party.
Otherwise, how would you explain a wife who always goes to the market just around the time her husband is crying for his lunch? Or the wife who gives the only bottle of beer to her husband's friend, knowing dammed well that the poor thing wouldn't eat his dinner satisfactorily without his bottle of cold beer? Not only that, she can't hide her sadistic pleasure at her husband's fury as she opens the bottle of beer - for another man!
For the twelve years she was married to her husband, Folake, an executive officer in one of the ministries, avoided cooking bush-rat stew because it was taboo in the village her husband came from. Recently, the tactless man announced with relish that he was taking a second wife and he did. In no time at all, meal-times were divided between two wives. "Three weeks ago", Folake told me, "a friend of his travelled to his village and brought me smoked bush rat. The thing was edible afterall and I quickly made vegetable soup with it. My husband couldn't praise my cooking enough as he ate his pounded yam. I felt guilty so I decided not to say anything about the meat. But when I remembered the arrogance with which he told me of his taking a second wife I just had to have the revenge I planned for.
"When I told him what he was eating, he yelled, hurriedly depositing a lump of half-masticated bush-rat into his palm and gaggled! He looked so apoplectic that I almost felt sorry for him. He swore I could have poisoned him if I had the opportunity. He was over-reacting, of course, I told him. I showed my resentment the only way I knew how, that didn't mean I wanted him dead.
So, if, suddenly, the stew madam dishes out starts tasting too salty, the meat too tough and the wrong type; or if tears run freely from your eyes from too much hot pepper in the stew, ask yourself if your wife isn't trying to send you a message before the message turns into text messages from her lover. A few mishaps could be overlooked but when it becomes a daily occurrence, you must be doing something wrong!
Sanni has just had the most nightmarish experience of his life, he confessed. He's apparently still reeling from the impact of the blow of his humiliation. But, as he bravely puts it, the price of love is pain. It all started five years ago when Sanni met Ify, a forty-five year old 'general' contractor who had more money than she knew what to do with. A stark illiterate, she has more than her share of intelligence. She also believed she had the misfortune of falling in love with men who took one look at her and saw a punching bag!
Sanni, now in his early fifties, swore he was never a violent man. That it was Ify's arrogance and contempt for him that always brought out his violent streak. He told me that "six months after I met that witch, I had deserted my wife and kids to move in with her. It was at her insistence but she was like a fever in my blood. I would have killed for her.
"Through my office, I helped her to get yet more contracts. She bought me two brand new cars in appreciation of all the business contracts I got for her. I also travelled abroad regularly on trips for her. I, sometimes, took a girlfriend who happened to be a casual friend of my woman's daughter. She got to know about it and that was where my hell started. Shortly after, I lost my job-and the contracts I could wangle for her stopped. She didn't find that funny at all! I had to depend heavily on her, financially, as my own money went into a house I was building at that time.
She always handed me money with a sneer. To crown it all, she brought a lover to the house. It was her house, she insisted, and she could bring whoever she wanted into it. I knew what to do if I didn't like the idea. "That did it! A wave of fury took over the pain and humiliation of the last few months. I slammed my clenched fist into her smug face over and over again; deriving a morbid sense of satisfaction from her yelps of pains as she cowered in fright.
"When she broke free, she ran to the police. Told them I had fraudulently bought the two cars in my name after I took money from her. She told them she couldn't read, or write and didn't detect the fraud until her daughter pointed it out to her recently. She alleged I started pummeling her when she confronted me with my 'crime'.
"I was locked up, of course, pending full investigations. She pulled a lot of strings to put me behind bars but after a couple of days, my friends got me out. I was told she'd already sent my things to my elder brother's place. There were thugs with big wraps of Indian hemp guarding the house when I later called for the rest of my things she hadn't sent. I fled. Any man could easily kill that kind of a woman out of provocation. She is wicked and heartless. She always complained that her men beat her up. She's lucky she hasn't been killed yet."

Original_Search_Title: Price of Rice Escalates in Abuja Markets
Headline: Nigeria: Price of Rice Escalates in Abuja Markets
Body_Text:
Abuja — The price of a mudu of rice yesterday rose from N200 to N300 in most markets in the Federal Capital Territory (FCT).
Our reporter who visited Nyanya, Karu and Karmo markets, observed traders complaining about the high cost of rice.
A trader, Anemetu said a big bag of rice sells for between N9,000 and N11, 000 depending on the type as against N7, 000 sold previously.
"It is not our fault.
Tell government to do something about the increase before it rises to N15, 000 a bag," she warned.
Buyers who could not afford to buy a full mudu because of the price settled for half.
At roadside restaurants in the capital city, the high price has also affected the quantity that was served per plate.
At Wuse, a plate of rice that usually sold for N100 has risen to N150.
Most people inside a restaurant visited had shunned the rice meal for beans and yam.
"When I requested for rice and saw the small quantity being served, I changed my mind for yam and beans in order to get value for my money," said a man in the restaurant.
It would be recalled that the Minister of Finance, Dr Usman Shamsudeen, recently warned of an impending increase in price of rice.

Original_Search_Title: Prices of Foodstuff Increase in Benin
Headline: Nigeria: Prices of Foodstuff Increase in Benin
Body_Text:
Benin — Prices of major foodstuff have maintained a steady increase in major markets in Benin since after the Easter celebrations, a survey by the News Agency of Nigeria (NAN) has shown.
NAN reports that the increases were most noticeable in the three major markets of Eki-Osa, Oba Market and the New-Benin Market
Traders are putting the blame for the increases on the scarcity of fuel, which has led to high cost of transporting the goods.
Prices are, however, slightly lower in smaller markets at Uselu, Oliha, Oregbeni and Oka all in the Edo capital.
At the big markets, a milk cup of melon sells for N70, Ogbono is N160 and the cheapest grade of rice goes for N25 per milk cup.
A bag of Mama-Gold rice, which sold N8, 000 earlier in the year now sells for N9, 500 while Mama-Africa and Riceland go for N9, 000, as against N8, 000 earlier in the year.
The price of a tuber of yam varies in price from N100 to N350 depending on size, while a paint bucket measure of gari costs N170 Gino 5-litre vegetable oil increased from N1, 350 to N1, 500, while a litre of the oil now sells for N400.
However, Gino tin tomatoes remained at N25, while fresh tomatoes and onions were still on the cheap side, but with the cost of fresh pepper on the high side. (NAN)

Original_Search_Title: NTDC Yam House to Host Iwaji Festival
Headline: Nigeria: NTDC Yam House to Host Iwaji Festival
Body_Text:
Kebbi — With the successful commissioning of the Nigerian Tourism Development Corporation (NTDC) fish house in Argungu, Kebbi State, the director-general NTDC, Otunba Olusegun yesterday in Abuja said the NDTC yam house in Igbo Ukwu, Anambra State, will be officially, commissioned for use at this years Iwaji Ndi Igbo National Festival billed for August 25-30.
According to the NTDC boss, the multi-purpose facility built by the tourism corporation was conceptualised to raise the profile of the annual international festival which attracts Igbo sons and daughters from far and near, as well as friends of the Igbo race.
"When we started this project, many people did not understand why we had to start a fish house and a yam house in Argungu and Igbo Ukwu respectively. However, with the commissioning of the fish house in March during the Argungu International fishing festival, other states have been coming to us to extend the projects to their states. Osun State, for example, is asking for Odua house, while Kano wants pyramid house to mention only a few.
The essence of the project, the director general said is to add value to this festival and more importantly, contribute to the economy through the employment of people who will take care of the facilities and, stressed that job creation is crucial to ridding the society of crimes and other social vices.
Ealier, the traditional rulers from the five eastern states led by Igwe Dr. Martin Nwafor Ezeh, Idu II of Igbo-Ukwu and chairman board of trustee, Mbido Igbo Association, said the association chose to flag off this year's New yam festival celebration at the NTDC in view of the fact that Otunba Runsewe is the chief promoter of tourism.
"We decided to perform the ceremony at your exalted office as a privilege to your achievements so far. Your promise to institutionalize the Igbo race New Yam Festival exactly last year 4th May, 2007, has sealed the unity of this country and you have given the Igbo people a sense of belonging in everything, as culture is the summation of the people's activities. Ezeh poured encomiums on the NTDC boss for his innovative and creative energy which has impacted greatly on tourism and the country's image abroad.
The programme for the Iwa ji Ndi Igbo National Festival as endorsed by the association and the tourism corporation opens with pre-event publicity programmes, traditional dances, colloquium, drama to be centered on yam, masquerade and cultural display, traditional wrestling for the strongest man in Igbo land, a beauty pageant, fashion and cultural parade, palm wine tapping competition, food fair and the new yam Festival proper.

Original_Search_Title: The Value of Nigerian Woman
Headline: Nigeria: The Value of Nigerian Woman
Body_Text:
Lagos — There is one thing, amongst very many, that I absolutely adore about the Nigerian woman, and it is the fact that she is, with no reservation whatsoever, totally vain.
In any gathering in any cosmopolis of any corner of this earth, it is as easy to pick out the Nigerian woman as it is for an ant to pick out the smell of sugar.
She is the one with impeccable hair, up to the minute dress, shoes that cost a fortune and nails that look as though she has never been in a kitchen all her life except that we know already that upon all her other qualities, she is, as well, a fabulous cook.
It therefore appeared somewhat irrelevant when, a few years back, the public debate broke out over what true African beauty was or was not.
Agbani Darego had just clinched the Miss World crown then, a feat that was to become one of the few dividends of democracy that we would ever witness, let alone enjoy.
Many Nigerian commentators, particularly men, then tended to think the South South beauty, though being rather cute and sweet, was not the best we had to offer.
The general consensus amongst that sex was that she might have been more up to scratch, in their eba, akpu and pounded yam beclouded eyes, had she been endowed with more prominent curves.
It was remarkable, then, that the average female commentator did not agree and thought that Agbani, with her awkward height and gangly frame, deserved that famous crown as well as any other.
Was it then, that in spite of her supreme confidence, the Nigerian woman is not as satisfied with her curves as it was thought, or is it that she had, while accepting herself the way she was, also conscious of some larger world out there whose specifications and opinions are of a larger significance than hers?
These few years on, it is clear as day that the increasingly independent Nigerian woman has, in spite of the enduring insistence of the majority of her men on a "bit of meat here and there" almost completely embraced the waifish look over the curves. Surely, this is understandable.
As Western designs become less and less sparse and the local fashion designer less and less imaginative in general, it makes perfect sense that a sparer figure would be better suited to the showing of flesh than a curvier one, not to mention the air of anonymity it confers on the dresser.
And so as Nigerian social scenes become bolder and bolder, clearly owing to the air of security conferred by the return of civil rule, the quest for a slimmer figure has become more and more popular amongst us.
There are bright sides to this, certainly. The more obvious ones are those with the health implications. There are countries in which every woman with a waist size over 31 inches and man with a waist size over 34 inches are sent on compulsory health checks in search of signs of diabetes, kidney trouble and heart disease.
Indeed, it has been a known fact for a long time now that size, at a certain age, is inversely proportional to health and that the smaller frame has a longer life expectancy than the larger one.
Interestingly, it is in some of these places that people have taken to the more extreme measures in the search for the lighter frame, including using psychotropic drugs such as cocaine, which are believed to significantly reduce food craving.
But while the Nigerian woman, great self image and all, is as unlikely as ever to be the victim of anorexia nervosa or bulimia, the two best known eating disorders, her newfound prosperity and increasing independence have had it so that she is going to the extreme in her search for more exhibit-able waists, legs and bustlines, and girl talk and party talk topics now necessarily include gossip about who and who has had what lifted or done and/or exchanges of contact details of cosmetic surgeons on the local as well as international scenes.
If the truth is to be told, it is almost becoming epidemic and while Akunyili's NAFDAC is at it, they might want to take a closer look at many of the weight loss medicines, cosmetic injections and energy drinks that find their way into the country.
Interestingly and ironically, some Western societies are going to uncommon lengths and making extraordinary efforts to reverse these sorts of trends. In the UK for instance where the media has been an active partner in the creation of this new consciousness, a size 16 model was this year admitted into the finals for the Miss England pageant.
She is, of course, unlikely to win. Indeed, her winning would be too radical to be true, and would appear too political. Yet, a statement like this from the judges of that competition is not only going to send a strong, a positive message, it is most welcome.

Original_Search_Title: Big Brands Battle for a Bite of Breakfast Market
Headline: Kenya: Big Brands Battle for a Bite of Breakfast Market
Body_Text:
In millions of Kenyan homes, food marketers are salivating over changing eating habits at breakfast tables.
If these people were to have their way, more middle class families would be consuming cereals, edging out Kenyans' obsessions with time consuming prepared breakfasts, which feature pastries such as mandazi, bread, chapati or fleshy organic roots such as yams, arrow roots and sweet potatoes.
They would also have more Kenyan men - who consider cereals to be food for children- crunching cereals mixed with cold milk as their first meal of the day.
As the economic fortunes have continued to rise, creating more middle class families with working mothers, Kenyan culinary culture is starting to adapt to packaged processed foods.
Though cereals have been in existence in Kenya for decades, with Weetabix remaining one of the oldest and most popular brand according to Consumer Insight, this Sh1.5 billion market is now experiencing a proliferation of local brands like Proctor & Allan cornflakes and imported foreign ones such as Kelloggs, Nestle Cornflakes and Bokomo.
Even with the rising middle class, higher disposable incomes and increasingly smaller and younger Kenyan families seeking more convenience in a modernising retail environment, getting a bite of breakfast cereal market and muscling into the larger processed food market remains a challenge.
Cooked breakfasts still reign supreme in Kenya and many consumers believe that cereals are mostly for children, which has forced manufacturers to become more inventive in their marketing efforts to woo more customers to their brands.
Some consumers also believe that breakfast cereals are too expensive compared to other products like bread, mandazi, chapati, sweet potatoes, eggs and other foods commonly eaten at breakfast.
"I used to buy cornflakes but stopped early this year because rising prices of most products has made it too expensive to afford them so now my husband and I have tea and bread and only buy Weetabix for our son," says Tina Akello, a business woman in Nairobi and mother of a seven-year-old. Patricia Syomboa, mother of a two-and-a-half year old son also believes that breakfast cereals are expensive.
In their home the family takes tea and bread on most days and sometimes sweet potatoes or mandazi. Her friend Mary Shilwatso, who is single usually takes yams or an egg at home then has tea with bread and butter at her work place.
These breakfast alternatives are the biggest competitors of breakfast cereals says Mr Ahsan Manji, the managing director of The Breakfast Cereal Company (K) Limited which manufacturers Weetabix, the leading brand in Kenya.
A secondary challenge, which the company has been trying to address through a major marketing campaign, is the perception that breakfast cereals are for children. This campaign is aimed at consumers like Mr Anthony Maeobe, a father of two aged 16 and 12 years.
"I am an African brought up in a very traditional way and yes I believe breakfast cereals are for children. You only have to look at most of the packs and the way the products are presented to realise this," he says.
Mr Maeobe says his children used to take cornflakes but have since graduated to a "proper breakfast" consisting of tea and bread, mandazis and occasionally pancakes and fruit. Mr Maeobe himself rarely takes breakfast especially when he's been drinking alcohol the day before. "I usually have soup with boiled meat around 11:00am which is like a late breakfast-cum-lunch (brunch)," he says. Research by Consumer Insight, a marketing research firm shows that only 30 per cent of Kenyans eat breakfast cereals.
Consumers in the middle to upper social class (AB) are more accepting of breakfast cereals with half of consumers in this segment eating them but in the lower social class C1 to D even fewer than 30 per cent consume breakfast cereals. Consumers are also becoming more health conscious, which is influencing the products they buy.
"On weekdays we're careful about what we eat and we generally restrict breakfast to cereals high in fibre and fruits, but on weekends we can have more fatty things like bacon and sausages," says Aggrey Baraza, the restaurant manager at Holiday Inn.
Mr Baraza believes that traditional food such as sweet potatoes and arrow roots are the best because they are high in fibre and therefore healthier, but says that modern lifestyles and living in urban areas makes most people abandon these foods in favour of western foods like breakfast cereals.

Original_Search_Title: Road Accident Claims 16
Headline: Nigeria: Road Accident Claims 16
Body_Text:
Lafia — Sixteen people, yesterday, lost their lives in a ghastly motor accident involving an 18-seater Toyota Hiace bus and a lorry loaded with yams along the Makurdi/Lafia road.
Six others also sustained varying degrees of injury in the crash that occurred mid-day as the lorry heading from Lafia swerved from its its track while trying to avoid a herd of cattle and collided with the Hiace bus which was heading from Makurdi to Kano on the outskirts of Kardarko town in Nasarawa state.
The bodies of the victims have since been deposited at the Lafia specialist hospital while the survivors have been moved to the Federal Medical Centre, Lafia for treatment.

Original_Search_Title: 120-Yr Old Woman Still Going Strong
Headline: Sierra Leone: 120-Yr Old Woman Still Going Strong
Body_Text:
120-year-old Mammy Hannah Douglas who lives in Bobo village along the western rural peninsular area of Tokeh in the outskirts of Freetown could be the oldest woman so far in the country.
Last year Mike Stibich Ph.D.'s Longevity Blog stated that apart from nine others countries in sub Saharan Africa, Sierra Leone with a life expectancy of just 37 years holds the record for the world's shortest life expectancy.
Problems of poverty and childhood death are expected to drive the life expctancy even lower. Over 70% of the population is unemployed and 30% of children do not live passed the age of 5.
Unlike Maria Esther de Capovilla of Ecuador who died at 116 in 2006, Douglas may not have been recognized by Guinness World Records because her claim to the title, as the oldest person in the country, was not particularly supported by detailed documentation provided by her family.
However, the outcome of a week-long investigation corroborated by documents in the possession of Williams Carbind, her 55-year-old nephew, could be pretty much convincing.
Douglas, born on March 16, 1888 at Gbangabato village, yesterday said that she grew up helping everyone in her community and that her positive lifestyle has allowed her to live this long. She is a barren.
Except for the partial blindness which attacked her some ten years ago the old woman sounded hopeful that she has more time in the world to serve her God.
She advised all who wish to live long "to serve God, respect their elders, listen to their parents and elders, and have self knowledge and respect." These according to her are among the reasons she is still alive today.
"I can still walk, feed myself, talk clearly and my teeth are still strong to chew kolanut. My only problem is the fact that I cannot see clearly," she said.
She added: "I was 29 years old when I got married in 1917. I stayed with my husband for complete 68 years before he died in 1985. Since I got married I have never known any other man apart from my husband. I do not have any child but took care of my sister's children. Now they are taking care of me. I eat fofo, yam, rice, kolanut, biscuit, rice porridge and fish or any soft food.
She said her husband was forced to take another wife because she could not give him any children. But the new wife could not stay long perhaps because their husband was not a rich man.
"But I have always remained patient. In fact that was why my husband decided not to take any other woman as wife again. I am the oldest person in the peninsular area," she concluded.

Original_Search_Title: Takoradi Market Women Shun Apremdo Market
Headline: Ghana: Takoradi Market Women Shun Apremdo Market
Body_Text:
APREMDO Market, a multi billion market complex, constructed to cater for marketing activities in Sekondi-Takoradi and it environs, is lagging behind in brisk business activities.
This is as a result of persistent refusal by traders and hawkers, who have on numerous occasions defied the directives of the Shama Ahanta East Metropolitan Assembly (SAEMA) to relocate to the place to conduct marketing activities there.
Due to this, the few market women left there have also threatened to abandon the market, and join their colleagues, at Kwesimintsim, Apremdo Town, Assakai, and other areas, which have on many occasions refused to join them.
Traders of the aforementioned towns are all to leave their respective markets, and move to the multi-billion cedi Apremdo Market, but are being adamant.
The SAEMA has on three consecutive times, conducted decongestion exercises to get the illegitimate street hawkers and traders, who mostly deal in second hand clothing, to move to the aforementioned market.
But this directive has fallen on deaf ears, as traders and hawkers continue to sell their wares openly, on the pavements along the Takoradi Market Circle, in the full glare of the SAEMA authorities.
A visit by these reporters, to the Apremdo Market, revealed that apart from the lack of brisk business activities, another problem the market was facing, was stealing. The market has no security, to watch over the goods of the few market women, who continue to trade in perishable goods, such as fish, tomatoes, pepper, among others there.
There is also no electricity at the portion where these perishable goods are being traded.
About 20 market women, whose sheds were last year gutted by fire, have also not returned leaving the place very empty. The roof of the market is also leaks badly, whenever it rains, giving additional work to the traders, who have to mop the water before they conduct business activities.
An executive member of the Apremdo Market Women Association, Madam Rossina Essien in an interview with The Chronicle deplored the rampant pilfering of their goods at the place. She recounted how last two weeks two bags of tubers of yam were stolen by unidentifiable men. Blaming the rampant pilfering on lack of security and electricity at the place, Madam Rosina added that if the trend continues, they would have no option than to abandon the market.
"We cannot continue to trade here for thieves to come and steal our goods. So the best option is, if SAEMA is not providing security, then we have to move out."
To this end, Madam Rosina appealed to the authorities of the SAEMA, to provide them with electricity and security men, to watch over their goods, to arrest the problem of pilfering.
The place also has problems with transportation, as no commercial vehicles run, to and from the place, thereby making transportation very difficult. Other stores numbering about hundred, and which have been allocated to other individuals for trading activities, have been locked up and abandoned by the owners, for reasons best known to them.

Original_Search_Title: Professor C.S. Momoh - Two Years After
Headline: Nigeria: Professor C.S. Momoh - Two Years After
Body_Text:
Lagos — IT is exactly two years that Campbell Shittu Momoh, a professor of African Philosophy in the Department of Philosophy, University of Lagos, died. For me, his death was a personal loss and I have not fully recovered from it.
I came into the gravitational pull of Momoh's personality as an undergraduate in the aforementioned department. The first course Momoh taught me was a compulsory course entitled "Ancient African Philosophy."
One of the materials he asked us to read was an extract from his doctoral thesis at Indiana University, Bloomington, United States of America, which dealt with Uchi Metaphysics. Then, as a neophyte in the arcane discipline called philosophy, I was intrigued by the arguments contained in the handout.
However, Professor Momoh tried his best to help us, his students, comprehend the discussion. Infact, it was in that very course that I learnt that there had been heated debates about the existence or nonexistence of what can authentically be characterised as African philosophy, distinct from what some philosophers whom Momoh aptly described as African logical neo-positivists derogatorily referred to either as African Traditional Thought or as Ethnophilosophy. It was in the debate about, and elucidation of, African Philosophy that Momoh made his greatest mark in the world of philosophy.
Professor Momoh was a staunch believer in the existence of African Philosophy.
He trenchantly criticized, in several of his publications, those African philosophers who uncritically accepted the racist undertones of the denial of philosophy, properly so called, in traditional African communities.
The late professor of African Philosophy was a fairly well published academic. Some of his books include The Philosophy of A New Past and an Old Future, The Funeral of Democracy, anthologies such as The Substance of African Philosophy, and a three volume work, Nigerian Integrative Discourses.
His academic papers can be found in several journals domiciled in Nigeria and elsewhere. Professor Momoh was the founder of National Association For Religious and Ethnic Tolerance (NARETO) and the senior editor of Nigerian Studies in Religious Tolerance, published by CBAAC/NARETO in four volumes. Definitely, as an academic interested in, and actuated by his socio-economic and political environment, Momoh had paid his dues.
Some of the outstanding character profiles of the late professor which endeared him to generations of his students were love of intelligence and humane tolerant approach to issues. Without being immodest, Professor Momoh believed that I was one of the brightest students he ever taught.
Therefore, he liked engaging me in intellectual duels, and I usually felt humbled by his frankness and open-mindedness, although sometimes he would say jocularly, "Haba Douglas! Are you mad! You are not working hard enough!"
Professor Momoh usually referred to himself as 'Cee Es', a coinage from the initials of his first and middle names. I remember the year 1996 when one of his elder brothers was crowned the Otaru of Auchi. Professor Momoh invited me and Jim Unah, now a professor of phenomenological ontology in the department of philosophy.
We spent four days in Auchi. Professor Momoh ensured that we thoroughly enjoyed ourselves. I noticed that Cee Es was well liked in his hometown. Everywhere we went people came around to greet him. He was a compassionate and pragmatic man. In our department, Cee Es was a peacemaker whenever there was disagreement.
He had a way of making heated arguments mild with apt proverbs and aphorisms. Professor Momoh was a straightforward person; he was not very good at prurience and cunning. Usually, without embellishment or attempt at double-speak, Cee Es would tell you, what he felt about any matter under consideration.
He treated junior colleagues and everyone in the department like human beings, and did not gather any airs around himself, unlike some senior academics who throw their over bloated weights around, wanting others to "bow and tremble" before them.
In fashion, Professor Momoh was conservative. He always wore suits of the "old school" type, or simple native attire.
I recall a number of occasions when he rebuked me for abandoning corporate mode of dressing for casual wear. I wish he is alive now that I have gone back to corporate clothes. As a humane person, Professor Momoh was always willing to listen to students and colleagues whenever they had problems - personal, financial, academic etc.
He not only listened, he also helped whenever he could. Cee Es was a jolly good man; he liked pounded yam, vegetable soup and cold beer, especially Small Stout, Star and Harp. But he was a smoker, a habit I urged him repeatedly to drop because of the health hazards associated with it. I remember one occasion at the Senior Staff Club, University of Lagos, when, after a fairly delicious meal of pounded yam, he lit a stick of Benson cigarette.
I reminded him of the need to quit smoking. I even pledged that I would, till he stopped smoking, give him carrots trimmed to the shape of cigarette sticks so that anytime he felt like smoking he would eat the carrots. Professor Momoh laughed and told me that he knew when he would die and that he can quit smoking any time he decides to do so.
Several months later, I was pleasantly surprised when he told me that he had stopped smoking, and, to the best of my knowledge, he did till the very end. Anytime I remember my last encounter with Professor Momoh before he died, I feel how empty, how fragile and unpredictable our existence on earth is. I cannot recall the exact date, but on that particular day, around 9.00 pm or thereabout, I was searching frantically for my bunch of keys in my car.
Any person who had ever lost his or hers knows how disturbing such an occurrence could be. Professor Momoh, apparently on his way home, came to me and said, in his usual jovial voice. "Anele, you and this your car, you are always doing one thing or another with it."
I replied "Prof. I am looking for my keys." I abandoned my search temporarily and conversed with him for about ten to fifteen minutes. He asked me about my wife, and why we are delaying having children. Professor Momoh loved children.
He reminded me that he came from the royal family in Auchi and that his father married more than ten wives and had more than two hundred children. "So young man" he said, "I came from a large family and, I love children." As we bade each other good night and he walked away, I asked whether I should take him home in my car.
He told me that his car was just a few metres away. I resumed my search, which eventually proved unsuccessful. I went home dejected. I never knew that that was the last time I would see Cee Es. The following morning, as I was driving to the campus, one of my friends called my cell phone.
I managed to pick up the phone, although I was conscious of the danger of driving and answering a call. My friend sounded urgent. After exchanging greetings, he said: "Sorry o, for Professor Momoh." I did not understand what he meant until I got to school.
As I entered the Arts Block, I saw a condolence register with Momoh's photograph placed above it. My heart skipped several beats. Later, I confirmed that the professor whom I spoke with the previous night had died. I was profoundly shocked. Of course, I travelled to Auchi for his burial; I had to fight back tears on that occasion.
On the whole my late teacher, Professor C.S. Momoh, was a good man. As a scholar, he was not very rigorous and analytical. Some of his theories, for example, his doctrine of active oath-taking, are relatively easy to debunk. Professor Momoh was lenient, sometimes to a fault. I admire his tolerant attitude to religion, although his claim to being a christlem can be interpreted as evidence of theological confusion on his part.
Death is part of life because life and death are inextricably connected in the process of living. It can be described as the existential equivalent of a black hole. In death we experience the nothingness at the core of every living being, infact the deepest mystery at the heart of the universe.
That is why those of us alive need to constantly remind ourselves of the inevitability of death, and the necessity to make life more worth living for our fellow creatures. Professor Campbell Shittu Momoh has played his part in the drama of existence.
He was an asset to the Department of Philosophy, where he taught for more than two decades. For us his former students, his memory will not fade away completely because he touched our lives positively.

Original_Search_Title: Man Struck By Mysterious Ailment After Killing Snake
Headline: Nigeria: Man Struck By Mysterious Ailment After Killing Snake
Body_Text:
Abuja — Malam Muhammad Lawal of Unguwar Sarkin Fawa in Toro local government, Bauchi State, did not harbor any premonition that killing a snake would turn out to be a source of a mysterious illness that has led to his body becoming petrified and his skin moulting.
Some 17 months ago, it was like every other day for Malam Lawal, a cattle trader having closed from business and was on his way home around four in the morning. As fate would have it, he suddenly saw a snake along the road not far from Rishi Hospital. He instinctively struck at the snake with rocks and a stick until he ensured that it was dead. And he continued with his journey home. On reaching home, the cattle trader started contemplating as to whether the reptile was actually dead. This uncertainty triggered Lawal's resolve to go back to the scene for confirmation.
When he got there, Lawal could not find the snake he killed, but a bunch of dry grass blades.
Narrating his strange ordeal to our correspondent in his sick bed in Toro General Hospital located in Miya Barkatai town along Bauchi-Jos Road, the mysterious ailment started immediately he returned home. Amidst visible pain, the patient explained that the first two weeks was hell as it was characterized by ceaseless pain all over his body and passing blood instead of urine and faeces. This, he added, was followed by rashes all over his body that inflicted on him serious pain.
The patient, who is also a father of nine, stated that since then he had visited more than eleven hospitals and several traditional healers in Bauchi, Plateau, Kano and the FCT but with little or no improvement in his rare condition.
Rather, the mysterious illness only succeeded in gulping his entire savings. Already, his sick bed in Miya Barkatai has for a long time become a centre of attraction as people troop there to have a glimpse of Lawal's mysterious condition.
Lawal told our correspondent that his entire body solidified during last Ramadan. Adding that he has been bed ridden for the last three months watching his entire skin peeling off "not unlike a roasted yam's."
Despite his critical condition, the patient is still grateful to the personnel of Toro General Hospital who he described as very caring. Adding also that Alhaji Ahmadu Mai Dori, a traditional medical expert residing in Jos, was of great help to him.
While the patient expressed his gratitude to those nurses in Toro and Ahmadu Mai Dori of Jos, the reverse was the case with the officials of Toro local government and Jama'atul Nasril Islam. Toro local government, according to Lawal, had pledged to aid him with 40,000 Naira to settle his medical bills. But, up till the time he spoke to our correspondent, the local government had not redeemed its pledge to him. He further said that Jama'atul Nasril Islam has equally refused to come to his aid as promised.
Malam Lawal, who was visibly experiencing excruciating pain, also appealed to Governor Isa Yuguda of Bauchi State as well as well-meaning Nigerians to come to his aid. This, he said will enable him settle his medical bills as well as cater for his family that include his nine children.

Original_Search_Title: Now We Can't Even Buy Yam!
Headline: Nigeria: Now We Can't Even Buy Yam!
Body_Text:
From Allah we come and to Him we shall return! Inna Lillahi Wa Inna Ilaihi Raji'un! That is the recommended Muslim phrase in the face of any calamity, such as what happened on Sunday March 2, when my car was snatched in broad daylight by armed robbers.
I had stopped my car at 12 noon (yes, 12 noon) a little after Tafa on the Abuja-Kaduna expressway, just to buy yam. Is it wrong to buy yam? With hindsight, it is apparently now a miscalculation to stop and buy yam on the road, at noon. Even though Nigerians love yam. And the poor yam sellers need the business. And the nation needs the jobs so created.
Most likely, the armed robbers were trailing my car, an ash-colour KIA CERATO with registration number Kano AA 723 WRA. Noticing that all attention was on the haggling of the price of yams, the robbers had stealthily approached and had suddenly shoved guns at the sides of my driver and my brother. When the crowd noticed what was happening, the armed robbers screamed that all should lie prostrate on the ground, much as the nation lies prostrate. Some of the women yam sellers started screaming in abject fear, relative to their abject poverty. So the robbers shot seven times into the air, apparently one shot per each of the nation's 7-Point Agenda. Helter-skelter, pandemonium, chaos.
The robbers bundled my driver and my brother into the car at gunpoint, while another robber took the wheel and headed Kaduna-wards. They were shouting: 'Where is the security?' 'We are going to kill you!' while continuously beating my people on the head and body with the butts of guns. The robbers were told there was no security in the car, yet the robbers continued to beat the heavens out of them. When they realised that there was really no electronic security in the car, such as could shut off the fuel supply, they made their way into a nearby bush, gave my people more thorough beating, took everything they had on them - caps, wristwatches, money, handsets, slippers, glasses, everything - beat them some more and pushed them out of the car. They ordered them not to look back, or they would be shot. They did not, could not.
The robbers drove back onto the road. Eyewitnesses report that they drove on the wrong side of the road for several kilometres. It is said they were even accosted by a police patrol, but they apparently explained themselves. They continued to drive on that same one-way until they reached a u-turn. They then took the way back to Abuja; glaringly passing by the yam-point they had a few minutes earlier attacked at gun-point. Everyone saw them drive by at top speed. But alhamdu lillah no life was lost, and there was no major injury. Except for the trauma, the shock, the absolute disbelief that such a thing could be perpetrated at 12 noon on a major highway by robbers who did not even care to don masks! By the Rule of Law! By Lawlessness!
Several months ago, I had lamented on these pages how my South African medical doctor friend, Dr. Fakroodin, once stated that in his country, the innocent lives behind bars and the criminals who should be the ones behind bars, roam free.
