That was in 2001 when I was at the University of Kwazulu-Natal, Durban. During those days, South Africa's major commercial centre, Johannesburg, used to be referred to as the world's crime capital. Behavioural scientists had always rationalised South Africa's raging crime rate as a backlash from black Africans who had been so short-changed during the apartheid years.
When I left South Africa and returned home fully, I realised that if that country was the frying pan as far as insecurity is concerned, my own country is the fire. In no time, I had learnt how to enhance personal security. I started out with seven padlocks on my main gate which doubles as both a garage and entry door. To be able to enter my garage and park my car, I would need to struggle with a bunch of keys so heavy and cumbersome no pocket could easily carry them. The locks and the keys are numbered for 'ease of reference' and it has become quite a task going through the same mundane motion every evening and morning.
In almost every bedroom in Nigeria today, there are layers upon layers of steel bars and wire grids of all kinds, all assembled in the vain hope that we sleep and wake up alive the next morning. This state of siege which Nigeria's insecurity has landed us in is enough to conclude that we all now live in maximum security prisons in our own homes. Not that these safeguards are of any use when the men of the underworld come a-visiting (and may ALLAH perish them!) Nigerian armed robbers have become so sophisticated as to have precision equipment with which they easily cut through heavy steel and all types of barricades to gain access to people's lives and property. Most Nigerians know that all the safeguards we employ are there just for the emotional reassurance. For most Nigerians, there is this foreboding dread that it is just a matter of time before it comes to their turn to pay their 'dues' in this most horrible of all injustices: armed robbery. No one sleeps with any assurance of safety and security in this country, except for the very few who occupy the hallowed spaces called State or Government Houses.
A lighted environment is a securer environment. But to make matters worse, due to inadequate electricity, Nigeria is the darkest country on earth (both figuratively and literally). Since there is no light, most people just go to bed as a perfunctory requirement of the human body, not to relax or sleep, but just to wait for tomorrow. Most people now sleep only after returning from the dawn, or subh, prayer, when it is light, Inna Lillahi Wa Inna Ilaihi Raji'un!
The Yam-Point Gun-Point Tafa robbery of Sunday March 2 at noon was not the first in this writer's experience. In 2000, fresh from another sojourn overseas, I was a victim of highway robbery on the same Abuja-Kaduna road. The 'operation' occurred at dusk when it was still light, not too far from the Kaduna-end of the tollgate. My then driver, David of the Convoy, was so traumatised that I had to act driver for several days afterwards. It was then that I learnt from David the Christian equivalent of our Inna Lillahi Wa Inna Ilaihi Raji'un! He had been chanting 'By the blood of Jesus'!
Back then in 2000, I had the next morning after the robbery, gone to see the then Kaduna State Commissioner of Police to lodge a complaint. When I explained to him what happened and where, his reaction was more shocking than the robbery itself. After he himself described the contours of the road and the surrounding bushes to ensure he had got the correct venue, to which I concurred, the CoP had said: "Shegu! A daidai nan suke wannan aika-aika" (Bastards! That's where they perpetrate their dastardly acts). He even knew the place, which meant it was preventable.
That spot just before the Kaduna-end of the tollgate on the Abuja to Kaduna expressway had been notorious for several years. Since my own traumatic experience, robberies have occurred in the exact same place: a senior military officer was killed there; two Kano politicians (both incidentally former presidential candidates) were attacked there at different times; so was a journalist friend; so was an academic friend; so was so was so was...Yet for several years, the police did not deem it fit or proper to establish a permanent station or outpost at that place. One wonders whether one will ever be safe in this country. Insecurity and electricity are our two greatest failures as a nation. When one is not safe in his or her own home or road, what success can any government claim? First experience in 2000 and now another in 2008, a period of two terms which have not been enough to address the two most important ingredients of development: security and electricity.
But by far the most traumatic of all crimes is to be robbed and have your wife or daughter violated by these ugly, despicable vermins called armed robbers. Perhaps in anticipation of this traumatic experience, Prophet Muhammad (upon whom be peace) had said that whoever dies defending his (and his family's) honour would be admitted by ALLAH directly to heaven. Armed robbers belong in the opposite abode: hell. For the robber-violator, the punishment should be two-fold: castration, then death! A former Nigerian justice minister had once suggested that a violator should be made to forfeit the tool which he used to perpetrate his crime. Aye, seconded!
I remember one such traumatic news item I had had to edit while a radio producer at the BBC in the late 1990s. One of our correspondents had filed in a report of an armed robbery incident from a Northern city. Part of the report was an actuality; the voice of one of the arrested armed robbers confessing to the robbery and also, Inna Lillahi Wa Inna Ilaihi Raji'un, confessing to violating the woman of the house. A colleague and I insisted that that portion of the interview be excised. We reasoned that since the interview with the State Police Commissioner had mentioned the identity of the house, it would amount to double jeopardy for the family to allow the robber's violation-confession onto the airwaves. That could kill the victim, we argued. But other colleagues reasoned that as 'news is sacred' in the Western sense, any excision would be self-censorship and that the world should be allowed to hear the tragedy called armed robbery. Back and forth went the argument until finally our side won and that particular portion was discarded.
All that now fades into history as the trauma of Sunday March 2, hits home. In the broad daylight of 12 noon. At Tafa, the most crowded of all roadside towns on the Abuja to Kaduna expressway. At a yam point. At gun point. We had dutifully reported to the police (and I must remember here to thank the Kano Police PRO Baba Mohammed, my one-time student at Bayero University Kano, who is taking the matter personally and coordinating the search for my car with his colleagues in other states. Baba is the reason why one could notice I have been so mild on the police in the foregoing paragraphs).
Irony of ironies! Just last Saturday, a Kano-based societal reorientation agency had convened a Public Forum (called Zauren Shawara in Hausa) to discuss, you guessed it, INSECURITY! The Zaure was chaired by a one-time AIG now District Head of Albasu in Kano State, Alhaji Bashir Albasu. Papers were presented by the Kano Police Commissioner (represented by his PRO Baba), the Kano Hisba Commander-General and an academician. I was host of the event and had ranted and raved at the nation's insecurity. I didn't know they could hear me so far away at Tafa!
As at the time of filing this report, evening of Thursday, March 6 2008, there has been no report of the sighting of my car, the ash-coloured KIA CERATO with registration number KANO AA 723 WRA. Many sympathisers are already urging me to 'Just Let Go', and count this experience among the reasons 'Why My Life Expectancy Is So Low'. Some wicked ones even suggest that 'Tomato Faces' need a dose of reality! Fie!
My KIA! My Ash-Coloured KIA. Ash! Remnant of fire. Hellfire. For the armed robbers. Allah Ya isa!

Original_Search_Title: Clerics Dedicate Ugochukwu Over Governor's Task
Headline: Nigeria: Clerics Dedicate Ugochukwu Over Governor's Task
Body_Text:
Umuahia — Chief Onyema Ugochukwu has been anointed and dedicated to God in preparation for taking over the mantle of leadership in Abia State, following the tribunal ruling, which returned him as the duly elected governor of Abia State.
The ceremony was performed at a thanksgiving service organised at his Umule Ohuhu country home as part of activities to ccelebrate his victory at the tribunal.
Bishop Dauda Maiwaazi of the Wonders Chapel Abuja, said the "spiritual enthronement and anointing would give Ugochukwu the power to contain pressure from false friends and enemies"
He explained that the religious ceremony was necessary because most times when elected leaders enter government with good intentions they get misled by bad advisers and political associates.
In his sermon, the Reverend Benson Ezem said that Abia has started to witness the redemption of God, adding that there would be peace and prosperity in the land.
He likened the redemption of Abia as a moving train which nobody can stop, adding that Ugochukwu is the divine tool that would be used to make Abia truly God's own state.
The thanksgiving service, which attracted 17 clerics from different denominations, also featured the anointing of Ugochukwu's wife, Joyce, Ugochukwu's running mate, Chinwe Nwanganga and his wife.
Addressing the large crowd that attended the service, Ugochukwu expressed gratitude to members of his party who had remained steadfast and hopeful
throughout the long drawn legal battle to recover his mandate.
He said: "In the normal course of things in our country, when one party 'loses' an election, politicians tend to gravitate towards the 'victorious' party, which in a sense has both the proverbial yam and the knife. But in this case, our party members
have remained faithful and resolute, refusing to give up, despite threats and inducements."
Meanwhile the Abia State chapter of the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) still basking in the euphoria of its victory at the tribunal has described the tribunal ruling that returned its governorship candidate as "epoch-making judgment"
Briefing newsmen on the outcome of the legal battle the acting chairman of the party, Reverend Benson Ezem said God has answered the cries of Abians and
liberated them from nine years of bondage, which started from the administration of Orji Uzor Kalu extending to its offshoot.

Original_Search_Title: Join Hands to Fight Bribery & Corruption- NCCE
Headline: Ghana: Join Hands to Fight Bribery & Corruption- NCCE
Body_Text:
Tamale — The Northern Regional Director of the National Commission on Civic Education (NCCE), Mr. Issah A. Nasagri says it is about time stakeholders in government and within the private sector join in the fight against bribery and Corruption, which affects the economic fortunes of the country.
He noted that, it was the responsibility of all right thinking Ghanaians to fight vigorously against the bribery and corruption menace at all sectors of the economy.
Mr. Nasagri said this at a two-day workshop in Tamale organized jointly by the Ghana Integrity Initiative (GII)- a local chapter of Transparency International and the NCCE to promote democracy and good governance in Ghana. The workshop, which is still underway at the Radach Memorial Centre, is being held under the theme "Fighting Corruption-An Agenda for Democracy and Good Governance".
The purpose of the workshop is to create awareness about the negative effects of corruption and to sensitize citizens at the regional level to demand responsiveness, accountability and transparency from leaders and institutions at their communities and the regional administration structures. It is also meant to collaborate with development partners to help government and the citizenry to put in place more systematic and sustainable reforms for long term goal of eradicating corruption and poverty especially at community levels.
Mr. Nasagri however stressed the need for government of the day not to hesitate to sack corrupt public office holders who may be found guilty of any corrupt act. The long silence was broken when some of the participants censured traditional rulers in the Northern Region for consistently dishing out gifts such as cows, guinea fowls, goats, yam and smocks among others to government officials who were entrusted to execute projects in their communities.
They observed that such acts amounted to bribery and corruption in the highest order and advised the chiefs to adopt ways of supervising projects instead of giving gigts and accepting same from the contractors. The Participants maintained that, it was the duty of the government to provide the people with social amenities and other developmental projects.
Madam Linda Ofori-Kwafo, Programmes Manager of GII gave a brief history about her outfit, which she said works to increase levels of accountability, transparency and monitoring the performance of key institutions, and pressing for the necessary reforms in a non-partisan manner.
She however called on Parliament, as a matter of urgency, to pass the Freedom of Information bill to complement the Whistleblowers Act to allow for greater access to public information and to encourage the exposure of wrong doing in public offices as well as foster transparency in governance and pubic administrations.
The Northern Regional Minister Alhaji Mustapha Ali Idris and the Metropolitan Chief Executive (MCE) for Tamale, Mohammed Adam Amin-Anta graced the occasion.

Original_Search_Title: Achebe - Why He Was No Literary Genius
Headline: Nigeria: Achebe - Why He Was No Literary Genius
Body_Text:
Nairobi — GOING BY THE preparations taking place, one might think the celebrations have something to do with a beloved monarch: Events have been lined up for the whole of 2008; a commemoration scheduled for April has been sponsored by the New York City Hall; Western universities are vying to host speakers as the media jostle for interviews; and Postsecondary Education Network International is selling lecture tickets online.
How things change. When Chinua Achebe sent the handwritten manuscript of Things Fall Apart to a typing agency in London in 1957, the agency appeared in no hurry to work on the document. It did not bother to respond to Achebe's numerous letters enquiring about the progress of the document, and it was not until an Englishwoman who was then his boss at the Nigerian Broadcasting Corporation travelled to London and threatened the agency that it quickly typed and sent back the manuscript.
But that was just the first hurdle. At the time Achebe sent the document to Heinemann for publication, there were few novels on Africa by Africans, so they could not find a specialist in African literature to evaluate it.
Luckily, someone at Heinemann knew a London School of Economics professor who had been "over there."
His verdict: "The best first novel since the war."
On the strength of this recommendation, Heinemann editor Allan Hills took a risk and printed a handful of copies in what many saw as a crazy move that could bankrupt him. However, to everyone's surprise, the copies sold out.
Emboldened, Hills took an even greater gamble and printed the novel in paperback.
Years later, hills was to receive a knighthood for starting the African Writers' Series. And that long-suffering manuscript, now in book form, was to sell 11 million copies.
Something had gone right. A void had been identified, and once Achebe took a step to fill it, a torrent of talent flooded the Heinemann offices - Ngugi wa Thiong'o, Okot p'Bitek, Ayi Kwei Armah.
For the publishing world in general, it was as if a new world had been discovered and colonised. African literature introduced an entirely new, exciting language and narrative to world literature.
Professor Ernest Emenyonu, Chair of Africana Studies at the University of Michigan-Flint, wrote that "Chinua Achebe's remarkable influence on contemporary African literature is as much in the establishment of the art of the African novel in his fiction as it is in the articulation of African poetics and aesthetics in his extra-fictional pronouncements."
Achebe has been called the father of African literature, while later writers have been described as the "sons of Achebe."
TO DATE, HE HAS RECEIVED some 25 honorary doctorates and was recently named as one of the 100 most influential writers of the 20th Century.
As the Igbo praise-singers in his novels say, this man went to the land of spirits and wrestled the hydra-headed spirit to the ground and returned home unscathed!
It is not that he wrote great books - although they are not bad - but it is the historical context within which his writing appeared that makes him important. It is important to separate Achebe's achievement from his literature.
His achievement: For millennia after Moses led the Israelites out of Egypt, the land of black people had occupied a permanent place in the written word of the Judeo-Christian world.
But it was a place of disgrace, an impression created by some interpretations of the Bible. Black skin became associated with evil, underachievement, laziness and lack of self-will. It justified slavery and colonialism. In literature, the past 500 years have seen the condemnation of the black man, from Shakespeare's Othello, Emile Bronte's Heathcliff and in the work of Joseph Conrad, who compared the black man to an animal.
In less totalitarian terms, Africans have been mocked in the books of Heminway, in Joyce Cary's infamous novel, Mr Johnson and defamed in the books of V.S. Naipaul.
The Chronicle of Higher Education's Review wrote of the times in which Achebe's novel appeared: "Literary conceptions of the continent were still steeped in reductive romance, primitivism, and colonial sentiment. Providing an expansive alternative to those conceptions was, to say the least, a mighty accomplishment for an author still in his 20s."
Until then, black people everywhere were under white control. This attack on personal freedom also came with attack on the African body: the hair, shape of the nose, size of lips, the caricatures of wide-eyed whites set in black skin.
The assertion of a black point of view needed to be a massive counter-attack. The urgency for a return to colonialism has been a constant threat. Achebe came to be associated with this call to arms. In 1975, at a lecture, when he launched an attack on the legacy of Conrad in a lecture, he was also raising an alternative literary point of view. Since then, serious discussions of Achebe have also been serious discussions of Conrad, and this says something about the stature of Achebe himself: for decades, Conrad was venerated for telling "the truth." Essentially, this truth is asserted in Heart of Darkness. It says that "?all that Africa has? is screaming, shrieking, howling and animal sounds?"
Achebe himself has been rather cool about this, telling an interviewer in the Paris Review that, "?if you don't like someone's story, you write your own. If you don't like what somebody says, you say what it is you don't like." By the time his book came out, some of this Conradian truth, which was itself Darwinian cynicism, had been somewhat toned down by the two wars. There was confusion and humble soul-searching among Western intellectuals about what had gone wrong. New points of view were needed.
So, had it appeared before the Second World War, Things Fall Apart might have been ignored.
It would be too much to call a man like wa Thiongo, a "son of Achebe" because, although his book came out later, books take long to take shape in writers' minds, and Ngugi appears to have formed his ideas long before he heard of Achebe. Writers like Wole Soyinka and Okot p'Bitek achieved more in terms of creativity.
What happened was that Achebe crossed the line first. When you read his books outside the colonial context, Achebe comes across as a small-scale entrepreneur of the novel. He was no literary industrialist like Soyinka. He was a retailer of proverbs, bucolic wisdom and anecdotes. It is his presentation of his indictment of colonialism that made him. This may have prevented him, as well as writers like the late Senegalese Sembène Ousmane and Ugandan Okot p'Bitek, from seeing narrative possibilities after colonialism. This is where the criticism that Achebe had little talent beyond fighting colonialism appears to stick.
In contrast, his compatriot, Wole Soyinka, went beyond plays such as Death and the King's Horseman, a work with thematic similarities to Arrow of God, and works such as Ake: The years of Childhood and Set Forth at Dawn.
Telling purely human tales - of men as individuals rather than as spokespersons for an age - was not among Achebe's talents. There is a leanness of narrative in his work. Of course, there is gripping passion and a serious scholarliness, which explain his popularity. But there is also a crippling lack of strength in his voice.
He has constantly been compared with Soyinka as the two vie for top position in African literature in the same manner Fyodor Dostoevsky has been measured against Leo Tolstoy for the world's greatest novelist. It is silly, for the moon is not the sun; Achebe is a man of intense feeling who walks the night rescuing the soul of a condemned race where Soyinka basks in the glare of his own genius.
He went for a single subject. Real literary genius was not his forte. He goes at a subject in a monotone that can dull the mind. His characters are carved out in two dimensions. That third dimension- of characters coming outside the book's themes to act in a world of senses rather than of social change - is tragically missing.
But if he sold his literary potatoes by the kilo or salted groundnuts by the spoonful, then at least, he was no McDonald's, inflating his customer's waistlines with dubious fat concentrates.
And this is the truth about books: towering literary works, like war and peace, are admired for the brilliance of the authors. But the power to change society, to transform a world-view, belongs to simplicity: simplicity of delivery, style, language, and even ideas.
The directness with which Achebe wrote and the singularity of his narrative structure fudged with little. His committed novels depicting a convalescent continent connected so directly with readers that he is one of the very few novelists to sell 11 million copies of a single book.
Achebe is the common man's author. Barring ideological and theoretical interpretations, he is something of a folk hero who operated on the ground where cultural revolutions are fought. His books are simple; village folksingers come to the literary metropolis with drum and flute and plain-speaking poets sing about village girls, about clever eneke birds and evoke the smell of yams and palm wine.
But African literature has moved on as a new generation of writers continues struggling to find a voice to describe the new millennium. It is unfortunate that Achebe is being commemorated in the West, but little is being done in Africa to recognise him.

Original_Search_Title: Wakaman - Obj, the True Evil Genius
Headline: Nigeria: Wakaman - Obj, the True Evil Genius
Body_Text:
Abuja — Ayinde Barrister is a great man. I am no fan of the Yoruba Fuji exponent, but I have heard on radio, some lyrics from one of his songs which goes thus - they call him a madman, he eats pounded yam with goat-leg/ They call him mad man/ He eats amala with ram leg/ They call him mad man/ He climbs horses in the daytime and women at night.
I do not know who he refers to, but of recent, I've found Nigerians vilify Obasanjo with such venom and hatred while the man continues to ride smooth on the intelligence of all his detractors.
Take the imminent PDP convention for example. There are several contenders but the contest has been narrowed to Anyim Pius Anyim and his erstwhile governor, Sam Egwu - between the newbreed and Obj's cronies. In essence, all the other over half a dozen aspirants, (if you believe the speculators) are already gonners. It takes an evil genius to bestrode the political landscape like a ubiquitous maverick - Obj.
Other contenders have traversed the length and breath of this country, pitching their own positions and selling their own manifesto. But we are told that only Garrison Commanders will determine who win the race. As I write, nobody knows how the eventual winner will be determined - will it be the way Ahmadu Ali was foisted on the party under Obj or will the PDP live up to par this time around, allowing delegates to cast their votes and let the votes count? Take a cue from Iwu!
Obasanjo, the man who has become everybody's whipping boy has dragged himself into the fray by accepting a constitutional (PDP constitution - silly!) amendment which gave him power to oversee things as Chairman of the Board of Trustees. He has said that nobody can rout him from his exalted position, which by the way we must clearly state he needs to keep himself away from jail. If you ask his detractors, his fingers and mouth are soiled.
Speculators have also alleged that Obasanjo sees farther from behind than his detractors. For instance, there were several gladiators in 2006/7 who believe they are the best candidate for the presidency. Most of them said they were close to the seat of power, they were mostly blatantly corrupt but overtly loyal Obj subjects who got their approval to run. That was before Obasanjo brought out his joker - Umaru Yar'Adua. The silence that greeted that move dinned the proverbial din of the graveyard. Rumbling, but the genius had his way - evil!
But according to speculators, that was just the icing on the cake. Obj chose Yar'Adua because he believed that the reclusive Katsina governor might kick the bucket before swearing in. That was after it became apparent that Ibrahim Mantu will not be able to deliver his Third Term poisoned chalice. So if you ask
Theophilus Danjuma, he got what he couldn't through the law by the back door. Danjuma, a quondam friend of the former president believes that Obj is the drummer remotely controlling Yar'Adua's tadpole from the depths of the political sea.
It does not end there. Obasanjo went further to make sure that David Mark, a surrogate ex-soldier and loyalist was elected President of the Senate. So, if Kutigi's boys eventually push out Yar'Adua whenever, its not Kutigi, regarded as too tough to be lobbied that wins the ticket, but Mark. Danjuma and the G-21 cannot stop that happening except of course, they are able to influence the tribunal to nullify Mark's 'electoral victory.' The way things are going, there are enough spanners in the tribunal's works that they may not meet the deadline for the determination of petitions. Obj's joker works anyday.
But if Mark is thrown out - on, during or before the Supreme Court judgement, the acting President becomes Dimeji Bankole? Wrong, Dimeji is one year short of the age requirement for that position. So, Kutigi is the likely candidate. Well, it will not look good for the man whose boys throw out the president to take the job no matter what way you look at it. But then, age is only a question of calculation - Obj himself does not know his true age and depending on what is in Bankole's INEC form (again Iwu could make it look good), he may get it. But is Bankole an Obj man? Well, it will appear from the way things have gone (in the field of speculation), that the duo are not good friends.
But wait a minute, Ali sent out Bankole from a lawful meeting, then Obj later apologised and embraced Bankole and then to cap it all, Yar'Adua gave Bankole a 'party honour' masqueraded in national garb. So, is this politricks or is it another geni of the evil? Big question for speculators. But methinks we are too eager to give people titles, and neither Francis Arthur Nzeribe (of blessed memory) nor Ibrahim Badamasi Babangida qualify as evil genius. The real evil genius is Obj. According to Danjuma, Obj nearly rejected his offer of head of state after Murtala, but he endeared himself to logic by handing over to Shagari and meandered his way back as an elected president. Now, he is hung on our shoulder as a de jure president, coute que coute, as the French will say.
We are not about to get rid of the real evil genius who has vowed that nobody can remove him as BOT Chairman. This mad man is the genius of Barrister's ballad and we are the real fools!

Original_Search_Title: Lagos Night Fire Razes 3 Factories, Mile 12 Market
Headline: Nigeria: Lagos Night Fire Razes 3 Factories, Mile 12 Market
Body_Text:
Lagos — CASH, goods and properties running into hundreds of millions of naira were destroyed in a Friday night inferno that gutted about three factories, two warehouses and several lock-up shops at Sadiku Street, Ilasamaja area of Mushin, Lagos.
A popular fast food outlet in the area was also consumed by the fire.
Sources said but for the efforts of the men of Lagos State Fire Service, the rampaging fire would have extended to the Ilasamaja Branch of the Oceanic Bank International situated at Sadiku Bus Stop. It was gathered that some staff of the bank alerted the firemen and they responded and fought tirelessly to cut off the fire from extending to the bank.
Also yesterday morning, precisely between 3:00 a.m. and 7:00 a.m., a section of the popular Mile 12 Market in Kosofe Local Government Area of the state was razed by fire. Although none of the factories and warehouses consumed by the Ilasamaja fire said to belong to Indians and Lebanese nationals had any visible signboard to identify them, Sunday Vanguard gathered they were involved in the production and importation of household wares and cooking utensils such as aluminum plates, basins, buckets, spoons, slippers and plastic products, nylons, televisions, tape recorders, DVD and other electronics products.
Sunday Vanguard gathered that one of the warehouses started stocking electronics just over a week ago, while another brought in three trailers of imported aluminum household products few days and was planning to bring more within days before the fire. The affected lock-up shops housed the fast food outlet, supermarkets and electronic shops.
However, one of the warehouses that stored imported lace materials was miraculously left untouched even though the fire burnt surrounding warehouses, factories and lock-up shops. Men of the of fire service were still seen Saturday morning battling to put out the fire which was said to have started around 8:00 p.m. on Friday. Sunday Vanguard also gathered that there was a clash between a detachment of the police drafted to keep watch and prevent looting and some street urchins (area boys) in the area.
The cause of the fire was not immediately known as eye witnesses gave contradicting causes. An eyewitness claimed the fire started from the nylon making factory and that when it started, some area boys around the area that noticed the fire informed the securityman who had locked the gates. According to the eyewitness, the area boys asked the securityman to open the gate so that they could put out the fire before it degenerated, but the securityman refused, noting that since it was an insignificant fire, it would quench by itself.
However, while a lady who claimed to be a staff of a burnt slippers making factory attributed the cause of the fire to a gas explosion in the kitchen of the fast food outlet, another eyewitness said the fire started following a spark in one of the warehouses when the Power Holding Company of Nigeria (PHCN) restored light in the night. The eyewitness, who preferred anonymity, told Sunday Vanguard that the fire spread rapidly due to the combustible materials stored in the warehouses and the factory.
He dismissed the idea that the fire started from the fast food outlet, arguing that if the fire had started from there, there was no way life could not have been lost because people were still eating in the restaurant when the fire started. According to him, "I can tell you that even those in the kitchen and in the main restaurant could have found it difficult to escape. In fact, some of them assisted the operators of the restaurant to recover the wares and other valuables before the fire got to the restaurant."
The eyewitness lamented that, apart from the bank, none of burnt factories and warehouses had fire extinguishers or underground water tank from which they could draw water to fight the fire. He lamented that they had to be shuttling from a water point at a distance to the fire scene and that even owners of the affected companies refused to part with money to source water from a tank water close by.
The firemen, who had two fire fighting trucks at the scene, said they battled all through Friday night to Saturday morning to put out the fire. One of them told Sunday Vanguard that if appropriate government agencies especially the factory inspection unit of the ministry of labour had been doing its works; probably the fire would have been averted.
He doubted if government was aware of the clusters of factories and warehouses without any visible signposts for identification. However, one of the owners of the burnt lock-up shops alleged that the firemen came without water and demanded N20,000 to enable them buy water to fight the fire.
Meanwhile, the fire which razed the Mile 12 Market in the early hours of yesterday disrupted business transactions in the market. Eyewitnesses said the fire was first noticed at about 3:00 a.m. and by 7:00 a.m. it was still raging. However, prompt arrival of policemen prevented looters from having a field day as they kept people at bay.
A story building housing a mosque and other shops was also seen burning at about 6 a.m. The fire affected many lock-up shops and the entire butchers stall. The main market where wholesale goods are sold was not affected but transactions were disrupted.
Prices of major food stuff such as pepper, yams and vegetables remained stable but the price of tomatoes soared as that section razed by the fire was close to the burnt area. Policemen prevented all vehicles from going through the service lane close to the market . The burnt section of the market is the retail section of Mile 12 market which deals in wholesale of food items .

Original_Search_Title: Sharia - 46 Amputation Sentences Await Ratification
Headline: Nigeria: Sharia - 46 Amputation Sentences Await Ratification
Body_Text:
Bauchi — Forty-six persons sentenced to amputation for various offences under the Bauchi State Shariah law will know their fate after the state's Shariah Consultative Council concludes its studies of the sentences and make, appropriate recommendation to the state government.
Acting secretary of the council, Alhaji Ishiaku Tijjani, who made this known to the media, disclosed that the convicts were currently awaiting ratification of their sentences by the state government as provided by the Sharia law.
Tijjani, who said the council as an advisory body was currently studying all the sentences, the laws and judgements, with a view to making recommendation to government, affirmed that the exercise would guide the council to advise the state government on the right way to implement the legal code.
Among the culprits is a 46-year old yam seller, Husseini Maidoya who allegedly chopped off the leg of his wife, one Amina Husseini over alleged infidelity. Maidoya was in 2002 sentenced to amputation without the application of anaesthesia.
It was learnt that since the introduction of the Islamic legal code in 2001, six persons have been sentenced by stoning. Among those awaiting stoning are Yunusa Rafin-Chiyawa (38) convicted in 2002 for allegedly making love 13 times to his friend's wife, and Umaru Tori (52) convicted in 2004 for impregnating his 15-year old step daughter.
Tijjani assured that the 18-member council would soon commence a review of all Sharia activities in Bauchi State.

Original_Search_Title: Oshiomhole Backs Call for Obasanjo's Probe
Headline: Nigeria: Oshiomhole Backs Call for Obasanjo's Probe
Body_Text:
Former President of the Nigeria Labour Congress (NLC), Comrade Adams Oshiomhole has thrown his weight behind the agitation for a probe of the Chief Olusegun Obasanjo administration, saying Nigerians are entitled to know how clean he was while in office.
Besides, he is in support of the decision of President Umaru Yar'Adua not to spend any money again on the energy sector since electricity supply in the country has remained at its lowest ebb in spite of the huge amount of money devoted to the energy sector by his predecessor in the last eight years.
Oshiomhole who spoke with journalists at the Benin Airport on his way to Abuja said whosoever commits any crime should be dealt with, as nobody should be above the law.
His words, "I don't know about development but whoever has committed crime should be dealt with. Nobody should be above the law whether it is past or present."
"I really don't want to believe that corrupt practices in Nigeria have only taken place at the level of the states. After all, one of the reports of Transparency International says that 60% of corruption in Nigeria is in the presidency".
"So, the man who presided over it for 8years, people will like to know how clean he was. And if Yar'Adua says N10 billion was spent and Dimeji Bankole (Speaker, House of Representatives) says no, it was 16, whether it is 10 or 16 and we are still in the dark, somebody must account for that".
"Nigeria cannot make it as the 20th largest economy by the year 2020 if this level of abuse of public office continues. All those who administer these resources at the power sector, they are all enjoying themselves in various spheres of our national lives, so what lessons are you bequeathing to other people"?
"I think Yar'Adua took the correct attitude and say look, am not going to throw more money into this business of power because he is convinced and rightly too that you don't just throw money at a problem.
He called on President Yar'Adua to establish in a very transparent fashion what happened to the fund expended on the energy sector in spite of the Due Process Office, saying that a lot of procurement were made when there were no facilities on the ground to install them.
"They cannot install them. Somebody was just interested in procurement and the commission and the fraud that goes with it rather than giving power to Nigerians. If you excuse those kind of criminals, then you have no business arresting people in the state who stole yam and chicken when the man who stole the elephant is enjoying it".
Comrade Oshiomhole commended the judicial arm of government for its courage and genuine commitment to doing substantial justice.
He also expressed happiness that President Yar'Adua on his part, has ensured that once pronouncement were made, they were immediately implemented.
"The only way to avoid Kenyanising Nigeria is that people must believe that it's possible to get justice without recourse to the cutlass and the judiciary is therefore helping to protect the system and preserve it, otherwise Nigeria is almost out."

Original_Search_Title: Burden of Proof And Senator Nuhu Aliyu's Frustration
Headline: Nigeria: Burden of Proof And Senator Nuhu Aliyu's Frustration
Body_Text:
Abuja — Recently, Senator Nuhu Aliyu made a heroic statement on the floor of the upper legislative chamber only for him to reverse the statement when he was asked to hit the nail on the head. BEN ADOGA perused the statement against the background that the Senator may be acting under frustration.
In the past weeks, the nation has been stired by the allegation of the existence of 419ners in the National Assembly. What caught the attention of Nigerians most was the promise to name the 419 members of the National Assembly. Nigerians became curious and anxiously waited for the names of the 419 members in the National Assembly.
Serious as the issue was, it generated the kind of interest and attention Nigerians gave it because it came from a Senator of the Federal Republic of Nigeria, not just that still, but a man who rose through the ranks in the police force and was a different times in charge of CID and the Criminals Investigation Bureau, CIB. It would appear he knew what he was saying and what he was doing. Having served in the police at sensitive positions and now in the upper legislative chamber for about nine years now, Senator Nuhu Aliyu could not be said to be ignorant of the law or the consequences of his actions.
At the time when Nigerians waited for the all important Appropriation Bill to be passed, the upper legislative chamber succeeded in distracting the nation. Even as it is a well known fact that Nigerians, especially villagers at the grassroots where this pliticians come from, knew exactly those who have made money dubiously, rigged elections and even those who stole goats, yams or did not pass their school certificate examinations, but have fraudulently acquired degrees and are in the National Assembly today, they still waited for Nuhu Aliyu to name those he knew, especially as an ex-police man who should know better.
Unfortunately, at the middle of the matter, an anticlimax was what Nigerians got as Senator Nuhu Aliyu made a U-turn on the advise of his lawyers, at least he said so.
The Niger State PDP Senator is by all standards an elder statesman, well repected among his colleagues in the legislative and his community that has trusted him with mandate three times. He equally had a fruitful career in the police. The questions Nigerians are asking is, if Senator Nuhu Aliyu was not aware of his encumbarances before his outburst. Some simply say his excuse of being dragged to court is not tenable as he enjoys legislative imunity. Statements made on the flour of Senate, House of Representatives are not actionable.
Senator Nuhu Aliyu while appologising instead of naming the 419 legislators said he was advised by his lawyers to the contrary, and that if he did, he would have been in court. He explained that he knew that no matter the thoroughness of the investigation by the police, if such a culprit was not properly convicted by a court of competent jurisdication, the person could not rightly be termed a criminal or a 419ner.
The question Senator Anthony Agbo asked would have nailed the matter on the head. Since the elder-stateman senator was advised by his lawyers not to dare mention names. Does that mean that there were no 419ners in the National Assembly again, or that either the system or something else has constrained him from mentioning names.
Listening to Senator Lee Maeba, any discerning mind should understand the meta-language and the sub text of the matter. "He (Senator Nuhu Aliyu) has a big predicament, last week, in plenary, he would have mentioned names. I prevailed on him not to, till he saw his lawyers,"Lee Maeba stated. If he was that passonate and was ready to mention names, was it really the advise of his lawyers that changed his mind? Thouth Nuhu Aliyu made it clear that he received no threat from any where.
What is Nuhu Aliyu's predicament? A close look at the three time Senator suggests that he is rather frustrated. A man driven by the zeal to improve the society in his life time, and one who thinks given his wealth of experience he could contribute to the anti corruption struggle by hitting corruption, on the head is by expossing people in high places "known" to be or to have been criminals. But the law says till he is convicted by a court of law, he could not be termed criminal or 419ners. Here lies Nuhu Aliyu's frustration. To some, the Senator was simply playing god by arrogating to himself the powers of the court to declare some people quilty before the courts say so, or even when such persons may have been discharged and acquited by courts of competent jurisdiction.
For Senator Nuhu Aliyu, the persons he investigated were guilty by the facts available to him, but he is not the law court, that also is a frustration. It is like the proverbial story of a villagers taken to court for stealing a goat. At the law court, his lawyers argued that his client took a rope and at the end of the rope was a goat, so the "thief" was discharged and acquited. In the eyes of the law the man is not a thief, but to the villagers who caught him with the goat belonging to Mr. X, that man remains a theif.
Facts before the law may be different from the truth, but for the purpose of sanity in society. The law must remain law so that anarchy do not creep in, and Nuhu Aliyu is no law court, and no matter how he investigated and proved that Mr. XYZ, how senator or Rep member were guilty of offences charged, even if the senator or Hon XYZ bribed his way through and was not convicted, he remains not convicted and claims the title Hon or distinguished. If you think Senator XYZ stole your vote or your mandate, the law says he is still distinguished till the tribunals or the law courts extriguishes" him.
Some analysts, however, say that senator Aliyu's U-turn is cowardise, especially of him standing. They say it is his stock in trade, that he is used to through up issues and abandoning Nigerians when they are all ears. Making reference to the former deputy sentate president, Ibrahim Manatu's probe by the same committee investigating him now, he told Nigerians that he had proof, only for him not to prove his case when the chips were down. A columnists arger is that Nuhu Aliyu would have been courageous enough not to dissappoint Nigerians this time as he didn't have to go far looking for anwers. The newspaper columnists said Senator Aliyu would have taken the easy escape route and mentioned the well known and recent cases in the National Assebly. Recently, a honourable member was removed for not having his school certificate which he claimed to have, such cases still abound. Better still, the columnist asked why Senator Aliyu did not mention Ette-gate, Iyabogate alias Damilolala Akinlawan", Arimugate and several other colleques who have been sacked by the tribunal for stealing votes and mandate. He wold have just saved his head with these culprits rather than dissapoint Nigerians again, the columnist stated.
One thing still remains, even as celebrated as some of these cases are, there are there are yet to be conclusive for one to be sure that a court of competent jurisdiction has convicted such and such a person. Here also lies Nuhu Aliyu's frustrated to the grave, may be he should just write a book and rush to the court to secure an injunction against the burning of the book. Then the offenders will go to the courts for character assassination, the case will last till both the offenders and the offended to go their graves.

Original_Search_Title: I'm Not Scared of Assassination in Osun -Aregbesola
Headline: Nigeria: I'm Not Scared of Assassination in Osun -Aregbesola
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Action Congress (AC) governorship candidate in Osun State in the April 2007 election, Engr. Rauf Aregbesola has been on 'exile' in Lagos for over six months.
In this interview Sheriff Balogun speaks on his plan to return to the state and re-energise his political machinery despite alleged threats to his life and those of his supporters
You have been in this struggle for the actualisation of what you think should be your mandate for some months now. How has it been and what are the challenges? Have you suffered any trauma or any kind of difficulties?
Well, it's been quite hectic. But you must know that leaders are not made out of nothing but leaders are putting through tough experience and actions. So, whatever might been my experience before the election, after the election is quite expected and normal. You don't get peel of yam on your skin without serious itching. I've taken what happened as part of my normal course of development. Its difficult exertion that really was of significance. The remarkable aspect of the experience so far is the unrelenting efforts of those who we know robbed us of victory, who stole the mandate of the people, to eliminate us, to get rid of us.
It's the threat to ones life and the continued harassment, persecution, oppression, terrorism and open violence against our supporters, our party members and ourselves that are the source of worry.
So where is the source of threat to your life you've been talking about?
From Governor Oyinlola. I have said it several times. On two occasions I addressed pressmen, I held press conferences in which we even gave specific names and identity of vehicles that were to be used to eliminate us. It was not a secret, and we made those claims openly. We got information about those who are being hunted for elimination. I want people to really understand that much as one respects the military profession, there are some elements there that are a pain on the integrity of the pride of those who should be seen as officers of the military. It is no secret, and we are not gainsaying that we have a fictitious opponent who is Olagunsoye Oyinlola. He has not hidden his animosity against us.
August 2006, I was set up for elimination in Oshogbo during the Oroki Festival. Masked men shot at me, I narrowly escaped because I was also equipped. During the campaign, my father's house was vandalized and there was no justification for it. I was not there, I don't live in my father's house. Aged women, relations at the residence of the family compound were all hospitalized and this was made public, pictures of it were all over the papers. A week to the election, I was attacked by three armed groups. I could only return to Ilesha on the eve of election, 12.30pm. So what are we talking about? And it has never stopped.
After the election reports got to us that efforts were afoot to still eliminate me because it was clear to them that they had no victory. In all the places they awarded themselves outrageous figures they had to kill. There was no single location in which they have porous electoral victory where there were no assassination. Is it in Ikirun, Igbajo, Ede , Ife? All over the places, Osu, even Iwara they killed and the records are there. So, what we are saying is not just the question of presumption, it is reality. And I challenge him to contradict me.
The real issue, the real cause of concern, what has fagged my energy is not the efforts at gathering evidence, mobilizing lawyers and witnesses to prove my case and ensure victory at the tribunal, no! That's not it, the real worries, the real cause of anxiety, the real trauma is the threat to my life, the threat to the lives of my witnesses, harassment of my supporters, party leaders and members and brazen use of terror and violence in places where our people are strongly with us. That is the major challenge to me and that is the cause of our worries.
Can you tell us whether the tempo of violence has gone down and secondly if you are planning to re-launch your political activities to the state very soon?
Well, it depends on how you want to qualify violence. The siege on Osun is continuous. An unpopular government is foisted on the people and when a government that does not get its mandate from the people is in power it can only survive through the use of violence. So, violence is a permanent feature in Osun by virtue of the fact that an unwanted government is in place.
There is no way Oyinlola can be in power in Osun and violence will be eliminated. Oyinlola being am interloper, being unwanted and consequently be expunged will naturally promote violence. And that explains why since our emergence in April 2005, Oyinlola in his desperate efforts to enforce himself and his administration on the people has not spared anything. Since April, you can watch the temperature of political activities, without them admitting that he is the cause of the political instability. He would blame every other person except himself. In fact, he is fond of blaming me, when in reality all I do is promote healthy democratic engagements. And I would want all right thinking people to actually assess our style and method of engagements. We have introduced a sophisticated, enlightened democratic culture, we enunciated our programmes, we addressed the needs of the people and we mobilised them on those things.
We have never been involved in any thing that is violent, our engagement is civil, our approach is calm, and our activity is popular. I do not believe that we have in any way instigated violence but violence is prevalent in Osun because the government that is in position is unpopular, is unwanted, and is incompetent. That further compounded the problem because it's incompetent and visionless.
When you have a combination of these weaknesses the implication is that there will be restiveness. Instead of him to admit that his competence and visionless could turned economic depression and when you have economic depression the other end of it is anger, and when there is anger you cannot rule out instability.
How soon do you hope to re-launch your political activities in Osun state?
I didn't use the word re-launch. What I said was that I would soon be going back to Osun to motivate my people, encourage them to keep hope alive. Political activities are unending. I've been communicating with the people, my party is active among the people contrary to whatever insinuation or belief that is prevalent within the opposition camp.
The truth is that we only instructed our people to boycott the local government election, the obnoxious September 15 local government election. And there was compliance all over the state, the people of the state totally complied. I don't think anybody or party could have in any way swayed the people in that manner.
Given what you term unfriendly atmosphere in Osun State, how confident are you that your going back to Osun State will not generate more violence. Don't you think the opposition would want to attack you and probably attack your followers?
The truth is we have told the world that we are going to Osun and that we are going to engage our people within the confines of the law, we will lead our people lawfully, we are in a democracy. In any democratic setting there's no timetable for political engagements. For as long as there is a constitutionally given right to freely associate, you must and you should be active politically. My party members meet regularly at their ward, local government and state levels. I'm also a part of that process. I have a case in the tribunal, but I think the time has come for me to be active in the tribunal. I should be there like any other person and as a Nigerian I don't think there's any law limiting my presence anywhere. That's just what we are saying.
The need to let my media friends know my motive is to alert the public to the threat that we suspect would come from the opposition. And because of the antecedent in which they did not spare any attempt at attacking and eliminating me. So, before I am assassinated I want you all to know that we have free citizens of Nigeria having withdrawn ourselves for this while and having been in Lagos for that much we want to physically engage our members, supporters and admirers in Osun politically.
Can we know the position of your case at the Tribunal and the level of your faith in the Judiciary?
One, the case is progressing very, very well. We are still calling our witnesses, and that is how it is. The Judiciary, generally, nationally has proved itself very well, if we have to be honest, with landmark judgments that reposed democratic confidence. One cannot but be hopeful in the ability of Judiciary to maintain justice. I have confidence in the ability of the Judiciary to ensure justice, especially with the way the Judiciary has carried itself so far. The Judiciary has done well in guaranteeing the survival of democracy in Nigeria with so many landmark remarkable judgments in defence of democracy and in the assurance of protection for the weak.
Do you still believe you would govern Osun State?
I have no doubt about it, I won the election. It's not just a matter of believing alone. I won the election, I am the one popularly elected by the people of Osun to govern them. Those who are there today are simply pretending, they didn't win any election. In all the places where they got their concocted victory, there were not just injuries, there were deaths, people had to die for the PDP to have its porous results with which they are today forcefully there. There was no way PDP could have been returned, their governor was unpopular, their group was unpopular, and they were unpopular because they lack vision. They could not motivate the people, the people themselves know their desire and they want an end to their ambition and difficulties. There was just no way for PDP to have been returned.
Can you comment on what Governor Oyinlola's recent admonition in The Sun newspaper that you should be a law abiding citizen.
I don't know what he means by that, but with what I've told you one should ask, who is lawless among the two of us? Let me do a quick recap, in 2006, I was set up on the Oroki/Igboki Road and was detained for between two and three weeks at the end of it. A court of competent jurisdiction set me free and awarded me a damage of about N5 million, which they gave to the police. They've not yet settled that court debt, but one thing is key that I've not had any breach with the laws of the land. I operate at all time within the confines of the laws of Nigeria . I do not engage in open political activities without obtaining the necessary permits.
Besides that, I ensure that the police are notified of our activities and I ensure that they provide their presence in all my activities. I have policemen with me right now as I'm here from Lagos Police Command and whenever I'm going to Osun I demand for police escort. So, a lawless person will not in any way do this, and since I began these activities I've not been to any part of Osun without first intimating all security agencies. That is a standard practice, which is why I give police and other agencies advance notice of my presence anywhere in Osun.
As a follow up sir, there's portion in the publication which s stated that you are to be held personally responsible for any breach of the peace in the state, as a result of your plan to commence campaign even without any election in sight. How do you react to this?
That shows his total lack of knowledge, the total shallowness of the Osun State Governor, really if that statement is truly from Osun State Governor. We are in a political dispensation, campaigns, rally and other political activities are not in any way limited to election. During this period, politicians are free to mobilise themselves, to organise activities that would keep the tempo of their consciousness. So, it's a manifestation of his ignorance of politics.
As for holding me responsible, for as long as we are in politics, we are going to execute the most decent, the best character, the most honorable methods of engaging our supporters and equally our opponents. We have no cause to worry about that statement, we only hope that they too will live up to the expectation that they have expressed. I hope that they would gladly accept responsibility for the maintenance of law and order everywhere.
As I'm talking to you, the leadership of PDP is planning to have hoodlums clothed in our vest and uniform, harassing and tormenting innocent people all in their own efforts to hang such responsibilities on us. But because we know they have that intention we will expose them before they do it. We will alert the public this time on such diabolical act.
I must not fail to say it here that the December 15 election which we boycotted was actually a plot, was actually put together to forment a worst form of crisis so as to scuttle fair hearing at the tribunal.
The intention really for December 15 election was to promote such a high level instability which would have even been a justification for another round of mass arrest, mass detention and ultimately geared at ensuring that we do not have people witnessed in the tribunal. That was the objective. So, our boycott totally displaced that plan and that was even why they secretly wept in their hidings, having noticed the failure of their plots.

Original_Search_Title: Better Coaches in Bars
Headline: Cameroon: Better Coaches in Bars
Body_Text:
Poverty and your personal problems notwithstanding; there's one irrefutable reality that binds us these days: Ghana 2008. Probably in no other time of the year have players, referees and coaches undergone so much strain and stress.
Take, for instance, our outburst in bars. "You fool, pass that ball to the left." "Look at this useless player." "This coach is a disgrace." "Why didn't he head that ball to the left? " "That number 10 is a yam, he should be removed." "Even a pregnant lady could have kicked better." And you name the other countless invectives.
Observers are not only amused but "impressed" with the knack of Cameroonians to coach better (though in bars) than renowned names currently coaching teams in
Ghana. With so many coaches that we have in bars, and if they put their heads together; who says an African country will not win the forthcoming World Cup football tournament in South Africa.

Original_Search_Title: 'How to Resolve Extra-Judicial Killings'
Headline: Nigeria: 'How to Resolve Extra-Judicial Killings'
Body_Text:
Umuahia — The use of extrajudicial killings popularly known as 'jungle justice' to deal with suspected criminals would begin to wane in social relations across the nation once every actor in the justice system learns to respect the rule of law.
This was the observation of the Executive Director of the Centre for Victims of Extra-judicial Killings and Torture (CVEKT), a non-governmental organisation, Rev. Fr. Tony Amarube, in his address at the 2007 edition of CVEKT annual lectures held at Michael Okpara Audito-rium Umuahia.
"Due process entails our ability to exercise self-restraint in the face of crimes, instead of resorting to instant justice, which is often heavy-handed and arbitrary," he said, adding that, "With due process policy, government is required to follow pre-fixed, transparent and fair procedures before it can deprive a person's life, liberty or property."
He condemned the practice of using torture by the police to extract confessions from suspected criminals, saying that "it is the responsibility of the judge or magistrate to ensure that statements extracted under torture or ill-treatment were not invoked as evidence in court proceedings."
Amarube insisted that "the police must try a different and more professionally accountable approach to crime prevention and fighting instead of persisting with a suspect policy of mowing down suspects."
He therefore, advised that the police "should devise a rigorous method of gathering intelligence on true crime suspects and update its manual for solving crime instead of indulging in extrajudicial slaughter of impoverished Nigerians."
According to him, the application of due process/rule of law in the justice system would be of immense benefit to the poor who are mostly the victims of detention without trial and extrajudicial executions.
The CVEKT executive direct noted that the Nigerian Bar Association
(NBA) has a big role to play in ensuring equal justice for all citizens irrespective of economic or social status.
He decried a situation where Nigerian lawyers concentrate on providing legal assistance, "mainly to the rich, corrupt officials in possession of plundered and pillaged resources but ignoring the abject conditions of those hundreds of thousands of fellow Nigerian citizens who have been accused of loitering, wandering and accused of stealing yams of bicycles."
"The provision of legal representation could help ensure due process is adhered to, challenge prolonged detention and increase the likelihood of a fair hearing," Amarube said.

Original_Search_Title: Equato-Guinean Relations Deteriorate After Kidnap of Refugee
Headline: Cameroon: Equato-Guinean Relations Deteriorate After Kidnap of Refugee
Body_Text:
After the kidnap of an Equato-Guinean refugee in Cameroon recently, diplomatic relations between the two countries are once more said to be in the doldrums.The government is very worried that the kidnap of Colonel Cipriano Nguema Mba recently, allegedly hatched by his country's authorities, has portrayed Cameroon as a country that does not provide adequate protection to refugees.
The Cameroon government has protested about the kidnap of the refugee that is likely to be executed back in his country.That is why the Equato-Guinean President, Obiang Nguema dispatched his Minister of Foreign Affairs, Pastor Micha Ondo Bile, November 11, to seek to appease the Yaoundé authorities on the matter.
The Equato-Guinean special envoy that Prime Minister, Ephraim Inoni received at the Star Building, came after that country's Ambassador was summoned by the Minister of External Relations, Henri Eyebe Ayissi, last week. He protested the fact that against humanitarian law, the right of refugees, the Equato-Guinean authorities hatched and executed such a diabolic plan.
The Ambassador denied involvement in the kidnap. The issue was said to be a hot potato in the hands of the Equatorial Guinea-Cameroon Ad hoc joint Commission that met at the Mont Febe Hotel Yaounde last Tuesday. Given the sensitive nature of the meeting, journalists were not allowed to follow its deliberations. Only Cameramen of the CRTV were allowed to take pictures.
Authorities of the United Nations High commission for refugees are said to be really worried about fate of the refugees.The Cameroon government has disassociated itself from the kidnap by heavily sanctioning the police-people who kidnapped colonel Nguema and took him to the borders.
The Presidency of the Republic issued a release on November 4, sacking the two policemen from the police corps.According to security reports, the two policemen whisked away Col. Nguema away from his Etoudi residence after the Equato-Gunea promised to pay the CFCA 15million. He was later taken back to Equatorial Guinea where he is currently being detained.
Col. Ngeuma reportedly fled Equatorial Guinea in 2003 after he was accused of organising a foiled coup against the president of his country.He fled to seek political asylum in Cameroon after he was given a 30-year term. The court later charged the refugee with treason and the embezzlement of FCFA 300,916 million and sentenced him to death in absentia.
While the Cameroon government, and international human rights organisations argued that the man had the right to be protected in his refugee status, the Equato-Guinean authorities say he should be given the treatment of a criminal. The Equato-Guinean Minister of Forein Affairs said the man was supposed to be caged at all costs because he ran away with a huge sum of money.
That is why many observers fear that despite the diplomatic sweet talk by the Equato-Guinean envoy on the matte at the Star Building, the Obiang Nguema government will still go ahead to execute the colonel.

Original_Search_Title: Food Prices Up On Rains, Ramadhan
Headline: Uganda: Food Prices Up On Rains, Ramadhan
Body_Text:
Kampala — Heavy rains, which have made rural roads impassable, and the just-concluded Ramadhan season, have led to an increase in food prices, a weekly mini-survey has shown.
Trucks carrying food from the rural areas have got stuck due to the impassable roads. This has led to a reduction in supply, hence an increase in prices.
Traders said matooke, sweet potatoes and meat products (chicken, goat meat, beef and eggs) were affected by the rains.
Irish potatoes, millet grain, rice, turkey, fish, fruits and vegetables also recorded high prices.
According to a market report from Infotrade, Masaka is receiving heavy rains, a situation that is likely to reduce milk prices because there will be sufficient pasture for the cows. The milk costs sh800 per litre. In Kisoro, the price of cow peas has increased to sh1,600.

Original_Search_Title: He Wouldn't Harm a Fly, But He Stings Opponents
Headline: Uganda: He Wouldn't Harm a Fly, But He Stings Opponents
Body_Text:
His friends describe him as a man who wouldn't hurt a fly. One of his players, Brian Umony, describes him as a nice, warm, receptive and encouraging human-being. He said that of him before KCC football club won the league in the period he presided over 13 league games of which the club won 12. This was KCC's first league title in eleven years.
George Nsimbe had just become caretaker coach upon the resignation of Moses Basena (now at URA). For this achievement, the soccer fraternity christened him George 'Best' Nsimbe. George Best is a legendary soccer player from Northern Ireland who is regarded Manchester United's best footballer ever.
Born on December 29, 1966, Nsimbe is third in a family of two girls and three boys.
His father was a Luo from Kenya called Richard Siambe (RIP). He worked in Uganda for a while but returned to Kenya, leaving Nsimbe's now deceased mother Monica, pregnant.
Monica got married to the late Paulo Nsimbe who raised Nsimbe like his own son, including giving him his name.
"I had two fathers, I may say. But I was closer to Paulo because he raised me and nurtured me. But I also loved my real father although I did not know him much," said Nsimbe.
Nsimbe went to Wandegeya Moslem for his primary education. He later joined Kololo High for S1 - S3 before his football talent landed him at Lubiri Senior Secondary School (SSS) for his S4 and S5.
Nsimbe was later snatched by Kololo SSS for his S6 where he admits he scored only one pass in History, Economics and Geography because of spending most of his time playing football.
"I will not lie; I was not reading my books but playing football," he said.
It was this football craze that saw Nsimbe join Red Cross FC at 15 years when he had just joined secondary. He spent just a year there before he was lured to Wandegeya FC for another year and later on National Insurance Corporation from 1983 to 1984.
Nsimbe was spotted by KCC through a person he idolizes. Moses Nsereko. Nsereko was both a KCC and Cranes icon whom Nsimbe watched first when he was in Senior One. Nsereko played as a number eight but Nsimbe started out at number seven and nine before he was discovered as a good anchorman at NIC.
Nsimbe joined KCC in 1985 as a successor to the retiring coach/player Nsereko but as a number six this time.
At that time, the policy at KCC was that every retiring footballer had to spot a successor and bring him to the team. Nsereko brought Nsimbe while former KCC Skipper, Sam Musenze brought Charles Masiko in defence.
"It was a dream come true for me, because I had always admired the way KCC played their kakeka (passing) football," he said. "To be [Nsereko's] successor was just an honour," Nsimbe said. This was despite the fact that his father supported Express FC.
It took Nsimbe just four matches to establish himself as a first team player at KCC.
"It was against Nsambya in 1985 that really broke the deadlock for me and I never looked back," he says. That season climaxed with another league trophy. He went on to win the Kakungulu Cup in 1987 and the league in 1991.
In 1987 Nsimbe got his first call to the Cranes but was dropped later because he was vying for the slot against seasoned campaigners: Moses Ndawula, Paul Nkata and Twaha Kivumbi.
But in 1988, he made it to the CECAFA Senior Challenge Cup with the Cranes in Malawi where he got his first caps when Nkata got injured.
His former Cranes Coach Polly Ouma says: "He was a very meek young man; very likeable, hardworking and always pushed his team-mates to go for a win and I was not surprised when he guided KCC to the league. He was so humble, disciplined and a gentleman."
Nsimbe was assistant captain to Masiko in KCC from 1993 to 1994 and full captain in 1995 and 1996 after Masiko was kept out by injury. Nsimbe retired in 1996.
"I played five consecutive seasons without rest for club and country. I suffered a burn-out and my body could not carry on with the pace of the game, so I hang up my boots," Nsimbe says.
A soft spoken man and a friend to everyone, Nsimbe says his closest buddies are Masiko, Tom Lwanga, and the late Majid Musisi, with whom he grew up tormenting opponents on the football fields of Mulago.
Mujib Kasule, a former KCC player, told The Weekly Observer sometime back that Nsimbe is a likeable man and coach any player would be endeared to.
A devout Catholic, Nsimbe says that as a rule he has to pray at Mulago Catholic Church every Sunday.
He is married to Florence with whom they have three children: Collin Nsimbe (21), Cedric Nsimbe (19) and Joan Nsimbe (17). Nsimbe stays in Kyebando where he built a house that he says is a fruit of football. It is again through football that Nsimbe landed a job at KCC where he works from 8a.m to 2p.m as a street lighting technician.
"I have an inborn skill of operating electronics," he says.
The smiling coach told The Weekly Observer that his favourite meal has got to have posho, matooke and sweet potatoes with beef or fish.
Nsimbe has an ambition of becoming Uganda Cranes coach some day although he does not expect to coach beyond the age of 50.
He loves swimming and Afrigo band, especially their classic hit Olumbe Lw'obwavu. Nsimbe is also a fan of Arsenal Football Club.
"My saddest moment in life was when Nsereko was killed in cold blood," he said with a frown. Nsereko, his mentor, was murdered in 1990.
Nsimbe has started the new season the way he ended the last one. His high flying KCC has started its defence of the league trophy with four wins in four games played. With such results, he will be expecting to be confirmed as head coach of KCC soon, the club he has won trophies for, as a player, and coach. Then, at long last, his career will have come full circle.

Original_Search_Title: Glad to Be Home
Headline: Burundi: Glad to Be Home
Body_Text:
In this second and final part, Harriet S. Okot tells of unconventional meals, curfews and corrupt police officials during her trip to Burundi.
On the way, it became clear how corrupt policemen in this nation really are. We were stopped by one who demanded that we give him money if we wanted to go anywhere. We had no option but to give him what he wanted.
As I sat there waiting for this soldier to take his bribe and leave, I saw a young boy who must have been about 14 or 15 years old light a cigarette. Before I could process the information, I saw another one who was only about 10 also puff away.
A young man called Excellence introduced himself as our escort and drove ahead of us in his car. We were driven straight to where the Pam awards launch was taking place. I was watching the clock closely because all I wanted was to eat and sleep. Finally, we were taken to a restaurant on the other side of town. By the time the food was brought, we had eaten most of the salad. The pieces of meat were almost the size of my hand but very delicious.
On getting to the hotel, we had to scramble for keys for the various rooms. I settled in very well and dozed off very soon. At around 3a.m., I heard the banging. At first I thought it was one of the people in our group being silly, then I heard voices speaking a language that I could not understand.
Although at first they were not knocking at my door, they soon started when the occupant of the next room refused to open his for them. I was forced to get out of my bed and attend to them. They began demanding for something that they had left in the room and really needed. So I gently knocked on his door and asked him to open up. After a long wait, he opened the door only to hand over a shoe brush. All that knocking for a shoe brush!
In the morning when I woke up I could not wait to see what is really on this side of Lake Tanganyika, only to be told that there is a curfew and travelling around in large groups is prohibited before 10.30a.m. Then there was the issue of breakfast which was soda and roasted sweet potatoes. I had earlier declined to eat sweet potato chips and was not about to have roasted potatoes for breakfast so I hung in there.
When we finally got on the road to head back home, we had to make stops for police checks. We soon realised that each and every officer wanted a bribe and were not giving up until they got what they wanted.
As we were getting to the border, we noticed a young couple walking along,
The newly wed couple that had us scrambling to take pictures of them. PHOTO BY BRUNO BIRAKWATE
escorted by a group of other villagers singing along with them. The bride had decorated her head with wrapping paper and they had a multi-coloured umbrella to protect them from the scorching sun. We were all desperate to take photos of the couple that Emmanuel, a cameraman from a local television station decided that he could not do without the footage.
He leaned out of the window and all of a sudden, he was on the ground screaming. We rushed out of the vehicle to see what was happening when we noticed he was in intense pain. We carried him back in and placed him at the back of the bus while we debated on what to do; whether it was easier for him to be taken back to Kampala or to a hospital in Kigali. We realised that we were not getting home that day because the border would be closed by the time we got there.
We were stuck in Kigali. One young man in our group who knew Rwanda well insisted on taking us to a private hospital which turned out to be a pharmacy. There, we were told to go to the national hospital. We took the patient to the hospital that from the onset had all the features of a state hospital in Africa.
We then went in search of a hotel but found that the only hotels left were either too expensive or too disgusting. Finally, I found somewhere to rest my head. The next morning, most of us were awake by 6a.m. but the driver was nowhere to be seen because someone had taken him to an unknown hotel.
After hours of arguing, he finally appeared. Then we set off. The rest of the journey was less eventful. Only when we got onto Ugandan soil did the travellers jump up and down and refuse to hear any words that reminded them of Rwanda or Burundi.
A crate of beer was loaded and we were soon on our way to Kampala. After a few more stops and fights, we were back to a traffic-infested city that I can proudly call home.

Original_Search_Title: Security Men Have Beaten Me Severally for Defending People's -CB
Headline: Nigeria: Security Men Have Beaten Me Severally for Defending People's -CB
Body_Text:
Charles Oputa (aka Charly Boy) is one musician in the country that has taken music beyond entertainment. When he came into limelight, his kind of music generated a lot of gossip as not a few Nigerians imagined that there could be a clash of roles between the young music talent and his father, Justice Chukwudifo Oputa (rtd), who at the time was considered as a no-nonesense judge. But the young Oputa just proved them wrong by fashioning his own style of music that blends moral sensitisation with human rights activism. In this exclusive encounter with leadership, Charly Boy who bounced back to the music arena after 8 years of sabatical leave narrates to our Solomon Nda-Isaiah his experience so far. Excerpts:
At 55, what would you say is your most cherished achievement?
A lot of people get frustrated, because they get carried away with either societal conformity, peer pressure, overbearing parental pressures, keeping up with the Joneses. Most Nigerians are just a bunch of hypocrites with a capital HIP.
