Do you know the pain I went through in trying to become, when most Nigerians never gave me a chance, when for almost 15 years all that I had was my dreams and an empty pocket? Do you know what it means to leave your comfort zone and venture into the unknown in a society that negatively branded me, not giving me a chance? Do you know the battles one has fought not only for self respect, but respect for labour, respect for character, respect for the poor and the less privileged? Do you know how many times I have been beaten up, by the Army, Nigerian Police, NDLEA, etc just for standing up for other people's right?
They said 'he can never be'; now look at who is having the last laugh, yes Charly Boy. That's my most cherished achievement, being me Charly Boy!
Your newly released CD, have received very positive rave reviews and i think for most people, shock that you can still come up with fresh and contemporary stuff?
Charly Boy is a vitality brand, so it must remain youthful at all times. Charly Boy's main constituency is the youths so that will forever remain his target audience.
In a country that has remained so insensitive to the yearnings of the Nigerian youth, we need Charly Boy to share with them, his experience, dogged with tenacity, hardwork and tremendous focus. The brand Charly Boy has remained for a long time an inspiration to the youth of this country. So tell me, do you expect him to be playing 'baby pancake' or 'Joromi' in this time? No! he must play the sound the young can relate to, be it hip hop or highlife hop or afrohop. But knowing Charly Boy, he will forever shock and awe his critics. As the brand creator, one thing I will never do is to take the boy out of the man, and because of that all that he does in that wise will always be fresh and youthful. As the future belongs to the youths .and music is one of the many mediums we can use to inspire, encourage and motivate them, it has to be in their own language.
There was a lot of collaborations in your latest work, ninja bike, was that intentional?
It was a privilege to work with a lot of creative and talented Nigerians. I was looking for a lot of sparks on this new CD and like I say, for every Michael Jackson, I could give you a dozen in my area. It just reaffirms my belief in this country's youth. From your Sound Sultan to Rasmike to Oceesensy, Rugged Man, Terry G, K Solo, Cohbams Asuquo, Makoyo, Asha and Clarenc.e Peters, I felt like a child in toy shop having the fun of my life working with such gifted Nigerians, never had that kind of fun in a long time.
I remember how in the shooting of the video for Ninja Bike, Clarence the video director drilled me and turned me into an emergency Michael Jackson dancing for hours on end. And each time I see that video, I wonder if it's me or the Area Fada Charly Boy. It was hard work, but it was fun. When you work with a bunch of loff d hook' kinda guys you can't help but be bitten by the bug.
Am hoping to do something new and very big soon. God, I just got started. Move over, the thing don over sweet me.
You have been applauded for a great comeback, why did it take you 8yrs?
I terminated my sabbatical. Eight years seemed long enough, I missed all the fun. I thank God that one is blessed with so many sides that keeps the creative juices following.
Your private part (the book), was it another medium for reengineering the brand?
My Private Part is a testimony of my life as Charly Boy, my goofs, my struggles and my conquests. It is meant to be a signpost to the Nigerian youth, saying 'whatsoever you choose for yourself as long as you are devoted to it, you will be blessed'. The love and support with which the Nigerian youth have received the "Charlyboyism" message has given me a renewed sense of purpose. They have all encouraged me to dig a little deeper, kept me on my toes and made me come up with more.
The Private Part is a book that seeks to reveal the deeper parts of Charly Boy, it is a compilation of the different activities the man behind this image, Charles Oputa, has been through to keep the image extremely active and controversial for over 25 years.
Charly Boy's image in this part of the world came as a shock to a lot of people and it took only consistency to turn it around to be accepted by society as a norm, for Charly Boy of course. His controversial ways have brought mixed feelings from friends and foes amongst which are admiration, disgust, anger, confusion, amusement, respect, loyalty and expectation. While his image is in full glare for all to see, his sensitive nature, love life and deeply rooted family upbringing are not.
My Private Part therefore brings out the true nature of the brand Charly Boy, as he represents these various constituencies effectively. The arrangement of the book using a scrap book style is in the Charly Boy manner, unusual but serious and understandable. The concept being one designed to make the reader probe deeper and understand better. At the end of the day, the reader is left with his own conclusion about what kind of man Charly Boy really is. I wonder what that would be crazy? Wise? Creative? Troublemaker? It's all up to the reader. Whatever one does, always be consistent, be exceptional and stand out from the boring crowd! This is the philosophy of the enigma, Charly Boy!!!
You seem to wear your image so proudly, what is the trait you most deplore in yourself?
To always stay focused, to always keep my eyes on the ball even if the end of the world is at hand. Sometimes am too hard on myself like my wife will always say to me, but I have realized that its always good to demand the best for and from yourself, because there are so many dreams to fill. So the more you raise the stakes, the bigger the headache, but I have never liked easy things, I have always been drawn to the things that I find challenging.
I approach life from a certain perspective, a certain kind of openness that has drawn me to accepting the beauty of who I am, who I could be and who I have always been.
So I am comfortable in my skin, I know who I am and am proud of who I am. This is due to the notion of always trying to have a better understanding, to build a better me, to be a better giver, listener, and a better motivator.
Self confidence comes when you understand and know who you are, I guess that's why I wear, and hang around Charly Boy with pride.
Are you a religious person? Do you pray? All your scary rings and weird ways, where does that come from?
There is nothing weird or scary about my getups or my physical appearance. I hope you are not confusing me Charly Boy with Mr. Oputa. I am an entertainer and as such, flamboyancy and razzmatazz comes with the territory. That's why I say you have to look past the things you see.
As for praying, my life is already a prayer for God's mercy in showing me what my divine purpose on earth is all about, because I believe now that I am living on purpose, and since that realization I have inherited a certain kind of peace that helps me focus more on the things I need to do.
I pray that God will continue to guide my decisions, to make me a better person and for me to touch as many lives as possible before I exit. That's why my whole life is a prayer.
No! I am not a religious kinda person, who is in church every Sunday of the week, but I know I am as spiritual as I am sensitive and passionate and I know I walk in God's favour.
What is your most treasured possession?
Family is my most treasured possession.
Family is the rock of who I have come to become. Family is the reason why I could give love because we were born in love. Family is the reason why I have character, being brought up on an overdose of morals, values, principles all those things we seem to have discarded in our pursuit for survival.
I thank God daily for the kind of family He had blessed me with, my eight lovely children, some of whom give me so much stress sometimes, my beautiful grandchildren, my sweet friend and lovely wife Lady Diane and my kid brother. I thank God.
Being Charly Boy was easy because I had the support of members of my family even when they did not in the beginning quite understand what I set out to do, neither did I, they still supported it believing in my judgment.
I thank God that I still have my parents, who are still alive and healthy to rejoice with me in my many successes and dreams come true. I thank God for everything.
What is it about your wife that has kept your marriage for almost 28 years?
I grew up seeing both parents till today, thank God. I grew up seeing how they managed themselves living together for almost 60 years, and even though I was initially wild and restless which saw me through three unsuccessful marriages maybe owing to my immaturity, but all that changed with time and experience.
It was the little child in me that connected with the little girl in my wife.
At first we were just friends, but over time our friendship grew into a serious bond, let me just say that we had the right kind of chemistry. So when she asked me to marry her I couldn't say no. That's another blessing from The Almighty.
In my 15 years of going through trials, tribulations and hunger, due to my stubbornness in being exactly who I wanted to be, she was there and that's probably why I succeeded, because of her encouragement. You see in those days nothing pointed to the fact that the building of the Charly Boy brand will be this successful, she hung in there with me through fire, hunger, frustration and great uncertainty.
You have to not forget the fact that after about three failed marriages, I got better in the business, I became a lot more matured form experience. Plus that my parents were to me like an ideal couple, and I had always admired that about them, so there was no way of running away from my rich heritage and background. An apple can't fall too far from its tree, abi!
It has certainly not been all rosy and easy. We have had our share of crises in our relationship but like great friends, we have also learnt how to overcome our tribulations. I never talk when I am angry. She can read my mind when am not saying anything and most times I find myself telling her everything.
In short, she is a great contributor not only to the brand but also to peace and calm I have in my life right now. The chemistry works well for and above all she is a very nice person, if I say so myself. Am truly blessed.
Your NGO "CODE RED", what is it all about?
CODE RED like you mentioned is a non - governmental and a non-profit making organization that I set up many years back to help initiate contribute to the positive changes that needs to be made not only enhance people but to make society a better one.
Part of what we do is
To challenge and inspire the creative energies of Nigerian youths through the gospel of self reliance, since there are very few jobs to round for the multitude of graduates coming out of the universities need to look inwards on how best to positively use our time ; energies in developing ourselves rather than being the devil's workshop
ii) To help channel the productive energies of the youth towards projects and enterprises that are development oriented.
iii) To embark on regular education and enlightenments that would the youths free themselves from violence and all form of negativism
iv) To serve as a viable vehicle for crisis resolution, to contribute to peace stability and security by bridging the gap between the government the youths. The foundation has been in the forefront of peaceful resolving the army and railway pensioners and even the okada national ban issue.
Code red is an outlet for me to fulfill my social responsibility and value to my environment in my own way. It has also helped me in doing lot of advocacy work.
It has been noticed that you are not really a social animal, you seem to shun social life, aside from your family who are your friends?
No, I am not antisocial, I have attended more than a few events, away from my business, but I guess I am more reserved than people can imagine. Growing up so fast, so quick spurred me to a lot of things, so right now, unless its very important am first a coach potato and that's why I've made my house a home, where I can put my feet up and chill. Nevertheless, every now and again, I come out for fresh air. As for friends, I just have one! I found out that in this neck of the wood, we use the word friendship very badly, and now I know better.
Friendship is something which connects people through mutual knowledge, esteem and affection. It revolves around loyalty, empathy, genuine affection for each other and the ability to understand others. Today's world has become very fast and artificial. People try to take advantage of one another, all in the name of friendship, so friendship somewhere down the line lost its significance.
Friends are people you can really count upon and trust, these days in Nigeria that's kind of extinct. Of course, we mistake associates and acquaintances as friends, but like I said the word is used loosely here, like 'my brother'!
If you could change one thing about yourself, what would it be?
The fact that am too trusting! This is because I have been taken advantage of so many times, in very bad ways. The fact that at my age I still smoke cigarettes, now that's very bad! But no one is perfect so I have sort of accepted those weaknesses as my imperfections. You know, I have kept my head when all about me people were losing theirs and blaming it on me, e.g. when I was PMAN President. I have from Jump Street trusted myself when most Nigerians doubted me, e.g. building of the Charly Boy brand.
I have in my life as Charly Boy met with a lot of triumph and disasters, and guess what? I treated those two imposters just the same. I have walked with crowds and have kept my virtue; I have walked with kings yet never loosing my common touch. So for the few things that I did like to change and the many I am blessed with. I love me, am in love with me because I still haven't found another me. And as imperfect as me is, there is a whole lot about me that's oozing with positivity. All I need to do is keep working on me, to keep updating me so I am compliant with my creator.
How far have you gone with the youth center? What will be the vision?
My brother, sometimes I wonder how I stay so motivated in a system of misplaced priorities. To get financing for a worthy cause like this, one starts to get the feeling that no one cares, but I won't let that demoralize me and that's the whole point.
Work has started, for now we are taking it slow and steady. I am building a ministry if you like, where young people can come together to fellowship with a lot of mentoring going on, basically get the youths to look inwards and be the best in whatever the want to be, starting with the belief in self.
If we can't be captains, we have to get to be the crew. There is something for all of us here, there is big work to do and there are less hands to do it. It isn't by size that one wins or fails; it is in being the best of whatever you are.
The vision and mission of the center simply put will be to re-direct and re-focus the mindset and mentality of a lot of our young ones. Am very good with young people, I could fix the most hopeless case, I think it's a gift from God and because I have been so humbled by the way the youths of this country have such great belief in me. I feel obligated to do this, not only that it brings me great joy in knowing that I will be saving lots of Nigerian youths.
Work has started on the site, like I said, its slow but steady, God will grant its final completion.
Your tenure as the pman president was an eventful one but almost marred by a lot of controversy; what's your take?
Because am not from this neck of your wood, my approach - typical Charly Boy style can not be like any other. It must stand out. No one throws sticks at a barren tree, if nothing was happening, it would have become a non - event like the tenures that preceded mine. I have a knack for turning negatives to positives, well, if you are talking about the negative press; I got that at the tail end of my tenure. Those who for whatever reason aside from being objective who reported from their own perspective only did so out of ignorance and you damn well know that I have no time for simpletons, small-mindedness or baba-sala kind of journalism.
It was embarrassingly clear to those who had eyes that I was dealing with a bunch of yahoos and semi - illiterates who had come from an insipid background, having no pedigree or intellect to understand we came to make history. And as childish and frivolous as their pranks were, I also gave them hell because they just couldn't survive in the kind of futuristic idea we had for the union.
PMAN during my time demanded that an artiste should be respected and that our job and act can compete with the business of oil. I succeeded because our artistes started to get more pay and more respect.
With a series of non - stop events, PMAN was back in reckoning after it was almost dead. I won for the Nigerian artiste even respect from our foreign counterparts because I insisted that there was never again going to be such obnoxious marginalization of the Nigerian artiste amongst their foreign counterparts. For this fight I was locked up behind bars by Nigerian Breweries, however what came out of that: Wyclef Jean featured Sound Sultan, 2face and Face on his album, which also opened the floodgate for much such collaboration with international stars. MTV, Channel 0 started during my time, which further exposed Nigerian acts.
I gave piracy an intelligent fight, which got the government to take a second look at the money they are loosing by their nonchalant attitude. I could go on and on, there's no way anyone can take my achievement away from me.
PMAN for me was a big sacrifice because the only way I made it work, was to lend the union my goodwill, which was plenty; my time, which was precious; my creativity which was fascinating, my rugged dogged and bulldozing attitude that struck fear in my opponents and detractors. I gave it my intellect which was kinda rare to find in that union, so why wouldn't I stand out!
Do I look like anybody you know, or am I in the same pot with some of those winches? My brother, na over try worry me, but above all am proud to say that most of the stuff I started are still felt today positively by Nigerian acts and I thank God. I borrowed Charly Boy to PMAN.
PMAN was like a mini Nigeria, where all most of the union heads wanted to do was share money. Money that was meant for the union, money with which to build a stronger foundation, well wasn't going to be a part of that kinda shit. But I had a good time my brother. Am sure I mesmerized a lot of people, and moreover I gave it my best shot! (He laughs).

Original_Search_Title: Swapo Rides Through Storm
Headline: Namibia: Swapo Rides Through Storm
Body_Text:
Windhoek — After a stormy politburo meeting 10 days ago followed by an equally heated and fiery central committee meeting over the weekend, the Swapo Party yesterday projected an image of a united front at a press conference in the capital.
President Hifikepunye Pohamba came under heavy fire at the central committee meeting with some of his decisions questioned from the floor.
On the other hand, Swapo Party Secretary General, Pendukeni Iivula-Ithana, was in a buoyant and celebratory mood yesterday as she addressed the press having won what some observers believe is a significant victory by way of a stay of the decision to split her positions in government and the resolution to send her packing to party headquarters.
In a related development, the Swapo Party Youth League (SPYL) who were expected to be hauled over hot coals at the meeting, in fact, emerged the biggest victors while Prime Minister Nahas Angula and his deputy, Dr Libertina Amathila, who have publicly discredited the SPYL must be having second thoughts.
It was sweet victory for the SPYL against some of the old guards and many are wondering how they pulled it off, or whether there is in fact a third force in the party. But be that as it may, the party has a new drive and Founding President Sam Nujoma witnessed this from a vantage point, quietly observing the deliberations of the third central committee meeting after congress without much participation in the deliberations.
Meanwhile, at the press conference yesterday, Iivula-Ithana announced the expulsion of the councillor for Tobias Hainyeko Constituency, Erasmus Hendjala from the party, which she said is in accordance with the Swapo Party constitution.
She did not elaborate why Hendjala was expelled, but noted that it is due to misconduct.
Hendjala has been accused of campaigning and organising meetings for the Rally for Democracy and Progress and that could have led to his expulsion.
Iivula-Ithana also announced the endorsement of Helmut Angula as a member of the politburo, to replace John Pandeni who died earlier this year.
Surprisingly, in the statement the Swapo Party did not refer the contentious issue of the proposed separation of the Ministry of Justice and the Office of the Attorney General or whether the secretary general's position will be a full-time one as per congress resolution.
However, responding to a question, the party secretary for information and mobilisation, Jerry Ekandjo, said the issue is a concern to non-Swapo Party members because the incumbent is a woman.
Ekandjo said the resolution that the position of secretary general should be a full-time position was taken at the first congress in 1991 and was never fully implemented.
He noted that when the resolution was taken for the first time, Andimba Toivo ya Toivo, was the secretary general but still remained Minister of Mines, while his successor the late Moses Garoeb was both secretary general and Minister of Labour.
"Even the current President, when he was secretary general of the party, he was Minister of Lands and Ngarikutuke Tjiriange was Minister of Justice and later Minister of Veteran Affairs while he was secretary general."
The issue of the AG has become a hot potato and at the last central committee meeting, Pohamba was at pains to explain his intention to split the ministry.
Some central committee members felt the President had been insensitive by planning to split the ministry while the incumbent, Ithana, was still mourning the death of her husband.
A central committee member said the deputy chairperson of the National Council, Margret Mensah-William, bombarded Pohamba with questions regarding the decision to strip Ithana of the AG title and Pohamba, who had carefully read the mood of the central committee did not respond.
Even central committee members who allegedly defended Pohamba where quiet and the President only briefly noted that he consulted three people on the issue, one of them being Judge President Petrus Damaseb.
Pohamba refused to reveal the name of the other two. Nujoma, Geingob and Ithana were all silent on the matter.
The question of consultation also led to the central committee demanding that the President informs the central committee whom he consulted when he appointed Namibia's High Commissioner to Botswana, Hadino Hishoongwa, as well as Namibia's Ambassador to the US, Kaire Mbuende.
A central committee member said although the meeting was very tense, it was one of the best because it discussed issues frankly and did not sweep anything under the carpet.
The party at the central committee also resolved to continue with mobilisation towards the 2009 Presidential and National Assembly elections, and affirmed through the regional reports that the process is on course.
The central committee called on its party members in all constituencies to ensure a resounding victory for the Swapo Party but it did not reaffirm its decision that Pohamba should remain the sole presidential candidate for the Swapo Party. A central committee member said this was deliberate.
The central committee member said the central committee was not generally happy with the conduct of the President and will monitor his future actions.
"We are coming to an end of his first term and what has he done? Will he continue consulting the opposition or bring in more RDP people in the system?" said one Swapo member of the central committee.
At the central committee, the party also decided to appoint Hans Boois as the party chief whip to replace Hage Geingob, who is the vice president and thus a member of the top four.
Union leader, David Namalenga, and Billy Mwaningange, were endorsed as new members of the central committee to replace John Pandeni and Gabes Shihepo - who both passed away this year.

Original_Search_Title: Annual Headline Inflation Rate Falls
Headline: Tanzania: Annual Headline Inflation Rate Falls
Body_Text:
The annual headline inflation rate decreased to 9.1 per cent in May 2008 from 9.7 per cent in April due to a slight decrease in food prices, the National Bureau of Statistics has said.
"Food inflation decreased to 11 per cent in May 2008 as compared to April, which was 11.6 per cent. Mbeya, Ruvuma and Rukwa centres registered the lowest food prices during the month," said a statement posted at the NBS website yesterday.
Food has a weight of 55.9 percent in the basket of items that the bureau uses to measure inflation.
Among the food items whose prices fell were cereals, sweet potatoes, vegetables, fruits, pulses (beans and cow peas), and groundnuts.
Cell phone prices had also gone down. However, prices of non-food items like clothes, charcoal and furniture went up.
Meanwhile, monthly headline inflation for May 2008 rose by 0.1 per cent due to a hike in kerosene, diesel and petrol prices.
Other items that contributed to price increases include clothing, charcoal, furniture, insecticides, shaving creams, football (entry fees) and school bags.
NBS also said from May 2002, the value of 100 shillings (and thus the purchasing power) has been decreasing, declining to 68 shillings and 87 cents in May 2008.
The National Consumer Price Index (NCPI) covers prices collected in 20 towns in Mainland Tanzania.
Prices are gathered for 207 items, and prices collected are prevailing market prices.
In the state of the economy speech yesterday in Dodoma the minister for Finance and Economic Affairs said annual average inflation stood at 7 per cent last year.
Comparatively, Kenyan annual headline inflation for May 2008 stood at 31.5 per cent while the rate for Uganda was 11.2 per cent.

Original_Search_Title: Makerere Increases Accommodation Fees
Headline: Uganda: Makerere Increases Accommodation Fees
Body_Text:
Privately sponsored students planning to reside in halls of residence at Makerere University next semester will have to dig deeper into their pockets following a hike in accommodation fees.
The students, who have been paying Shs340,000 per semester as accommodation fees, would have to pay an extra Shs120,000 according to the new arrangement.
Accommodation fees cover rent, meals and other services provided by the halls.
The Dean of Students, Mr John Ekudu Adoku, said the increase in the accommodation fees was due to the soaring food prices.
"You (students) have seen and experienced increasing food prices on the market. Food prices have gone high and may go higher," a circular dated May 30 by the Dean of Students reads in part.
"We (University administration) have been feeding you at Shs 1,070 per day with very much difficulty. We have budgeted to feed you at Shs 2,000 per day at least." the letter further reads.
Mr Ekudu said the government will have to pay the Shs2000 per day for meals for government sponsored students instead of Shs1070 it has been paying.
Towards the end the second semester which ended last Friday the university was forced to reduce on the quantity of food served due to the soaring food prices.
The menu also changed to mainly posho and beans while rice, sweet potatoes, chicken, meat ceased to feature on the menu.
"It is understandable that food prices have risen. But increasing fees by over 30 per cent is extremely being unfair," lamented a first year student of Social sciences staying in Lumumba Hall.
"The administration has taken the unfortunate decision at a time when the semester is over, " a student of Nkrumah Hall said, "But believe me next semester we (students) are starting off in a unique way with a strike,".
"I have been paying the Shs 340,000 with a lot of difficulty. But now with the extra Shs120,000, I say bye to Lumumba Hall," said a second year student.
The increase in accommodation fees by the university's administration comes days after most primary and secondary schools in the country increased the school fees for the second term by between Shs10,000 and 30,000 .
The prices of most food items including those locally produced have more than doubled since the start of this year.
A kilogramme of maize flour costs about Shs1300 up from 800 by the end of last year, a kilogramme of beans has gone up from Shs1,200 to 1,800.
And meat now costs between Shs4000 and 5000 up from the Shs3000 per kilogramme at the start of the year.

Original_Search_Title: Braai... If You Can Buy!
Headline: Zimbabwe: Braai... If You Can Buy!
Body_Text:
THE MOST frequent question posed of me these days is: How much?
That is, how much must the questioner pay for a meal, a drink, a night's kip or air fare and I'm rarely able to reply accurately, as everything spirals upward in cost almost daily, sometimes twice a day!
A UK visitor, hoping (but failing) to see political history made in Zim, complained coffee and toast in a leading Harare hotel doubled in 24 hours.
He was lucky.
Had he drunk the cheapest lager at the same gaff, the bill would have shot up 150% (from $100 million to a quarter of a billion dollars) between lunch and sundowners and that not three weeks after a previous 100% plus booze price hike.
Glad I quit cigarettes, due to the mother of my kids' asthma, coincidentally on a day government upped them from $2,80 (of the old loot) to $3,50 a pack.
I bought three packets a day and was damned if I'd shell out more than $10 daily!...$300+ a month, on "gaspers". This week cancer sticks rose from around $15m for 20 to about $75m.
That was of course, $75 BILLION 18 months ago, before "Giddy" Gono slashed the zeros!
Club catering once meant great value for money and probably still does if you can get an ever-spinning head around our record hyper-inflation.
Had a truly magnificent steak at Moth Club, Braeside, cooked and served by new chairman, John Naested's wife, Jenny and daughter, Lana.
It was excellent export-quality sirloin, at least 300g, post-cooking, with a thin layer of well-marbled fat, grilled to a crisp, golden brown, rich, delicious (sadly, artery clogging!) perfection.
The beef was basted in olive oil and garlic, seasoned, then speedily seared on a super-heated criss-cross pattern griddle so all juices, goodness and flavour were sealed in a lump of gorgeous meat, full of taste, as tender as a nun's heart.
It came with a generous stack of good, big, crisp, golden chips, comprehensive salad, Melba toast and butter.
Don't think I've ever seen as wide a range of condiments -- local and imported -- available on each solidly built -- by John -- strategically placed table on the verandah.
He also made robust ethnic-patterned wrought-iron and timber chairs. Napery is in ethnic designs; cutlery heavy, well-balanced, gleaming bright; wooden handled steak knives so sharp you could slay a man-eating shumba with one, or perform delicate surgery!
Entrepreneur John has a passion for great food. I've been fortunate enough to be invited a few times to the family's magnificent Hollywood-style home, perched eyrie-like on a Ruwa gomo with panoramic views over a game park and well-stocked birdlife-rich dams, towards Mutoko and Shamva on the heat-haze shimmering horizon.
There I (well, WE: there were a dozen of us each time) ate like kings and queens and drank like Carmelite padres.
He says the Moth Club will soon boast a second-to-none wine list of local and imported vintages to complement the fine food.
My only reservation, then, was that the meal was $350m ($250m if substituting a satisfactorily plump quarter-chicken for steak.) Wasn't that a bit high in a club? I'd eaten at Rainbow Towers (the 5-star former Sheraton Hotel) the previous day, in the plush Komba Hari Grillroom, when steak WAS $280m.
No one can YET accuse the Moth Club of plushness, but it's getting there, starting with a superb centrepiece bar counter built (again by John) in black quarry tile.
Anyway, $350m obviously isn't now so dear. That was the cost of set Sunday lunch at Harare Club, for home-made soup and toast, roast and/or sweet-and-sour chicken and rice, pudding, tea or coffee, which I heard was fine. (We munched 'wors or sausages in bridge rolls, sinewy T-bones or chops, sadza or cold potato salad and green salad under the thatched gazebo at City Bowling Club at a "bring-and-braai" do after a fun pub quiz there.)
My problem was no local shops had anything braai-able on sale (well no grub at all, except suspect-looking polony in an improbable pink hue!) so could buy nothing to braai.
Neither could a couple more participants, but my London visitor, who'd moaned about hotel coffee hikes, worked the oracle: he'd enough graze for four.
At Harare Club, Monday, a splendid meaty-mincey-herby cottage pie with lots of creamy mash and veg, and no hint of portion control, was also $350m. At least five guys said cottage/shepherd's pie was their absolute top dish.
I love anything with mince: lasagna, moussaka, chili-con-carne, spagBol, mince-and-tatties, or on toast, etc and ate with relish.
Our host was odd man out, enjoying a sizeable chunk of roast dead huku, at the same price.
The club's always excellent home-made veggie soup with toast was $80m, curried hard-boiled eggs as a starter (as a cub reporter I lived on them as mains!) $100m and chocolate ice-cream and coffee sauce $80m.
I'd spent 27 minutes ATM queuing to be grudgingly allowed to withdraw my daily allocation of $500m (in six excruciatingly slow transactions); the food element of lunch was $510m (Giddy, please note!)
Fortunately Noo Yawk paid, so most of my half-billion could be funnelled into reluctantly boosting NatBrew's obscene profits! (If overseas readers are stunned by the thought of $500 000 000 going on beer...it's worth about US$9!)
For the record, I was again voted president of Greendale Good Food & Wine Appreciation Society for (I think...we've no exhaustive records!) the 23rd or 24th consecutive year.
Didn't really want the job, and my deputy, former BMATT chief-of-staff in Zim, Colonel Chuck Ivey, must do much deputising this year as I plan to wander the world again.
Feel sure GGF&WAS will try the Moths soon.

Original_Search_Title: Inflation Soars On Back of Rising Costs
Headline: Uganda: Inflation Soars On Back of Rising Costs
Body_Text:
Kampala — Inadequate supply of food items in the markets in March pushed overall prices higher with inflation hitting a 14 month peak of 8.1 per cent.
Uganda Bureau of Statistics attributes increases in food items to seasonal factors, which have led to reduced supplies of food in the market causing higher price levels and high inflation figures as well.
The rising food and utility prices are putting enormous pressure on the spending of households and businesses and could dampen growth on the back of limited consumption.
In March food prices went up by 2.3 per cent with up country centers such as Aura, Gulu and Mbale centers registering the highest increases in food prices.
The Uganda Bureau of Statistics says high utility costs mainly energy and fuel are a major factor in the rising cost of goods and foods.
While releasing the monthly results, the director of Macro economics at Uganda Bureau of Statistics, Mr Matthew Ssenwanyana said on March 31 that despite the rising prices, the inflation rate is still manageable going by government policy of keeping inflation at 5 per cent.
"In the real terms the economy is experiencing high inflationary pressure because of persistent increase in price of goods and services. However, the situation is not running out of hand because the levels are still manageable meaning that government is still comfortable with the current inflation rate although the situation calls for a policy concern," he said.
Over the past 15 years Uganda's economy has experienced real Gross Domestic Product GDP growth rate averaging between, 5 per cent to 6 per cent per annum with inflation rate contained at about 6.5 per cent. This time round though government has projected real GDP growth rate of 7 per cent this fiscal year the pace at, which the inflation figures are moving are faster and are higher the economic growth.
"The current inflation rate is not yet a threat to the health of Uganda economy. It would posse a lot of threats if the figures were double digits, which we don't think it will happen soon," Mr Ssenwanyana said.
The consumer price index for March shows that the annual head line inflation rate for the year ending march 2008 rose to 8.1 per cent from 7.5 per cent that was registered for the year ended February 2008.
The CPI figure show that food prices went up in most centers with significant contribution coming from sweet potatoes, Irish potatoes, passion fruits, sweet bananas, cabbages, onions, dry beans rice, millet flour, bread, and salt. Also prices of meat, fish, refined cooking oil and fats went up in a number of centers.

Original_Search_Title: It Wasn't the Hotel's Fault!
Headline: Zimbabwe: It Wasn't the Hotel's Fault!
Body_Text:
MEIKLES' publicist shot to my table during main course at the Institute of Directors' dinner saying, breathlessly: "Don't dump all over the hotel, in your piece, as the food's cold...it was ready ages before we got the nod."
I'm sure it was. Sadly this type of event, in mid-Mashonaland, rarely works with Swiss or Prussian efficiency.
I heard of concerns, when cost was set at $500 million three weeks earlier, over possible consumer resistance (presumably by number-crunching directors?) On the night, half-a-billion bucks bought two-and-a-bit cheese burgers, sans chips, at a junkfoodery near the world-famous hotel. Overseas readers will be stunned to hear that in this land of freedom it is illegal to carry more than $500 000 000 cash. At the time of writing, that's worth about US$10!
One beer in Meikles bars was $96m (brown bottles; $140m, green.) Ensconced with a group of "lads" for pre-event cocktails, we had three or four tipples each (including Scotch, good brandy and Cape wine.) This overture was optimistically named "African Sunset": ironically it rained carpet rods non-stop and a "light show" was scrubbed. The marquee's sides were dropped (why, when rain was perpendicular?) "canapés" (mainly chicken and steak) were braaied. Heat and humidity soared, eyes watered from acrid smoke and booze slid down thirsty throats. Many of the 400 guests drank $500 000 000 worth of "cocktails" before the MC's announcement had them finding usherettes with brollies to shelter finery from the monsoon, racing from sodden roof garden to dinner.
Talking of finery, tickets said it was strictly "black-tie", but not 30% of male attendees (including IoD bigwigs) wore DJs! As I'd grafted 11 hours disabusing under-strappers and various other folk that the word "deadline" = "joke", in sweaty shorts, golf shirt and trainers, and finding no Zesa or water at my flat, I checked into Meikles -- with tuxedo, frilly shirt, studs, cufflinks, bow-tie, cummerbund, black socks and shoes -- to at least bathe before the do.
(Booked out without much of this kit, as a keen, sweet-natured, temp waitress knocked a big glass of orange juice down my left side, and the girl on my left's right side. Meikles kindly offered to dry-clean: the bill for which will be more than the suit cost!)
Fellow diners eyed starter "Total Eclipse" warily: a trio of layered tomato and basil soup, avocado and vichyssoise with garlic croutons. "We don't get much soup, cold or otherwise, in Tafara!" I heard. But all 10 on my table tried -- and finished -- it. No-one at the next table sampled a spoonful. Shame!
Theme was Star Wars, underscored by table décor and hi-fi music, but decorations were so tall you couldn't see folk over the table and it's hard to chat across "rounds" of 10 or 12. A matte black paper napkin drifted onto a tiny ornamental candle: table linen blazed briefly!
Entree was a tiny, but delightful "Pulsar": succulent mushrooms in puff pastry casing; welcoming music a smooth medley of standards and jazz by Annette Eastwood, piano, and clarinetist John Kelly.
The programme gave a minute for the MC's welcome, then half-an-hour's whisky tasting by world-renowned drink expert Dave Hughes, of "Saywen" Stellenbosch, I found fascinating. But, I was the only one for tables around to abandon buppie fruit juice and sniff, sample and slurp John Jameson Irish whiskey (smoother than I recall it during "The Troubles"), White Horse, Johnnie Walker Black Label and Glen Ord single malt. It could have been fun to add a "Chitungwiza scotch" (Two Keys, say), to the array!
I groaned when the programme apparently gave David Mutambara 75 minutes for his address and key speaker, a South African Asian number-cruncher whose speech (to this humble hack) might have been made in Hindi with enhanced clarity, had 30 minutes.
Thankfully they didn't use allotted time, but the wordfest dragged on and when mains: corn-fed chicken breast poached in French tarragon sauce, "married" to prime grilled Zimbabwe beef fillet in a beef reduction (gravy!) with whole-grain mustard came, it was....cold! No one asked did we want the vegetarian option of squash filled with lentils and pine nuts. Both came with William pear potato (shaped croquette) and a "symphony" of fresh, seasonal vegetables. Call it what you may...it was cold. There's only one thing worse than burnt food (apart from no food!) and that's cold grub (when meant to be hot.)
But it clearly wasn't Meikles' fault.
Pudding was Starburst: golden tulip basket filled with panna-cotta, brandy snap cigar "enrobed" in a strawberry coulis; wines, Flamingo Bay Chenin-Blanc/Sauv-Blanc and/or Du Toitskloof Shiraz (a favourite of mine.).
Feel sure few tried Milky Way (Meikles' luxury cheeseboard, with petites-fours and tea or coffee) as it wasn't served at the time awards ended -- after 11 -- when almost everyone bombshelled yawning (fatigue, not boredom) leaving Victor Kunonga's music (an hour late) apparently unheard or danced to!
I had only eight floors to ascend by lift, gingerly removing juice-splattered breeks, before a quick shower, then the sleep of the just.
At breakfast next day ($351m) Dave Hughes and I talked, ploughing manfully though cereals, porridge, fresh fruit, fruit juice, lovely omelette, sausage, flapjacks, beans, grilled tomatoes etc; toast, a little cold meat and gallons of coffee; more toast and marmalade. The more you eat, the more you need: by lunch I was ravenous and I usually have one main meal a day.
A week earlier, on Meikles' opulent 13th floor pooldeck, Grapevine Club members enjoyed a cocktail-tasting and erudite talk on the disputed history of these mixed drinks from Afdis' Michael Gaskin, sartorially elegant in tuxedo. (I wore, as usual, shorts, golf-shirt, trainers!)
Among cunningly combined comely compatible concoctions consumed were a delicious Blue Lagoon (cane, lemonade, Blue Curacao, crushed ice, garnished with orange slice and cherry); Silver Sunset: vodka, apricot brandy, orange juice, ice, orange slice and cherry trim and Springbok: layered Amarula and crème-de-menthe. But, I found 007's famous vodka-martini (shaken, not stirred), with tart green olive and sharp lemon slice, dreadfully dry. James Bond can keep it!
Lensman Peter Barron snapped the proceedings but, unimpressed with rainbow-hued hooch, stoically sipped a cleansing ale at the al-fresco marble bar. I joined him!

Original_Search_Title: Kilimanjaro Diner, Lounge Home of African Dishes
Headline: Zimbabwe: Kilimanjaro Diner, Lounge Home of African Dishes
Body_Text:
Harare — There are a number of new restaurants sprouting up around town these days and challenging the older ones despite the economic difficulties.
Some restaurants are emerging with the main thrust of accommodating clients to the changing environment. In Greendale there is a new kid on the block that is meant to cater for residents in the eastern parts of Harare as well as others from various areas.
Just when many (myself included) had concluded that the once famous Red Fox Hotel had lost its lustre and needed a total makeover, something is already in the pipeline that is set to take Harare diners by storm. On my visit to the hotel this week, I was introduced to the Kilimanjaro Diner and Lounge, a restaurant whose specialisation is African dishes from the southern, eastern and western parts of the continent.
The restaurant, although having just opened its doors to the public, is still undergoing renovations and expects to be in full swing in April. Just like the Mt Kilimanjaro located in Tanzania, the restaurant aims to be a cut above the rest as it takes diners along a journey of a variety of African dishes.
New as it is I had my lunch there this week and indeed the quality of the food impressed me.
I was treated to a plate of sadza and poke bones with thinly sliced green vegetables while my colleague was served sadza with rich beef stew and a cold glass of coke. The meal was delicious and I just hoped that they would be able to maintain the standards and that it was not just one of the short-lived impressions of new restaurants to lure customers. Served at the new joint is Huku yekumusha (Karoad runner) with green vegetables, Kilimanjaro sadza (that is either heaped white sadza or sadza rezviyo).
Maguru and Matumbu (tripe and intestines), pork bones, beef bones, mufushwa une dove, muboora, rape and tsunga with peanut butter and sweet potato in sauce are some of the dishes to expect at Kilimanjaro. Other dishes include Kilimanjaro chicken that is grilled half chicken with a selection of vegetables, chips, rice or dovi rice, chilli chicken stew and green vegetables in season, braised pork chops, grilled T-bone steak and Kariba bream.
African dishes from various countries are served on different days. The restaurant also carries with it a bar that is quite stocked with different beverages.

Original_Search_Title: Fuel Crisis - Traders Stranded
Headline: Uganda: Fuel Crisis - Traders Stranded
Body_Text:
Kampala — A GROUP of Ugandan and Sudanese traders operating in the South Sudanese capital Juba are stranded in Arua town after failing to get trucks to transport their merchandise.
The general secretary Juba united transporters association, Francis Andama, said the vehicles were scarce due to the high cost of fuel.
"Trucks were available before Christmas but since the fuel crisis set in, very few are willing to go to Sudan. "We used to buy 20 litres of diesel at sh70,000 but now it costs over sh120,000," he said.
Andama added that because of the crisis, the cost of transporting merchandise from Arua to Sudan has gone up. The baggage fee for a sack of sweet potatoes went up to sh35,000 from sh28,000 while a sack of beans is charged sh40,000 up from sh30,000.
Only beer remained unchanged at sh5,000 per crate due to what Andama termed "low profit" on the beverage.
Fuel became scarce following the December 27 general elections in Kenya that resulted into violence. As such fuel trucks could not move from Mombasa port and Eldoret refinery.
When The New Vision visited the loading stage at Metilo in Arua town on Saturday, over 20 traders mainly female, were anxiously awaiting trucks.
"We have been here for almost a week. There are no trucks and the few that come are so expensive. We can't afford the charges," Lucy Hindi, a Sudanese female trader, lamented.
She said the unexpected delay was forcing them to use part of their capital on accommodation and upkeep. Hindi added that the traders would increase the price of their imports to South Sudan.

Original_Search_Title: Nile Breweries Aids Orphans
Headline: Uganda: Nile Breweries Aids Orphans
Body_Text:
Kampala — NILE Breweries has launched a programme to assist orphans and vulnerable children in the communities that grow epuripur sorghum for it.
The company, working with Health Initiatives in the Private Sector (HIPS), last week distributed assorted beddings and school items to 100 orphans in Lira and Soroti, respectively.
"Some of these children lost their parents to HIV/AIDS," said Nile Breweries' corporate affairs director,
Onapito-Ekomoloit.
He said since 2007, Nile Breweries has been running HIV/AIDS programmes in the sorghum-growing communities.
The beneficiaries were from Apac, Amolatar, Dokolo, Lira, Oyam, Amuria, Katakwi, Kaberamaido and Soroti and received items worth sh12m. These included mattresses and bed sheets.

Original_Search_Title: Sokoto Donates N36 Million Relief Materials to Flood Victims
Headline: Nigeria: Sokoto Donates N36 Million Relief Materials to Flood Victims
Body_Text:
The Sokoto State Government has donated assorted relief materials worth about N36 million to the victims of flood disasters in eight local government areas of the state.
A statement by Dr Kulu Haruna , Special Adviser to the Governor on Relief and
Ecological Matters, said that materials were given to victims in Shagari, Tambuwal, Tureta, Silame, Binji, Goronyo, Wamakko and Sokoto South local government areas.
The statement issued on Saturday in Sokoto said that the relief materials included rice, sorghum, millet, maize, roofing sheets, woods, blankets, mats and wrappers.
"The materials were donated to alleviate the sufferings of the victims," it said.
"Sokoto Emergency Relief Agency hopes that the local government councils will ensure justice and fairness in the distribution of the materials," it added.
Meanwhile, the Director of the Agency , Alhaji Sidi Al-Mustapha, has said that the gesture was never meant to compensate the flood victims for the losses.
"Rather, it is the widow's mite of the state government. Disasters like floods come from nature and they can only be stopped by God," he told the News Agency of Nigeria (NAN).
Reacting to threats by some of the flood victims in Shagari Local Government Area (LGA) to reject the relief materials, Al-Mustapha advised them against behaving like ingrates.
"The gesture by the government should only be appreciated by the people rather than making some obnoxious complaints," he said.
In a related development , the Secretary of Shagari LGA, Alhaji Adamu Romo, has called on the people not to allow some "unscrupulous politicians" to instigate them against the government's gesture .
"Nobody should unduly politicise the flood disasters; all natural calamities come from God and not from human beings or any administration," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Meet the Face of Africa Representative
Headline: Africa: Meet the Face of Africa Representative
Body_Text:
A look at Botswana's representative at the soon-to-start Face of Africa, Totwana "Tito" Tema says much about the young woman's character.
Tema certainly has the body and height that meet the M-Net's Face of Africa set standards.
The minimum height requirement for this year's Face of Africa is 1. 72 m, while the maximum hip requirement is 96 cm. The 24-year-old physiotherapist had her moment when she was selected as a finalist from a field of hopefuls who recently thronged the Gaborone International Convention Centre (GICC). But who is Totwana? Firstly, her name, Totwana, refers to one of the most beautifully handcrafted cultural items found in many households in Botswana, either serving as storage for grain like sorghum, vegetables and fruits. Totwana, commonly referred to as Tlatlana in the southern part of the country, can also be used for interior decoration.
Tema, who hails from Tutume, Central District, attended Baobab Primary School before doing her junior certificate at Moselewapula Junior Secondary School in Gaborone West. She later moved to Mater Spei Senior Secondary School in Francistown, but returned to Gaborone for her Form Six at Legae Academy.
After that, Tema went to South Africa, where she graduated in physiotherapy from the University of Cape Town (UCT) in January this year.
Tema, who prefers to be called Tito, describes herself as a people's person. She says she is a compassionate person and currently serves as the Zebras' senior national soccer team's physiotherapist. Tema is an ardent soccer fan and her English Premiership side is Liverpool. The young model is also an animal-lover who owns a cat and a Maltese puppy. She has only one sibling, a sister currently studying in Australia.
She told Showbiz that she loves reading, mainly non-fiction books. She also enjoys watching movies during her spare time and her genre of choice is comedy. Tema says she is not too much into partying but rather enjoys outdoor activities, including hiking and horse -riding. A big supporter of non-governmental organisations (NGOs) outreach programmes, Tema says she enjoys cooking and gardening as well.

Original_Search_Title: Food Riot At Refugee Camp in Darfur Kills One, Injuries Six Others - UN
Headline: Sudan: Food Riot At Refugee Camp in Darfur Kills One, Injuries Six Others - UN
Body_Text:
One person was killed and six others were injured during a food riot inside a camp that houses Chadian refugees in the Sudanese region of Darfur, the United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees (UNHCR) reports.
The agency said the incident occurred on Tuesday morning at the camp in Um Shalaya, about 70 kilometres southeast of El Geneina, the capital of West Darfur state.
Hundreds of stick-bearing women demonstrated noisily against a temporary reduction in the availability of sorghum, which is widely consumed in both Chad and Sudan, because of logistical problems. Continued insecurity has made it difficult to safely transport food from El Geneina to the camp at Um Shalaya.
About 6,600 refugees live in Um Shalaya, with new asylum-seekers arriving each month as they flee violence in their home areas of eastern Chad, where rebels have been fighting Government forces for months.
UNHCR reports that an estimated 50,000 refugees, mostly Chadian, have settled in border areas of Darfur, which has been beset by its own deadly conflict between rebels, Government forces and allied militiamen since 2003.
This week's food riot has occurred amid mounting UN concern about repeated attacks against humanitarian convoys throughout Darfur, a vast and impoverished region on Sudan's western flank.
The joint UN-African Union peacekeeping mission to the region, known as UNAMID, reported that a World Food Programme (WFP) mobile truck was carjacked yesterday near Moraya, which is about 55 kilometres northwest of Nyala, the South Darfur state capital.
The truck's drivers were robbed of their money and mobile phones, and the whereabouts of the WFP staff members and the vehicle are unknown, although the tracking device indicated the truck was heading in the general direction of the Jebel Marra area.
Three vehicles belonging to the UN Children's Fund (UNICEF) were also ambushed yesterday by unknown gunmen as the convoy returned from a visit to Yara in South Darfur.
A patrol from UNAMID was deployed to rescue the members of the convoy, which included a delegation from Khartoum and the head of UNICEF's office in Nyala. Two vehicles were rescued while the third reached Nyala with its passengers unharmed but looted of their property.

Original_Search_Title: Hepatitis E Claims 104 in Kitgum District
Headline: Uganda: Hepatitis E Claims 104 in Kitgum District
Body_Text:
Kampala — A TOTAL of 104 people have died in Kitgum District as a result of the deadly Hepatitis E virus that hit the district last October. To prevent more deaths, the district health department is carrying out massive community sensitisation, urging the public to maintain hygiene and sanitation discipline in their homes.
Kitgum deputy resident district commissioner Sylvester Opira said on Friday that 6,530 accumulative cases of the hepatitis E infected people have been registered at the district's health facilities.
Opira, also the chairman of the hepatitis E Kitgum district task force, said there is remarkable progress in the fight to eradicate the disease, although the death toll is going up.
The district chairman, John Ogwok Komakech, said by-laws are being enforced to ensure proper sanitation and adherence to public health requirements.
Ogwoksaid local leaders at the different community levels have been asked to ensure that each household has a pit latrine so that people stop defecating in the bushes.
"We have warned the community against keeping drinking water in clay pots. We have advised them to keep their water in jerry cans instead," Ogwok said.
We have also directed that the brewing or making Kwete, a local brew should be stopped until further notice since the Kwete which is made from maize or sorghum flour is one of the sources through which the hepatitis E disease is spread." the district chairman said.
The community, he said, are being mobilized and sensitized to avoid communal hand washing and to always wash their hands after visiting latrine as a measure to reduce on the risk of spreading the disease.

Original_Search_Title: Chibuku Road to Fame to Go Regional
Headline: Zimbabwe: Chibuku Road to Fame to Go Regional
Body_Text:
Harare — CHIBUKU Road to Fame - the country's music talent searching show - is expected to go regional beginning next year.
Delta Beverages public relations and promotions manager Amen Makonese said they intend to go regional in Sadc countries that sell Chibuku brand of opaque beer.
"After the Sorghum meeting held in Victoria Falls last weeks and the subsequent Victoria Falls and Matabeleland North provincial finals held in the resort town we resolved of a possibility of holding regional finals.
"Chibuku is brewed in Zimbabwe, Zambia, Malawi and Botswana - so next year there might be regional finals where winners from the four countries will compete for top honours in the region," Makonese said.
Makonese said top musicians from the four Sadc countries would then provide entertainment at the regional finale.
"We are still mapping the issue but regional finals for this year won't be possible but next year something is coming up.
"We want to give Chibuku Road to Fame a further step by making a regional showcase.
"Winners from their respective countries automatically qualify for the regional finale," Makonese said.
If Chibuku Road to Fame goes regional it would be the second talent searching shows involving live bands after Music Cross Roads.
Music Cross Roads is held annually in the region where winners in different countries converge for a showdown.
Meanwhile, Victoria Falls Express and Bulawayo's Say band were chosen as Matabeleland North and Matabeleland South provincial winners in competitions held last week.
Victoria Falls Express won in the provincial finals held in the resort town while Say Band got the nod in the finals held in Bulawayo.
Sungura musician Gift Amuli provided entertainment at the Victoria Falls finals where Cuthbert Maziwa of the Eye Q joined him.
This weekend the show rolls to the Gold City of Kadoma where one winner in three provinces taking part will make it to the grand finale scheduled for September 27 at the Glamis Arena.
The Kadoma show will be held at Kadoma Leisure Centre in Monomotapa suburb.
Sungura giants Tongai Moyo and Alick Macheso will provide entertainment at the grand finale where they will be sharing the stage for the first time in a full year.

Original_Search_Title: Macheso, Moyo Set to Share Stage Again
Headline: Zimbabwe: Macheso, Moyo Set to Share Stage Again
Body_Text:
Harare — ALMOST a year after musicians Alick Macheso and Tongai Moyo ended their Sungura War hatchet and shared the stage - organisers of the Chibuku Road to Fame are battling to bring the two musicians together for another joint show.
The two musicians are expected to share the stage at the Glamis Stadium on September 27 as guest performers at the annual talent search show.
Macheso and Tongai shared the stage last year on September 29 courtesy of Delta Beverages who succeeded where many local promoters had failed.
Delta Beverages public relations and sponsorship manager, Amen Makonese, this week revealed that Chibuku Road to Fame was seeking entertainment from Tongai and Macheso.
"I can confirm that we are hunting Macheso and Tongai so that they provide entertainment during this year's edition of Chibuku Road to Fame.
"Three top musicians will perform at the Glamis as we reward the best upcoming musicians. Gift Amuli is certainly one of them and it is time he gets his recognition, as he is a good musician.
"Macheso and Tongai should be the other two," Makonese said.
Tongai said he has always been in good relations with Chibuku Road to Fame sponsors and he was looking forward to the day.
"I have worked well with Delta Beverages so I am taking part at the event.
"While we, musicians, might want to raise certain things it is certainly the truth that fans want Macheso and I to share the stage. It is a common wish for fans that we play together.
"Deep down in our hearts we know what people want.
"I am looking forward to sharing the stage with Alick and I tell you it would be a wonderful show," Tongai said.
While Macheso could not be reached, he is on record as saying that despite running shows under his company he would always support talent-searching shows.
Meanwhile, selection in the provinces begins today with provincial finals for Matabeleland North to be held in the resort town of Victoria Falls.
The show will be held at Chinotimba Beer Hall where it coincides with the SAB Miller Sorghum Manufacturers Conference to be held in the border town.
Executives from Zambia, Botswana, Malawi and the host country will witness the Victoria Falls and Matabeleland North provincial finals.
Tomorrow, Matabeleland South and Bulawayo will hold their provincial finals at Manor Hotel while Mash West, Masvingo and Midlands will battle for honours in Kadoma's Leisure Centre in Monomotapa on August 2.
Chitungwiza and Harare groups will have their showdown at Vito Tavern on August 8 while Mash-East, Central and Manicaland will lock horns on August 15 at the Cleopas Inn in Marondera.
Makonese said the winning group in each provincial competition will pocket $10 trillion while they will unveil the grand finale prize closer to the final.
"We will reveal the grand finale prize as the date gets closer. As you know we are in an inflationary environment so we will talk of the prize later as we might adjust it to suit the prevailing situation," Makonese said.
The other prize is a recording contract with Gramma Records for a cassette, CD and the DVD.
"We will take only five winners and they will each play four songs and adjudicated on those in the final showdown. Gramma also indicated that the musicians must have at least four good songs on the album - so that the product sells on the market," Makonese said.

Original_Search_Title: Global Biofuels Invests N87.5 Billion in Ethanol Project
Headline: Nigeria: Global Biofuels Invests N87.5 Billion in Ethanol Project
Body_Text:
Lagos — Global Biofuels Limited, the first biofuels refinery in Nigeria, endorsed by the Nigerian National Petroleum Corporation (NNPC), has committed the sum of N87.5billion to ethanol project in the country.
To foster rapid production, the company has also brought in two varieties of sweet sorghum to be produced for feedstock from PRAJ in India while more varieties of feedstock had been developed by the company.
Managing Director and Chief Executive Officer of Global Biofuels, Dr. Felix Babatunde Obada, made this disclosure, at the ground breaking and foundation laying ceremony of the project in Ekiti State, over the weekend, revealing that the company has also invested N12.5 billion in the same project in Ekiti.
According to him, NNPC is the organisation charged with the responsibility of regulating and generally growing the biofuels sector. He listed PRAJ India, ICRISAT, IAR Samaru, Zaria, Mariano Marcos State University of Philippines, African Development Bank (ADB), UBA Global Markets, Skye Bank Plc and Santrust Securities Limited as other partners working with the company on the ethanol project in Nigeria.
The Global Biofuels boss explained that Nigeria stands a good chance of benefiting from ethanol production as nations around the world are looking at biofuels to help reduce spiralling oil import costs, and to mitigate pollution and global warming.
He added that developing, manufacturing, installing and maintaining renewable energy technologies as is the case with ethanol has the potential to create new Nigeria-led jobs and modern industries.
"This would not only serve to empower entrepreneurs and companies to build vibrant and diverse economy, it is also a wise economic choice for long term development. As fossil fuel depletes, Nigeria would have diverse dependable energy sources and be able to strategically benefit from our oil resources," he stressed.
Also speaking, the Governor of Ekiti State, Segun Oni, assured the company of his full support on the project in terms of road construction to link the project site with the surrounding communities and other infrastructures needed to make the project optimise outputs in the State.
He expressed optimisim that Ekiti would become the ethanol hub of Nigeria within a short possible time.

Original_Search_Title: Nyamambi - the Cobbler From 'The Mines'
Headline: Botswana: Nyamambi - the Cobbler From 'The Mines'
Body_Text:
With a natural flair for business, it took him no time to identify the entrance to FurnMart in downtown Francistown as a strategic point to spot shoes in need of mending. And as Correspondent Gale Ngakane found out, Pelling Nyamambi was right
He vividly remembers the day he started his cobbling business under the veranda of Furniture Mart here.
But it was way back in 1992. After a long spell in Johannesburg where Pelling Nyamambi gorged the bowels of the earth and shovelled the gold ore into waiting wagons, the day came when he decided to come back to his native country.
Diminutive in stature, his legs crooked like those of a footballer of old, the youth-faced Nyamambi is reluctant to reveal his age. But with five children, the eldest (a girl) doing Form 5 by correspondence after an unsuccessful attempt last year, it is easy enough to tell that the man must have been born at about Botswana's attainment of independence in 1966.
It must have been yesteryear's norm for men who went on 'joini' to the mines in South Africa to come home kitted out in a nice suit, a bicycle decorated with mirrors, a headlamp and 'stoplights' (reflective lights), and money to burn.
A story goes about one such a man who sat at the bar counter and could never seem to finish his beer. Once, when a friend enquired into the beer that never seemed to get finished, the man shook his head from side to side as he put his palm to his nape and said disdainfully:
"My nape pains when I look up. Besides, is not drinking and looking up supposed to be for the chickens and their feathered friends?"
But after some time, the money of such men was to get finished, the bicycle would lose its shiny mirrors and the suit get frayed, its trousers reduced to no more than Bermuda shorts.
And instead of sitting at the counter quaffing lager and leaving tins half empty, 'your man from the mines' would become a regular at grass-thatched huts.
In time he would be seen chopping firewood for a "s'kale" of mokuru or bojalwa (a measure of traditional sorghum beer), even khadi (a more potent homebrew reliable for a quick fix) at the humble homesteads.
And thereafter, over his well-earned drink, the man would delve into epic accounts of why and how he had lost his incisors in a fistfight with a Xhosa moegoe (idiot) who ended in a hospital bed.
But Nyamambi was never of such ilk. As a man raising a family and caring for a widowed mother who looks to him for her upkeep, there was no way he could just sit home twiddling his fingers or hopping from shebeen to shebeen.
So it happened that in 1988, Nyamambi enrolled for a shoe cobbler's course at the Senyawe Brigade. Upon completion, he spent some time trying his newfound skills in his home village of Butale mending neighbours' shoes and his own children's before deciding to try the more lucrative market of Francistown.
Armed with a natural flair for business, it took Nyamambi little time to identify the entrance to FurnMart downtown Francistown as a strategic point to spot shoes in need of mending.
Monitor found him at the beginning of his day's work on a recent Wednesday morning. A typical day begins with Nyamambi pulling out a shoe laden, hand-drawn cart from a hideaway warren in which he leaves it every evening when he 'repairs' to his home.
Then, sitting on a stool with his back to the wall, he daintily takes the shoes out of the bag and places them - in accordance with state of disrepair - on a mat on the floor before getting down to serious work.
When he is done with a pair, it is proudly displayed on the pavement, the owner's name conspicuously printed on a visible part.
"When I first came here, that bank (he says, pointing across the street at a Standard Chartered branch) was not there. Nor were any of these other buildings around here.
Furniture Mart was still here, of course. I was the first cobbler on Blue Jacket Street and Haskins across here."
Nyamambi says business is as brisk as ever. But what he finds irksome in this business is that it seems there will always be people who drop shoes off and then disappear for up to three months.
These are usually people who say they are "just gonna nip into the bank and be back in a jiffy," Nyamambi says.
But he is not about to 'steal' another person's property. "Understandably, some cobblers end up selling the shoes of these defaulters to defray their costs," he explains. "But not me; my conscience doesn't allow me to take such a course."
As for retirement, 'our man from the mines' who is of the Zion Christian Church persuasion says he will only consider that after his second born child, a son, finishes school.
"With the money I am making here, I pay my children's school fees and cover their various needs. There is no how I could retire when I still have these obligations," Nymambi points out.
"I am hoping to stop working when my son finishes school. He is very intelligent. I am optimistic he will do well so he may look after his younger siblings."

Original_Search_Title: Govt Investment in Roads is a Bad Choice
Headline: Uganda: Govt Investment in Roads is a Bad Choice
Body_Text:
Kampala — We have all read or heard that it is government's lubimbi (duty) to build and maintain the country's infrastructure. The president has on many occasions talked about transport system (roads, railways, ships, aeroplanes etc) as the main physical infrastructure necessary for development.
The government has therefore gone ahead to repair and maintain some roads while also building and upgrading others. My experience, analysis and training however leads me to the conclusion that roads are not the best investment for a developing country like Uganda.
Over the recent past and today, government has worked on the roads to Fort Portal, Kisoro, Nebbi, Arua and others. By improving the road system in this way, costs of delivering goods and services to the market should either reduce or producers should earn higher dividends or both.
However, in one of President Museveni's "lectures", he said that despite Kabale district having a tarmac road, the Bakiga still grow stunted sorghum. This is of course true and indication that life has not changed for a typical Mukiga since 30 years or so ago.
Over their short whole life, roads are expensive, although they may be comparably cheaper to build. Roads are as such only of populist appeal as opposed to having a high infrastructural value. Governments all over the world recognise this and tend to invest in alternative transport systems. In particular they recognise that roads offer limited haulage and speeds and can't be expanded easily.
On the other hand rail and water transport offer real benefits both in the short and long term. Of course government has not invested in these areas in any appreciable form over the last 20 years.
A railway line to Arua, Kisoro, Kasese and Kotido, would be immensely valuable as a transport infrastructure. These railway lines supported with a water transport infrastructure would be capable of transporting massive quantities of goods and services within and at Uganda's borders.
The way this works is that roads are constructed to radiate from railway-trading-centres to the hinterland. In this approach emphasis is on feeder roads that support the central transport system.
Cabbages, cattle, sorghum, rice, matooke, coffee etc would be quickly collected from the hinterland and massively hauled to the markets where they are needed. People who are assured travelling at less cost, time, and pollution and more comfortably would opt for rail transport.
Equally, water transport which is more expensive may be built to support the railway transport system. Ferries on the lakes and major rivers, invested into to serve as a network would be more effective than roads.
There may be arguments against these approaches and they will mainly rely on cost of construction. In such an analysis it is preferable to carry out a whole life cost analysis.
Comparing a 1000km road network with an equivalent length railway network that is built and maintained over 50 years would give a fair comparison. A railway system may easily be privatised than a road network.
Let us not make the same mistakes that others have made but rather learn from them for a better Uganda and our children. By concentrating on feeder roads, we work directly for the wealth producers and take out the many middlemen.
It should also be noted that railway systems are easy and cheaper to expand in capacity, speed and haulage rates.
The writer is a researcher, Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering, National University of Ireland

Original_Search_Title: SABMiller in Profit Growth
Headline: Uganda: SABMiller in Profit Growth
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — Even in the face of rising fuel prices and a host of other input cost increases, Uganda's Nile Breweries parent company SABMiller plc, has managed to register a strong rise in earnings from their 2008 financial year.
According to results announced here at the Sandton Convention Centre, May 15, the overall net cash generated from operations after working capital movements was 6 per cent above that of the prior year (2007), reflecting an increase in working capital across the Group as at March 31, 2008.
"This strong outturn to the year is particularly pleasing given the scale of the challenge we faced at its outset, with exceptional prior year comparatives, rising input costs and an increasingly competitive environment in many of our markets. It is a clear testament to the strength of our brands and the group's operational capability," said Meyer Kahn, Chairman of SABMiller.
Total beverage volumes were up by 6 per cent, up to 288 million hectoliters while total lager volumes went up by 11 per cent, up to 239 million hectoliters.
These volumes were also boosted by the impact of acquisitions in China and Europe. In Uganda, lager volumes grew by a rather modest 4 per cent after three prior years of exceptional growth.
Vital to note is that after excellent growth in recent years, volumes of the sorghum-based Eagle brand declined following an excise duty increase (which currently stand at 30 per cent) while the mainstream lager brands Nile Special and Club grew in mid double digits.
The Uganda market continues to grow and has nearly doubled in the last four years. SABMiller's Managing Director for Africa, Mr Mark Bowman. said the percentage growth would have possibly been higher if it were not for the rather exorbitant increase in excise duty.

Original_Search_Title: GCB Manages 768 Enterprises With a Value of Gh¢23 Million- MD
Headline: Ghana: GCB Manages 768 Enterprises With a Value of GhÂ¢23 Million- MD
Body_Text:
Mr Lawrence Adu-Mante, Managing Director (MD) of the Ghana Commercial Bank (GCB) has said that the Bank with its wealth of experience over the years had nurtured local business for the economic development of the country.
He said the Small and Medium Scale Enterprises (SMEs) Division of the Bank set up four years ago was managing about 768 facilities with a current value of GHc 23 million.
Mr Adu- Mante said this in a speech read on his behalf at a workshop for SME customers in Tamale on Thursday.
He said the Bank had established satellite offices in Kumasi and Takoradi to bring quality services and an improved relationship with its customers.
The workshop brought together SMEs from the Northern, Upper East and West Regions and had the theme: "Towards effective management and business profitability".
Participants at the workshop were lectured on Credit documentation for small and Medium Scale Enterprises, Client relationship management, Facility account operation, international trade finance for SMEs, Understanding your business as an entrepreneur and the Basic bookkeeping for SMEs.
Mr Adu-Mante noted that one of the most positive stories of industrial development in post independence Ghana had been the growth and diversification of the small scale sector and the emergence of a class of first generation entrepreneurs through the ranks of the small scale industry.
He said SMEs contributed significantly towards the growth of the economy and was essential for competitive and efficient market and critical for poverty reduction as well as a source of technological innovation and new products.
Mr Adu-Mante said the workshop was unique for the region because of certain developments, which had brought it to the limelight to enjoy facilities such as the Millennium Challenge Account (MCA), a new sports stadium, rehabilitation and expansion of the Tamale water system and the revival of the cotton and sorghum cultivation business.
He mentioned other developments such as the re-opening of the Northern Star Tomato Factory, the construction of strategic depot for petroleum products and the reconstruction of the Wa-Bamboi road.
The MD said unlike other Banks, which closed down their branches when they were not making profits GCB had stayed to offer banking services and support.
"You are our partners and we will never shun your companies because we believe in the ability of SMEs to provide employment, especially new jobs in most communities and to rekindle economic activities," he added.
Alhaji Mustapha Ali Idris, Northern Regional Minister urged the GCB to open new branches in the region and introduce facilities such as mobile banking to bring banking services to the doorsteps of the people.
He also urged the bank to intensify its public relations drive by educating the people on the facilities and opportunities they offer.
The Regional Minister called on the chiefs of the region to impress on their people to continue maintaining the peace it is enjoying and noted that all the current developments taking place as a result of the relative peace in the area. GNA

Original_Search_Title: Delta Requires 50 000t of Maize
Headline: Zimbabwe: Delta Requires 50 000t of Maize
Body_Text:
