Harare — DELTA says it requires 50 000 tonnes of maize to cover the full year to March 2009 but is only left with just under two months' stock at the Grain Marketing Board and its breweries used mainly in the production of the Chibuku Scud brand.
Chief executive Mr Joe Mtizwa said because of the national grain deficit this year, the group had contracted 2 500 hectares of maize with an expectant yield of 10 000 tonnes.
Delta, however, has sufficient malting sorghum (for Chibuku) in stock "which gives us room for formulation changes". Barley, used in lager production, was enough to last to September this year with the group having contracted 8 000 hectares against a target of 9 000 hectares.
Mr Mtizwa said malt exports had been curtailed to 68 percent of last year to ensure full domestic supply although overall volumes of lager had declined 17 percent in the financial year ending March 31. Sorghum volumes were down 15 percent while sparkling beverages were 8 percent down, plastic 36 percent lower and glass 48 percent down. The reporting year had been "a bit of a mix" with margins narrowed in the first half and costs running ahead of revenue growth as price controls were imposed in July. "The second half has shown a much better performance despite curtailed volumes. A more flexible and pragmatic approach was adopted by NIPC to our price applications," Mr Mtizwa said.
In the year to March 31, the group reported a 180 436 percent increase in revenue to $677,011 trillion from $375 billion. Operating income was $928,614 trillion from last year's $145 billion, an increase of 369 865 percent. Finance director Mr Rob Maunsell noted that the growth was above turnover because they had included gains on realised investments and bio-assets adjustments. Mr Mtizwa said the realised gains were from the sale of Sparletta. Operating margins were 163,7 percent against 74,2 percent last year.
Mr Maunsell said "margins expanded significantly in the first quarter compared to the previous year but declined in the second quarter following the price freeze". They increased considerably in the second half as inflation took hold. He said the target in the current hyperinflationary environment was 40 percent. Pre-tax profit was $891,886 trillion (2007: $270 billion), and attributable profit was $534,6 trillion against $166 billion last year, an increase of 321 977 percent.
Earnings per share amounted to $511 523 although excluding revaluations EPS would amount to $193 427. Mr Maunsell noted March alone made up 70 percent of "normal" operating income while March-on-March operating income, adjusted to remove all the unusual items, increased by 443 338 percent.

Original_Search_Title: Food Prices And the Poor
Headline: South Africa: Food Prices And the Poor
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — FRESH fruit and vegetables have long been zero rated for value added tax (VAT) purposes; so are staples such as milk, maize meal, brown bread and rice. Even canned pilchards are on the zero ratings list.
But from time to time there have been calls for the list to be extended. And with food prices soaring, it's at least worth considering whether the plight of poor households might be eased a bit by zero rating more foodstuffs.
That the government is doing, though it's not entirely clear why the cabinet has chosen baby food, sorghum and chicken as the three items to target. The administrative systems are there so it's easy enough to grant further exemptions from VAT. But that always means some loss of revenue to the fiscus. And the crucial issue is whether the poor would indeed derive the bulk of the benefit. Often when there are calls to exempt extra items from VAT, it turns out that these are consumed in larger proportions by the rich, so more of the benefit goes to them than to the poor. There would appear to be a good case for zero rating a widely consumed protein such as chicken, in particular -- but only if the arithmetic of costs and benefits stacks up to ensure any such move would be pro-poor.
Zero rating would in any event provide only a small discount on the price of basic foods. Far more promising are the more targeted initiatives the government is looking at to help poor households survive high food prices. There is scope to extend existing initiatives: boosting school feeding schemes is one option; another is to make more use of the social relief of distress grant that is designed to provide short-term aid to poor people who don't have access to other social grants. The government is also considering new initiatives such as food stamps that would target the poor. This approach is far more likely to reach those who need it than elaborate price controls or subsidies that could distort markets and ultimately do the poor more harm than good. Whatever the health minister might believe, markets need to be allowed to work.

Original_Search_Title: Hanging Onto Dilapidated Houses
Headline: Uganda: Hanging Onto Dilapidated Houses
Body_Text:
The Naguru and Nakawa housing estates were not supposed to last for more than 30 years.
Now more than half a century later, almost all the 1,431 houses still stand, and the thousands of occupants still fight to stay in them, shabby, mouldy and dilapidated as they are. But home is best. Even if it is a crumbling, scruffy, structure.
According to the 1954 Governor Cohen's report, these houses were built on an owner-occupier scheme, which states that once a tenant occupies a house for more than 15 years, he/she is free to begin acquiring the property through a mortgage scheme.
The report also mentions the Ntinda, Entebbe, Jinja, Tororo, Mbarara estates, some of which, like those of Ntinda have already been bought off and turned into modern picturesque housing facilities by their previous occupants, a chance the Nakawa and Naguru tenants would like to be given.
John (not real name) an employee with UN has lived in group A of the Naguru quarters since the 70s. An old boat, an old motorcycle and two old vehicles abandoned in the back of his house can clearly attest to this. His father moved there when he was just a young boy. Now, all grown up, he has taken up the same house that his father lived in. "It is not a good place, it is not in good shape, but I would grab the opportunity to develop it given chance," he says.
Numerous families in the estates echo this desire.
But the Naguru Nakawa tenancy is shrouded in mystery. Of a ghost association that sold off good will tenancy to the highest bidders while they intimidated existing tenants into selling off and moving out.
An association headed by a self appointed chairman (Godfrey Kaganda) that even sold off and registered plots that do not exist, invented a new block of houses (group Z) and went behind the tenants' backs and held talks with the ministry that resulted in the Naguru tenants getting eviction letters. It is a conspiracy. That is the cry that escapes the Naguru tenants' lips. A scam that is currently being investigated by the Inspector General of Government.
But the only claim that these tenants have to these houses is that they have stayed in them for long. And yet have left them to go to ruins, most have never painted them since they moved in, fixed the drainage or even constructed better latrines, while others have turned them into havens for malwa drinkers. Quarters previously built for civil servants, now look a little more like slum dwellings.
The bigger part of group B has been turned into a small enguli brewing village strewn with pots, dried sorghum husks, shanty drinking places held together by tired looking, hole riddled iron sheets.
It is slightly after 2p.m., but there is already a healthy crowd in one of the bars, seated around a bucket (formerly used to hold detergent) of the bitter fermented porridge, bubbles foaming at the top, drinking from one straw. I walk in and join a group of five men, who excitedly ask me to join them as they pass the straw around to where I am.
This is a defiant group who are not shaken by the eviction notices. None of the other tenants are this confident. Maybe it is the alcohol, or maybe this group of drunken people knows something that others don't.
Chatting with a neighbour through the window is 22-year-old George William Ejukat who has lived in B10 for most of his adult life. He understands that there is need for development, but he disagrees with the way it is being handled. "The tenants are not being given all the information. For example we do not know exactly who is going to develop this place, or what exactly is their plan to do with us the tenants."
Such grievances drove the tenants to form an association that would ensure that their interests would be taken care of. LC III Chairman Mr. Simon Barigo, also chairman of the association says they are fighting for the rights of tenants to acquire low income, high rise flats under the Condominium Law. This law gives the opportunity for the private sector to put up houses for rent whereas reserving rights for one to own a title for the housing unit he lives in.
The fight for these rights has seen Naguru tenants battle it out with the government in court since January in a bid to stay in those homes, through their lawyers Alaka and Company Advocates. The tenants seem to be winning this battle so far, as eviction plans have been halted by the Kampala High Court.
But while they still tussle it out in court, tenants continue to sit (or sleep?) on gold, because the Naguru Nakawa properties, in terms of ground rent and leasing are rated as highly as the likes of Bugolobi, Nakasero and Ntinda. And Opaque Prime Properties, the company contracted to develop these areas will make a killing, should court ever decide that it is time for the tenants to pack up and go. And most of them do not have a place to go.
For the most part, the estates look neglected and abandoned. The best looking of the lot could use a paint job, just to appear decent enough to house people, but that is only a small fraction.
Some, especially those along the railway line have crumbled completely. For the rest of them, it looks like it is only a matter of time before the roofs come down on their heads, what with the leaking roofs, cracked walls and the shaky foundations on which they stand.
Whether by private investor or by individuals that live in them, these houses need to be revamped. And soon.

Original_Search_Title: Refugees Struggle With Life in Adjumani Camps
Headline: Uganda: Refugees Struggle With Life in Adjumani Camps
Body_Text:
Adjumani — The West Nile region is home to thousands of Sudanese refugees despite being devastated by the West Nile Bank Front rebels and the LRA wars over the years.
The Sudanese refugees, mainly of the Dinka tribe, have lived in the camps in West Nile region for close to 25 years since the Sudanese Peoples Liberation Army (SPLA) led by the late Col. John Garang waged a war against the Kartoum regime in 1983.
The refugees are also scattered across the northern region and parts of the West such as Kiryandongo refugee camp in Masindi and in the districts of Moyo, Arua, Nebbi, Adjumani and Koboko in West Nile.
The Chairman of the refugees at Mirieyi camp in Adjumani District, Mr William Maral, says the camp was established in 1994 and that most of the refugees have intermarried with the Madi in the area.
Despite the intermarriages and the good relations the refugees enjoy with the residents, life inside the camp is difficult.
They depend on food aid from the United Nations High Commission for Refugees (UNHCR) and have to do other odd jobs to meet their other needs.
"We basically survive through support by the UNHCR which gives food items which include: beans, cooking oil, maize and sorghum," said Mr John Majuru, a refugee from Jongale State. " Sometimes these food items delay and we find it hard to cope," said Mr Samuel Akot from Bar el Gazel State.
Mr Edward Manyoro told Daily Monitor that though the UNHCR supplies maize and sorghum, it does not give money for grinding. And that this forces the refugees to sell part of the food items to raise money for grinding as well as meet other needs.
"An item like cooking oil for instance, has also not been delivered for the last two months (February and March)," said Mr Samuel Akot.
Maize, beans and sorghum dealers who buy from the refugees take the items to Sudan where they reap big profits. A kilogramme of sorghum in Sudan costs 35 Sudanese pounds, an equivalent of Shs7,500.

Original_Search_Title: Kuguru Overcame Odds to Build Soda Empire
Headline: Kenya: Kuguru Overcame Odds to Build Soda Empire
Body_Text:
Anyone looking at Mr Peter Ngibuini Kuguru will most likely envy his business empire.
Not reflected on his face, however, are the several disappointments he has had to overcome to get to where he is today.
How would you know that availability of credit facilities and at times patronage have played a role in his success?
Early in his life, Kuguru spent his time grazing his father's livestock. Then one day he accompanied his mother who used to run his father's grocery shop in Karatina. That was his induction into the world of business.
As a student at Kagumo High School, Kuguru was in charge of operating the school's film projector.
At Kenyatta College where he did his A Levels, he landed a similar role as the chairman of the film club. This entailed acquiring and screening movies to other students at a fee.
No wonder when The Sound of Music was released in 1965, its lyrics inspired his mantra. The theme song encouraged people to climb mountains, search low and high and make sure that they followed every rainbow till they found their dream.
Kuguru also bought a box camera and used it to take photographs. Since then, he has ventured where many would rather not.
He was behind the launch of the controversial MEGA Bank, Kuguru Foods Complex whose alcoholic beverages like Kibuku Sorghum Sake and International Beer were banned and now the Softa range of soft drinks.
These are projects that potray a man not ready to give up on his ambitions. Over the years, Kuguru has been trying to live his dream - sometimes faltering, other times breaking through.
On several occasions, he has unsuccessfully vied for the Mathira parliamentary seat. His most recent attempt wast last year.
The man has toyed with Kuguru Noodles which was financed through a loan from ICDC and assistance from Kenya Industrial Estates (KIE), two government arms set up to assist entrepreneurs.
He has also tried his hand in the hotel business with his Karatina Tourist Lodge, a Chinese restaurant, ice cream vending and even freelance photography.
While at Kenyatta College, he acquired an old motorcycle which he rode to surrounding townships to sell ice cream. Most of those attempts are now forgotten.
As he launched his autobiography titled Trailblazer- Breaking through in Kenya, last week, it was his Softa Bottling Company that dominated the event.
Whether in the city centre or estates like Eastleigh and Huruma where the low income earners live, Softa has been quenching many thirsty throats. At Uhuru Park, one will most times find crowds sipping from bottles of Softa, which targets the mass market.
Also on sale here are the more expensive soft drinks. Though they have been in the market longer, these drinks are apparently not as popular with these consumers.
Being what it is - a free public recreational centre - Uhuru Park attracts visitors who cannot afford the luxury of lunch worth more than Sh50. Softa comes in handy for these folks.
When Softa Bottling Company first introduced these drinks in 2000, cynics dismissed the business as a futile attempt.
Then, few companies targeted the mass market, especially in the soft beverage market, which were perceived as products that could only be sustained by upmarket consumers.
"It is like crushing granite. Most folks equate soft drinks with Coca- Cola to the extent that anything else is not soda," Kuguru told the East African newspaper in 1999, a year after relaunching Softa, a trademark acquired from the Highlands Mineral Water Company.
Mr Peter Ngibuini Kuguru
But why crushing granite? Softa was venturing into a market that had been dominated by Coca-Cola, a multinational giant whose headquarters in Atlanta, United States, wallow in money and whose advertising spend is enormous.
Today, Softa soda comes in 11 flavours. After the launch of Softa, Kuguru's efforts started getting noticed.
But that was after a long trek.
Softa Bottling Company came to life in 2000 as a subsidiary of Kuguru Food Complex Ltd to concentrate on manufacturing of carbonated and non-carbonated beverages.
Softa caused ripples in the market and, in reaction, Coca-Cola reduced its prices.
The journey of Softa started in 1991 and is a family business.
Earlier in 1990, Kuguru, who is the son of the late Davidson Kuguru Ngubuini, was slowly getting initiated into Kanu's national politics table, albeit by accident.
At some point, he met the then president Daniel arap Moi and his aides at the Sagana State Lodge and slowly started getting contacts.
At some point, he ventured into Kanu politics. His father was the main Kanu man in Mathira constituency for many years. Thanks to his father's cronies who were getting uncomfortable, the man withdrew from active politics then.
To keep busy, Kuguru first ventured into beer business.
Chibuku Beer Company was shutting down. In the Kenyan market, the Chibuku trademark was held by the Lonrho Group. Being an expert in food technology, Kuguru approached Lonrho and bought the business.
To buy it, he borrowed from banks, Lornho itself and even sold a piece of land. In 1992, he released the first bottles of Chibuku beer before he renamed it Kibuku. The brand targeted the low income group.
Kuguru writes: "I spent a lot of time observing the consumers of cheap alcohol and studying their habits... a large population of Kenyan population enjoys alcoholic drinks. Most Kenyan drinkers drink to get drunk and not jut to enjoy the drink."
When people got drunk, trouble was brewing for the Kugurus as people took to the streets in condemnation of some of the brands. This gave birth to the Softa idea.
"My family recommended that it was undesirable to continue the brewing business and so we shut it," he writes.

Original_Search_Title: Humanitarian Situation Worsening - UN
Headline: Somalia: UN Says Humanitarian Situation Worsening Faster Than Expected
Body_Text:
The humanitarian situation in Somalia is deteriorating faster than expected, owing to an unusually harsh dry season, rising insecurity and soaring inflation rates, the United Nations Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs (OCHA) said today.
The UN has revised upwards the number of those living in a state of humanitarian emergency from 315,000 to 425,000, and the number of newly displaced people from 705,000 to 745,000.
The total number of those needing assistance in the country is estimated to be around 1.8 million, but that figure could increase to 2 million once current assessments are concluded, OCHA said in a news release.
Somalia has experienced an extremely dry season from January to March with high temperatures and unusually dry winds. The dry conditions have also affected other countries in the region, including Djibouti, Ethiopia, Eritrea and parts of Kenya.
In addition, the country - like many others - has witnessed a dramatic increase in food prices over the past year. The prices of locally produced maize and sorghum have increased by 300 to 400 per cent, while imported foods such as rice and vegetable oil have increased by some 150 per cent. At the same time, the Somali shilling has depreciated by 65 per cent.
Along with the dry weather and rise in food prices, there has also been a diarrhoea outbreak in the Sanaag region, which has claimed seven lives among the 300 cases recorded since 10 March.
Meanwhile, further clashes were reported last week between Ethiopian/Transitional Federal Government (TFG) forces and anti-Government elements in many parts of south-central Somalia.
Deteriorating security in recent months has made it more difficult for aid workers to assist those in need in the strife-torn East African nation, which has not had a functioning government since 1991.

Original_Search_Title: High Commodity Prices Affect West Nile
Headline: Sudan: High Commodity Prices Affect West Nile
Body_Text:
Kampala — The high commodity prices in South Sudan have greatly affected West Nile region and made life difficult for local residents and visitors to the region.
A price survey in and around Adjumani town indicates that prices of some essential commodities such as sugar, salt, posho flour, rice and soap among others are 100 percent higher than those in most places in the west, central and eastern parts of the country.
A kilogramme of fish for instance, which costs Shs6,000 in Adjumani town is reported to be at Shs15,000 in Juba in South Sudan, making fish-mongers rush to Sudan instead of selling the fish in Adjumani.
Super rice which sells at 1,400 per kilogramme in Tororo for instance, goes for Shs2,500 in Adjamani while maize flour which sells at Shs800 per kilogramme in Mbale goes for Shs2000 in Adjumani.
A 50kg bag of maize and sorghum going for Shs15,000 in most parts in the Eastern region is sold at 1,750 Sudanese Pound, an equivalent of Shs375,000. The souring prices of food items have made prices of food in restaurants and other eating places in Adjumani town.
Ms Sarah Apio, a food vendor in Adjumani town says it is a matter of survival to run any business in the town.
"The price at which you sell is dictated by the price you purchase the item. Everything here goes to Sudan because
the business people who go there sell at higher prices and benefit a lot due to the high demand for commodities there.
We who do not go to Sudan also have to survive by selling at a price where we we make some minimal profit," she said. High prices in Ajumani also forced schools to abandon the National Primary Schools Athletics Championship that took place March 31 to April 5.

Original_Search_Title: Commodity Prices Affect West Nile
Headline: Uganda: Commodity Prices Affect West Nile
Body_Text:
Adjumani — The high commodity prices in Southern Sudan have greatly affected West Nile region and made life difficult for local residents and visitors to the region.
A price survey in and around Adjumani town indicates that prices of some of the essential commodities such as sugar, salt, posho flour, rice and soap among others are 100 percent higher than those in most places in the West, Central and Eastern parts of the country.
A kilogramme of fish for instance, which costs Shs6,000 in Adjumani town is reported to be costing Shs15,000 in Juba in Southern Sudan, making fish-mongers rush to Sudan than sell the fish in Adjumani.
Super rice which sells at Shs1,400 per kilogram in Tororo for instance, goes at Shs2500 in Adjamni and Posho which sells at Shs800 per kilogram in Mbale goes at Shs2000 in Adjumani.
A 50kg bag of maize and sorghum which sells at Shs15,000 in most parts of Eastern region is sold at 1750 Sudanese Pounds, an equivalent of Uganda Shs375,000.
The souring prices of food items have made prices of food in restaurants and other eating places in Adjumani town astronomical.
A plate of posho and beans which goes at Shs500 in local eating places in Tororo, Mbale and Soroti for instance, goes at Shs2000 in Adjumani.
Ms Sarah Apio, a food vendor in Adjumani town says that it is a matter of survival to run any business in the town.
"The price at which you sell is dictated by the price you purchase the item. Everything here goes to Sudan because the business people who go there sell at higher prices and benefit a lot due to the high demand of commodities there.
This has affected everything here. We who do not go to Sudan also have to survive by selling at a price at which we make some minimal profit," she said.
Most of the district teams which came to Adjumani for the National Primary Schools Athletics Championship March 31 to April 5,were forced to leave before the event ended after their budgets failed to sustain themas the food and other commodity prices were much higher than their budgetary estimates.
Mr. Richard Tomor, a shoe dealer in town blames the high cost of living and prices in Adjumani to the Sudan price factor, fuel and transport costs.

Original_Search_Title: A Dream Company
Headline: South Africa: A Dream Company
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — MANY listed companies have been accused of not being transparent enough, but rarely is a company so opaque that one even questions the existence of it on the board . Sorghum brewer Awethu Breweries is one such company.
This week it reported a 34% increase in revenue to R7m from R5m in the six months to December. Net profit leapt a whopping amount to R475250 from R952. But, apart from saying that the company's Carltonville brewery was not yet in production, and stating that it supplies "goods and services to the informal markets and the mining and catering markets", no other information of value was supplied.
The brewer, apparently one of two such brewers in SA, does not have a website. A diligent search turned up three phone numbers, of which one worked, but went through to a message system shared with a company that seems to repair engines.
MD and majority shareholder Tony Ford does not seem to have a residential phone number either.
Ten years ago when the company listed, it aimed to raise R12m to fund growth opportunities in the sorghum beer industry. That dream, and a recent turnaround plan, seem to not yet have born fruit.
The Bottom Line is Edited By Edward West

Original_Search_Title: Arms Trafficking Could Harm Northern  Peace Deal
Headline: Uganda: Arms Trafficking Could Harm Northern  Peace Deal
Body_Text:
Kampala/Gulu — AS northern Uganda prepares for a post-war recovery programme that will see thousands of displaced people resume a normal life, arms trafficking is becoming a serious security concern among the stakeholders.
Security and local leaders fear that the problem of illegal guns and weapons said to be in the hands of former LRA combatants, Karimojong warriors, army deserters, robbers, arms traffickers from Southern Sudan, Somalia and DR Congo could seriously dent efforts to realize total peace in a region ravaged by the Lords Resistance Army (LRA) 22- year rebellion.
"Much as our people are heading home from poorly facilitated Internally displaced People's (IDP) camps there is still a problem of illegal guns held by wrong elements that pose a real threat to the resettlement project," Ms Eromina Ongom, an IDP representative in Pader said in an interview recently.
According to Pader Deputy Resident District Commissioner, Mr Charles Kulwa, Karimojong warriors exchange guns for sorghum, thereby increasing arms proliferation in the rural areas of Northern Uganda.
"The Topotha from Sudan, the Turkana and Pokot from Kenya have all been the key source of illegal arms in the region. Meetings have been held with the leaders of Topotha, the UPDF has deployed and that's why arms trafficking seem to have reduced a little bit ," Mr Kulwa said.
According to local authorities, there is fear that illegal guns will be used in settling land wrangles once a final peace agreement is signed in the South Sudan.
The Kitgum District LC 5 chairperson, John Ogwok Komakech said, "We can't rule out the possibility of illegal arms being used in solving land disputes in Acholi sub-region once the people return to their villages," Mr Ogwok said.
He argued that for the resettlement programme to succeed, the government should ensure it gets rids of all illegal arms in the region.
The UPDF has already suspended the amnesty for people who are holding guns illegally in the Karamoja and wider northern region.
The UPDF northern Uganda army spokesperson, Capt. Ronald Kakurungu identified the commonly trafficked arms as Sub Machine Guns and pistols.
"These arms pose a more immediate threat to the population in the region. Some of these arms have been used in robbing people already," Capt. Kakurungu added.
Since March 5, this year, UPDF, Internal Security, External Security and Chieftaincy of Military Intelligence recovered 20 AK-47 Rifles, three G-2 (PK) guns, 16 full magazines of SMG ammunitions, 2,313 loose SMG ammos, 2,547 loose ammo of G-2, 48 Tortoise hand grenades, four Anti-tank mines and 52 Anti-personnel mines from Yumbe district alone.
On March 10 to March 13, another assortment of arms worth millions of shillings were recovered from West Nile region.
The development followed the arrest of a suspect in Arua on February 28, who has been undergoing interrogation by Security and Intelligence operatives. Government in a statement released at the Media Centre said the captured arms are suspected to be coming from neighbouring DRC and Sudan.
Gulu RDC, Walter Ochora said there are thousands of guns still in the hands of former LRA rebels who surrendered to government and got amnesty.
He said in the last one month, over 40 guns have been recovered from former LRA rebels in Pader district after they launched an appeal for former combatants to surrender the weapons in their possession.
"There are a lot of illegal arms in northern Uganda. Most of LRA rebels who surrendered and got amnesty are still holding guns illegally. This is a big security threat," Ochora said.
"There are over 20,000 LRA combatants who surrendered. But if you go to the army, the records show very few weapons surrendered by the combatants. This means there are many guns still at hands of former rebels," he said.
Col. Ochora cited an example of former LRA director of operations, Alfred Onen Kamdulu who surrendered to the army without a gun, but was last year arrested for armed robbery. "Where did Kamdulu get the rifle?" Col. Ochora asked.
He said that in Gulu district, they have launched a project with United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees of registering former LRA rebels who hand in their guns.
In a recent report, the United Nations Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs noted that the presence of illegal small arms and unexploded ordnances in Gulu and Amuru districts remains a significant challenge for improved security.
"The lack of basic services/infrastructures in areas of return, and anxiety of insecurity amongst the population are amongst key factors posing a challenge for movement back home," the UN report obtained by Daily Monitor reads in part.
Northern Uganda regional police commander, Phineus Arinatwe Katureebe warned those in possession of illegal arms to surrender them or risk prosecution.

Original_Search_Title: UN to Continue Aiding North After Juba Talks
Headline: Uganda: UN to Continue Aiding North After Juba Talks
Body_Text:
Pader — THE United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees, Mr AntÃ'nio Guterres, has said the international community will not abandon war-affected northern Uganda after the signing of a peace deal in Juba, South Sudan.
Mr Guterres and Mr Jean-Louis Schiltz, the Luxembourg minister for Cooperation, yesterday pledged to rally other developed countries, especially from the European Union, to ensure aid continues to flow to northern Uganda.
"I am very honoured to be here to learn from your courage and example. We are here to help the Uganda government to do what the people of northern Uganda want," Mr Guterres said at Kalongo IDP camp in Pader on Monday.
Mr Guterres arrived in the country on Monday on a eight-day mission to Uganda and Tanzania to review the implementation of UNHCR programmes for refugees, returnees and IDPs in the two countries.
The two officials, accompanied by the Minister for Disaster Preparedness and Refugees, Prof. Tarsis Kabwegyere, yesterday flagged off 450 Sudanese refugees who have been living in a camp in Madi Okollo, West Nile.
"The UNHCR will help double the number of people wishing to return to Sudan," Mr Guterres said in response to complaints from the anxious refugees that the repatriation was taking too long.
The UNHCR boss said the international community has high hopes in the ongoing negotiations between the LRA and the Uganda government as it is one of the ways to make the region stable.
During the visit in Pader, Mr Guterres was touched by horrifying stories of rape of women, killings and abductions by the LRA.
"Please help pay school fees for at least two of my children. I have nine children. My husband is very sick," Ms Sidonia Anyek, 45, pleaded with Mr Guterres in Acholi as he visited her tiny house of mud and wattle which the family shares. In the compound were grains of sorghum spread on the ground for drying, which mesmerised the UNHRC chief.
"What is this?" he asked Ms Anyek through Pader LC5 Chairman Peter Odok W'oceng. "What is it used for?" Anyek said the sorghum had helped her to survive in the camp, without which there would be no food or soap.
"I hope in few months, you will be able to go back to your home and have enough land for your millet," Mr Guterres said.
"I pay tribute to your courage. You faced war, your houses were burnt and children abducted. There was violence against women, you need support of the international community," he said at a meeting with the IDPs.
Mr Schiltz said they had not abandoned the IDPs and would continue to be there for them.
He said for the northern Uganda population, 21 years away from their homes was too much and "time has come to turn the pages."
Earlier on a visit to Mulanda Transit camp in Tororo where Kenyan refugees are living, Mr Guterres said the coalition government in Kenya must promote "true reconciliation" if the people are to overcome the ethnic tension.
Luxembourg is by far UNHCR's top donor per capita, contributing $25 per inhabitant in 2006 and 2007.

Original_Search_Title: WFP to Use Railway to Deliver Food for Vulnerable
Headline: Ethiopia: WFP to Use Railway to Deliver Food for Vulnerable
Body_Text:
Addis Ababa — The United Nations World Food Program (WFP) last week signed an agreement with the Ethiopia-Djibouti Railway to transport food stocks to the agency's and partners' warehouse hubs in Dire Dawa.
The UN agency signed the agreement on the 15th February 2008 to allow food transportation from Djibouti to destinations in Ethiopia and it hopes the rail transport service would soon be extended to Nazareth, the location of WFP's biggest warehouse centre in the country.
An initial 1,300 metric tons of sorghum and 30 metric tons of fortified food was dispatched from Djibouti to Dire Dawa between 23 and 26 February by rail," the UN agency said in a statement issued on Friday.
It said seven locomotives and forty-six train wagon were used to transport the food to Dire Dawa, from where it was immediately loaded onto lorries bound for the Somali region of eastern Ethiopia.
"WFP's global logistical operations are often quite complex, and involve deploying planes, helicopters, boats and Lorries to ensure that food reaches those who need it the most," said Abnezer Ngowi, WFP Acting Country Director in Ethiopia said in the statement.
"But equally, our logistics can also be extremely simple. In Ethiopia, thousands of donkeys are utilized in many pastoralist areas, such as in Afar, to get food to beneficiaries. We are always exploring new ways of assisting people quickly and efficiently, and the resumption of our agreement with Ethiopia-Djibouti Railway line, is a very positive development in this regard," he added.
WFP is the world's largest humanitarian agency: this year, WFP plans to feed more than 70 million people in around 80 countries.

Original_Search_Title: Woman Held Over Husband's Murder
Headline: Uganda: Woman Held Over Husband's Murder
Body_Text:
Kabale — THE Police in Kabale are holding a 43 -year old housewife for allegedly murdering her husband.
The Deputy Officer in charge of Criminal Investigations Kabale, Mr Silver Tumwesigye on Wednesday confirmed the arrest of Ms Jeneroza Mubonehe.
Mr Tumwesigye explained that the incident took place on Sunday at Nyakanengo in Hamurwa Sub-county at night. He said the wife hit her husband Felix Baryakabona with an axe on the head after a quarrel.
"The area LC3 James Kabatereine reported this matter to the police. The victim was rushed to Kabale Referral Hospital where he died after over bleeding. The accused person handed her self to the police a day after the incident," Mr Tumwesigye said. The accused person said she hit her husband with a log of wood on the head because he threatened to sell her sorghum with out her consent," police said.
"When I stopped him from selling my sorghum, he kicked my lower stomach and I felt a lot of pain because I have had two times. Then I picked a log of wood and hit him in defence. I did not intend to kill him," Ms Mubonehe said.
Some residents from the village where the incident took place told the press at Kabale police station that it was the second time this woman was attempting to kill her husband. He also revealed that the police are hunting for other suspects.

Original_Search_Title: Food Prices Almost Triple in Sotik District
Headline: Kenya: Food Prices Almost Triple in Sotik District
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Traders have increased food prices in Sotik District following a severe shortage occasioned by violence that hit the area recently.
Business people who talked to the Daily Nation said that they were forced to almost triple the prices because of the many middlemen involved before supplies reach them.
The dealers said that they now have to make orders from as far as Cheseon in Kericho District and some parts of Kisii District thus increasing their expenses.
The price of two a kilogramme tin of millet stands at Sh200 up from Sh50 prior to the last General Election.
A survey by the Daily Nation at Sotik trading centre also found that a bag of potatoes which was initially selling at Sh1,000 now goes for Sh2,000.
The price of sorghum has also risen to Sh150 for the two kilogramme tin up from Sh50 while that of green grams has shot up to shoot to Sh200 from Sh80. Ms Faith Chepchirchir and Ms Joyce Chepkemoi, both traders at Sotik market said that business was poor in the area as most clients can ill afford the new prices.
The traders said they incurred losses due to perishable products especially vegetables getting stale.
Ms Chepchirchir said at times, they are forced to sell them at throw away prices to at least recover some expenses.
Getting less
"I used to get Sh30,000 previously from my business but now I get less than Sh5,000," she said.
Mr Joseph Langat who sells pineapples at the centre, however, reported brisk business due to the price hikes.
A spot check at some of the popular drinking joints also found out that bar owners had taken advantage of the violence and less supplies to raise the prices of beers.

Original_Search_Title: Local Gin Used to Fuel Motorbikes
Headline: Uganda: 'Kanyanga' Can Ride Bicycles
Body_Text:
Kigali — 'Kanyanga' or Waragi, a local potent gin, does not only make the consumers dead drunk after a few sips but it can also serve as a cheap source of fuel, RNA has established.
A Ugandan lawmaker from Lira Municipality in war-torn Northern Uganda Mr. James Akena proved this yesterday when he launched a 50CC engine that uses local waragi mixed with petrol to power an ordinary bicycle, the New Vision reported on Friday.
The crudely brewed gin known in Rwanda as 'Kanyanga' and Waragi, from Swahili, in other regional countries, is commonly consumed in eastern Rwanda but remains banned. In Uganda and Kenya, even in Tanzania, it is loved by the low income earners mainly in rural areas.
The engine is the first of its kind in the Uganda and enables the bicycle to move without the cyclist peddling.
The engine costs sh350,000 ($205 or about Rwf. 110.000), and has a capacity of two litres and it is ideal for both rural and urban users, the lawmaker Akena is quoted to have said.
"A mixture of one litre of waragi and one of petrol may take the bicycle 30km, with luggage of about 80kg," Akena said. Like any other machine, the engine requires servicing. "Servicing can be done after every 45 days." Akena, who said he had imported the engine from China, fixed it on a Roadmaster bicycle.
The MP rode the bicycle from Kololo, a posh suburb of Kampala, to Parliament, leaving many, including fellow MPs, perplexed.
He said: "Fifteen percent of waragi can be mixed with 85% of petrol to ignite the engine. But the waragi must be 75% flammable."
The bicycle has an accelerator like that of a motorcycle and uses the normal bicycle rubber braking system in addition to two chains that pull the behind wheel.
"The engine is undergoing further testing. I want it to use 80% of waragi because it can serve the people in case of fuel shortages," the lawmaker said.
Pump prices of fuel shot up in Rwanda, just like Uganda following the post-election violence in Kenya.
'Kanyanga' is made from fermented maize flour or millet or even sorghum that is put together with tar. The mixture is heated in a sealed container at record high temperatures.
A specially crafted opening, through which a tube is inserted into the container, allows through evaporating gas which changes to liquid drops that flow continuously into another small container. It is this vapour-turned liquid that is 'Kanyanga' or Waragi.
The laborious process involved to fill up a small container means the process goes on year round and consumes vast quantities of wood for heating.

Original_Search_Title: Karamoja Resurrects Education
Headline: Uganda: Karamoja Resurrects Education
Body_Text:
At Lolachat Primary School grounds in Nakapiripirit, December 6 was a day of dance and colourful celebration. As the Karamoja Achievement Day activities went on, the most recongised guests weren't powerful dignitaries, but children.
Adorned in beads, anklets, head-dresses, flashy round skirts or shukas - their ceremonial dress, the children sung songs of liberation from illiteracy as banners flew high, with the elaborate theme of the event; Education is a right, embrace ABEK to support transition to Universal Primary Education (UPE).
The event lauded a programme that has brought these children from the kraals, sorghum fields and stone quarries to theclassrooms. One that is steadily laying the foundation for complete literacy in the region - Alternative Basic Education for Karamoja (ABEK).
"ABEK has assisted Karimojong children and helped them join UPE. I'm grateful to my ABEK facilitator who has encouraged me and taught me math, my favourite subject.
I wish more children could join," says Joseph Louse, a graduate of the project and now a Primary 7 pupil of Naitakwe Primary school. His gratitude is fitting.
When UPE started in 1997, it hoped to provide a literate foundation for the whole population. And the impact was evident. In many regions across the country, the programme pulled little children, their parents as well as grandparents to class.
It became common to see greying men and women trudging with slates in hunger for the formerly expensive knowledge, now made free. But not so in Karamoja.
Left behind
While the rest of the country was in a quest for knowledge, the Karimojong remained stuck in their reverence for their traditions. Here, education was associated with a curse from as far back as the Second World War. During this war, Karamoja's strong warriors were registered, using pen and paper, and taken to fight for a cause they knew nothing about.
Later, with the same tools, animals were counted and taxes levied, and those who resisted such policies were imprisoned and humiliated. Worst of all was that the children of the land who learnt to read and write later resented their nomadic traditions.
This culminated into a ceremony in which Karimojong elders buried a pen and a book - suggesting the death of education in the region. Just two years before the initiation of UPE, an exciting ritual marked the 'rebirth of the pen' - thus the rebirth of education. But the folks' attitudes were yet to change.
This ritual also signified the birth of ABEK. Formally initiated in Kotido District in 1998, the project goes out to teach the people in their natural societal setting.
Having established that the Karimojong's attitudes and pastoral livelihood were the greatest hindrance to formal school enrolment, ABEK brought with it an idea of taking knowledge to the people, according to Save the Children in Uganda's National Advisor on Basic Education, Zakaria Kasirye. This organisation founded the programme, and hopes to spend an estimated Shs263m on it.
Convenient
Under ABEK, tree shades and manyattas (homesteads) become teaching centres, and uniform is not mandatory. One doesn't even have to buy a book; a slate will do just as fine. And, the pupils can go to class and still keep an eye on the family animals while they graze. School times depend upon their schedule.
Whichever time they choose, evenings included, the facilitators are present to impart the sought after knowledge. The project, arranged in three stages likened to primary 1 to 3, teaches the people in their local language.
Presently, the 277 ABEK centres in the sub-region pride in a population of about 40,000 pupils. Besides aiding transition into formal education and helping the girl child escape forced early marriage, ABEK also focuses on saving Karimojong children from domestic labour.
It was against this background that a sister project, Karamoja Integrated Child Protection Project, for the enhancement of child protection and fostering education was launched on the achievement day.
In Karamoja, domestic labour is entirely left to the women, while men trek the flatlands in search of pasture or on cattle rustling missions. In turn, the women relinquish some of the labour to children, sometimes including working in stone quarries to bring home the family's bread during days of food shortage.
This has rendered Karamoja sub-region the least literate in the country, with about 80 percent of its population still unable to read or write, according to a UNDP report. The region too has an average formal school enrolment of 35 percent and a pupil retention rate of 15-20 percent, also the lowest in the country.
Though more investment in education is needed here, ABEK intends to break this record, in accordance with the Millennium Development Goals. Coincidentally, Abek is a word in akarimojong for 'hatch' - the newly born being Abekun. The more reason the project received the elders' blessing. And, its impact so far proves that it is positioned for lighting the candle of literacy all over Karamoja.

Original_Search_Title: Three Corpses Stolen From Soroti Municipality Mortuary
Headline: Uganda: Three Corpses Stolen From Soroti Municipality Mortuary
Body_Text:
Soroti — THREE corpses have gone missing from Soroti Municipality mortuary. The bodies were of accident victims. The accident occurred last week at Apalamio village, Katine Sub-County in Soroti District. Four people died on the spot and ten were rushed to Soroti Referral Hospital with injuries.
"I'm as surprised as any other person. We cannot either establish whether it is the relatives of the dead who sneaked in and took away the bodies," the Soroti District Traffic Officer Sulaiman Ssemisambwa said.
The police had kept the bodies in the mortuary as it waited for relatives of the dead to claim them.
Mr Ssemisambwa attributed the theft of the bodies to poor security at the dilapidated mortuary.
"I don't want to sound like I'm accusing anybody but the mortuary is completely abandoned," Mr Ssemisambwa said.
He said by Wednesday, only one body had been claimed by a group of people from Mayuge District who took it away.
"But this morning [of Thursday] the other bodies were missing," the traffic officer said. Mr Ssemisambwa said the casualties that were admitted at the hospital also escaped.
The truck that was loaded with 82 bags of sorghum and charcoal was reportedly heading to one of the eastern border towns.

Original_Search_Title: World's Oldest Man is 113 Years And Namibian
Headline: Namibia: World's Oldest Man is 113 Years And Namibian
Body_Text:
Tatekulu Wilhem Shinima Niilenge "aka" Shelengane was born on 6 April 1894. He is still alive at 113 years and living in a village called Elondo in Uukwaludhi in the Omusati Region in northern Namibia.
Born before the turn of the 19th century at Okalonda village in Uukwaludhi, Tatekulu Niilenge has lived across three centuries.
He is 17 months older than Japan's Tomoji Tanabe, claimed to be the world's oldest man who celebrated his 112 years on 18 September, 2007.
Tatekulu Niilenge was among the more than 90 legends of the Namibian liberation struggle who were conferred with honorary military ranks and medals during the Heroes' Day commemoration on 26 August 2007 at Eenhana in the Ohangwena Region by the President of the Republic of Namibia, Hifikepunye Pohamba.
Tatekulu Niilenge received the honours as one of the pioneers of the liberation struggle of Namibia.
Namibia was engaged in a protracted war against apartheid colonialism that lasted for more than two decades, under the umbrella of the South West African Peoples' Organisation (SWAPO) - a liberation movement that brought about the country's independence on 21 March 990.
Tatekulu Niilenge joined SWAPO in 1964 and he was initially involved in the distribution of membership cards and dissemination of information about SWAPO to the local communities. He later joined the South West Africa Liberation Army (SWALA) - SWAPO's military wing that preceded the People's Liberation Army of Namibia (PLAN) in 1965 when he was 69 years old.
He received his first military training at Otamazi, specialising in infantry. At 70, Niilenge was part of the group of first fighters who participated in the battle that marked the first step of the armed liberation struggle against the South African apartheid military force at Omungulu Wombashe, in northern Namibia.
According to a booklet: Trials and Tribulations of the Pioneers of the Struggle for the Liberation of Namibia 1960 - 1973, (2007), published by the Ministry of Defence in Windhoek, Tatekulu Niilenge was captured in 1966 and incarcerated in prison in Pretoria, South Africa.
Two years later, he was found guilty under the Terrorism Act in the Pretoria court and sentenced to life imprisonment and banished to Robben Island where South African political icon, Nelson Mandela, was imprisoned for close to three decades.
Tatekulu Niilenge was released from Robben Island in May 1984 at the age of 90 years, together with one of the living legends of the Namibian liberation struggle, Herman Andimba Toivo ya Toivo, who spent 15 years on Robben Island.
Like most of the men who joined the struggle for independence, Tatekulu Niilenge had worked in the central part of the country.
Tatekulu Niilenge returned to his home at Elondo, from Robben Island, where he reunited with his wife, Petrina Eeru, his children and relatives. His wife died a year later. He had eight children, five of whom are still alive. His last-born child is 18 years old and lives with him at his homestead at Elondo.
Tatekulu Niilenge is a grandfather of more than 20 grandchildren.
When he returned to his motherland, Tatekulu Niilenge began to organise meetings with learners in his community to educate them about the political situation in their country.
Tatekulu Niilenge lives about 8 km from Outapi. The old man is in good health and enjoys eating all kinds of Oshiwambo traditional food such as pap, meat, fresh and dried spinach and drinks the non-alcoholic oshikundu (a traditional soft drink made from a mixture of millet and sorghum flour).
In addition, the family tries to get some fresh food for him from shops whenever they can afford it. Although he has lost memory of some events, friends and members of the community, including teachers continue to seek wisdom from him on the political history of his time.
Mpingana Simon Niitembu is Tatekulu Niilenge's great grandniece (his sister's granddaughter). She is 25 years old and is the elected spokesperson of Tatekulu Niilenge. She went to school up to Grade 10 and dropped out due to financial problems.
Mpingana said her great grand uncle enjoys talking about politics. She said he told them stories about his life in the Robben Island prison.
"He told us that they were always stripped naked and showered with cold water at 8 o'clock in the morning and again at 7 o'clock in the evening, before they would be beaten up and tortured with electricity," Mpingana said.
Recalling the time when her great grand uncle returned home from prison, the family received him with mixed feelings because he was brought home by "Koevoets" in a "Casspir".
"He was happy and felt so free to greet us but we had doubts because he was brought home by the enemy," Mpingana noted, adding that "but we realised later that those who brought him home were not happy with him because they harassed him."
"We welcomed him. He began to talk about politics of the liberation struggle. He told us he was a freedom fighter. He would also talk about members of the family who have died and we did not know them," Mpingana recalled.
Tatekulu Niilenge is in touch with a few of his old friends and inmates on Robben Island. One of them is Tatekulu Gaus Shikomba. He also talks about his inmates, Toivo Ya Toivo and Kaxumba Kandola. The last time Shikomba visited his comrade was in July this year. During these visits, the war veterans talk about their ordeal in prison and what the Government of Namibia has been doing for them.
Tatekulu Niilenge benefited from a housing scheme of the Government for war veterans, Robben Island prisoners and brutalised persons. The house was handed over to him in July 2006. He survives on the old age pension of N$370, out of which he pays for water and other core needs.
Describing his delightful moment when his honorary military rank was disclosed to him as Colonel, and was presented with his medal, which was brought to him at home by Mpingana who represented him at the Heroes' Day commemoration, the great granddaughter said the old man was very happy.
"When I came back home from Eenhana, I dressed him up in his military uniform and decorated him with his medals. He kept on touching them and expressing his appreciation," narrated Mpingana. She said that Tatekulu Niilenge displayed his sense of humour by making a joke as to what would the enemies have called him if they infiltrated the country and found him without a gun. Tatekulu Niilenge often goes out of the hut and sits outside in the sun to feel the warmth. His vision is very blurry.
He has a female cousin who is aged 108 years and lives in her own homestead, attended by her granddaughter. His mother, Naambo Nekwaya and father, Niilenge Kenuukwao, died when he was a little boy. He had two sisters and two brothers who also passed away when he was still young. His second wife, to whom he got married in 1987 and is the mother of his 18-year-old daughter, Julia, died in 2004.
Mpingana remarked that the old man tells the story of his family with sadness. Although he is blessed by God to live long, he feels lonely because his sisters, brothers, wives and parents have all died.
Tatekulu Niilenge is being cared for by his grandniece, 65-year-old Albertine Gebhard, the mother of Mpingana. Gebhard's four of her five children live with her at home and one of them is enrolled at the Vocational Training College (VTC) in Windhoek.
He worships at ELCIN church at Etambo village. He was baptised by the name Wilhem, while he was in prison on Robben Island.
- This article was originally published in the September 2007 issue of the Government Information Bulletin.

Original_Search_Title: You Want to Cope With Inflation?
Headline: Ethiopia: You Want to Cope With Inflation?
Body_Text:
Addis Ababa — Recent studies in a couple or so of developed countries have given some hint of how consumer behavior is fast changing in those countries in the face of overall prices relentlessly moving north.
Basically these behavioral changes are coping mechanisms. Nevertheless every coping mechanism is not totally gloomy. The study pointed out that not everything goes down even in difficult times.

Original_Search_Title: Tasks Ahead of New Aviation Minister
Headline: Nigeria: Tasks Ahead of New Aviation Minister
Body_Text:
In less than a decade, the embattled aviation industry in Nigeria is playing host to its 6th minister, with the appointment of the incumbent, Mr. Babatunde Omotoba.
His predecessors include Dr. Olusegun Agagu, now Ondo State governor; Mrs. Kema Chikwe; Prof. Babalola Borishade; Chief Femi Fani-Kayode and Mr. Felix Hassan Hyat.

Original_Search_Title: Museveni Grateful for War Blessing
Headline: Uganda: Museveni Grateful for War Blessing
Body_Text:
Kampala — President Yoweri Museveni attended Christmas prayers at the Nyamitanga Catholic Cathedral in Mbarara Municipality to thank Bishop Emeritus John Baptist Kakubi for blessing him in the fight against Idi Amin's regime.
Accompanied by his wife, Janet, the president thanked the Christians in Nyamitanga for supporting and praying for him.

Original_Search_Title: Former Chairman of Financial Crimes Body Fired
Headline: Nigeria: Ribadu Dismissed
Body_Text:
EMBATTLED former chairman of the Economic and Financial Crimes Commission (EFCC), Mallam Nuhu Ribadu, has been dismissed from the Nigeria Police as recommended by the Police Disciplinary Committee and adopted by the Inspector-General of Police (IGP), Mr Mike Okiro.
Announcing the confirmation of the dismissal of Ribadu, Deputy Force Secretary, Suleiman Abba, a Commissioner of Police, at a press briefing in Abuja yesterday said Ribadu was dismissed for acts of "gross indiscipline, insubordination and absence from duty which are punishable under rule 030401 of Public Service Rule 2006."

Original_Search_Title: Focus On Community Reconciliation in Kono
Headline: Sierra Leone: Focus On Community Reconciliation in Kono
Body_Text:
Freetown — Representatives of all the fourteen chiefdoms in Kono district on Saturday converged at Tombodu, Kamaa chiefdom to host the executive director of Fambul Tok, a non governmental organization geared towards reconciling communities through dialogue. Executive director John Caulker explained to the people the origin of his organization.
"We all know what happened during the war. We are talking about victims and perpetrators but the truth is that we all belong to the same family long before the conflict. We have an adage in Krio which says 'bad bush nor dae for throw away bad pikin'. The TRC and Special Court did not operate at village level. I would have wished for the South Africa type of TRC. Our people did not benefit from the TRC and Special Court and have opted for village dialogue as a means of settling their conflicts", Caulker said.

Original_Search_Title: Legislatures Tour Bushenyi Banana Factory
Headline: Uganda: Legislatures Tour Bushenyi Banana Factory
Body_Text:
Kampala — MPs on the finance committee have asked the management of the Presidential Initiative on Banana Industrial Development to expedite the construction of the banana factory.
The MPs said a lot of funds had been spent on the project, but production had delayed. The chairperson of the committee, Gaudioso Tindamanyire (Bunyaruguru), on Tuesday led the committee on a tour of the project at Nyaruzinga banana processing industry in Bushenyi district.

Original_Search_Title: Eddie Iroh - Excellence is Achievable
Headline: Nigeria: Eddie Iroh - Excellence is Achievable
Body_Text:
Dr Eddie Iroh is not your regular Nigerian. One quickly comes to this conclusion after a few minutes in his company. Dr Iroh is what many would describe as Old School: a stickler for what is right; for excellence and integrity. It seems like Dr Iroh and his ilk are fast disappearing. In a country where the love of money has eaten deep into the soul of many, Dr Iroh and 'old schools' like him are rare commodities; people we should frantically be seeking for. In this interview, he tells us his 'grass to grace' story. For him, poverty is not an excuse and excellence is achievable. Enjoy.
THE LAST TIME WE MET WAS AT THE BOOK LAUNCH AND 70TH BIRTHDAY PARTY OF MADAM VIOLET YOUGH. YOU WERE VISIBLY UPSET AND WALKED OFF THE STAGE WHEN YOU WERE REVIEWING THE BOOK. WHY?

Original_Search_Title: Busoga Chiefs Reject New Kyabazinga
Headline: Uganda: Busoga Chiefs Reject New Kyabazinga
Body_Text:
Kampala — A MEETING of Busoga Kingdom's clan heads has said the title of Kyabazinga is not gained through elections. The chiefs said the selection of the Isebantu (king) should be according to a schedule agreed to in 1933.
George William Mutekanga, their leader, who also hosted the gathering in his compound in Bukalambwa village, in Iganga on Saturday, said the Kyabazinga is determined in rotation.

Original_Search_Title: Bound to Violence
Headline: Nigeria: Bound to Violence
Body_Text:
The sporadic violence that buffeted Jos, the capital of Plateau state some weeks ago which left several hundred of people dead and maimed many others, once again illustrates the ease with which communal co-existence can break down and plunge society into mayhem and chaos.
The gory picture of charred human bodies; burnt out remains of cars and heart-rending stories of how lives were snuffed out with all manner of deadly weapons say it all -that as a nation we are bound to the cycle of senseless violence. A periodic communal descent into madness expressed in the psychopathic blood-letting and butchery which even the bitterest enemies would hesitate to inflict on one another.

Original_Search_Title: Dave Chingarandi Dies
Headline: Zimbabwe: Dave Chingarandi Dies
Body_Text:
Harare — DAVE "DC" Chingarandi - the affable former organising secretary of Premiership soccer side Dynamos - has died.
Chingarandi died in a car accident on Tuesday morning along the Masvingo-Beitbridge Road while on his way from South Africa where he had gone on a shopping trip with his son Dave (Junior).

Original_Search_Title: Deals With the Devil
Headline: Uganda: Deals With the Devil
Body_Text:
Every one who ever pursued the Bachelor of Economics will tell you that the one course unit they would never do again is Financial Management. Apparently, the subject is so hard that some might even swear that they would not do it for a million dollars. Yet, this guy, whom we shall call Tommy, has done it again and again for mere Ugandan shillings that inflate three points every month.
Tommy is what you would call an academic mercenary. You give him a call when there is a test or exam that you want to crack without trying. He is a specialist, too. He is the expert on how to pass Financial Management without lifting a finger. As you might have figured by now, he is a Godsend to many.

Original_Search_Title: Is Anyone Safe Anymore?
Headline: Zimbabwe: Is Anyone Safe Anymore?
Body_Text:
SCORES of soldiers have been causing mayhem in Harare's Central Business District (CBD) where they were seen in the past week beating up members of the public, dispossessing them of their property including cash, while destroying shops and ransacking anything valuable they could lay their hands on.
Just like ordinary citizens, the soldiers have been unable to access cash from the banking system because of the acute shortages of banknotes which the central bank has tried to explain to anyone who cares to listen. And in a fit of thuggery disguised as rage, the soldiers descended on illegal money changers, who have lost quite a fortune in the past week.

Original_Search_Title: Ohanaeze - New Leadership, Fresh Prospects
Headline: Nigeria: Ohanaeze - New Leadership, Fresh Prospects
Body_Text:
Lagos — Last Saturday, Ohanaeze, the Igbo socio-cultural umbrella body elected its new leaders with seasoned diplomat and politician, Ambassador Ralph Uwechue, emerging the President-General. Emeka Osondu, who covered the meeting of the body in Awka, Anambra State, where the leaders were elected reports
Ohanaeze Ndigbo, the apex Igbo socio-cultural body last weekend held its much anticipated election resulting in the emergence of a new leadership. The leadership succession crisis that had afflicted Ohanaeze had been there for the last few years thereby making It impossible for it to effectively play the kind of role Afenifere and Arewa Consultative Forum (ACF), its counterparts in the Southwest and the North respectively are playing. Ironically too, the Igbo ethnic group in recent times appears not to have its feet firmly rooted as a major stakeholder in the Nigerian polity. The genre of inspirational leaders in the mould of the nation's first president, Dr. Nnamdi Azikiwe, the former Prime Minister of the defunct Eastern region, Dr. Michael Okpara, former Governor of the region, Dr. Francis Akanu Ibiam, former Prime Minister of the Mid-West region and Dr. Dennis Osadebay appears to have totally become extinct in Igboland thereby making the people to grope in darkness.

Original_Search_Title: Five Feared Dead As Police Shoot Protesters in Ondo
Headline: Nigeria: Five Feared Dead As Police Shoot Protesters in Ondo
Body_Text:
NO fewer than five people including a pastor were reportedly killed in Idanre, Ondo State, as police in the town allegedly opened fire on a mob protesting the death of a woman whose death was allegedly caused by policemen at a check-point.
However, uneasy calm pervades the town following the incidents which had attracted wide condemnations while a self-imposed curfew has taken over the town.

Original_Search_Title: Change Comes to Villages
Headline: Uganda: Change Comes to Villages
Body_Text:
Kampala — In her village they call her 'councillor'. But Jenipher Namugwere is no ordinary councillor elected by the people to represent them in the local council.
She represents the women in her village, Kadoto, on an advisory council constituted by the Bulangira parish of Palisa district to assess and report their needs to a wider national movement that is seeking to involve women in the planning and budget-making process.

Original_Search_Title: A Visit to  Rutshuru
Headline: Congo-Kinshasa: A Visit to  Rutshuru
Body_Text:
Eastern Congo — At first observation, everything looked like it was staged. The area's 'President' and head of National Congress for the Defense of the People (CNDP) General Laurent Nkunda, was meeting General Olusegun Obasanjo.
The retired Nigerian leader is now serving as special envoy of the UN Secretary General. He is in Rutshuru, a stage of recent fighting between Congolese troops and rebel leader Nkunda.

Original_Search_Title: Govt Signs Trade Pact With Europe
Headline: Cote d'Ivoire: Country Succumbs to EU
Body_Text:
Brussels — Cote d'Ivoire became the first country in Africa to sign an economic partnership agreement (EPA) with the European Union this week, prompting fears that the accord will prevent the country from developing closer ties with its neighbours.
More than 80 percent of the taxes levied on imports from the EU will be eliminated over a 15-year-period as a result of the free trade deal, formally copper-fastened in Abidjan Nov. 26. Under the agreement, Cote d'Ivoire will immediately open its markets to chemicals and vehicles, that it does not produce domestically.

Original_Search_Title: Rakai Man Held Over Killing Wife
Headline: Uganda: Rakai Man Held Over Killing Wife
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE Police in Kalisizo, Rakai district have arrested a man suspected to have slaughtered his wife.
District Police commander Juma Okungo confirmed Mark Mwesigye's arrest, saying the residents had accused him of killing Leonanta Nakayima, 40.

Original_Search_Title: More Questions About Fair Trade Practices
Headline: Africa: More Questions About Fair Trade Practices
Body_Text:
Berlin — So-called fair trade is booming in Germany but it is facing strong criticism from activists against neoliberal globalisation on the grounds that companies taking part in "fair trade" do not live up to its humanitarian and developmental principles.
Fair trade refers to international trade that allegedly respects good development, environment and labour standards in the producing countries of the South.

Original_Search_Title: A Long Way to Go
Headline: Nigeria: A Long Way to Go
Body_Text:
Lagos — The depraved conduct of my motor mechanic last week reinforces the argument that we have a long way to go before reaching our final destination where hopefully there will be no more weeping and gnashing of teeth.
Sorry for bringing up this personal example today, but I think it eloquently testifies to the way we are in Nigeria. I complained to my mechanic that my car was squeaking especially when it bumps into a pothole. In response, he gave me a list of automobile spare parts which needed to be purchased and fixed underneath the car to get it back to shape. The automobile parts included: 2 thyroids, I chaff rubber, 4 front and back bushings etc. Trusting his judgment and honesty, I gave him the money to purchase the items. Off he disappeared with the money and my car. In the evening he reported with an unusual charming smile and said to me in Pidgin English: "Oga mi, I don finish. The car don dey move well now". You go use this car tire without any complain again". "Are you sure everything is okay", I asked him. "The law, no bi today I begin work for you. Make you dey trust me now. I say everything don dey okay", he answered. I thanked him and collected the ignition keys from him. On my way to work the next day, I noticed that the condition of the car was worse. I rang up the mechanic. He swore to high heavens that he replaced the faulty automobile parts in my car with new ones. To verify his claims, I sought the services of two independent motor mechanics. The two confirmed that my mechanic had hoodwinked me: that my mechanic did not replace the faulty parts as he claimed; that he merely put oil in the front wheel of my car. Outraged, I telephoned my mechanic again.

Original_Search_Title: Youths Relocate to Find Jobs
Headline: Sierra Leone: Unemployment Forces Youth to Abandon Their Communities
Body_Text:
Freetown — Resident youths of both Banana Island and Kent village in the Freetown Peninsular have come together and resolved to abandon their communities for what they considered as lack of employment opportunities in their areas.
Youth chairman of Banana Island, Emeric Kallie Kamara said they are in a very sorry state as idleness seems to be their only occupation due to the fact that no companies or factories operate in the area to engage the youth. He said this has been troubling them over the years and have now seen reason to relocate elsewhere where their potentials skills can be utilized.

Original_Search_Title: BPE and Self-Seeking Individuals
Headline: Nigeria: BPE and Self-Seeking Individuals
Body_Text:
The beauty of separation of powers by the three arms of government is that each organ is allowed to exercise its statutory responsibilities without any let or hindrance.
This keeps each of the organs in check. It is on this score that one may commend the House of Representatives Chairman on Privatisation, Ahmed Gella Njidda for constantly taking on the Bureau of Public Enterprises (BPE) to keep its management on its toes. But when this obviously becomes a pastime, it calls to question, the real motive. Perhaps it is pertinent for me to begin with an interpretation of what I think the Bureau of Public Enterprises (BPE) is not.

Original_Search_Title: Ribadu - Rights Group Berates Security Operatives
Headline: Nigeria: Ribadu - Rights Group Berates Security Operatives
Body_Text:
Human Rights Writers Association of Nigeria, (HURIWA) has criticised the reported maltreatment of the former Chairman of the Economic and Financial Crimes Commission (EFCC) Nuhu Ribadu by security operatives and his alleged physical abduction from the premises of the Nigerian Institute for Policy and Strategic Studies (NIPS) Kuru, Jos Plateau State.
The Rights Group said the action has painted Nigeria wrongly in the international community as a banana Republic which is led by fascists and despots.

Original_Search_Title: Praise Nyandimo Wins A-Academy Grand Finale
Headline: Zimbabwe: Praise Nyandimo Wins A-Academy Grand Finale
Body_Text:
Bulawayo — BULAWAYO'S Praise Nyandimo did the City of Kings proud over the weekend by winning the A-Academy grand finale.
Nyandimo became the first ever woman to win the A-Academy singing talent show after Jonah Sithole and Joseph.

Original_Search_Title: AGF Applauds London Ruling on Ibori
Headline: Nigeria: AGF Applauds London Ruling on Ibori
Body_Text:
Abuja — The Attorney General of the Federation and Minister of Justice, Michael Aondoaaka yesterday described Monday's London court ruling which rejected the evidence brought before it by the Economic and Financial Crimes Comm-ission (EFCC) against former governor of Delta State, James Ibori, as a vindication of the rule of law as espoused by President Umaru Yar'Adua.
The London Court had last Monday dismissed the evidence brought by the EFCC contending that it did not come to it through the Nigerian Attorney General and Minister of Justice, as provided for in the bilateral legal assistance treaty between Nigeria and Britain.

Original_Search_Title: 'We're Waiting for Yuguda's Return to PDP'
Headline: Nigeria: 'We're Waiting for Yuguda's Return to PDP'
Body_Text:
Bauchi — A chieftain of the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP), in Bauchi State, Alhaji Bibi Dogo, spoke on the problems bedevilling the state's chapter of the party, saying those who betrayed the party in the last election and want to return will not be welcome. Excerpts:
Weekly Trust: The PDP in Bauchi State appears to be losing popularity since its defeat in the April 2007 gubernatorial election. What is actually responsible for this?

Original_Search_Title: Kent Police Post in Deplorable State
Headline: Sierra Leone: Kent Police Post in Deplorable State
Body_Text:
Police officer of Kent village has disclosed that the present condition of the police post at Kent village was deplorable.
Sergeant Emmanuel Sankoh told Concord Times Sunday at Kent that the police post lacks light facility and at the same time, the police post deserves water and toilet facility.

Original_Search_Title: Banana Island Clamours For Help
Headline: Sierra Leone: Banana Island Clamours For Help
Body_Text:
Freetown — An elder of Banana Island Sunday told Concord Times that the present status of the community was very bad and would need a central government intervention.
Pa Molai Turay said the whole area doesn't have proper schools, community center, electricity or security. He said that any time there is any problem on the Island, they have to travel to Kent and request for police help. "We really want the government to focus on the wellbeing of the people on the Island." Turay said.

Original_Search_Title: Anenih, Edo PDP Extend Support to Oshiomhole
Headline: Nigeria: Anenih, Edo PDP Extend Support to Oshiomhole
Body_Text:
Benin City — The Former Chairman of the Board of Trustees of the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP), Chief Tony Anenih, and the Party-led Edo State House of Assembly has shown visible signs of getting over the outcome of the Appeal Court judgement that handed the reign of the state to the Action Congress (AC) with the promise to work with the Governor Adams Oshiomhole towards ensuring that development and democratic dividend are made available to the people of the state.
Indication of the PDP leadership's change of position emerged on Monday during a call on the newly sworn-in governor by the members of the State House of Assembly led by the Speaker, Zakawanu Garuba (PDP).

Original_Search_Title: Kampala a Mountain of Garbage
Headline: Uganda: Kampala a Mountain of Garbage
Body_Text:
Kampala — In November last year, Kampala glittered, following a facelift ahead of the Commonwealth Heads of Government Meeting (CHOGM). A year after, The New Vision' Frederick Womakuyu examines the state of the infrastructure and the city cleanliness.
The preparations for CHOGM put a magnificent sparkle on the streets of Kampala. Today, if one of the visitors returned, they would get lost.

Original_Search_Title: Aid Workers Fear New Wave of Sexual Violence
Headline: Congo-Kinshasa: Rape Crisis Set to Worsen Amid Kivu Chaos
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Soaring insecurity in eastern Democratic Republic of Congo has raised fears of a new wave of sexual violence in a region termed "the worst place in the world to be a woman" by aid workers.
During the first six months of 2008, there were more than 5,000 reported rape cases in the flashpoint province of North Kivu, according to data collected by doctors at health centres. The true figure is likely to be far higher, as women are too traumatised or afraid of stigma to seek help.

Original_Search_Title: Govt Should Push Agri-Business
Headline: Uganda: Govt Should Push Agri-Business
Body_Text:
Kampala — During a recent talk in Fort Portal businessman Patrick Bitature said that western Uganda milk producers earn sh500b annually. He also revealed that the turnover of matooke from the region is easily a billion shillings a day.
Uganda is the largest producer of bananas in the world but does not even export 1% of its bounty. Uganda lags behind Kenya and Tanzania in milk production.

Original_Search_Title: Big UK Demand For Bananas
Headline: Uganda: Kibuzi Bananas Follow Citizens to London
Body_Text:
Kampala — "We have lots of orders for apple banana. There is a ready market for kibuzi in London. It is eaten from Monday to Monday. But how to get it there is the issue. Almost 60 percent of the cost goes to freight."
This is one of James Kanyije's main complaints. He is the director of Icemark Africa, a leading Ugandan company that exports vegetables and fruit, including bananas. Bananas for cooking, commonly known as matoke, are gaining popularity in the UK and U.S., which have large populations of Ugandans in the Diaspora.

Original_Search_Title: For the Comrade Governor, Now a Different Battle
Headline: Nigeria: For the Comrade Governor, Now a Different Battle
Body_Text:
Lagos — Last week's Court of Appeal judgment declaring Comrade Adams Oshiomhole winner of the 2007 Edo State gubernatorial elections was historic.
Historic in the sense that it would be the first time that an incumbent governor installed based on the disputed polls would be removed from office and his challenger declared winner on the basis of an election petition and no re-run ordered.

Original_Search_Title: Injury Worries for Boks Ahead of Test
Headline: South Africa: Injury Worries for Boks Ahead of Test
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — INJURIES at the worst possible time for Springbok coach Peter de Villiers could derail his plans for his team's toughest and final Test of their year-end tour against England on Saturday.
After an unconvincing 14-10 victory against Scotland at Murrayfield this past weekend and a narrow 20-15 win against Wales two weekends ago, the Springboks will need their strongest team to beat Rugby World Cup finalists England at Twickenham.

Original_Search_Title: Refugees Increase Cholera Threat
Headline: Uganda: Refugees Increase Cholera Threat
Body_Text:
Kampala — FIGHTING between the Congo government army and the rebels flared up recently sending refugees into Uganda. Mathias Mugisha is at the Uganda Congo border following the story.
An unknown number of Congolese refugees have settled in Bunagana, which borders Uganda, straining the chronically poor sanitation facilities in the town and making the threat of a cholera outbreak real.

Original_Search_Title: Agencies Cannot Reach Trapped Civilians
