What is even more confusing is that I heard an opposition leader say that there exists a climate conducive to the holding of free and fair elections in Zimbabwe, but that a particular outcome will be ensured by Zanu (PF) through the manipulation of the voters' roll and the creation of pro-government ghost voters.

Original_Search_Title: How Helpful is Food Aid Really?
Headline: Rwanda: How Helpful is Food Aid Really?
Body_Text:
Kigali — A few weeks ago I happened to meet an interesting new acquaintance; this person was a member of UNICEF who'd arrived in Butare on his way to see what he could do to help the earthquake-ravaged populace in Cyangugu town and other parts of the Western Province.
We had a chance to sit down to chat and what a conversation it was. The UNICEF man had flown in from Zimbabwe, and once I learnt that, the conversation quickly turned to the realities on the ground, more especially of a political nature, in that country.

Original_Search_Title: Poor Millionaires
Headline: Zimbabwe: Poor Millionaires
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Zimbabwe is fast becoming a joke gone too far. What would you say of a country where people carry money in sacks just to buy groceries? What we would take for granted like a piece of ripe banana that costs Sh10 on the streets of Nairobi and even less with bargaining is currently going for Zim$1 million (Sh2,000). A loaf of bread, when available, costs about Zim$5 million (Sh10,000).
It is a land where essential commodities are so scarce that cooking oil is traded by the tablespoon, in the markets and in some residential estates.

Original_Search_Title: Over 2000 to Get Tap Water Soon
Headline: Cameroon: Over 2000 to Get Tap Water Soon
Body_Text:
Some 2000 Likomba inhabitants in Tiko Subdivision will soon drink water from public taps, following the laying of the foundation stone of an FCFA 11 million water project on March 20.
The Secretary General at the Southwest Governor's office, Bernard Ndode Mesape, laid the foundation stone of the project as part of activities to mark World Water Day in the Province. The project is financed with funds from the Heavily Indebted Poor Countries Initiative, HIPC I, The Post learned.

Original_Search_Title: Country to Lose FCFA 1,036 Billion to Economic Agreement
Headline: Cameroon: Country to Lose FCFA 1,036 Billion to Economic Agreement
Body_Text:
Experts have qualified the economic partnership agreement Cameroon signed with the European Union on December 17, 2007 as a veritable jinx that would inflict a heavy decline on customs revenue.
The intermediary agreement that spans over a period of 15 years stipulates a tariff-free trade between Cameroon and the European Union, EU.According to the Director of Customs, Mrs. Libom Li Likeng, Cameroon will lose FCFA 1,036 billion of customs revenue in 15 years.

Original_Search_Title: Man Full of Praise for Vilified Plant
Headline: Kenya: Man Full of Praise for Vilified Plant
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Despite his age, he is swift and efficient. He packs small rolls of dried leaves, wrapped with banana fibres and neatly tied together, into several sacks. Hordes of clients stream into his small kiosk for the product.
With droplets of sweat on his forehead, Simon Ngahu attends to several customers. They seem to know the routine and there is not much talking.

Original_Search_Title: Food Prices in And Around Kampala Go Up
Headline: Uganda: Food Prices in And Around Kampala Go Up
Body_Text:
In a shockingly short space of about three months, food prices in Kampala and towns across Uganda have shot up and urbanites, almost without noticing, have had their monthly food bills nearly double.
Prices of just about every food category - grains, bread, cereals, flour, vegetables, bananas, beef, fish and others - have inexplicably climbed up so sharply that consumer experts and economists seem confounded.

Original_Search_Title: Fourth Term Campaigns Start in Kibaale
Headline: Uganda: Fourth Term Campaigns Start in Kibaale
Body_Text:
Kibaale — CAMPAIGNS to retain President Museveni in power after his third term have started in Kibaale district.
State Minister for Internal Affairs and Buyanja county MP Matia Kasaija held a consultative meeting with Buyanja county residents on Sunday and urged the electorate to retain the President for another term.

Original_Search_Title: Internal Affairs - Solving a Huge Education Problem
Headline: Rwanda: Internal Affairs - Solving a Huge Education Problem
Body_Text:
Kigali — We have a huge education problem: students are not getting an education because teachers are not getting enough money to stoke their passion for education!
Education in primary and secondary schools is getting a failing grade both on the wanted content, education and on the unwanted content, eradication of genocide ideology.

Original_Search_Title: The Ube Scandal
Headline: Nigeria: The Ube Scandal
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Universal Basic Education (U.B.E) Scheme was designed to promote mass literacy, but it has turned out to be a universal basket of corruption amongst most states of the Federation.
The Independent Corrupt Practices and Other Related Offences Commission's (I.C.P.C) recent probe of the funds allocated to the scheme in 36 states and Federal capital between 2005 and 2006 has uncovered massive fraud totalling N3.32 billion. The money was diverted by state government officials. Of the stolen funds, about N2.3 billion has been recovered from 20 states while six states are still to refund N1.016 billion.

Original_Search_Title: Bandele's Burma Boy Tours Country
Headline: Nigeria: Bandele's Burma Boy Tours Country
Body_Text:
Lagos — AWARD winning playwright and novelist, Biyi Bandele, will be visiting Nigeria to promote his latest novel Burma Boy.
The book tour, organized by Farafina Books and partly sponsored by the British Council, will begin in Lagos on April 5, with a book reading at the British Council, Thompson Avenue, Ikoyi.

Original_Search_Title: Dance Show At NTN
Headline: Namibia: Dance Show At NTN
Body_Text:
Windhoek — The African Performing Arts Department at the College of the Arts is proud to present the dance production "Etyapulo" (Celebration) today and tomorrow at the National Theatre, it was announced in a press release.
"Etyapulo" is the celebration of life through music, dance and drama and will be choreographed and directed by Trixie Munyama, Angelika Schroeder, Banana Shekupe, Emmanuel Karumazondo, Sandy Rudd and Lucky Pieters who are all tutors at the College. Implemented in 2006, the African Performing Programme was established to offer a three-year full-time diploma course in Namibian/African performing arts. This musical will be presented by the students who will showcase their talent and skills through outstanding performances.

Original_Search_Title: Liberty Banking On UK Malls
Headline: South Africa: Liberty Banking On UK Malls
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — UK-BASED property company Liberty International, which has a significant South African shareholding, said yesterday that it was courting international retailers who might take space in the company's prime shopping centres.
During previous downturns in the retail market, Liberty International CE David Fischel said, large prime shopping malls in the UK had continued to trade well and were "defensive" in nature during difficult times.

Original_Search_Title: Govt Welcomes ICTR's Seromba Life Sentence
Headline: Rwanda: Govt Welcomes ICTR's Seromba Life Sentence
Body_Text:
Kigali — The Government of Rwanda yesterday expressed satisfaction on the verdict handed down by the UN-backed International Criminal Tribunal for Rwanda (ICTR) against Catholic priest Athanase Seromba.
Seromba was on Wednesday sentenced to life imprisonment by the Appeals chamber of the Tanzania-based tribunal following his appeal against an earlier 15-year jail term handed down by a lower trial chamber.

Original_Search_Title: Need for Home Grown Solutions
Headline: Kenya: Need for Home Grown Solutions
Body_Text:
Those of us who are accustomed to the British Council Leadership Forums had a rare treat in the February edition. Of particular concern to this writer is how Kenya as a country has lagged behind others like Singapore whose status compared well with each other in the 60's.
What can we in fact learn from Singapore and others today? To start with, no country can develop through implementing prescriptions from others who have taken many decades or even centuries to achieve their own status. This effectively means that a country has to make its own experience based on its unique circumstances.

Original_Search_Title: Elections Must Restore Citizens' Dignity
Headline: Zimbabwe: Elections Must Restore Citizens' Dignity
Body_Text:
Harare — Elections are supposed to be an expression of the will of the people and those being held in Zimbabwe at the end of this month must be no exception.
I bet my last worthless million dollars that right now ,the most cherished wish of the majority of struggling Zimbabweans who are not well positioned and well connected enough to be swimming in riches is to lead normal lives again. And in this instance normalcy is referred to in its most basic sense. It means the hassle-free ability to do the most basic, routine and mundane things with dignity and freedom. This is impossible in the prevailing atmosphere and the presidential candidate most capable of turning things around will surely strike a chord with the electorate.

Original_Search_Title: 'It's Ok to Belong to More Trade Blocs'
Headline: Uganda: 'It's Ok to Belong to More Trade Blocs'
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE East African Community (EAC) in November 2007 signed interim agreements with the European Union (EU) after the December 31 expiry of the Cotonou arrangement that gave preferential treatment to Africa, Caribbean and Pacific countries accessing the EU market. Peter Kaujju, spoke to Chungu Mwila, the director for investment promotion and private sector development at the Common Market for Eastern and Southern Africa (COMESA).
COMESA being Africa's largest economic bloc, how much have you achieved in trade promotion?

Original_Search_Title: Bukomansimbi By-Elections - What It Takes to Become MP
Headline: Uganda: Bukomansimbi By-Elections - What It Takes to Become MP
Body_Text:
Kampala — If there is a constituency that has a history of conflict in Masaka district, it is Bukomansimbi.
Although many people have shown interest in the post of MP, the seat has been dominated by Kisiki Lubyayi and Kagimu Kiwanuka.

Original_Search_Title: Ministry Teams Up With Private Sector to Inject New Life Into White Elephants
Headline: Kenya: Ministry Teams Up With Private Sector to Inject New Life Into White Elephants
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A number of government projects under the Regional Development Authorities are up for grabs by the private sector.
The Government has signed an agreement to work with the Kenya Private Sector Alliance with the aim of reviving some of its stalled projects across the country.

Original_Search_Title: There is More to Good Education Than Numeracy And Literacy
Headline: Uganda: There is More to Good Education Than Numeracy And Literacy
Body_Text:
Since mid January, the local press has been awash with articles on the 2007 primary leaving examination (PLE) results. Up to now, hardly a day goes by without a letter or article in the press on these results. It has been a celebration galore across the country.
Some of the proud PLE graduates have even been in the papers sharing their career ambitions with the nation. Uganda is one of the few African countries that I have known to celebrate PLE results that much. In other countries, the results are lucky to be mentioned in the small prints of the inside pages of the news dailies.

Original_Search_Title: Cyclists Attack Family
Headline: Uganda: Cyclists Attack Family
Body_Text:
Kampala — A family of five on Sunday survived being burnt to death when bodaboda cyclists set their house ablaze. The incident occurred at 11:00pm at Semu Katebarirwe's home at Mihambo village in Itendero, Bushenyi district.
Several property, including mattresses and books, was burnt to ashes.

Original_Search_Title: Fruits Shortage Hits Kisumu Town
Headline: Kenya: Fruits Shortage Hits Kisumu Town
Body_Text:
Kisumu town has been hit by a shortage of fruits, due to low supply from key producing zones and high transport costs.
The scarcity has been attributed to lack of rains late last year.

Original_Search_Title: Police Moves to Probe Kajura Land Row
Headline: Uganda: Police Moves to Probe Kajura Land Row
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE Police chief, Maj. Gen. Kale Kayihura, has sent a team to Hoima district to investigate a land dispute between the 2nd deputy premier and residents, reports Amlan Tumusiime.
The team from the land fraud unit at CID headquarters in Kampala that is led by Patrick Wacha, arrived in Kabwoya sub-county on Saturday. They will study the row between Muganwa Kajura and the residents.

Original_Search_Title: There And About - Trying to Ban Zimbabwe Not Fair
Headline: Zimbabwe: There And About - Trying to Ban Zimbabwe Not Fair
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Some political and sports officials in Britain incline toward myopia when dealing with Zimbabwe's President Robert Mugabe. They don't see the president has fossilised into dying while still in office.
Last weeks's talk in London of a blanket ban of Zimbabwean sports people from competing in the United Kingdom, as the BBC reported, illustrates the myopia. Correctly, the BBC enumerated potential complications.

Original_Search_Title: What Are the Mungiki Gangs Up To?
Headline: Kenya: What Are the Mungiki Gangs Up To?
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A protest march by the Mungiki along the Nairobi streets last week left people wondering what the outlawed sect is really up to.
What is clear, however, is that the youths, in an apparent show of might, were sending a message or sounding a warning in the demonstration, which caught the Government security organs unawares.

Original_Search_Title: Experts Say Govt Wrong on Trade
Headline: Cameroon: ACP-EU Free Trade Accord - Experts Say Gov't is On Wrong Track
Body_Text:
Economic experts have said Government has derailed in the ongoing negotiation on the Economic Partnership Agreement, EPA, with the European Union, EU Commission.
The negotiation is supposed to be within the context of the Cotonou Accord, which was signed in 2000 between 77 African, Caribbean and Pacific, ACP countries, and the EU.

Original_Search_Title: Agriculture Talks Stuck On Import Surge Safeguard
Headline: Africa: Agriculture Talks Stuck On Import Surge Safeguard
Body_Text:
Geneva — The Group of 33 developing countries has denounced the draft text on the special safeguard mechanism in the current Doha Development Round of World Trade Organisation (WTO) talks as "extremely inadequate stringent, restrictive, burdensome (and) ineffective".
The special safeguard mechanism (SSM) has been proposed in response to the frequency of food import surges into developing countries' markets over the past two decades.

Original_Search_Title: Local Fruits On High Demand in Ireland, Denmark
Headline: Uganda: Local Fruits On High Demand in Ireland, Denmark
Body_Text:
Kampala — FRESH and dry organic fruits from Uganda are on high demand in Ireland and Denmark but the supply cannot meet the demand, writes Peter Kaujju.
"We are committed to the quality of our exports but there are supply limitations since the fruits are seasonal," Aliya Shivji, the director of the Ntinda-based African Organic company, said.

Original_Search_Title: Tusker Auditions - Singing At Gunpoint
Headline: Uganda: Tusker Auditions - Singing At Gunpoint
Body_Text:
With bad luck, even a ripe banana can break your tooth. It actually happened during the Tusker Project Fame (TPF) 2 auditions that took place from February 29 to March 2 at the Golf Course Hotel.
More than 500 contestants made it for the first two days of audition. The third day was intended for about 100 contestants sieved out of the 500 for the second round of auditions from which the final thirty were selected.

Original_Search_Title: Commonwealth? What Commonwealth!
Headline: Gambia: Commonwealth? What Commonwealth!
Body_Text:
One African brother, dressed in a crisp English blue suit and blue tie, walked into the Daily Observer offices yesterday and tried to hassle free coverage for the Commonwealth Secretary General's annual statement. Look, we said, "Can't the mighty Commonwealth, based in the most expensive square mile of London, St James, afford to pay a poor African newspaper for advertorial space?".
My blue-suited brother, well educated as he is from the land of Makerere, was so comfortable in his English that I was afraid to engage him in my favourite Swahili. He tried to explain how wonderful the Commonwealth is ("a family of nations" - he said).

Original_Search_Title: Zambezi Water Level Subsides
Headline: Namibia: Zambezi Water Level Subsides
Body_Text:
Katima Mulilo — Amid concerns that low-lying areas in Caprivi would be swept away by what many fear could be the worst flooding in recent memory, the water level of the Zambezi River has gradually subsided causing partial relief.
Caprivi Regional Governor Leonard Mwilima who chairs the Regional Emergency Unit (REMU) in the far north-eastern region, yesterday said the Department of Water Affairs whose officials compile daily water level readings have indicated a downward shift. A daily chart of the water level in the Zambezi indicates that since the level started to rise last month it reached 6.12 metres on February 25 2008, but it then started to dip.

Original_Search_Title: Nation Demonstration Tit-Bits
Headline: Cameroon: Nation Demonstration Tit-Bits
Body_Text:
Bamenda:Northwest Governor, Achidi Achu, J. B Ndeh Tear-gassed
Northwest Governor, Abakar Ahamat, who had braved it to the Bamenda Commercial Avenue grandstand to conclude his maiden tour of the seven Divisions on Tuesday morning, was greeted by angry rioters.

Original_Search_Title: Hundreds of Pupils to Be Evacuated
Headline: Namibia: Hundreds of Pupils to Be Evacuated
Body_Text:
Katima Mulilo — Hundreds of secondary and primary school learners and dozens of teachers will soon be evacuated from schools severely affected by floods in eastern Caprivi.
Caprivi Regional Governor, Leonard Mwilima, made the announcement during an interview with New Era last Friday.

Original_Search_Title: Here Comes the Motions of Support
Headline: Cameroon: Here Comes the Motions of Support
Body_Text:
In the next couple of days, the media will be flooded with a special kind of information, call them motions of support. The concern, this time, is the unabated violence and social disorder that took place last week nation-wide.
As expected, the signatories of the declarations will castigate the incident and pledge their unflinching support to the Head of State. Normal. It is always comforting to know that one is not alone in a venture; that there are shoulders on which one can lean on, especially in times of trouble.

Original_Search_Title: FDLR Disarmament This Month
Headline: Rwanda: FDLR Disarmament This Month
Body_Text:
Kigali — The exercise to disarm members of the genocidal forces in the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC) is set to begin this month.
According to an agreement reached between Rwanda and DRC in Nairobi, Kenya last year, the disarmament process was due to commence this month to pave way for their eventual repatriation to Rwanda.

Original_Search_Title: Pilgrimage to Kigungu
Headline: Uganda: Pilgrimage to Kigungu
Body_Text:
Kampala — The pilgrimage to kigungu is supposed to be a hallowed commemoration of the arrival of the first catholic missionaries in uganda. but what Andrew Ndawula found was far from hallowed
IT is the cradle of the Catholic faith in Uganda. Kigungu landing site in Entebbe is where the first Catholic missionaries landed 129 years ago. Every year on February 17, Catholic faithfuls from all over the country and beyond, make a pilgrimage to Kigungu to commemorate the arrival of the two French missionaries, Father Siméon Lourdel (Mapeera) and Brother Amans Delmas (Amansi).

Original_Search_Title: A Humanitarian Disaster Unfolds
Headline: Congo-Kinshasa: A Humanitarian Disaster Unfolds in Eastern DRC
Body_Text:
Kibumba, Democratic Republic of Congo — In a mist-shrouded valley between the Mount Nyiragongo volcano and a pair of its dormant cousins looming in Rwanda to the east, nearly 3,000 souls wait in limbo, having fled a conflict that has succeeded in making this lush corner in eastern Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC) nothing less than hell on earth for its people.
"We arrived here fleeing the war," says Gilbert Naimrwango, president of the Kibumba camp's displaced persons association and a primary school teacher in his former life, speaking amidst a sprawling collection of grass and banana-leaf huts covered with thin sheets of tarpaulin. As he speaks, he is surrounded by dozens of other residents and shoeless children, some with distended bellies and reddish hair that suggest severe malnutrition. "Life here is very complicated, and we have much difficulty finding food."

Original_Search_Title: Frieze Films Revitalises Logo
Headline: South Africa: Frieze Films Revitalises Logo
Body_Text:
Cape Town — Frieze Films, the boutique production house with an enviable reputation for interpreting agency storyboards with flair and empathy, has kicked off 2008 with a revitalised logo which is more fluid and contemporary than that decided on when the company opened doors some 10 years ago.
In addition, the new logo graphically suggests how Frieze Films' core competencies synergise to provide its clients with a seamless, streamlined service. Executive producer, Jo Barber, explains: "At Frieze Films, our strengths are myriad but overall they are grounded in the fact that we define ourselves with conceptual and stylish filmmaking.

Original_Search_Title: St. Patrick Church Launches 180th Anniversary Celebrations
Headline: Sierra Leone: St. Patrick Church Launches 180th Anniversary Celebrations
Body_Text:
Freetown — The St. Patrick Church in Kissy last Sunday launched its 180th anniversary celebrations at the church premises in Freetown.
Giving the background of the celebration, chairman of the celebration committee, E. A. Wilson-Taylor said their greatest grand parents had left them a holy heritage to go by.

Original_Search_Title: Tusker Project Fame Auditions
Headline: Uganda: Tusker Project Fame Auditions
Body_Text:
Kampala — For the second successive year, the journey for the mega Tusker Project fame prize of a music recording deal in South Africa gets underway.
This time though, the winner will also walk away with Shs 5m. February 29 and March 1 are the audition days when five Ugandans will make it to the finalists' house.

Original_Search_Title: My Husband Was Hacked to Death As I Watched
Headline: Uganda: My Husband Was Hacked to Death As I Watched
Body_Text:
Kampala — JANET Tumuhebwe's life will never be the same again. It will take the 30-year-old time to get used to dressing, feeding and bathing herself.
Tumuhebwe was born normal, but on August 11, 2007, both of her hands were cut off.

Original_Search_Title: Amnesty International's Reaction to Juba Talks Smacks of Arrogance
Headline: Uganda: Amnesty International's Reaction to Juba Talks Smacks of Arrogance
Body_Text:
Kampala — LETTER FROM GULU
THE news from Juba has been exhilarating. Either the parties to the talks have found a slope or they have a powerful engine propelling their forward motion. Headlines screamed that the Lord's Resistance Army (LRA) had walked out of the peace talks. Before the morning papers had made their way to Gulu, the facts had changed.

Original_Search_Title: Fuel Supply Tender 'Reasonable And Robust' - NAMCOR
Headline: Namibia: Fuel Supply Tender 'Reasonable And Robust' - NAMCOR
Body_Text:
Windhoek — The National Petroleum Corporation of Namibia (NAMCOR) refuted claims in various media that the fuel supply tender that was awarded to Afroneft, was fraught with inconsistencies.
Speaking to New Era last Friday, Managing Director of NAMCOR, Sam Beukes, said Afroneft received the 12-month fuel supply tender after three companies were short-listed from the seven that had applied. Two independent auditors' firms confirmed that the tendering process was above board, with Deloitte & Touche describing the tendering process as 'reasonable and robust', said NAMCOR Corporate Legal Advisor and Company Secretary, Toni Beukes.

Original_Search_Title: Constitutional and Legal Reforms are the Solution
Headline: Kenya: Constitutional and Legal Reforms are the Solution
Body_Text:
Nairobi — THE LAST FEW WEEKS HAVE witnessed events which may define the future of Kenya politics. The chief mediator of the post-election stalemate, Mr Kofi Annan, stated that his preference in resolving the political issue in the country lies in forming a grand coalition.
The American Government on its part averred that a return to democracy and power-sharing among the protagonist parties was the way forward. Western diplomats based in Kenya also embraced the power-sharing concept.

Original_Search_Title: I Have No Ambition to Replace Mark - Nuhu Aliyu
Headline: Nigeria: I Have No Ambition to Replace Mark - Nuhu Aliyu
Body_Text:
Abuja — Senator Nuhu Aliyu (PDP Niger) recently stirred the hornet's nest when he told Nigerians that the National Assembly is populated with dubious characters.
When his colleagues dared him to mention names, he kept sealed lips. To assuage the anger of his colleagues who were angling for his probe over the allegation, he quickly apologised and preferred to let sleeping dogs lie. In this interview, Aliyu speaks on why he apologised to his colleagues over the 419ners allegations, among other issues. Excerpts

Original_Search_Title: Ndeeba, the Home of the Bodaboda
Headline: Uganda: Ndeeba, the Home of the Bodaboda
Body_Text:
They can be found in every almost every corner of the country, but almost all boda bodas that whiz past us on the roads have once been 'residents' of Ndeeba
Every town has a distinctive feature that makes it stand out from the rest and Ndeeba certainly meets that standard. From 1993, when B.M.K (U) Ltd initialised its motorcycle sales, Ndeeba has been the leading motorcycle sales station in Uganda; pouring millions of the boda bodas onto the roads that we now see.

Original_Search_Title: Can't We Have Electoral Civility?
Headline: Uganda: Can't We Have Electoral Civility?
Body_Text:
Kampala — Since the chaotic electoral process in Kenya, the question much asked has been; what lessons can be drawn from the Kenyan experience? The government has said that this should act as an example for people not to take the security ushered by the NRM government for granted. On the other hand, the opposition has also pointed out that government should learn that electoral malpractice can culminate into chaos and therefore should never take people for granted.
But rather than narrow our focus, we should cast our nets further and draw lessons from a little more clean electoral process going on in the US. The electoral process for the US presidential primaries provides a much better experience than that of chaotic Kenya.

Original_Search_Title: Mentors, Proteges and Morons
Headline: Zimbabwe: Mentors, Proteges and Morons
Body_Text:
Gaborone — There is a raging battle about what is going on in Zimbabwe. Because of its undisputed importance in the region, the happenings on the Zimbabwean political landscape are of interest to many people, in and outside Zimbabwe.
There is undeserved euphoria about Simba Makoni, a ZANU-PF prodigal son who clearly owes his political identity to Robert Mugabe, the dictator he seeks to replace today.

Original_Search_Title: ITC Helps African Organic Exporters Showcase Their Goods at World Fair
Headline: Africa: ITC Helps African Organic Exporters Showcase Their Goods at World Fair
Body_Text:
Nuremberg/Geneva — Thanks to the International Trade Centre, 15 innovative companies and business networks from five African countries have travelled to Nuremberg, Germany, to take part in the world’s largest organic goods fair, where business worth millions of dollars is done every year.
ITC, the UN/WTO agency whose mandate is to help small, developing-country businesses export, invested some $100,000 so that firms and organizations from Rwanda,

Original_Search_Title: Diamond Firm Accuses Officials of Corruption
Headline: Namibia: Toscanini Diamonds Turn Russian Roulette
Body_Text:
Windhoek — Diamond Commissioner Kennedy Hamutenya and the Director General in the National Planning Commission, Helmut Angula, have stepped in to fight off accusations of corruption levelled by Nambib Resources, the owners of the Toscanini diamond reserves in the Skeleton coast.
Hamutenya also expresses concern at the negative impact the Toscanini diamond saga will have on Namibia's reputation within the international diamond fraternity.

Original_Search_Title: Sand Filling - Lagos in Danger, Says Fashola
Headline: Nigeria: Sand Filling - Lagos in Danger, Says Fashola
Body_Text:
Lagos — Lagos State Governor, Mr. Babatunde Fashola (SAN), yesterday raised alarm over the continued illegal mining of sand from the coastline of the state saying the miners were putting the state in great danger.
Fashola also ordered the stoppage of mining activities going on near the Banana Islands where an attempt is currently illegal being made to create another island.

Original_Search_Title: Why the Cote d'Ivoire Disaster is Echoed in the Country's Crisis
Headline: Kenya: Why the Cote d'Ivoire Disaster is Echoed in the Country's Crisis
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Like Kenya in East Africa, Cote d'Ivoire was the model of economic prosperity and political stability in West Africa for nearly three decades until something went terribly wrong and the country unravelled in a way that has several uncanny parallels with Kenya. In this first of a two-part series TONY ELUEMUNOR, NATION Correspondent, West Africa, reports
Until about a decade ago, Cote d'Ivoire was, as people on the street would say, the "Kenya of West Africa" - politically stable as its neighbours succumbed to brutal military coups and civil strife.

Original_Search_Title: Modernised Slavery Amongst Citizens
Headline: Nigeria: Modernised Slavery Amongst Citizens
Body_Text:
Lagos — Some centuries ago, people were evacuated from the shores of Africa to Europe, America and other parts of the world as slaves.
Recall that prior to this time, nothing is called slave trade but somebody somewhere sat down and imagine this out and executed it. It is called Trade because something is exchange for something.

Original_Search_Title: Museveni Advises Bushenyi Residents On Credit
Headline: Uganda: Museveni Advises Bushenyi Residents On Credit
Body_Text:
Bushenyi — PRESIDENT Yoweri Museveni has advised residents of Bushenyi District to access affordable credit to fund their projects through Saccos.
He made the comments last week at Nyakasharu Sub-county headquarters in Bunyaruguru County on the final day of his visit to Bushenyi.

Original_Search_Title: Fine Dining At Katch the Sun
Headline: Uganda: Fine Dining At Katch the Sun
Body_Text:
Kampala — Place: Katch the Sun
Location: Bugolobi, Plot 3 Bandali Rise

Original_Search_Title: 'Success of Free Education Boosts Bonna Bagaggawale'
Headline: Uganda: 'Success of Free Education Boosts Bonna Bagaggawale'
Body_Text:
Kampala — The Government is implementing the Prosperity-for-All (Bonna Bagaggawale) programme to eradicate household poverty following the success of the universal primary and secondary education.
Speaking at Nyakasharu sub-county on Thursday during his tour of Bushenyi district, President Yoweri Museveni told the locals that the Government would facilitate six families in each parish every financial year.

Original_Search_Title: Obasanjo's Affront On Speaker Bankole
Headline: Nigeria: Obasanjo's Affront On Speaker Bankole
Body_Text:
We are faced with a disaster in Nigeria.
There is the need for all and sundry to make concerted efforts to ensure that this seeming tide of recklessness is checked before we finally wipe out the genuine picture of leadership with integrity out of the political and social landscape of the Nigerian nation.

Original_Search_Title: Love Banana Unveils Artistes
Headline: Nigeria: Love Banana Unveils Artistes
Body_Text:
Lagos — Some of Nigeria's topmost comedians and musicians are billed to perform in his year's edition of the nation main Valentine show, Love Bananas which holds on Sunday, 17th February, 2008 at the Shell Hall, MUSON Centre, Lagos.
According to a release made available to the press, the line up includes Gordons, Koffi and Owen G. Also to thrill the audience during event are Wambilele and the leading lady comedian, Lepasious Bose.

Original_Search_Title: The Public Must Know the Judges
Headline: Botswana: The Public Must Know the Judges
Body_Text:
In a stable democracy such as Botswana the judiciary, particularly the judges, are respected and believed to be impartial. They are hardly ever second guessed whenever they deliver judgements.
Judges preside over many cases of political and economic importance, and sometimes cases that are about the life and death of individuals. People generally trust that the judges will always have the wisdom of Solomon and deliver what is in law the fairest judgement.

Original_Search_Title: Yar'Adua And Rule of Law
Headline: Nigeria: Yar'Adua And Rule of Law
Body_Text:
Abuja — The disposition of President Yar'Adua to the rule of law, which he has been demonstrating since he assumed office last year, is a clear manifestation that he is determined to take the country to the promised land. ANDREW OOTA observes that the rule of law is the basic tenet of liberal democracy.
President Umar Musa Yar'Adua, no doubt conjures the image of one with a sound mind, and a personality who always has his destination properly defined before embarking on a journey. So it was not surprising that on assumption of office last year May 29, he made it clear that never again will people's right be trampled upon, that rule of law must be respected. If I read him well, that was not to say that self-confessed felons and dupes have been thrown into a safety net or that a veritable environment has been created for lawlessness.

Original_Search_Title: NRM Friction Spills to Spy Agencies
Headline: Uganda: NRM Friction Spills to Spy Agencies
Body_Text:
Kampala — A recent shake up of top directors at the Internal Security Organisation (ISO) has sparked a storm amid claims that the changes are more than a mere realigning of officers.
Sources within ISO claim that intrigue and infighting within the ruling NRM party has burst the boundaries and is steadily creeping into the security organisations.

Original_Search_Title: Senate - Setting a Mark for Leadership
Headline: Nigeria: Senate - Setting a Mark for Leadership
Body_Text:
Lagos — Since stepping on the saddle as Senate President on June 5, 2007, Senator David Alechenu Bonaventure Mark has surprised many of his colleagues in the Senate with his disciplined, accountable and sharply-focused leadership, reports Sufuyan Ojeifo
This is about the Senate President, Senator David Alechenu Bonaventure Mark-retired Brigadier General of the Nigerian Army, former Military Governor of Niger State, former Minister of Communications and former active member of the defunct Armed Forces Ruling Council (AFRC) under the General Ibrahim Babangida military regime- who will be 60 years old on April 8, this year.

Original_Search_Title: Yar'Adua Assures On Quick Resolution of Land Disputes With Lagos Govt
Headline: Nigeria: Yar'Adua Assures On Quick Resolution of Land Disputes With Lagos Govt
Body_Text:
Abuja — PRESIDENT Umaru Musa Yar'Adua yesterday assured the people of Lagos State that all current disagreements between the Federal and the Lagos State Governments over the ownership of land and landed properties in the state will be swiftly resolved "in conformity" with existing laws.
Responding to a memorandum presented to him at the Presidential Villa by the Council of Lagos State Eminent Citizens led by the Oba of Lagos, Oba Rilwanu Akiolu I, President Yar'Adua promised that his administration will speed up the process of dialogue already initiated on the resolution of the disputes.

Original_Search_Title: Land Disputes With Lagos - Rule of Law Will Prevail - Yar'Adua
Headline: Nigeria: Land Disputes With Lagos - Rule of Law Will Prevail - Yar'Adua
Body_Text:
Lagos — President Umaru Musa Yar'Adua yesterday in Abuja assured that current disagreements between the federal government and the Lagos State government over the ownership of land and landed properties in the state will be swiftly resolved "in conformity" with existing laws.
Responding to a memorandum presented to him at the Presidential Villa by the Council of Lagos State eminent citizens led by the Oba of Lagos, Oba Rilwanu Akiolu I, President Yar'Adua promised that his administration will speed up the process of dialogue already initiated on the resolution of the disputes.

Original_Search_Title: Land Disputes With Lagos - Rule of Law Will Prevail -President Yar'Adua
Headline: Nigeria: Land Disputes With Lagos - Rule of Law Will Prevail -President Yar'Adua
Body_Text:
Abuja — President Umaru Musa Yar'Adua on Monday February 11 in Abuja gave a firm assurance that all current disagreements between the Federal Government and the Lagos State Government over the ownership of land and landed properties in the state will be swiftly resolved "in conformity" with existing laws.
The President who was responding to a memorandum presented to him at the Presidential Villa by the Council of Lagos State Eminent Citizens led by the Oba of Lagos, Oba Rilwanu Akiolu I, promised that his Administration will speed up the process of dialogue already initiated on the resolution of the disputes.

Original_Search_Title: Substituting Rule of Law With Lawlessness
Headline: Nigeria: Substituting Rule of Law With Lawlessness
Body_Text:
Lagos — President Umar Musa Yar'Adua, no doubt conjures the image of one with a sound mind, and a personality who always has his destination properly defined before embarking on a journey.
So it was not surprising that on assumption of office last May 29, he made it clear that never again will people's right be trampled upon, that rule of law must be respected. If I read him well, that was not to say that self-confessed felons and dupes have been thrown a safety net or that a veritable environment has been created for lawlessness.

Original_Search_Title: World Trade Organisation Goes in to Bat for Big Banana Producers
Headline: South Africa: World Trade Organisation Goes in to Bat for Big Banana Producers
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — WHAT is it about bananas and trade? Years ago, the UK tabloid The Sun printed one of the most memorable stories about the European Union (EU), simply reproducing the EU's regulations about how bent a banana has to be to gain access into the union's market.
The story was designed to typify the absurd regulatory constriction that restrains economic growth, and consequently became part of eurosceptic folklore.

Original_Search_Title: Citizens Should Strive to Place a Positive Value On Diversity
Headline: Kenya: Citizens Should Strive to Place a Positive Value On Diversity
Body_Text:
Nairobi — I have just come from Rift Valley and Central Provinces a saddened man, dejected and horrified.
After witnessing crimes of unprecedented scope being perpetrated, the murder of hundreds of innocent children, women and old people, I feel like I am going crazy.

Original_Search_Title: Eldoret Airport Buzzes After Years of Slow Trade
Headline: Kenya: Eldoret Airport Buzzes After Years of Slow Trade
Body_Text:
After a series of missteps that characterised its establishment and history, Eldoret International Airport, one of the previously most maligned facilities in the country, turned out to be a significant lifeline to traders in the region in the post- election violence.
When fish, fruits or flowers produced in the region could not even find their way to markets in the nearest towns in the province due to the many illegal roadblocks, traders easily airlifted their cargo to international market through the airport. "The airport has been very busy in the past few weeks.

Original_Search_Title: 'The Economist' And That Little Matter of Stereotyping
Headline: Kenya: 'The Economist' And That Little Matter of Stereotyping
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Economist magazine likes to go for what is racy and stylish. That's why it's so entertaining to read. It will avoid inconvenient angles if they get boring. In its most recent issue it had a cute little story comparing Kenya with Gaza.
"Both have too many people, or to be more exact, too many young men without jobs or prospects," the magazine pontificated. Yet even this exalted journal hesitated to say Kenya's "present discontents" can be explained entirely through that.

Original_Search_Title: New Salute for Fourth Term
Headline: Uganda: New Salute for Fourth Term
Body_Text:
Kampala — A four-finger salute was the curtain raiser at the Tarehe Sita celebrations in Masindi. The symbol is gradually being associated with campaigning for a fourth term for President Yoweri Museveni.
Many NRM supporters are waving it like they did the three-finger salute and the dry banana leaves in 2003 to popularise the third term.

Original_Search_Title: Police Killing Fields
Headline: Nigeria: Police Killing Fields
Body_Text:
Abuja — Have you ever visited a police station and also spent quality time visiting the police cells?
Have you had the misfortune of experiencing the detention by the Nigeria police of somebody you know very well and can vouch for the person's integrity and yet was alleged to have committed a crime? Do you accept that the police operative next door is your friend in truth? Do you have a brother or sister in the Nigeria police? Have you ever wondered why no comprehensive and transparent audit of all the police detention centers has been carried out by the government at all levels in Nigeria? Can you volunteer meaningful information to the police in order to curb the increasing rate of crime in our society? What is your perception of the Nigeria police operative vis-à-vis other police officials you have met on the streets of Central London, Bangkok, Thailand, China or Hong Kong? These were the questions this writer posed to members of the Human Rights Writers Association of Nigeria during our recent parley in the nation's capital.

Original_Search_Title: Protectionism Hurts Both Consumers And Environment
Headline: Uganda: Protectionism Hurts Both Consumers And Environment
Body_Text:
Kampala — DELEGATES from "major economies" concluded a summit in Hawaii last week aimed at "supporting the collective effort to negotiate a new international agreement" to restrict greenhouse gas emissions globally.
In the background, politicians in the European Union (EU) and the United States have been peddling protectionism-threatening to impose restrictions on imports from countries which refuse to reduce greenhouse gas emissions. This is no doubt music to the ears of the labour unions and environmentalists that have been calling for such tariffs, but it is bad news for everyone else - especially the poor.

Original_Search_Title: Nana Akuoko Sarpong is Right
Headline: Ghana: Nana Akuoko Sarpong is Right
Body_Text:
Accra — The Daily Graphic yesterday quoted the Paramount Chief of the Agogo Traditional Area in the Ashanti Region, Nana Akuaku Sarpong, as calling on the Government of Japan to do more to correct the trade imbalances, between Ghana and Japan, for a more productive and beneficial partnership.
According to the Paramount Chief, though he was aware that Japan imported Ghana's cocoa for the production of chocolate, Ghana's exports to Japan were relatively small, because trade between the two countries was one-sided, in favour of Japan.

Original_Search_Title: Kenya Crisis Threatens Regional Aid
Headline: East Africa: Crisis Ripple Effects Felt Across the Region
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Unrest in Kenya threatens humanitarian and commercial operations throughout the Great Lakes region, potentially affecting more than 100 million lives, according to analysts.
Southern Sudan, Uganda, Burundi, Rwanda and the eastern Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC) have experienced shortages of fuel and other essential supplies because of insecurity along the Kenyan section of the Northern Corridor, one of the most important transport routes in Africa. It runs from the Kenyan port of Mombasa westwards through Uganda and the Great Lakes.

Original_Search_Title: Yes, It Can And Did Happen to Kenya Too
Headline: Kenya: Yes, It Can And Did Happen to Kenya Too
Body_Text:
Last year, Kenyans were persuaded to vote. They were told an election was one way to speak and be heard. And vote they did, in millions. But the election results (or the tallying) provoked an ugly dispute.
And so the scorned populace reacted publicly but extremely unwisely by turning against their neighbours, attacking and killing. It is the worst form of expression, the worst form of action to cause change, but perhaps it was not entirely unexpected, as years of simmering anger burst forth.

Original_Search_Title: Protectionism Harms Consumers And the Environment
Headline: Rwanda: Protectionism Harms Consumers And the Environment
Body_Text:
Kigali — Last week, delegates from "major economies" concluded a summit in Hawaii aimed at "supporting the collective effort to negotiate a new international agreement" to restrict greenhouse gas emissions globally.
In the background, politicians in the European Union and the United States have been peddling protectionism - threatening to impose restrictions on imports from countries which refuse to reduce greenhouse gas emissions. This is no doubt music to the ears of the labour unions and environmentalists that have been calling for such tariffs, but it is bad news for everyone else - especially the poor.

Original_Search_Title: Is the President Aware of Brigandage?
Headline: Nigeria: Is the President Aware of Brigandage?
Body_Text:
Abuja — The media the world over have always fallen victim to bad governments and dictators. For understandable reasons, dictators don't like to be called what they are, and there could be grave consequences when you look a dictator in the eye and call him a bloody dictator.
It is usually worse with those pretending to be democrats. In Pakistan , the media will someday write the history of their travails in the hands of President Pervez Musharaf who still cannot read the clear handwriting on the wall that his time is up. In Nigeria , the AIT and its chairman, Raymond Dokpesi, will someday tell their own full stories of how they survived the very unpopular Obasanjo administration. No government or leader that chooses to be hostile to the media ever ends up well - and that's the only way for bad governments.

Original_Search_Title: Man Punishes Fene Cheats
Headline: Uganda: Man Punishes Fene Cheats
Body_Text:
Kampala — A traveller on the Iganga-Tororo highway returned to haunt a group of traders who had cheated him earlier on. The man had bought three jackfruits, tomatoes and bananas from traders at Idudi roadside market but on getting to his destination, he realised that the fruits had not been loaded into his car boot as the traders had pretended to do.
On his way back to Kampala, he decided to pay the traders another visit. He bought more fruits and as they had done earlier, the traders pretended to be loading the items into the car boot but instead placed them on the tarmac just under the car.

Original_Search_Title: Yes, It Can And Did Happen to Kenya Too
Headline: Kenya: Yes, It Can And Did Happen to Kenya Too
Body_Text:
Last year, Kenyans were persuaded to vote. They were told an election was one way to speak and be heard. And vote they did, in millions. But, the election results (or the tallying) provoked an ugly dispute.
And so the scorned populace reacted publicly but extremely unwisely by turning against their neighbours, attacking and killing. It is the worst form of expression, the worst form of action to cause change, but perhaps it was not entirely unexpected, as years of simmering anger burst forth.

Original_Search_Title: Sh8bn Road Bypass Project Starts in April
Headline: Kenya: Sh8bn Road Bypass Project Starts in April
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Government has received Sh8.6 billion from the Chinese government for the Eastern and Northern road bypass project.
The 70-kilometre project consists of two major road links in the Nairobi Road Network master plan. It is expected to start in April and will be handled by the China Road and Bridge Corporation contractor.

Original_Search_Title: Pirates Fired Up
Headline: Uganda: Pirates Fired Up
Body_Text:
If you want to have an idea as to how this year's Guinness Rugby League that starts Saturday will shape up then you should have been at last Saturday's final round-robin Chairman's Cup game between G4S Pirates and utl Kobs.
It was a game of two halves, no tries and was settled with a sudden-death Anthony Kinene penalty that earned Pirates the bragging rights.

Original_Search_Title: 'Burma Boy' by Biyi Bandele
Headline: Nigeria: 'Burma Boy' by Biyi Bandele
Body_Text:
Burma Boy tells the little-known history of Nigerian soldiers who fought in the Chindits, a commando division of the British Army in Burma during the Second World War.
Only going on fourteen, Biyi Bandele's irrepressible if terribly naïve protagonist, Ali Banana, sneaks after his best friends to enroll in the army following a summons from "Kingi Joji, monarch of Ingila." A sea crossing and a bout of chickenpox later, Banana finds himself training in India with the West African Thunder Brigade, under the immediate command of Samanja [Sergeant] Damisa, also from Northern Nigeria.

Original_Search_Title: Yar'Adua's Govt is Too Slow -Odumakin
Headline: Nigeria: Yar'Adua's Govt is Too Slow -Odumakin
Body_Text:
Lagos — Dr. Joe-Okei Odumakin, is President, Campaign for Democracy (CD) and spokesperson, Coalition of Civil Society Organisations (CCSO). In this interview with WILLY EYA, she speaks on a wide range of issues bordering on how to move Nigeria forward. Excerpt:
WHAT is your assessment of Yar'Adua's administration?

Original_Search_Title: Son Machetes 75-Year-Old Mother to Death in Ilorin
Headline: Nigeria: Son Machetes 75-Year-Old Mother to Death in Ilorin
Body_Text:
Ilorin — Tragedy struck in the capital city of Kwara State, Ilorin, at the weekend when a middle aged man, Fatai Yusuf (aka) Agba-Akin, macheted his 75-year-old mother to death.
The suspect was said to have locked up his mother at their Awoli compound along Emirs road and butchered her with a sharp cutlass.

Original_Search_Title: Dreams of Military Service
Headline: Uganda: Dreams of Military Service
Body_Text:
Kampala — In the modest compound of Mubende's Resident District Commissioner, along the Fort Portal Road, the immediate fate of 100 people will be determined. A mix of people in plainclothes and military camouflage sit or lounge around as army officers speak loudly into their mobile phones. The civilians watch them expectantly.
They have gathered here from in the hope of being recruited into the Uganda People's Defence Forces (UPDF). Ms Madrine Namakula, one of the only two women present, sold bananas for a month to earn enough money for transport to Mubende. Like everyone else present, the lure of a steady Shs180,000 monthly salary convinced her to try her luck at army recruitment. Though she has a nursing certificate, she's been waiting for months to hear about her application to join Mubende Hospital.

Original_Search_Title: Some Thoughts for Smart Government
Headline: Uganda: Some Thoughts for Smart Government
Body_Text:
Kampala — Kenya's post election violence has left many socio-economic analysts and recent optimists in the mooted African renaissance as an emerging market very surprised. The 'optimism' of world leaders such as former US President Bill Jefferson Clinton was based on closed door schemes by the recent club of "revolutionaries'.
The will by African leaders to expose their unstructured and informal economies to ferocious market reforms without cushions was neither pragmatic nor progressive. It has not been progressive because incumbent regimes have deliberately and persistently ignored political reforms that have a direct bearing on the success of economic reforms.

Original_Search_Title: 'Papers Made in Arusha'
Headline: Tanzania: 'Papers Made in Arusha'
Body_Text:
Arusha — Papers eh? We all take them for granted; after all they have been around for as long as the human civilization itself. Enters one Seppo Hallavaiano an artist from Finland who has just taken permanent residency in Arusha and everything about papers seems to be about to change.
'Papers made in Arusha' could be the selling line once (and if) Seppo's organic papers become commercial. "I used ordinary raw materials such as the straws of African Mulberry, Sisal, Cotton, Sugar cane waste and banana fibers all these can be obtain locally and at no cost," said Mr. Hallavaino.

Original_Search_Title: MDC  - Misreading Kenya's Civil Unrest
Headline: Zimbabwe: MDC  - Misreading Kenya's Civil Unrest
Body_Text:
Harare — It is so clear what the MDC game plan ahead of March 2008 is. The MDC seeks to choreograph confrontation with the authorities, all for outside, western judgment.
The MDC seeks a decent pretext for boycotting elections it knows it is destined to lose. The MDC wants to win victim status, hoping western pity will re-launch it in the electoral aftermath. No one particularly worries about all these goals, ignoble though they are.

Original_Search_Title: Several Testify At TRC Hearing
Headline: Liberia: Several Testify At TRC Hearing
Body_Text:
Corporal Thompson of the Sierra Leonean contingent, then in the erstwhile Economic Monitoring Group of West Africa (ECOMOG), the military force that secured peace in Liberia in the 1990s, said Col. J.P Moinama of the 3rd headquarters battalion 'hit me in my left eye in 1995 which have made me as a handicap," moaned the fifth witness at the TRC public hearing yesterday.
Testifying before the commissioners at the public hearing on Thursday, Joseph Kamara said that a Liberian boy accused him of purchasing a stolen tape belonging to the peacekeepers.

Original_Search_Title: FAN Sued for Libel
Headline: Namibia: FAN Sued for Libel
Body_Text:
Windhoek — The Filmmakers Association of Namibia (FAN) has been sued for N$100000 for libel against the president of the Oruuano Artists Union.
This was confirmed by FAN's chairperson, Abius Akwaake, this week.

Original_Search_Title: Leather Industry On Track in Acquiring Processing Machines
Headline: Rwanda: Leather Industry On Track in Acquiring Processing Machines
Body_Text:
Kigali — Rwanda's leather industry is going to boom soon as a result of new machines that are going to process hides and skins. And of course, when one sector of the economy booms, the whole country benefits in one form or another.
Rwanda Leather Industries, the company behind the importing of these machines, expects to start exporting leather products by March, to the tune of 35,000 assorted pieces a month.

Original_Search_Title: Matooke in Plenty
Headline: Uganda: Matooke in Plenty
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE increased supply of matooke to Kampala's major markets has forced traders to reduce prices, a weekly mini-survey has shown.
A bunch of matooke costs about sh2,500 from sh10,000 during the festive season.

Original_Search_Title: Banana Farmers Count Losses
Headline: Kenya: Banana Farmers Count Losses
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Banana growers in Kisii are complaining of heavy loses since transporters into other towns stopped their operations.
A survey by The Standard established that growers were now selling bananas at throw-away prices at shopping centres.

Original_Search_Title: Country's Flying Start
Headline: Kenya: Country's Flying Start
Body_Text:
Nairobi — National women's volleyball team made a roaring start in their quest to qualify for Beijing by easily breezing past Senegal 3-0 in Algiers.
The West Africans, who had been earmarked by head coach, Sammy Kirongo as a potential banana skin, caved in to the former continental champions in just over an hour last evening.

Original_Search_Title: Rampage Claims 2 in Owerri
Headline: Nigeria: Rampage Claims 2 in Owerri
Body_Text:
The killing of a Lagos-based businessman, Ogechi Obiwulu, at Okwudo, Imo State, has led to rampage which resulted in the death of two other people.
Two traditional rulers in the area have also been invited for questioning by the police while 12 suspects have been arrested in the violence that trailed the incident.

Original_Search_Title: Yar'aduanomics And the Poverty Question
Headline: Nigeria: Yar'aduanomics And the Poverty Question
Body_Text:
Kaduna — One will be right by way of personification, to say that poverty and crime are twin brothers born of a mother whose name is unemployment. Some one once argued that, of all the evils known to mankind, worklessness is the worst. Unemployment is a direct source of poverty the worst affliction that man can suffer. This unemployment situation lowers self esteem and morale, it is indeed dehumanizing.
The level of poverty has since become a major bench mark for gauging the health of any nation's economy. According to Dudley Seers, a world renoun political economist, the questions to ask about a country's development are three, namely; What has been happening to poverty? What has been happening to unemployment? What has been happening to inequality? If all these three have declined from high levels, then beyond doubt, this has been a period of development for the country concerned". When you apply these standards or indices which are intertwined to the Nigerian situation, the answer becomes very obvious and does not need to be repeated, they have all been on the rise. Nigeria is currently facing profound socio-economic challenges, the most worrisome and distressing of them being wide spread poverty and high rates of crime especially armed robbery, corruption, murders, drug-related crimes and youth militant up-risings.

Original_Search_Title: All Eyes On Africa's Party
Headline: Africa: All Eyes On Africa's Party
Body_Text:
Lagos — World football's focus switches squarely to Ghana for the next month as the continent's top talent assemble for the CAF Africa Cup of Nations, which kicks off today in the Ghanaian city of Accra.
Sixteen finalists will chase the title of African champions, some with serious credentials and others who have traveled to uphold national honour but are not considered genuine threats to the established order. There are, however, no clear favourites in the field making it potentially the most fiercely contested instalment in the tournament's 51-year history.

Original_Search_Title: Ecowas EPA - the Facts And the Falacies
Headline: West Africa: Ecowas EPA - the Facts And the Falacies
Body_Text:
A tripartite meeting of the non-LDCs in West Africa (ECOWAS) consisting Nigeria, Ghana and Cote d'ivorie took place recently in Accra to review the implications of the present quagmire caused by interim agreements signed by Ghana and Cote d'ivorie under the Economic Partnership Agreement (EPA) with the European Union (EU).
The fundamental trust of the meeting which was originally initiated by NANTS, the Nigerian government and of course, the ECOWAS Commission was to look at the effects such interim agreement could have on the hard earned ECOWAS regional integration. In particular, the Nigerian government had tried to express her ill-feelings on the two West African countries and especially her close neighbour - Ghana for yielding to the pressures from the EU and endorsing an accord that is capable of dismembering the unity of the region.

Original_Search_Title: How Post Election Violence Has Ruined the Lives of Citizens
Headline: Kenya: How Post Election Violence Has Ruined the Lives of Citizens
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The violent protests following the announcement of disputed election results have negatively affected Kenyans across the country. Here, some of them share their experiences with the Saturday Nation.
GEORGINA KIMAGUT, Retail trader, Eldoret

Original_Search_Title: 'Macheso Should Be Proud of Being Source of Inspiration'
Headline: Zimbabwe: 'Macheso Should Be Proud of Being Source of Inspiration'
Body_Text:
Harare — JOSEPH Garakara has confessed that he is following in Alick Macheso's footsteps in sungura music "as there is honey in his path".
Last year Macheso included Garakara -- who is popular for the track "Idya Banana" -- on his list of those he called "copycats" or murondatsimbas, who thrived on imitating his work.

Original_Search_Title: NRM Spoils Village Romance
Headline: Uganda: NRM Spoils Village Romance
Body_Text:
We all know that I am not a big fan of the Movement. The only time I ever joined the Movement was at Charlie Lubega's Ange Noir, when I saw a chick with a long nose and broad-based hips. And my membership lasted as long as it took me to get her number.
What the Movement has done in my two villages will not help to endear me to it. After more than a year, I returned to my mother's village and my father's village, somewhere in western Uganda, to find that both villages now have piped water.

Original_Search_Title: Two Pitch Camp On Hill Over 'Ebyaffe'
Headline: Uganda: Two Pitch Camp On Hill Over 'Ebyaffe'
Body_Text:
Kampala — THEY had anticipated a comfortable night in the State House, instead, they slept in the cold under a tree at Bat Valley near Wandegeya on November 8.
Serwaniko Kasajja, 34, and Nambi Bbeerelimu, 42, from Luweero, were arrested by the security personnel as they tried to enter State House, Nakasero with fetishes.

Original_Search_Title: Museveni Unveils Wealth Project
Headline: Uganda: Museveni Unveils Wealth Project
Body_Text:
Kampala — PRESIDENT Yoweri Museveni has called on the wananchi (citzens) to exploit the potential of their land to fight poverty. The President was recently sensitising residents of Kikoni sub-county at Mutanoga Primary School on the Bonna Bagaggawale (Prosperity-for-All) programme.
Museveni and his wife, Janet, were touring Ruhaama county in Ntungamo district to set off the programme. Janet is the MP for Ruhaama.

Original_Search_Title: Kaduna Stands Still for Sardauna
Headline: Nigeria: Kaduna Stands Still for Sardauna
Body_Text:
Kaduna, headquarters of the defunct Northern Region, came alive yesterday as premier of the region and Sardauna of Sokoto, late Sir Ahmadu Bello, was fondly remembered in an annual lecture organised by the Centre for Historical Documentation and Research, an extension of Ahmadu Bello University, Zaria.
The occasion, which took place at Arewa House, Kaduna, was chaired by a former Senate president, Senator Ken Nnamani, while the guest speaker was Senator (Professor) Iya Abubakar.

Original_Search_Title: Divorce of Ex-President's Son Gets Nasty
Headline: Nigeria: Gbenga Obasanjo's Divorce Gets Nasty
Body_Text:
Lagos — Gbenga, first son of former president, Chief Olusegun Obasanjo, got contracts from the Nigerian National Petroleum Corporation (NNPC) and Niger Delta Development Commission (NDDC) while his father was in office, his estranged wife, Mojisola, has claimed.
Mojisola and Gbenga are currently seeking the dissolution of their seven-year-old marriage at the Lagos High Court, Ikeja Division, in Suit No. ID/172HD/2006.

Original_Search_Title: Cold Room to Boost Horticulture Exports
Headline: Rwanda: Cold Room to Boost Horticulture Exports
Body_Text:
Kigali — The cold room recently constructed at Kanombe Airport, Kigali, is expected to boost Rwanda's horticulture exports.
The refrigerated room which was completed in May 2007 can store a total of 30 metric tonnes of perishable products.

Original_Search_Title: Museveni Advises On Poverty
Headline: Uganda: Museveni Advises On Poverty
Body_Text:
Kampala — PRESIDENT Yoweri Museveni has advised Ugandans to avoid fragmenting land. The President, who on Tuesday toured Ntungamo district where he addressed residents of Rwashamaire town on the Prosperity-for-All programme, urged residents to form family-based companies and share profits accruing from the family-owned land.
He was accompanied by the First Lady, Janet. Museveni counselled the wananchi to lead decent lives by fighting household poverty using the infrastructure put in place by the NRM Government.

Original_Search_Title: Multiple Birth - a Gift of Joy Or a Tight Spot?
Headline: Uganda: Multiple Birth - a Gift of Joy Or a Tight Spot?
Body_Text:
Kampala — Chaos is the word. Four babies wailing at the same time and their mother looking on helplessly. From Linda whose flowery pink dress is soaked up in urine, to Roland in his immaculate white overall, the mother shifts her gaze, perhaps wondering whom to pick up first.
Since Sarah Rwakiziba gave birth to quadruplets in September last year at Nsambya Hospital, each day has been breaking with a string of challenges.

Original_Search_Title: Little-Known Cocoa Sector Posts Good Trends
Headline: Uganda: Cocoa Sector Posts Good Trends
Body_Text:
Kampala — Uganda's little known cocoa sub sector has continued to post very impressive growth figures.
Last year, Uganda exported some 10,006 metric tonnes of cocoa worth US$20.01 million up from 7,205 metric tonnes worth $11.4 million the previous year. For the 2008 season, it is projected Uganda will produce 12,800 metric tonnes worth some $25.6 million.

Original_Search_Title: Bananas, the Sweet Good Mood Food
Headline: Uganda: Bananas, the Sweet Good Mood Food
Body_Text:
Kampala — BANANAS are sweet fruits that children love. They are very soft when ripe that even a child who is teething can eat them. Their rich yellow colour is what I find most attractive about them. And, they are affordable.
A bunch of small sweet bananas costs sh500 to sh3,000, while the big ones (bogoya) cost sh100 to sh300 each, depending on where you buy it from.

Original_Search_Title: Country to Export 300 Monkeys to Russia
Headline: Uganda: Country to Export 300 Monkeys to Russia
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE Uganda Wildlife Authority (UWA) has licensed a wildlife dealer to export 300 Vervet monkeys to Russia. Yekoyada Nuwagaba, the proprietor of Navina Export, secured the licence recently. The company has been exporting chameleons and snakes to Europe.
"We have approved the export, which is expected to take place within three months. UWA considered his proposal to save communities where vervet monkeys have become a menace and at the same time sell them and benefit in economic terms," said Sam Mwandha, the UWA acting executive director.

Original_Search_Title: Plight of Kisii Refugees Grim
Headline: Kenya: Plight of Kisii Refugees Grim
Body_Text:
Kisii, Kenya — A group of men who a couple of weeks ago were busy at work huddle together idle. They have grown tired of rehashing tales of their horrendous experiences at the hands of their hitherto neighbours and friends. Now they watch the entrance to the church here, in the hope that any visitor brings something to silence their rumbling stomachs.
"I haven't eaten since yesterday. We gave the little food we received from well wishers to our children," says a man in his mid-40s. The other men around him nod in agreement.

Original_Search_Title: Exclusive - SOS Grey Johnson, Jammeh's Perfect Courtier
Headline: Gambia: Exclusive - SOS Grey Johnson, Jammeh's Perfect Courtier
Body_Text:
A while ago, I was at a social event in Washington DC where dignitaries such as the Gambian emissary to the US at the time, Crispin Grey Johnson, milled about. A couple of DC career diplomats and I were in a somewhat idealistic discussion about the prospects of democracy in Africa. After all, that was the year, 1998, that Foreign Affairs magazine had hailed the "new era" of African leadership in the form of Meles Zenawi of Ethiopia, Isias Aferworki of Eritrea, and Yoweri Museveni of Uganda, to name a few. We were saying perhaps the impending new millennium would usher in less suffering and wretchedness for a continent with immense potential.
Suddenly, Crispin Grey Johnson turned to us and asserted that democracy is not the only solution to Africa's ills and besides, democracy as a band aid to Africa's ills has not delivered much anyway. At the time, this came as a complete surprise to me because most of the people I know in Mr. Grey Johnson's inner circle advocated the virtues of rule of law, infrastructure and societal development and economic laissez faire. Out of respect for the honorable ambassador, I exercised great restraint by not offering a counter argument. That conversation has haunted me to this day and serves to explain the current state of affairs in Banjul.

Original_Search_Title: Cycling From South Africa to Uganda
Headline: Africa: Cycling From South Africa to Uganda
Body_Text:
Kampala — SITTING in a Robotics lecture at the University of Minnesota last year, Joel Hesch's mind drifted to Africa, a time when he and his parents spent eight years in Uganda. During this lecture, Hesch decided he would revisit the Pearl of Africa.
Only this time, he would not arrive by plane but on a bicycle. He would fly to Durban, South Africa, where his missionary parents live, before jumping onto a bicycle and riding all the way to Uganda.

Original_Search_Title: Judicial System Needs Overhaul
Headline: Zimbabwe: Judicial System Needs Overhaul
Body_Text:
Harare — Countless justice-seeking individuals have had their hopes shattered
Two significant developments ensued at the end of 2007, both of them having a negative bearing on our erstwhile reputable legal system.

Original_Search_Title: Okhuti - Uganda's Drogba
Headline: Uganda: Okhuti - Uganda's Drogba
Body_Text:
Christmas may be on December 25, but for Caesar Okhuti his Christmas last year arrived on a hot December 18 day in the hot Tanzanian conditions.
That was the day Ugandans glued on pay-TV GTV saw Okhuti open the scoring for The Cranes against Kenya. The 17-year-old striker didn't let a Kenyan second-half equalizer spoil his party. He converted the winning spot-kick that led Uganda to the CECAFA semifinals.

Original_Search_Title: Kigali Serena Hotel to Get Facelift
Headline: Rwanda: Kigali Serena Hotel to Get Facelift
Body_Text:
Kigali — In order to match standards with customer expectations, Kigali Serena Hotel, a five star hotel, is to be refurbished.
"We need to cope with the increased demand of our services, because Rwanda is growing very fast," Said Mugo W. Maringa, Kigali Serena Hotel's country manager.

Original_Search_Title: Country Will Lead the Emerging Markets in IP Explosion - Intel Boss
Headline: Nigeria: Country Will Lead the Emerging Markets in IP Explosion - Intel Boss
Body_Text:
The importance of Nigeria to the major IT players came to the fore recently when Intel Chairman, Mr. Craig Barrett visited the country. With earnings of over $35bn, Intel a global semi conductor leader is digging into the Nigerian market by way of being a major beneficiary when the IT market explodes.
And that is already happening, according to Barrett. He spared some time to speak to Vanguard's Asst Editor, Communications, Okoh Aihe.

Original_Search_Title: Kenyan Crisis Impacts Regional Economies
Headline: East Africa: Kenyan Crisis Impacts Regional Economies
Body_Text:
Kampala — On the pavements of Nairobi's broad streets, time always seems to pace ahead of them as they hurry, shuffling past each other, their minds intensely set on their next business task, never seeming satisfied with what is already done than worry so much about what has not been accomplished.
Add that to the widespread perception of Kenya's economy as considerably vast and advanced and Nairobi as one of Africa's most sophisticated cities and you have Kenyans flying high, telling every one how their country isn't your typical African backwater.

Original_Search_Title: The New Faces in Kibaki's Cabinet
Headline: Kenya: The New Faces in Kibaki's Cabinet
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Mr SAMUEL POGHISIO
The new Information and Communication minister could not hide his excitement about the new position.

Original_Search_Title: She Made an Ideal School for Iganga
Headline: Uganda: She Made an Ideal School for Iganga
Body_Text:
Kampala — EDUCATION, they say, is the key to success. How true that is, you might decide by the end of this story. What I am sure of, however, is that success comes in many ways, some of them painful.
Betty Kitakufe Kasoro calls her perfect rags-to-riches tale an outright blessing from God. Through pain and struggle, she now owns and personally runs Butt Villa Kindergarten, arguably the best and most outstanding nursery school in Iganga district.

Original_Search_Title: CDC to Create 5,000 Jobs in Five Years
Headline: Cameroon: CDC to Create 5,000 Jobs in Five Years
Body_Text:
The General Manager, GM, of the Cameroon Development Corporation, CDC, Henry Njalla Quan, has announced the creation of some 5,000 jobs by his establishment in five years to come.
"My dear colleagues, the establishment is taking bold steps to expand and increase production. We are on the way to creating more jobs. We have already started employing people and by that token, we would have in five years created at least 2,000 direct jobs and at least 3,000 indirect ones."

Original_Search_Title: Focus On Politics - Foroyaa Calls for President Jawara's Retirement
Headline: Gambia: Focus On Politics - Foroyaa Calls for President Jawara's Retirement
Body_Text:
After the withdrawal of Senegalese troops in 1989, Sud Hebdo, a Senegalese Daily newspaper, on 17th August 1989, reported the closure of the Senegal/ Gambia border. According to the Daily, fraud which was the order of business, occasions an annual loss of 20 million CFA Francs for Senegal.
It was also reported that Senegal adopted a policy to restrict the amount of Senegalese currency a person traveling to the Gambia could carry to 20,000CFAfrancs equivalent to 500 dalasi at a time.

Original_Search_Title: Introducing! Mrs Okorie and Mrs Akinlawon
Headline: Nigeria: Introducing! Mrs Okorie and Mrs Akinlawon
Body_Text:
Lagos — PERHAPS this column should have been titled "Ladies and Gentlemen, introducing Mrs Okorie and Mrs Akinlawon." But, the editor would probably have regarded that as too long. So pardon me for that. Hypocrisy is the homage paid by vice to virtue-De La Rochefoucauld, 1613-1680.
Now you can be forgiven if you don't know who on earth is Mrs Okorie. Never mind, I shall soon make her familiar to you. But, you cannot possibly be forgiven if you don't know who Mrs. Akinlawon is.

Original_Search_Title: African Miracles; Kibaki Pulls Off a Rare One
Headline: Kenya: African Miracles; Kibaki Pulls Off a Rare One
Body_Text:
The goalposts keep moving all the time. When the polls closed in Kenya's presidential parliamentary elections last Thursday, the Electoral Commission of Kenya (ECK) announced that the earliest provisional results would start trickling in by midnight, or failing that, on account of the record-breaking turn out of voters across the country, Friday morning.
By Friday afternoon it had become clear that a massacre of unprecedented proportions had occurred at parliamentary levels, claiming the scalps of cabinet ministers and perhaps a dozen junior ministers, not to mention dozens of ordinary Party of National Unity MPs. But about the presidential contest the world received only rumors from private television networks and other media outlets.

Original_Search_Title: Remembering the Days of Slavery
Headline: Sierra Leone: Remembering the Days of Slavery
Body_Text:
Banana Island, the Isle of remembrance of the Trans Atlantic Slave Trade has won the hearts of the Carlton- Carew EP Foundation by giving the historic Island a Museum Site and a temporary library that was provided by the inhabitants for construction that was laid by the Honorable Minister of Lands and Country Planning, Retired Captain Benjamin Davies.
Also recognized on that day was the mass grave (dumped hole), of the unfortunate African Slaves who lost their lives while in transit at Banana Island and, giving equipments and drugs to the poorly equipped lone Community Health center on the Island Vice Chair Person of the Carlton- Carew EP Foundation Cynthia Jarrett- Thorpe said she visited the Slave Island last year and felt the need to help the people of Banana Island.

Original_Search_Title: Pray And Work Hard - Museveni
Headline: Uganda: Pray And Work Hard - Museveni
Body_Text:
Kampala — PRAYER must be accompanied with hard work for it to be effective, President Yoweri Museveni told born-again Christians on New Year's Eve.
Accompanied by First Lady Janet Museveni, the President told the faithful at Kololo national ceremonial grounds that even Manna does not drop from heaven everyday.

Original_Search_Title: Zero Tolerance for Corruption?
Headline: Nigeria: Zero Tolerance for Corruption?
Body_Text:
Seeing Nigerian politics from afar is like watching Alice in Wonderland - Yar'Adua is the Cheshire cat that smiles and says very little, Obasanjo is the stupid Dodo; Iyabo, the greedy Duchess; James Ibori, the Knave of Hearts who stole the tarts (he probably has and not the edible sort); the attorney-general is the idiotic King of Hearts who doesn't understand anything about justice... and the Nigerian people are like the doormice who keep getting dunked in boiling hot tea by their politicians. When will the Nigerian people wake up like Alice and tell the politicians that they are nothing but a pack of cards - and jokers at that?
-A message posted on leadershipnigeria.com last week by a foreigner as a response to the removal of Nuhu Ribadu

Original_Search_Title: There's More to a Banana Peel Than Meets the Eye
Headline: Rwanda: There's More to a Banana Peel Than Meets the Eye
Body_Text:
With bananas being a staple food, every week thousands of banana peels are thrown away. That is big waste, because they can be transformed into fuel blocks, which you might use for cooking your next banana meal.
It is indeed a brilliant idea: to collect banana peels and other garbage, turn them into flour which you then compact into fuel blocks.

Original_Search_Title: Talks Near Collapse
Headline: Congo-Kinshasa: Talks Near Collapse
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Rebels are being blamed for the slow progress of talks seeking to end fighting in the eastern region of the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC).
The negotiations between the government and rebels of the National Congress for the Defence of the People, known by its French acronym CNDP, opened Dec. 8 in Nairobi, but it is emerging that the rebels' demands that the talks concentrate on the situation in the whole country rather than the conflict in the east, is the reason why there has not been significant headway in the talks taking place in the Kenyan capital.

Original_Search_Title: Fruit Producers Suffer Heavy Losses
Headline: Mozambique: Fruit Producers Suffer Heavy Losses
Body_Text:
Maputo — Mozambican fruit producers have lost about two million US dollars because of a ban on exporting fruit to South Africa, following notification of a fruit fly infestation.
